members.name: DivBackward
members.signature: DivBackward

methods.name: dispatch
methods.signature: int dispatch(backend::IBackend & be, const int & a, const int & b, const int & shape, Dtype dt)
methods.summary: Forward dispatch hook called by BinaryKernel::forward.

parameters.name: be
parameters.description: Active backend (CPU Accelerate or GPU MLX).

returns.description: Output storage holding $a / b$.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & grad_out)
methods.summary: Compute the storage-level gradients for both operands (quotient rule).
methods.extended:
**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{dA} = \frac{1}{b} \odot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}, \qquad
  \mathrm{dB} = -\frac{a}{b^2} \odot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}
$$

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Upstream gradient at the broadcasted output shape.

returns.description: ``(dA, dB)`` where ``dA = grad_out / b_broadcast`` and ``dB = -(grad_out * a_broadcast) / b_broadcast^2``.  Both still at output shape; :func:`BinaryKernel::apply` performs the broadcast reduction.

notes:
The implementation materialises intermediates (``b_sq``, ``g_times_a``,
``div_by_b_sq``) explicitly so each transient storage can be released
as soon as it is no longer needed.

warns.annotation: Division by zero in $b$ propagates IEEE-754 $\pm\infty$ / NaN into the
gradient; downstream optimizers should handle this case.

methods.name: grad_formula_impl
methods.signature: int grad_formula_impl(const int & grad_out, const int & a, const int & b)
methods.summary: Graph-mode (TensorImpl) variant of the gradient.

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Forward inputs, already broadcast-expanded to ``out_shape_`` by BinaryKernel.

returns.description: ``(grad_out / b, -grad_out * a / b^2)``.

members.summary: Autograd node for element-wise division $y = a / b$ with NumPy broadcasting.
members.extended:
Saves both forward inputs because the quotient rule for $\partial L /
\partial b$ requires both $a$ and $b$.  Broadcast-reduces each branch back
to the original input shape via :func:`sum_to_shape` inside
:func:`BinaryKernel::apply`.

**Math**

$$
  y = \frac{a}{b}, \qquad
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial a} = \frac{1}{b} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}, \qquad
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -\frac{a}{b^2} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}
$$

**Shape**

Inputs follow NumPy broadcasting; output has the broadcasted shape.  Each
gradient is then reduced to the original input's shape.

**See Also**

AddBackward, SubBackward, MulBackward
notes:
CPU dispatch uses Accelerate ``vDSP_vdiv`` / ``vDSP_vdivD`` for F32 / F64.
GPU dispatch uses MLX's broadcast-aware ``divide`` primitive.  Division
by zero follows IEEE-754 semantics (yields $\pm\infty$ or NaN); no
explicit guard is inserted.

attributes.name: kSavesInputs
attributes.description: ``true`` (inherited default from BinaryKernel) — both forward inputs are retained for the quotient-rule backward pass.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"div"``, schema version 1, ``AmpPolicy::Promote``, deterministic.

members.name: DropBlockBackward
members.signature: DropBlockBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & a, int block_size, double p, double eps, Generator * gen)
methods.summary: Sample the seed mask, dilate into blocks, and apply.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: 4-D input tensor of shape ``(N, C, H, W)``.

parameters.name: block_size
parameters.description: Side length of each dropped block.  Must be strictly positive.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical guard added to the denominator when computing $\gamma$ to avoid division by zero on small spatial extents.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is not 4-D, ``p`` is outside $[0, 1)$, ``block_size <= 0``, or deterministic mode is on without a Generator.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Backward: multiply ``grad_out`` by the saved keep mask.

members.summary: Autograd node for DropBlock — structured spatial dropout for 4-D convolutional feature maps (Ghiasi, Lin & Le 2018).
members.extended:
A sparse seed mask of Bernoulli samples is generated at the
per-element rate
$\gamma = p \cdot \dfrac{H W}{B^2 (H - B + 1)(W - B + 1) + \varepsilon}$
(with $B$ = ``block_size``) and then dilated by the backend into
contiguous $B \times B$ keep blocks so that whole spatial patches
are zeroed together rather than uncorrelated single pixels.  This is
far more effective than element-wise dropout on convolutional
outputs where neighbouring pixels are strongly correlated.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (name ``"drop_block"``, version 1, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``).

attributes.name: p_
attributes.description: Target block-level drop rate.

attributes.name: mask_
attributes.description: The dilated $\{0, 1\}$ keep mask, same shape as the input, saved for the backward multiply.

members.name: DropPathBackward
members.signature: DropPathBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & a, double p, bool scale_by_keep, Generator * gen)
methods.summary: Apply per-sample stochastic depth.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any rank $\geq 1$; the first axis is the batch axis.

parameters.name: scale_by_keep
parameters.description: If ``true``, surviving samples are multiplied by $1 / (1 - p)$ to preserve the expectation of the residual branch.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

raises.description: If ``p`` is outside $[0, 1)$ or deterministic mode is on without a Generator.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Backward: multiply ``grad_out`` by the saved per-sample mask.

members.summary: Autograd node for DropPath — per-sample stochastic depth (Huang, Sun, Liu & Weinberger 2016) used in residual / transformer blocks.
members.extended:
A single Bernoulli value is drawn per sample (mask shape
$(N, 1, 1, \ldots)$) and broadcast over every other dimension, so
either the entire residual contribution of a sample survives or it
is killed entirely.  When ``scale_by_keep`` is true the surviving
samples are pre-scaled by $1 / (1 - p)$ so that the *expectation*
of the residual branch matches a deterministic forward.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (name ``"drop_path"``, version 1, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``).

attributes.name: mask_
attributes.description: Per-sample mask broadcast to the full input shape; saved for the backward.

members.name: DropoutBackward
members.signature: DropoutBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & a, double p, bool training, Generator * gen)
methods.summary: Sample the Bernoulli mask and apply inverted-dropout scaling.
methods.extended:
**Math**

$$y_i = \begin{cases}
    \dfrac{x_i}{1 - p} & \text{with probability } 1 - p\\[4pt]
    0 & \text{with probability } p
  \end{cases}$$

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Drop probability in $[0, 1)$.  Must be strictly less than 1 (probability 1 would zero everything and produce NaN under the $1 / (1 - p)$ scaling).

parameters.name: training
parameters.description: If ``false``, the forward is a pass-through.

parameters.name: gen
parameters.description: Optional explicit RNG.  ``nullptr`` selects the process-wide ``default_generator()``; under deterministic mode the call raises if ``gen == nullptr`` and ``p > 0``.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

raises.description: If ``p`` is outside $[0, 1)$, or under deterministic mode if no Generator is provided.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Apply the saved mask to ``grad_out``.
methods.extended:
Returns ``{grad_out * mask_}`` when a mask is stored; otherwise
(pass-through case) returns ``{clone(grad_out)}``.

members.summary: Autograd node for standard element-wise (inverted) dropout.
members.extended:
During training, each scalar element of the input is independently
masked with probability ``p`` and surviving elements are rescaled by
$1 / (1 - p)$.  The *already-scaled* mask is saved in :member:`mask_`
so the backward needs only one multiply — no extra scaling step.
When ``p == 0`` or ``training == false`` the node is wired with an
empty mask and :func:`apply` clones ``grad_out`` unchanged.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (name ``"dropout"``, version 1, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``).  Marked non-deterministic because the mask depends on the Generator state.

attributes.name: p_
attributes.description: Drop probability used by the forward; stored for the backward. ``0`` indicates pass-through.

attributes.name: mask_
attributes.description: The scaled Bernoulli mask $m \cdot 1/(1 - p)$ saved for backward. Empty when ``p_ == 0``.

members.name: DropoutNdBackward
members.signature: DropoutNdBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & a, double p, bool training, Generator * gen)
methods.summary: Sample a channel mask and broadcast-multiply with the input.
methods.extended:
**Math**

$$y_{n,c,\ldots} = \begin{cases}
    \dfrac{x_{n,c,\ldots}}{1 - p} & \text{with probability } 1 - p\\[4pt]
    \mathbf{0} & \text{with probability } p
  \end{cases}$$

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of rank $\geq 2$ in layout $(N, C, \text{spatial}\ldots)$.

parameters.name: training
parameters.description: If ``false``, the forward is a pass-through.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a`` has fewer than 2 dimensions, ``p`` is outside $[0, 1)$, or deterministic mode is on without a Generator.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Backward: multiply ``grad_out`` by the saved full-resolution mask.

members.summary: Autograd node for channel-wise Dropout.
members.extended:
A single Bernoulli value is drawn per ``(batch, channel)`` pair on a
compact $(N, C, 1, 1, \ldots)$ mask, then expanded by the backend
to the full input shape so that entire feature-map channels are
zeroed together.  This is the C++ kernel behind the Python
``Dropout1d`` / ``Dropout2d`` / ``Dropout3d`` modules — input rank
must be at least 2 and the layout is assumed to be
$(N, C, \text{spatial}\ldots)$.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (name ``"dropoutnd"``, version 1, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``).

attributes.name: p_
attributes.description: Drop probability used by the forward; stored for the backward.

attributes.name: mask_
attributes.description: Full-resolution mask after the broadcast — same shape as the input.  Saved so the backward is a single multiply.

exception can be formatted directly into ``what()`` without depending on
the :cpp:enum:`Dtype` header.  Mapped to ``lucid._C.engine.DtypeMismatch``

members.name: Edge
members.signature: Edge

methods.name: Edge
methods.signature: void Edge()
methods.summary: Default-constructed empty edge with ``node == nullptr``.

methods.name: is_valid
methods.signature: bool is_valid()
methods.summary: Returns whether this edge points to a live consumer node.

returns.description: ``true`` when ``node != nullptr``, ``false`` for empty edges.

members.summary: A directed edge in the backward computation graph.
members.extended:
An ``Edge`` connects a producer node to a specific input slot
(``input_nr``) of a consumer node.  The owning ``shared_ptr`` keeps the
consumer alive while at least one producer references it, forming the
ownership chain that mirrors the forward computation.  ``input_nr`` is
the positional index into the consumer's :meth:`Node::apply` output
vector where the arriving gradient must land.

**See Also**

:class:`Node` — consumes edges as ``next_edges_`` during backward.
notes:
An invalid edge (``node == nullptr``) marks a structural hole — for
example an input that does not require grad — and is skipped by the
engine without raising.

attributes.name: node
attributes.description: The consumer node that should receive a gradient along this edge.

attributes.name: input_nr
attributes.description: Positional input index on ``node`` that this edge targets.  The engine writes the produced gradient into ``apply``'s output at this slot.

members.name: EluBackward
members.signature: EluBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & a, double alpha)
methods.summary: Custom forward that captures ``alpha`` on the backward node.

returns.description: Forward output with autograd edges wired to ``EluBackward``.

methods.name: cpu_kernel
methods.signature: CpuStorage cpu_kernel(const CpuStorage & a, const int & out_shape, Dtype dt, double alpha)
methods.summary: CPU kernel mirroring ``forward`` for the dispatcher-driven path.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Eager-mode backward: dispatches to ``IBackend::elu_backward`` with the captured ``alpha_``.

members.summary: Autograd node for the Exponential Linear Unit $y = x$ if $x \ge 0$ else $\alpha\,(e^x - 1)$ (Clevert et al. 2015).
members.extended:
Like Leaky ReLU, the scalar hyper-parameter $\alpha$ is captured on the
node via a custom ``forward()`` so the backward kernel sees a concrete
value rather than chasing it through call sites.  The piecewise backward
itself is delegated to the backend because the conditional branch is not
expressible through the generic storage primitives.

**Math**

$$y = \begin{cases} x, & x \ge 0 \\ \alpha\,(e^x - 1), & x < 0 \end{cases},
  \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}
  = \begin{cases} 1, & x \ge 0 \\ \alpha\,e^x, & x < 0 \end{cases}$$

**References**

Clevert, Unterthiner, Hochreiter, "Fast and Accurate Deep Network Learning
by Exponential Linear Units (ELUs)", [arXiv:1511.07289](https://arxiv.org/abs/1511.07289).

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"elu"``, ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32``.

attributes.name: alpha_
attributes.description: Negative-saturation scale captured at forward time; defaults to ``1.0``.

members.name: EmbeddingBackward
members.signature: EmbeddingBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & weight, const int & indices, int padding_idx)
methods.summary: Look up rows of ``weight`` at positions given by ``indices``.
methods.extended:
**Math**

$$y[\ldots] = W[\text{idx}[\ldots]],
  \qquad y[\ldots] = 0 \text{ if } \text{idx}[\ldots] = \text{padding\_idx}.$$

**Shape**

- ``weight`` : $(V, D)$
- ``indices`` : $(\ast)$ integer
- Output : $(\ast, D)$

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Embedding matrix of shape ``(num_embeddings, embed_dim)``. Must be exactly 2-D.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description: Integer-typed tensor of any shape with values in ``[0, num_embeddings)``.

parameters.name: padding_idx
parameters.description: Row whose lookup is forced to zero; pass a negative value to disable.

returns.description: Output of shape $(\ast\,\text{indices.shape}, \text{embed\_dim})$ — the input index shape with an extra trailing dim of ``embed_dim``.

raises.description: If ``weight`` is not 2-D.
If ``weight`` and ``indices`` live on different devices.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Scatter-add backward.
methods.extended:
Returns a single ``dW`` storage of shape ``weight_shape_``,
zero-initialized except where the saved indices accumulate
contributions from ``grad_out``.  Rows at ``padding_idx_``
accumulate nothing.

returns.description: Single-element ``{dW}``.

members.summary: Autograd node for the embedding lookup $y = W[\text{idx}]$ on a $(V, D)$ weight matrix.
members.extended:
Only the weight matrix is a differentiable input.  Integer indices
have no meaningful gradient and are not stored in the standard
``saved_inputs_`` slot — they live in :member:`saved_indices_`
instead, alongside their shape and dtype, so the backward can
scatter-add ``grad_out`` into the correct rows of ``dW``.

Forward dispatches to ``IBackend::embedding_forward`` which gathers
rows from the weight storage at positions given by ``indices``,
zeroing any output row whose index equals ``padding_idx``.  Backward
uses ``IBackend::embedding_backward`` to scatter-add ``grad_out``
into a zero-initialized ``dW`` at those same positions.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (name ``"embedding"``, version 1, ``AmpPolicy::Promote``).

attributes.name: padding_idx_
attributes.description: Index whose row is masked to zero in both forward and backward. ``-1`` disables the masking entirely.

attributes.name: weight_shape_
attributes.description: ``(num_embeddings, embed_dim)``; recovered during backward to size ``dW``.

attributes.name: saved_indices_
attributes.description: Integer index tensor saved from the forward pass.  Not subject to AMP because the dtype is integer.

attributes.name: saved_indices_shape_
attributes.description: Shape of the saved index tensor.

attributes.name: saved_indices_dtype_
attributes.description: Dtype of the saved index tensor (default ``I64``).

members.name: Engine
members.signature: Engine

methods.name: backward
methods.signature: void backward(const int & root, int grad_seed, bool retain_graph, bool create_graph)
methods.summary: Run reverse-mode automatic differentiation starting from ``root``.
methods.extended:
Walks the computation graph attached to ``root->grad_fn()`` in reverse
topological order, computes per-edge input gradients via each node's
``apply()`` method, accumulates contributions at branch points, and
hands terminal gradients to :class:`AccumulateGrad` for in-place
accumulation into ``leaf.grad``.

parameters.name: root
parameters.description: Output tensor to differentiate.  Must be non-null.  If ``root`` has no ``grad_fn`` it is treated as a leaf and the seed is accumulated directly into ``root->grad``.

parameters.name: grad_seed
parameters.description: Initial gradient injected at ``root``.  An empty ``Storage`` (default) is replaced by a ones-tensor of ``root``'s shape / dtype / device — the common case of differentiating a scalar loss.

parameters.name: retain_graph
parameters.description: When ``false`` (default), each node's ``release_saved()`` is called immediately after its ``apply()`` and ``root->grad_fn`` is cleared on return, so a second ``backward()`` call is impossible.  Pass ``true`` to preserve the graph for multiple backward calls.

parameters.name: create_graph
parameters.description: When ``true``, the backward pass itself is recorded in the autograd graph so that higher-order gradients can be taken on the resulting ``.grad`` tensors.  Implies ``retain_graph=true`` because the forward nodes are re-used by the new graph. Concrete nodes must override ``apply_for_graph`` for this mode; nodes lacking graph support raise a clear error naming the op.

raises.description: If ``root`` is null, if a node returns an ``input_grads`` vector whose size disagrees with its outgoing edges (and both are non-empty), or if ``validate_versions()`` detects an in-place mutation of a saved input tensor.

notes:
The engine consumes nodes destructively when ``retain_graph=false``:
``release_saved()`` frees the forward tensors each node had stashed
for its backward formula, and ``clear_grad_fn()`` on ``root``
severs the producer→graph reference so the chain of shared_ptrs
collapses.

members.summary: Reverse-mode autograd engine — drives the backward pass over a recorded computation graph rooted at a single output :class:`TensorImpl`.
members.extended:
``Engine`` is a stateless class that exposes a single static entry point
(:func:`Engine::backward`).  It is never instantiated; all per-call state
(worklist, accumulated gradients, visited set) lives on the stack of the
invoking thread.

Algorithm
---------
1. Compute a reverse-topological ordering of the backward graph using an
   iterative post-order DFS (the recursive form would overflow on deep
   networks).
2. Optionally run a fusion pass that collapses adjacent backward nodes
   (e.g. ``LinearBackward`` + ``ReluBackward``) into a single fused node.
3. Walk the ordering once, calling each node's ``apply()``; gradients
   arriving at a node from multiple producers are summed into a pending
   map before the node is executed.
4. Leaves (tensors with no ``grad_fn``) are reached through their
   :class:`AccumulateGrad` sentinel, which writes the final gradient into
   ``leaf.grad``.

Thread Safety
-------------
Two concurrent ``backward`` calls on the same graph are undefined
behaviour — nodes are consumed and (when ``retain_graph=false``) destroyed
exactly once.  Independent graphs may be driven from separate threads.

**See Also**

:class:`AccumulateGrad` : terminal node that writes into ``leaf.grad``.
:class:`Node` : abstract base for every backward graph node.

members.summary: Autograd node for the element-wise error function $y = \mathrm{erf}(x) = (2/\sqrt{\pi})\int_0^x e^{-t^2}\,dt$.
members.extended:
Saves the input ``x`` so the backward pass can build $e^{-x^2}$.
Defined on all of $\mathbb{R}$ with range $(-1, 1)$.

**Math**

$$y = \mathrm{erf}(x), \qquad
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,e^{-x^2},
\qquad
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} =
\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}\,e^{-x^2}\,\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}.$$
notes: Dispatch: Accelerate ``erf`` kernel (CPU) / MLX ``erf`` (GPU).

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered as ``"erf"`` with ``AmpPolicy::Promote``.

methods.name: grad_formula_impl
methods.signature: int grad_formula_impl(const int & g, const int &, const int & out)
methods.summary: Graph-mode backward: $\partial x = \tfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,e^{\mathrm{out}^2}\,g$ using the saved output, kept inside the autograd graph.

members.summary: Autograd node for the element-wise inverse error function $y = \mathrm{erfinv}(x)$.
members.extended:
Saves the *output* $y$ so the backward pass can form $e^{y^2}$ without
needing to invert $\mathrm{erf}$ a second time.  Defined on $(-1, 1)$;
the gradient is unbounded as $x \to \pm 1$.

**Math**

$$y = \mathrm{erfinv}(x), \qquad
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,e^{y^2},
\qquad
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} =
\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\,e^{y^2}\,\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}.$$
notes: Dispatch: Accelerate ``erfinv`` kernel (CPU) / MLX ``erfinv`` (GPU).

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered as ``"erfinv"`` with ``AmpPolicy::Promote``.

attributes.name: kSavesInput
attributes.description: ``false``.

attributes.name: kSavesOutput
attributes.description: ``true`` — saved tensor is $y = \mathrm{erfinv}(x)$.

methods.name: format_with_context
methods.signature: int format_with_context(const int & msg)
methods.summary: Returns ``"<op_>: <msg> [trace=<chain>]"`` (the ``[trace=...]`` suffix is omitted when the current :class:`ErrorContext` trace is empty). Used by the throw helpers that take a free-form message.

members.summary: Constructs and throws typed exceptions, annotating each message with the owning op name and the current :class:`ErrorContext` trace.
members.extended:
Every throw helper formats its message as
``"<op>: <msg> [trace=<chain>]"`` (the ``[trace=...]`` suffix is omitted
when the context stack is empty) and then dispatches to the appropriate
:class:`LucidError` subclass.  Helpers are marked ``[[noreturn]]`` so
callers can use them as drop-in replacements for ``throw`` without
confusing the compiler's control-flow analysis.

:class:`ErrorBuilder` is stateless beyond the owning op name — it never
holds a reference to a tensor and is safe to construct on the stack at
the throw site.

**See Also**

:class:`ErrorContext` : Owns the thread-local trace that ``ErrorBuilder``
    consumes.
:class:`Validator` : Layered on top of ``ErrorBuilder`` for tensor
    precondition checks.

parameters.name: op_name
parameters.description: Identifier of the op responsible for the throw.  Three overloads are provided so call sites can pass whichever literal type is most convenient.

members.name: ErrorContext
members.signature: ErrorContext

methods.name: push
methods.signature: void push(int op_name)
methods.summary: Pushes an op name onto the calling thread's context stack.

parameters.name: op_name
parameters.description: Identifier for the op entering scope.  Moved into the stack; subsequent :cpp:func:`trace` calls will include it until a matching :cpp:func:`pop`.

methods.name: pop
methods.signature: void pop()
methods.summary: Pops the most recently pushed name from the calling thread's stack.
notes:
No-op if the stack is empty — callers do not need to guard against
over-pop in error-recovery paths.

methods.name: trace
methods.signature: int trace()
methods.summary: Returns the current call stack joined by ``" > "``.

returns.description: ``"outer > inner > leaf"`` style summary, or an empty string when the stack is empty.  Callers can therefore test for emptiness to decide whether to emit the ``[trace=...]`` suffix at all.

methods.name: reset
methods.signature: void reset()
methods.summary: Clears the calling thread's stack.
notes:
Intended for test teardown or error-recovery paths that need to drop
a partially-built trace (e.g. after catching across a long-running
dispatch loop).  Production code should normally rely on
:class:`ErrorContextGuard` for balanced push/pop.

members.summary: Thread-local call-stack of operation names used to annotate error messages.
members.extended:
Each entry is the human-readable name of an op (e.g. ``"conv2d"``); the
stack records the current chain of in-flight ops so that any exception
can be formatted with a ``[trace=...]`` suffix identifying exactly where
the failure occurred.  The stack is intentionally a flat
``std::vector<std::string>`` — the depth is bounded by the model's
effective call chain and the cost of an extra ``emplace_back`` /
``pop_back`` is negligible compared to the op itself.

All members are ``static`` and act on a thread-local container, so no
instance is required and no synchronisation is performed.  Each thread
owns its own private stack; the stack is **not** propagated across
thread boundaries (e.g. when work is dispatched to a background executor),
so callers in worker threads should push their own context entries.

**See Also**

:class:`ErrorContextGuard` : RAII wrapper that is preferred to manual
    push/pop in any scope that may throw.
:class:`ErrorBuilder` : Consumes :cpp:func:`trace` when formatting
    thrown exceptions.

members.name: ErrorContextGuard
members.signature: ErrorContextGuard

methods.name: ErrorContextGuard
methods.signature: void ErrorContextGuard(int op_name)
methods.summary: Pushes op_name onto the thread's :class:`ErrorContext` stack.

parameters.name: op_name
parameters.description: Identifier for the in-flight op; moved into the stack.

methods.name: ~ErrorContextGuard
methods.signature: void ~ErrorContextGuard()
methods.summary: Pops the most recently pushed entry.

methods.name: operator=
methods.signature: ErrorContextGuard & operator=(const ErrorContextGuard &)

members.summary: RAII wrapper that pushes an op name on construction and pops it on destruction.
members.extended:
Preferred over the manual :cpp:func:`ErrorContext::push` /
:cpp:func:`ErrorContext::pop` pair in any scope that may throw — the
destructor guarantees the stack is balanced even when an exception
propagates through.  Move and copy are deleted to forbid transferring
ownership of a stack frame between scopes, which would break the LIFO
invariant.

parameters.name: op_name
parameters.description: Identifier pushed onto the thread's :class:`ErrorContext` stack on construction.  The corresponding pop is performed by the destructor.

members.name: ErrorPayload
members.signature: ErrorPayload
members.summary: Plain-old-data error descriptor carried by the error arm of :cpp:class:`Result`.
members.extended:
Designed to be trivially copyable so a ``Result`` can be moved across
async / FFI boundaries without pulling in the C++ exception machinery.
The message is a fully-formatted human-readable string — for typed
inspection (shapes, dtypes, ...) the caller should re-throw via
:cpp:func:`Result::value_or_throw` and catch the resulting typed
:class:`LucidError`.

attributes.name: code
attributes.description: Category of the failure.  Defaults to ``ErrorCode::Internal`` so a default-constructed payload is always a recognisable error rather than a misleading ``Ok``.

attributes.name: msg
attributes.description: Final ``what()``-style text.  May be empty when the category alone conveys the information.

members.summary: Autograd node for element-wise exponential $y = e^x$.
members.extended:
Saves the *output* $y$ rather than the input because the backward
formula is $\partial y / \partial x = e^x = y$ and reusing the saved
output avoids a second ``vvexpf`` pass.

**Math**

$$y = e^x, \qquad
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = e^x = y, \qquad
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} =
y\,\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}.$$
notes:
Dispatch: Accelerate ``vvexpf``/``vvexp`` (CPU) / MLX ``exp`` (GPU).
Large-magnitude positive inputs overflow to ``+inf``; large-magnitude
negative inputs flush to ``0``.

attributes.description: Registered as ``"exp"`` with ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32`` — half- precision inputs are upcast to F32 prior to dispatch to avoid premature overflow / underflow.

members.summary: Autograd node for element-wise floor: $y = \lfloor x \rfloor$.
members.extended:
Piecewise-constant; derivative is zero almost everywhere and undefined
at integer boundaries.  ``kHasGradient = false``; the backward node is
registered for schema completeness only.

**Math**

$$y_i = \lfloor x_i \rfloor$$  (largest integer not greater than $x_i$).
notes:
Integer dtypes are a no-op.  Negative values round toward $-\infty$
(NOT toward zero — that is ``trunc``).

attributes.description: ``false`` — nothing to save.

attributes.name: kHasGradient
attributes.description: ``false`` — autograd wiring is skipped.

members.name: FunctionCtx
members.signature: FunctionCtx

methods.name: FunctionCtx
methods.signature: void FunctionCtx()
methods.summary: Construct a ``FunctionCtx`` for a custom ``Function::forward`` call.
methods.extended:
The newly created context starts with empty ``saved_tensors_`` and
``extras_`` containers; the user's ``forward`` body populates them
via :func:`save_for_backward` and :func:`store` before returning.
Defaulted — no resources need acquisition at construction.

methods.name: save_for_backward
methods.signature: void save_for_backward(int)
methods.summary: Save tensors needed for the backward pass.
methods.extended:
Stores strong ``shared_ptr`` references so that the underlying
:class:`TensorImpl` buffers remain alive until backward completes,
even if the Python-side handle goes out of scope.  Mirrors the
semantics of :meth:`lucid.autograd.FunctionCtx.save_for_backward`.

parameters.name: tensors
parameters.description: Tensors to save, in the order ``saved_tensors()`` should return them.  Moved into the context.

notes:
Each call overwrites the previously saved list; saving twice does not
accumulate.  To stash non-tensor data (shapes, axes, scalars) use the
:func:`store` / :func:`load` extras bag instead.

members.summary: Per-call context shared between a custom op's forward and backward passes.
members.extended:
``FunctionCtx`` is the C++-side counterpart of :class:`lucid.autograd.Function`'s
Python ``ctx`` object.  A fresh instance is created on each ``Function.apply``
invocation and is passed unchanged as the first argument to both
``forward(ctx, ...)`` and ``backward(ctx, grad_output)``, providing the only
legal channel for state to cross the forward/backward boundary.

**See Also**

:class:`lucid.autograd.FunctionCtx` — Python-facing wrapper class.
:class:`PythonBackwardNode` — owns one ``FunctionCtx`` per custom op call.
notes:
The object is heap-allocated and jointly owned by the Python wrapper and
the associated :class:`PythonBackwardNode`, so it outlives the forward
call and remains valid for the entire backward traversal.  Capturing
tensors through Python closures instead of saving them through ``ctx``
bypasses this ownership tracking and leaks memory.

attributes.name: saved_tensors_
attributes.description: Tensors stored via :func:`save_for_backward` as strong references so they survive until the backward pass executes.

attributes.name: extras_
attributes.description: Generic Python key/value bag mirroring ``ctx.<name> = value`` / ``ctx.<name>`` access from Python.  Used for scalars, shape tuples, hyperparameters, and other non-tensor state.

members.name: FusionPass
members.signature: FusionPass

methods.name: FusionPass
methods.signature: void FusionPass()
methods.summary: Construct a fresh ``FusionPass`` with zeroed counters.
methods.extended:
Defaulted; the only state is the :attr:`stats_` ``Stats`` member,
whose POD counters are zero-initialised by their in-class default
initialisers.  Each :func:`run` call re-zeroes ``stats_`` at entry,
so re-using a single instance across backward calls is safe.

methods.name: run
methods.signature: int run(int & graph)
methods.summary: Scan ``graph`` for fusible patterns and apply matches in place.
methods.extended:
Walks the node list from front to back examining a 2-node window for
Linear+Activation fusions and a 4-node window for Scaled Dot-Product
Attention fusions.  When a pattern matches and the corresponding
``try_fuse_*`` helper returns ``true``, the consumed (non-surviving)
nodes are erased from ``graph`` and the index counter is rewound so
the merged node is re-examined on the next iteration.

parameters.name: graph
parameters.description: Topologically ordered backward graph, root first.  Modified in place: fused-away nodes are erased.

returns.description: Total number of fusions performed during this call (== :func:`Stats::total`).

notes:
The :attr:`stats_` counters are zeroed at function entry, so calling
:func:`run` repeatedly on the same instance is safe.

methods.name: stats
methods.signature: const Stats & stats()
methods.summary: Read the per-pattern counters collected by the last :func:`run`.

returns.description: Reference to this pass's counters.  Stable until the next call to :func:`run`, which zeroes them.

methods.summary: Attempt to fuse a Linear backward node with an adjacent activation backward node.

parameters.name: linear_node

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: Which activation variant this is (``LinearRelu`` / ``LinearGelu`` / ``LinearSilu``).  Selects the fused kernel that will eventually replace the pair.

returns.description: ``true`` when the pair was successfully fused (caller removes ``act_node`` from the graph), ``false`` to skip.

notes:
Current implementation is a placeholder returning ``true``
unconditionally — the actual fused-kernel substitution is staged
for a follow-up phase.

returns.description: ``true`` on success; caller removes ``bn`` and ``relu`` from the graph and leaves ``conv`` as the surviving fused node.

methods.name: try_fuse_sdpa

methods.summary: Attempt to fuse a four-node Scaled Dot-Product Attention backward chain.

parameters.name: mm1

parameters.name: scale

parameters.name: softmax
parameters.description: Backward node for the row-wise softmax.

parameters.name: mm2

methods.name: try_fuse_layernorm
methods.signature: bool try_fuse_layernorm(int & window)
methods.summary: Attempt to fuse a sliding window of LayerNorm backward sub-nodes.

parameters.name: window
parameters.description: Candidate backward nodes implementing the LayerNorm formula (mean, variance, normalisation, affine).  The vector may be modified in place when nodes are consumed.

returns.description: ``true`` if the window matched a LayerNorm pattern and was fused.  Currently always returns ``false`` (LayerNorm fusion not yet implemented).

members.summary: Backward-graph fusion pass that collapses adjacent fusible nodes.
members.extended:
:class:`FusionPass` is invoked once per backward call, just before the
Engine begins traversing the graph.  It scans the topologically-ordered
node list for known multi-node patterns (see :class:`FusionPattern`) and
rewrites them in place so that subsequent traversal launches fewer
kernels and allocates fewer intermediate gradient buffers.

The pass is stateless across runs except for the :attr:`stats_` counters,
which are reset on each call to :func:`run`.  No node ownership changes:
raw pointers reference nodes owned by their parent edges' ``shared_ptr``.

**See Also**

:func:`run_fusion_pass` — convenience entry point used by
    ``Engine::backward()``.
notes:
**Cost / benefit trade-off.** Fusing eliminates intermediate gradient
buffers and kernel-launch overhead but tightens the dependency between
adjacent ops — the fused node must materialise all saved tensors that
either sub-op would have needed independently.  For sub-graphs that
already share most of their saved state (Linear+activation, Conv+BN)
this is a clear win; for unrelated ops it is not.

**Detection only.** All ``try_fuse_*`` helpers currently return ``true``
as placeholders — actual kernel substitution will be layered in per
pattern in subsequent phases.  The counters in :attr:`Stats` therefore
reflect *detected* opportunities rather than executed fusions.
**Invariants.**

- FusionPass never takes ownership of any :class:`Node`.
- A successful fusion erases the consumed nodes from the graph vector
  and leaves the surviving (fused) node in place at the same index.

attributes.name: stats_
attributes.description: Per-pattern fusion counts collected during the most recent :func:`run` call.  Cleared at the entry of each run.

members.name: GeluBackward
members.signature: GeluBackward

methods.name: dispatch
methods.signature: int dispatch(backend::IBackend & be, const int & a, const int & s, Dtype dt)
methods.summary: Forward — calls ``IBackend::gelu`` to compute the tanh-approximation GeLU $y = 0.5\,x\,(1 + \tanh(\sqrt{2/\pi}\,(x + 0.044715\,x^3)))$.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Eager-mode backward: dispatches to ``IBackend::gelu_backward`` with the saved input and the upstream gradient.

members.summary: Autograd node for the tanh-approximation GeLU $y = 0.5\,x\,(1 + \tanh(\sqrt{2/\pi}\,(x + 0.044715\,x^3)))$.
members.extended:
The approximate form (Hendrycks & Gimpel 2016, eq. 3) is used by default
when callers do not request the exact erf-based variant; it is the form
implemented by ``F.gelu(x, approximate="tanh")`` in the Python wrapper.
The backward is delegated to the backend because the analytic derivative
involves both ``tanh`` and ``sech^2`` over a cubic polynomial of $x$, and
fusing them in a single kernel is materially faster than chaining storage
primitives.

**Math**

$$y = \tfrac{x}{2}\,\bigl(1 + \tanh\bigl(\sqrt{2/\pi}\,(x + 0.044715\,x^3)\bigr)\bigr)$$

**References**

Hendrycks & Gimpel, "Gaussian Error Linear Units (GELUs)", [arXiv:1606.08415](https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.08415).

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"gelu"``, ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32`` — the cubic + ``tanh`` chain is not numerically safe in float16.

members.name: GeluExactBackward
members.signature: GeluExactBackward

methods.name: dispatch
methods.signature: int dispatch(backend::IBackend & be, const int & a, const int & s, Dtype dt)
methods.summary: Forward — calls ``IBackend::gelu_exact`` to compute the erf-based exact GeLU $y = 0.5\,x\,(1 + \mathrm{erf}(x/\sqrt{2}))$.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Eager-mode backward: dispatches to ``IBackend::gelu_exact_backward``.

members.summary: Autograd node for the exact (Gaussian-CDF) GeLU $y = 0.5\,x\,(1 + \mathrm{erf}(x/\sqrt{2}))$.
members.extended:
Routed to by ``F.gelu(x, approximate="none")``.  Implementing this as a
dedicated op (instead of the prior ten-op Python composition built around
``_erf_approx``) lets the GPU backend dispatch to a single MPSGraph node
and keeps the autograd graph compact for memory accounting.

**Math**

$$y = \tfrac{x}{2}\,\Bigl(1 + \mathrm{erf}\bigl(\tfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\bigr)\Bigr),
  \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}
  = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\Bigl(1 + \mathrm{erf}\bigl(\tfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\bigr)\Bigr)
    + \tfrac{x}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-x^2/2}$$

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"gelu_exact"``, ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32`` — matches the tanh-approx variant for consistent AMP semantics across approximations.

:func:`next_uniform_float` — single-float variant.

methods.name: next_uniform_float
methods.signature: float next_uniform_float()
methods.summary: Generates a single uniform float in ``[0, 1)`` from the upper 24 bits of one Philox output word.

returns.description: Uniformly distributed value in :math:`[0, 1)`.

notes:
Internally derives one ``uint32_t`` from
:func:`next_uint32x4`, drops the lower 8 bits for uniformity in
the IEEE-754 ``float`` mantissa, and divides by :math:`2^{24}`.
The three unused 32-bit words from the Philox block are
discarded; callers that need higher throughput should batch
through :func:`next_uint32x4`.
Not thread-safe — see :func:`next_uint32x4`.

methods.name: mutex
methods.signature: int & mutex()
methods.summary: Returns the internal mutex so callers can hold it across batched sampling calls.

returns.description: Reference to the mutex guarding ``seed_`` and ``counter_``.

notes:
Exposed so the Python binding can wrap a whole vector draw under
one lock acquisition rather than re-locking on every uniform.

methods.name: counter
methods.signature: int counter()
methods.summary: Returns the current counter value.

returns.description: The number of Philox blocks consumed since the last :func:`set_seed` (modulo any direct :func:`set_counter` override).

notes:
Useful when serialising RNG state for a checkpoint: capture
``(seed(), counter())`` and restore via :func:`set_seed` +
:func:`set_counter` on reload.

methods.name: set_counter
methods.signature: void set_counter(int c)
methods.summary: Directly overrides the counter — used to restore a saved RNG state from a checkpoint.

parameters.name: c
parameters.description: New counter value.  Replaces ``counter_`` atomically with respect to other field accesses guarded by ``mu_``.

notes:
Normal use should call :func:`set_seed` first to fix the seed,
then :func:`set_counter` to restore the position within that
seed epoch.  This pair reproduces any previously observed state
exactly.

members.summary: Philox-4x32-10 counter-based pseudo-random number generator.
members.extended:
Owns a ``(seed, counter)`` pair plus a mutex guarding both fields.
Each call to :func:`next_uint32x4` produces four independent 32-bit
uniforms and increments the counter by one; the (seed, counter) tuple
uniquely identifies every emitted block, which is what makes the
stream reproducible across process invocations.

**See Also**

:func:`default_generator` — global Generator used when ops are
    called without an explicit ``generator=`` kwarg.
examples: Reproducible draws across two runs::
notes: Invariants enforced by the implementation:

1. ``counter_`` is monotonically increasing within a seed epoch.
2. ``(seed_, counter_)`` uniquely identifies each produced block,
   making the sequence fully reproducible.
3. ``mu_`` must be held by all mutating accessors when the generator
   is shared across threads.

Sampling calls are not thread-safe on their own — wrap them in a
``std::lock_guard<std::mutex>`` over :func:`mutex` when sharing.

attributes.name: seed_
attributes.description: The seed value last set via :func:`set_seed` (or the constructor). Read-only after construction except through :func:`set_seed`.

attributes.name: counter_
attributes.description: Monotonically increasing call counter; reset to ``0`` by :func:`set_seed`.  May be overridden directly via :func:`set_counter` for checkpoint restore.

attributes.name: mu_
attributes.description: Guards both ``seed_`` and ``counter_`` so concurrent samplers observe a consistent state.

members.name: GlobalResponseNormBackward
members.signature: GlobalResponseNormBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, double eps)
methods.summary: Run the forward pass.
methods.extended:
Computes the per ``(n, c)`` L2 spatial norm, normalises it by
the per-image mean across channels, modulates ``x`` by the
result, applies the per-channel affine + residual, caches
``saved_Nx_``, and (when grad mode is on) registers ``this`` as
the ``grad_fn`` of the returned output.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: 4-D input tensor of shape ``(N, C, H, W)``.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical-stability constant in the cross-channel mean.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Compute gradients for the three saved inputs.
methods.extended:
Returns ``{dx, d_gamma, d_beta}`` in the saved-input order
using the cached ``saved_Nx_``.

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Incoming gradient, same shape as the forward output.

returns.description: Three-element vector ``{dx, d_gamma, d_beta}``.

members.summary: Autograd node for Global Response Normalization (GRN), as used in ConvNeXt V2.
members.extended:
For 4-D input of shape ``(N, C, H, W)``, GRN first computes the per
``(n, c)`` L2 norm over the spatial axes, then normalises each
channel's norm by the mean across channels, and finally applies a
learned per-channel affine that *modulates* the input rather than
replacing it:

$$
  G_{n,c} = \|x_{n,c,:,:}\|_2,
  \qquad
  N_{n,c} = \frac{G_{n,c}}{\frac{1}{C}\sum_{c'} G_{n,c'} + \epsilon}
$$

$$
  y_{n,c,h,w} = \gamma_c \,(x_{n,c,h,w}\,N_{n,c}) + \beta_c
                + x_{n,c,h,w}
$$

(The trailing $+ x$ residual matches the ConvNeXt-V2 paper; the
affine is applied to the *modulated* signal rather than to
$\hat{x}$.)  ``saved_Nx_`` stores $N$ broadcast to the per-spatial
shape needed by the backward closed-form.

Inherits ``FuncOp<GlobalResponseNormBackward, 3>`` with three
saved-input slots — ``x``, ``gamma``, and ``beta``.

**References**

Woo et al., "ConvNeXt V2: Co-designing and Scaling ConvNets with
Masked Autoencoders" (CVPR 2023, [arXiv:2301.00808](https://arxiv.org/abs/2301.00808)).

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (``name="global_response_norm"``, ``version=1``, ``amp_policy=ForceFP32``, ``produces_grad=true``).

attributes.name: eps_
attributes.description: Numerical-stability constant in the per-channel normalisation denominator.  Default: ``1e-6``.

attributes.name: saved_Nx_
attributes.description: Normalised global response $N$, shape ``(N, C, 1, 1)``.

members.summary: GPU tensor storage backed by an :class:`mlx::core::array` lazy graph node.
members.extended:
The ``arr`` shared pointer keeps the MLX graph node alive for as long as
this :class:`GpuStorage` (and any :class:`TensorImpl` that holds it) is in
scope.  All GPU-stream operations dispatch through MLX; **never** cast the
MLX array's internal pointer to a CPU pointer — the buffer is GPU-private
until evaluated (see :func:`TensorImpl::eval`).

**See Also**

:class:`CpuStorage`     — CPU sibling variant.
:class:`SharedStorage`  — unified-memory sibling variant.

methods.name: is_enabled
methods.signature: bool is_enabled()
methods.summary: Returns whether autograd is currently active on the calling thread.

returns.description: ``true`` when ops should build autograd nodes, ``false`` when they should run in pure-inference mode.

notes: The value defaults to ``true`` for every newly-spawned thread.

methods.signature: void set_enabled(bool value)
methods.summary: Sets the autograd activity flag for the calling thread.
methods.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`NoGradGuard` — exception-safe scoped toggle.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: ``true`` to enable autograd recording, ``false`` to suppress it.  The change is only visible to the calling thread.

notes:
Prefer :class:`NoGradGuard` over manual paired ``set_enabled``
calls — the RAII form correctly restores the previous flag when
an exception unwinds the stack.

members.summary: Thread-local switch controlling whether autograd records ops on the current thread.
members.extended:
All methods are ``static``: there is no instance state — the class is
a namespace-style holder over a single ``thread_local bool`` defined
in :file:`GradMode.cpp`.  Querying or setting the flag is a single
TLS load/store with no synchronisation cost.

**See Also**

:class:`NoGradGuard` — RAII helper for scoped disabling.
notes:
The default value at thread startup is ``true`` (gradients tracked).
Threads created via :func:`std::thread` or worker pools each begin
with their own fresh ``true``-valued flag.

members.name: GridSampleBackward
members.signature: GridSampleBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & input, const int & grid, int mode, int padding_mode, bool align_corners)
methods.summary: Resample ``input`` at the positions specified by ``grid``.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: 4-D input image of shape ``(N, C, H_in, W_in)``.

parameters.name: grid
parameters.description: Sampling grid of shape ``(N, H_out, W_out, 2)`` with normalised coordinates in $[-1, 1]$.

returns.description: Resampled tensor of shape ``(N, C, H_out, W_out)``.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Backward pass: gradients w.r.t. ``input`` and ``grid``.

members.summary: Autograd node for differentiable image resampling (grid sample).
members.extended:
Reads a 4-D input image at the (typically non-integer) positions specified
by a sampling grid:
$$
  y[n, c, i, j] = \mathcal{I}\bigl(x_{n, c}, \; \text{grid}[n, i, j]\bigr)
$$
where $\mathcal{I}$ is the chosen interpolation kernel and the grid stores
normalised $(x, y)$ coordinates in $[-1, 1]$.  For bilinear interpolation
the four neighbouring pixels around the unnormalised coordinate
$u = (x + 1) \cdot (W_{\text{in}} - 1) / 2$,
$v = (y + 1) \cdot (H_{\text{in}} - 1) / 2$ (under ``align_corners_=true``)
are combined with the standard bilinear weights; analogous neighbourhoods
of four (nearest) or sixteen (bicubic) pixels are used in the other modes.

Out-of-bounds samples are handled by ``padding_mode_``: ``zeros`` returns
$0$, ``border`` clamps the source coordinate, ``reflection`` reflects it
off the image boundary.  The backward distributes ``grad_out`` back to both
the input pixels (through the interpolation weights) and the grid (through
the analytical derivative of the interpolation kernel).

**Math**

Coordinate unnormalisation:
$$
  u = \begin{cases}
    (x + 1) (W_{\text{in}} - 1) / 2 & \text{align\_corners} \\
    (x + 1) W_{\text{in}} / 2 - 0.5 & \text{otherwise}
  \end{cases}
$$
(analogous formula for $v$).

**References**

Jaderberg et al., "Spatial Transformer Networks" (NeurIPS 2015).

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"grid_sample"``, ``AmpPolicy::Promote``.

attributes.name: mode_
attributes.description: Interpolation kernel: ``0`` = bilinear, ``1`` = nearest, ``2`` = bicubic.

attributes.name: padding_mode_
attributes.description: Out-of-bounds policy: ``0`` = zeros, ``1`` = border, ``2`` = reflection.

attributes.name: align_corners_
attributes.description: Coordinate mapping mode (see file header).

attributes.name: input_shape_
attributes.description: Saved ``(N, C, H_in, W_in)`` for backward reconstruction.

attributes.name: grid_shape_
attributes.description: Saved ``(N, H_out, W_out, 2)`` for backward reconstruction.

members.name: GroupNormBackward
members.signature: GroupNormBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, int num_groups, double eps)
methods.summary: Run the forward pass.
methods.extended:
Computes per-``(batch, group)`` statistics, normalises ``x``,
applies the per-channel affine, caches ``saved_mean_`` and
``saved_rstd_``, and (when grad mode is on) registers ``this``
as the ``grad_fn`` of the returned output.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape ``(B, C, S_0, ...)``.  Rank must be ``>= 2``.

parameters.name: num_groups
parameters.description: Number of groups ``G``; must divide ``C`` evenly.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical-stability constant added inside the square root.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

raises.description: If ``C`` is not divisible by ``num_groups`` or if ``gamma`` and ``beta`` do not have shape ``(C,)``.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Compute gradients for the three saved inputs.
methods.extended:
Returns ``{dx, d_gamma, d_beta}`` in the saved-input order using
the cached statistics.  Closed-form summary (per channel $c$):

$$
  d\gamma_c = \sum_{b,\mathbf{s}}
             \hat{x}_{b,c,\mathbf{s}}\,
             \text{grad\_out}_{b,c,\mathbf{s}},
  \qquad
  d\beta_c = \sum_{b,\mathbf{s}} \text{grad\_out}_{b,c,\mathbf{s}}
$$

and the input gradient follows the standard GN backward formula
restricted to each ``(b, g)`` group.

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Incoming gradient, same shape as the forward output.

returns.description: Three-element vector ``{dx, d_gamma, d_beta}``.

members.summary: Autograd node for Group Normalization.
members.extended:
Computes per-``(batch, group)`` mean and reciprocal standard
deviation over the channels-in-group ($C/G$) plus all spatial axes,
then normalises and applies a *per-channel* (not per-group) affine
$y = \gamma_c\,\hat{x}_{b,c,\mathbf{s}} + \beta_c$.  The saved
statistics are required by the backward closed-form;
:member:`spatial_dims_` retains the per-axis spatial sizes so the
backward can recompute the reduction count.

Inherits ``FuncOp<GroupNormBackward, 3>`` with three saved-input
slots — ``x``, ``gamma``, and ``beta``.

**Math**

Let $g(c)$ denote the group containing channel $c$ (so
$g(c) = \lfloor c\,G\,/\,C\rfloor$) and write
$S = \prod_i \text{spatial\_dims}[i]$.  For each ``(b, g)`` pair:

$$
  \mu_{b,g} = \frac{1}{(C/G)\,S}
     \sum_{c\in g}\sum_{\mathbf{s}} x_{b,c,\mathbf{s}},
  \qquad
  \sigma_{b,g}^2 = \frac{1}{(C/G)\,S}
     \sum_{c\in g}\sum_{\mathbf{s}} (x_{b,c,\mathbf{s}} - \mu_{b,g})^2
$$

$$
  \hat{x}_{b,c,\mathbf{s}}
    = (x_{b,c,\mathbf{s}} - \mu_{b,g(c)})
      \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\sigma_{b,g(c)}^2 + \epsilon}},
  \qquad
  y_{b,c,\mathbf{s}} = \gamma_c\,\hat{x}_{b,c,\mathbf{s}} + \beta_c
$$

Mean and variance use the biased estimator.

**References**

Wu & He, "Group Normalization" (ECCV 2018, [arXiv:1803.08494](https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.08494)).
notes:
Thread safety: instances are created once during forward and
consumed once during backward — no concurrent access expected.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (``name="group_norm"``, ``version=1``, ``amp_policy=ForceFP32``, ``produces_grad=true``).

attributes.name: saved_mean_
attributes.description: Per-``(batch, group)`` mean $\mu_{b,g}$, shape ``(B*G,)``.

attributes.name: saved_rstd_
attributes.description: Per-``(batch, group)`` reciprocal standard deviation $1 / \sqrt{\sigma_{b,g}^2 + \epsilon}$, shape ``(B*G,)``.

attributes.name: B_, C_, G_
attributes.description: Batch, channel, and group counts captured from ``x.shape()``.

attributes.name: spatial_dims_
attributes.description: Per-axis spatial sizes ``[S_0, S_1, ...]`` retained so the backward can recompute the spatial reduction count.

members.signature: HardSigmoidBackward

methods.name: dispatch
methods.signature: int dispatch(backend::IBackend & be, const int & a, const int & s, Dtype dt)
methods.summary: Forward — calls ``IBackend::hard_sigmoid`` to compute $y = \mathrm{clamp}((x + 3)/6, 0, 1)$.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Eager-mode backward: dispatches to ``IBackend::hard_sigmoid_backward``.

members.summary: Autograd node for the Hard Sigmoid $y = \mathrm{clamp}((x + 3)/6, 0, 1)$ (Howard et al., MobileNetV3).
members.extended:
The derivative is the indicator of the active linear region
$-3 < x < 3$, scaled by $1/6$.  Backward goes through the backend to
share the piecewise kernel with the other hard-* activations.

**Math**

$$y = \mathrm{clamp}\!\Bigl(\tfrac{x + 3}{6}, 0, 1\Bigr),
  \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}
  = \begin{cases} 1/6, & -3 < x < 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"hard_sigmoid"``, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``.

members.name: HardSwishBackward
members.signature: HardSwishBackward

methods.name: dispatch
methods.signature: int dispatch(backend::IBackend & be, const int & a, const int & s, Dtype dt)
methods.summary: Forward — calls ``IBackend::hard_swish`` to compute $y = x \cdot \mathrm{hard\_sigmoid}(x)$.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Eager-mode backward: dispatches to ``IBackend::hard_swish_backward``.

members.summary: Autograd node for Hard Swish $y = x \cdot \mathrm{hard\_sigmoid}(x)$ (Howard et al., MobileNetV3).
members.extended:
The derivative is the three-region piecewise function
$0$ for $x \le -3$, $(2x + 3)/6$ for $-3 < x < 3$, and $1$ for $x \ge 3$.
Backward is delegated to the backend for a single fused pass.

**Math**

$$y = x \cdot \mathrm{hard\_sigmoid}(x),
  \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}
  = \begin{cases} 0, & x \le -3 \\
                  (2x + 3)/6, & -3 < x < 3 \\
                  1, & x \ge 3 \end{cases}$$

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"hard_swish"``, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``.

members.name: HuberLossBackward
members.signature: HuberLossBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & input, const int & target, double delta, Reduction reduction)
methods.summary: Compute the Huber loss with autograd wiring.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Ground-truth tensor of the same shape as ``input``.

returns.description: Reduced or per-element loss tensor.

raises.description: If ``delta <= 0``.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Backward pass: gradients w.r.t. ``input`` and ``target``.

members.summary: Autograd node for Huber (smooth L1) loss.
members.extended:
Combines a quadratic regime for small residuals with a linear regime for
large residuals, providing robustness to outliers without sacrificing
differentiability at zero:
$$
  \ell_\delta(\hat{y}, y) = \begin{cases}
    \tfrac{1}{2} (\hat{y} - y)^2                            & |\hat{y} - y| \le \delta \\
    \delta \bigl(|\hat{y} - y| - \tfrac{1}{2} \delta\bigr)  & |\hat{y} - y| > \delta
  \end{cases}
$$
The backward emits $\nabla_{\hat y} = \operatorname{clamp}(\hat y - y, -\delta, \delta)
\cdot g$ (scaled by $1/N$ for ``Mean``).

**Math**

$$
  \ell_\delta(r) = \begin{cases}
    \tfrac{1}{2} r^2          & |r| \le \delta \\
    \delta |r| - \tfrac{\delta^2}{2} & \text{otherwise}
  \end{cases}, \quad r = \hat y - y
$$
notes:
Smaller $\delta$ behaves more like an absolute-error loss (robust to
outliers); larger $\delta$ behaves more like MSE (sensitive to outliers).

attributes.description: ``"huber_loss"``, ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32``.

attributes.name: delta_
attributes.description: Transition threshold between quadratic and linear regimes; must be strictly positive.  Default ``1.0`` (SmoothL1).

attributes.description: Element-wise shape before reduction.

members.name: InterpolateBilinearBackward
members.signature: InterpolateBilinearBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & input, int H_out, int W_out, bool align_corners)
methods.summary: Compute the bilinear resize with autograd wiring.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: 4-D input tensor of shape ``(N, C, H_in, W_in)``.

returns.description: Resampled tensor of shape ``(N, C, H_out, W_out)``.

raises.description: If ``input`` is not 4-D.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Backward pass: scatters ``grad_out`` to source pixels using the same bilinear weights as the forward.

members.summary: Autograd node for 2-D bilinear interpolation.
members.extended:
Resizes a 4-D tensor of shape ``(N, C, H_in, W_in)`` to
``(N, C, H_out, W_out)`` by linearly interpolating between the four
neighbouring input pixels:
$$
  y[i, j] = \sum_{a, b \in \{0, 1\}}
    w_{i}^{a} \, w_{j}^{b} \, x[i_0 + a, j_0 + b]
$$
where $(i_0, j_0)$ is the integer floor of the source coordinate and
$w_i^a$, $w_j^b$ are the fractional bilinear weights computed from the
``align_corners`` rule above.  The backward distributes ``grad_out`` back
to the same four pixels weighted by the *same* bilinear coefficients,
recomputed in the backend (no per-sample weight cache is saved).

**Math**

$$
  x_{\text{in}} = \begin{cases}
    j \cdot (W_{\text{in}} - 1) / (W_{\text{out}} - 1) & \text{align\_corners} \\
    (j + 0.5) \cdot W_{\text{in}} / W_{\text{out}} - 0.5 & \text{otherwise}
  \end{cases}
$$
(analogous formula for $y_{\text{in}}$).

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"interpolate_bilinear"``, ``AmpPolicy::Promote``.

attributes.name: H_out_, W_out_
attributes.description: Requested output spatial dimensions.

attributes.name: align_corners_
attributes.description: Coordinate mapping mode (see file header).

attributes.description: Full ``(N, C, H_in, W_in)`` shape for backward reconstruction.

members.name: InterpolateTrilinearBackward
members.signature: InterpolateTrilinearBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & input, int D_out, int H_out, int W_out, bool align_corners)
methods.summary: Compute the trilinear resize with autograd wiring.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: 5-D input tensor of shape ``(N, C, D_in, H_in, W_in)``.

returns.description: Resampled tensor of shape ``(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)``.

raises.description: If ``input`` is not 5-D.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Backward pass: scatters ``grad_out`` to source voxels using the same trilinear weights as the forward.

members.summary: Autograd node for 3-D trilinear interpolation.
members.extended:
Resizes a 5-D tensor of shape ``(N, C, D_in, H_in, W_in)`` to
``(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)`` by linearly interpolating between the
eight neighbouring input voxels.  Conceptually the trilinear weights are
the product of three 1-D linear weights (one per spatial axis), giving
$$
  y[d, i, j] = \sum_{a, b, c \in \{0, 1\}}
    w_{d}^{a} \, w_{i}^{b} \, w_{j}^{c} \, x[d_0 + a, i_0 + b, j_0 + c]
$$
where the per-axis source coordinates follow the same ``align_corners`` rule
described in the file header.

**Math**

Output size formula (per axis $S \in \{D, H, W\}$):
$$
  S_{\text{out}} = \text{user-specified}, \quad
  s_{\text{in}}^{(k)} = \begin{cases}
    k \cdot (S_{\text{in}} - 1) / (S_{\text{out}} - 1) & \text{align\_corners} \\
    (k + 0.5) \cdot S_{\text{in}} / S_{\text{out}} - 0.5 & \text{otherwise}
  \end{cases}
$$

attributes.name: schema_v1

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Compute the gradient ``{∂L/∂A}`` from the upstream gradient.

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Upstream gradient $\partial L/\partial B$ matching the forward output shape.

returns.description: Single-entry vector ``{∂L/∂A}`` aligned with the one differentiable input slot.

members.summary: Autograd node for the matrix inverse $A^{-1}$.
members.extended:
The forward result $B = A^{-1}$ is captured in ``saved_output_``;
no input is saved because the closed-form gradient only requires
$B$.  This trades a single matrix-shaped retain (peak memory) for
avoiding a second LU factorisation during backward.

**Math**

$$
  B = A^{-1}, \qquad G = \frac{\partial L}{\partial B},
$$
$$
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial A} = -B^\top\,G\,B^\top.
$$

**Shape**

- ``A``: ``(..., N, N)``.
- ``B = A^{-1}``: same shape as ``A``.
- ``grad_out``: same shape as ``A``.

**References**

Giles, "Collected Matrix Derivative Results for Forward and
Reverse Mode Algorithmic Differentiation" (Oxford NA-08/01, 2008),
§2.2.3.
Petersen & Pedersen, *The Matrix Cookbook* (2012), §9.1.3.
notes:
- For very large matrices in deep graphs, holding $B$ alive until
  backward increases peak memory; users wishing to trade memory
  for compute would need to recompute the inverse (not currently
  supported here).
- Implementation uses ``matmul_op`` + ``neg_op`` so second-order
  gradients flow through automatically.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"inv"``, one input, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput`` so mixed-precision training does not silently downcast the saved inverse.

attributes.name: saved_output_
attributes.description: Storage of the forward result $B = A^{-1}$ retained for the backward.

members.summary: Autograd node for element-wise bitwise NOT: $y = \mathtt{\sim}x$.
members.extended:
Defined only for integer dtypes (including ``Bool``); floating-point
inputs are rejected upstream by ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``.  Bitwise
operations have no meaningful gradient — ``kHasGradient = false``.

**Math**

$$y_i = \mathtt{\sim} x_i$$

For ``Bool`` inputs this is logical negation; for unsigned integers
it produces the bitwise complement modulo $2^{\text{bits}}$.

:meth:`step` inside the training loop so the next iteration sees

notes:

members.summary: Base class for epoch-driven learning-rate schedules.
members.extended:
Holds a reference to the target optimiser, snapshots its initial
learning rate, and exposes a templated update loop driven by the
virtual :meth:`compute_lr_at` hook.

The internal counter ``epoch_`` is 0-indexed and is **incremented
before** ``compute_lr_at`` is consulted, so the first call to
:meth:`step` queries epoch 1 (matching the reference framework's
convention).

**See Also**

:class:`StepLR`, :class:`MultiStepLR`, :class:`ExponentialLR`,
:class:`CosineAnnealingLR`, :class:`CyclicLR`, :class:`NoamScheduler`,
:class:`LambdaLR`, :class:`ReduceLROnPlateau`.
notes:
Copy-constructed and copy-assigned by design — the bound optimiser
reference makes a copied scheduler ambiguous.
