members.summary: Noam schedule — the Transformer "warm-up then $1/\sqrt{t}$ decay" rule (Vaswani et al., 2017).
members.extended:
Linearly ramps the lr during the first ``warmup_steps`` then decays
it as the inverse square root of the step count.  Scaled by
$d_\text{model}^{-1/2}$ so that the peak lr matches the convention
used in the original Transformer paper.

**Math**

$$
  \eta_t = f \cdot d_\text{model}^{-1/2}
    \cdot \min\!\bigl(t^{-1/2},\; t \cdot w^{-3/2}\bigr)
$$
where $w$ is ``warmup_steps``, $f$ is ``factor``, and $t$ is the
step count (``compute_lr_at`` uses ``epoch`` directly as $t$).

**References**

Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need" §5.3 (NeurIPS 2017).
notes:
Despite the ``LRScheduler`` base, Noam is conceptually a *step*
schedule rather than an *epoch* schedule — call :meth:`step` once
per optimiser iteration, not once per epoch.

attributes.name: model_size_
attributes.description: Transformer hidden width $d_\text{model}$.

attributes.name: warmup_steps_
attributes.description: Number of linear-ramp steps before the inverse-sqrt decay kicks in.

attributes.name: factor_
attributes.description: Global scale on the schedule.

members.name: Node
members.signature: Node

methods.name: Node
methods.signature: void Node()
methods.summary: Construct a node and assign the next monotonically increasing sequence number via :func:`next_sequence_nr`.

methods.name: ~Node
methods.signature: void ~Node()
methods.summary: Polymorphic destructor; subclasses are deleted through ``Node*``.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Compute input gradients from the upstream output gradient.
methods.extended:
This is the core chain-rule contribution implemented by each concrete
backward node.  The returned vector has one entry per outgoing edge —
i.e. per input to the corresponding forward op — and the engine
dispatches entry ``i`` along ``next_edges_[i]``.

**Math**

For a forward op $y = f(x_1, \ldots, x_n)$, ``apply`` returns

$$
\bar{x}_i = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i}
          = J_f^{(i)\,T}\, \bar{y},
$$

where $J_f^{(i)}$ is the Jacobian of $f$ with respect to $x_i$.

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Upstream gradient $\partial \mathcal{L} / \partial y$ matching the forward output's shape, dtype, and device.  Implementations may assume it is non-null.

returns.description: One :class:`Storage` per forward input.  An empty / default :class:`Storage` at position ``i`` signals that no gradient flows to that input.

methods.name: is_barrier
methods.signature: bool is_barrier()
methods.summary: Whether this node is a barrier that batches gradients from many edges before executing.  Regular ops return ``false``; barrier nodes (e.g. ``AccumulateGrad``) override to ``true``.

returns.description: ``true`` if the engine should route incoming gradients through :meth:`accumulate_barrier_grad` / :meth:`apply_barrier` instead of :meth:`apply`.

methods.name: accumulate_barrier_grad
methods.signature: void accumulate_barrier_grad(int, int)
methods.summary: Accumulate an arriving gradient into this barrier's internal buffer.

parameters.name: input_nr
parameters.description: The slot the gradient is destined for.

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: The gradient produced by an upstream node.

raises.description: Always, unless overridden — calling barrier accumulation on a non-barrier node is a programmer error.

methods.name: apply_barrier
methods.signature: int apply_barrier()
methods.summary: Flush an accumulated barrier and produce the consolidated gradients.

returns.description: One :class:`Storage` per outgoing edge (same contract as :meth:`apply`).

raises.description: Always, unless overridden — calling barrier flush on a non-barrier node is a programmer error.

methods.name: apply_for_graph
methods.signature: int apply_for_graph(const int & grad_out)
methods.summary: Graph-recording variant of :meth:`apply` for higher-order autograd.
methods.extended:
Invoked by the engine when ``create_graph=True``: the backward
computation itself is performed through :class:`TensorImpl`-based ops
so that the resulting gradient tensors carry their own ``grad_fn``,
enabling differentiation of gradients (Hessians, double-backward).

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Upstream gradient as a live :class:`TensorImpl`, possibly with a ``grad_fn`` of its own.

returns.description: One :class:`TensorImpl` per forward input, each possibly carrying autograd metadata.

raises.description: If the concrete node has not overridden this method — create-graph is opt-in per op.  The message names the offending op via :meth:`node_name`.

methods.name: node_name
methods.signature: int node_name()
methods.summary: Return a short human-readable identifier for this node.

returns.description: Defaults to ``"unknown"``; concrete nodes override to expose their ``schema_v1.name`` (e.g. ``"matmul"``, ``"linear"``).

notes:
Used in error messages emitted by :meth:`apply_for_graph` and in
debugger / profiler output.

members.summary: Abstract base class for every node in the backward computation graph.
members.extended:
A ``Node`` is the unit of work that :class:`Engine` executes during
:func:`backward`.  Each concrete subclass saves whatever tensors and
scalars it needs at forward time (typically into its own member fields)
and implements :meth:`apply` to compute input gradients from the incoming
output gradient via the chain rule.

The reference framework's ``Function`` corresponds to a Python-side
:class:`lucid.autograd.Function`; on the C++ side that role is split into
a forward op (free function or struct method) plus this ``Node``
subclass.  Subclassing ``Node`` directly is unusual — most engine-resident
ops inherit through :class:`AutogradNode` / :class:`FuncOp`, which add
fixed-size storage for saved inputs and version checking.

**See Also**

:class:`Edge` — the directed link between nodes.
:class:`AutogradNode` — CRTP base with saved-input slots and version
    checking that most ops actually inherit from.
notes:
**Lifetime.** Nodes are jointly owned by the :class:`TensorImpl` that
produced them (via ``grad_fn``) and by the ``Edge`` objects of consumer
nodes.  Once :func:`backward` completes and ``grad_fn`` is cleared on the
root, the chain of ``shared_ptr`` references collapses and all temporary
nodes are destroyed.

**Thread safety.** ``Node`` is not thread-safe.  The engine runs the
backward graph on a single thread; concurrent :func:`backward` invocations
on the same graph are undefined behaviour.

attributes.name: sequence_nr_
attributes.description: Globally-unique creation order assigned via :func:`next_sequence_nr`. The engine uses this to break topological ties so that nodes closer to the loss are executed first.

attributes.name: next_edges_
attributes.description: Outgoing edges — one per input tensor of the forward op.  Populated by the op builder at forward time and read-only thereafter.

attributes.name: saved_versions_
attributes.description: Version counters of the input tensors as observed during forward. Compared against the live counters in :meth:`validate_versions` to catch in-place mutations that would silently corrupt gradients.

members.name: NormBackward
members.signature: NormBackward

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Compute the gradient ``{∂L/∂A}`` from the upstream gradient.

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Upstream gradient $\partial L/\partial N$ with shape matching ``out_shape_``.

returns.description: Single-entry vector ``{∂L/∂A}`` aligned with the one differentiable input slot.

raises.description: If ``ord_`` is not 1.0 or 2.0.

members.summary: Autograd node for the tensor p-norm.
members.extended:
Reproduces ``expand_back`` at backward time using the saved
``axis_`` / ``keepdims_`` hyperparameters; saves both the input
$A$ (for the L2 numerator) and the reduced norm $N$ (to avoid
recomputation).

**Math**

$$
  p = 2: \quad
    \frac{\partial L}{\partial A}
      = \frac{A}{\mathrm{clip}(N, 10^{-12}, \infty)}
        \odot \mathrm{expand}(\partial L/\partial N).
$$
$$
  p = 1: \quad
    \frac{\partial L}{\partial A}
      = \mathrm{sign}(A) \odot \mathrm{expand}(\partial L/\partial N).
$$

**Shape**

- ``A``: arbitrary float shape.
- ``N``: ``A``'s shape with ``axis_`` removed (``keepdims=false``)
  or set to 1 (``keepdims=true``); scalar if ``axis_`` is empty
  and ``keepdims=false``.
- Output gradient: same shape as ``A``.

**References**

Petersen & Pedersen, *The Matrix Cookbook* (2012), §2.5.
Horn & Johnson, *Matrix Analysis* (2nd ed.), §5.6 (matrix norms).

raises.description: If ``ord_`` is neither 1.0 nor 2.0 when ``apply`` is invoked.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"norm"``, one input, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput`` so the ``A/N`` division does not lose precision via AMP downcasting.

attributes.name: ord_
attributes.description: Norm order used in the forward pass (1.0 or 2.0 supported in backward; other values raise ``not_implemented`` at backward time).

attributes.name: axis_
attributes.description: Axes that were reduced.  Empty means a full reduction over all elements.  Indices may be negative; they are normalised when re-inserting axes during ``expand_back``.

attributes.name: keepdims_
attributes.description: Whether reduced axes were retained as size-1 stubs in the output shape.

attributes.name: saved_inputs_[0]
attributes.description: Storage of the forward input $A$ (needed for ord=2 numerator and ord=1 sign).

attributes.name: saved_output_
attributes.description: Storage of the reduced norm $N$ (used as the ord=2 denominator).

members.name: NotImplementedError
members.signature: NotImplementedError

methods.name: NotImplementedError
methods.signature: void NotImplementedError(int msg)
methods.summary: Construct a ``NotImplementedError`` from the pre-formatted message the call site built describing the unsupported dtype / device / config combination.  Passed through unchanged to the :class:`LucidError` base.

members.summary: Thrown when an op recognises its inputs but has no implementation for the requested dtype, device, or configuration.
members.extended:
Distinct from a user-error such as :class:`ShapeMismatch` — the caller did
nothing wrong, the engine simply has no kernel for this combination
(e.g. ``conv3d`` on integer dtypes, or an autograd op that has not yet
shipped a backward formula).  Mapped to
``lucid._C.engine.NotImplementedError`` on the Python side.

parameters.name: msg
parameters.description: Fully formatted message; passed through unchanged to the :class:`LucidError` base.

members.name: OpEvent
members.signature: OpEvent
members.summary: Record of a single op execution captured by :class:`OpScope`.
members.extended:
Each event is a snapshot of one dispatched op: its name, the device
and dtype it ran on, the output shape, the wall-clock duration, the
net memory delta, and an optional FLOP estimate.  Profilers store
vectors of these records and expose them to the Python layer for
trace export (Chrome / Perfetto) and aggregated summaries.
notes:
All fields are owned by value; the struct is freely copyable and
suitable for return from :func:`Profiler::events`.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Op name as recorded at OpScope construction (typically the schema name, e.g. ``"matmul"``, ``"conv2d"``).

attributes.name: device
attributes.description: Device on which the op executed.

attributes.name: dtype
attributes.description: Element dtype of the output tensor at OpScope construction time.

attributes.name: shape
attributes.description: Output tensor shape at OpScope construction time.

attributes.name: time_ns
attributes.description: Wall-clock duration from OpScope construction to destruction, in nanoseconds.

attributes.name: memory_delta_bytes
attributes.description: Change in :func:`MemoryTracker::current_bytes` for ``device`` across the scope.  May be negative when the op frees more memory than it allocates (e.g. in-place ops releasing temporaries).

attributes.name: flops
attributes.description: Estimated floating-point operation count, populated by :func:`OpScope::set_flops`.  Defaults to ``0`` when the op does not report a count.

members.name: OpRegistry
members.signature: OpRegistry

methods.name: register_op
methods.signature: void register_op(const OpSchema & schema)
methods.summary: Registers a schema under ``schema.name``.
methods.extended:
Acquires an exclusive write lock.  If an op with the same name was
already registered the entry is silently overwritten — this is
intentional, to let tests inject mocked schemas without going
through a deregister API.  Production code registers each schema
exactly once at static-init time.

parameters.name: schema
parameters.description: A schema whose lifetime exceeds the registry's (constexpr static is the canonical case).  Only the pointer is stored.

notes:
``schema.name`` is captured as a :cpp:class:`std::string_view`; do
not pass schemas whose ``name`` is backed by a temporary
``std::string``.

methods.name: lookup
methods.signature: const OpSchema * lookup(int name)
methods.summary: Looks up a registered schema by canonical op name.
methods.extended: Acquires a shared read lock.  Multiple threads may call concurrently.

returns.description: Pointer to the registered schema, or ``nullptr`` when no op of that name exists.  The returned pointer is stable for the lifetime of the process.

methods.name: all
methods.signature: int all()
methods.summary: Returns a snapshot of every registered schema.
methods.extended:
Acquires a shared read lock.  The snapshot is a freshly allocated
vector; the schemas it points to remain valid for the process
lifetime.

returns.description: All currently registered schemas, ordered alphabetically by ``name`` (the backing container is a :cpp:class:`std::map`).

notes:
Intended for diagnostics, profiler dumps, and Python introspection
— not for the hot dispatch path, which should use :func:`lookup`.

methods.name: size
methods.signature: int size()
methods.summary: Returns the number of registered ops.
methods.extended: Acquires a shared read lock.

returns.description: Current size of the registry.

members.summary: Thread-safe global op registry.
members.extended:
All methods are static; the underlying map and its lock live in a
function-local static inside :file:`OpRegistry.cpp`.  Schemas are stored
by non-owning pointer — the caller (typically a constexpr static in an
op's translation unit) must guarantee that the pointee outlives the
registry.
notes:
The map keys are :cpp:class:`std::string_view` references into each
schema's ``name`` field.  Because schemas are required to live for the
process lifetime, the keys are stable for the same duration — no string
copying or hashing of string contents per lookup is needed.

members.name: OpSchema
members.signature: OpSchema

methods.name: OpSchema
methods.signature: void OpSchema(int n, int v, AmpPolicy ap, bool det, int note)
methods.summary: Minimal constructor for deterministic ops with default arity.

parameters.name: note
parameters.description: Non-determinism explanation; ignored when ``det`` is true.

notes:
Leaves ``input_arity = -1`` (variadic), ``output_arity = 1``,
``stable_input_indices`` empty, and ``internal = false``.  Use the
full constructor below when these defaults are not appropriate.

members.summary: Per-op metadata record stored by pointer in the :class:`OpRegistry`.
members.extended:
Schemas are intended to be ``constexpr`` statics defined alongside each
op implementation; the registry stores raw pointers and assumes the
pointee outlives the registry (true for process-lifetime statics).
Constructing schemas with dynamic storage duration is supported but
discouraged.

attributes.name: name
attributes.description: Canonical op name; used as the :class:`OpRegistry` key and in diagnostic / error messages.  Must point into storage that lives at least as long as the registry (constexpr literals are ideal).

attributes.name: version
attributes.description: Monotonic schema version starting at 1.  Bump on backwards- incompatible changes — bumping invalidates checkpoints / traces that reference the previous version.

attributes.name: amp_policy
attributes.description: How this op responds to an active autocast context.  See :enum:`AmpPolicy` for the three values.

attributes.name: deterministic
attributes.description: ``true`` if the op produces bit-identical results given identical inputs and configuration.  ``false`` for ops that rely on atomics, non-associative parallel reductions, or other sources of run-to-run variation.  Consulted by :func:`check_schema_determinism` / :class:`SchemaGuard` when ``Determinism::is_enabled()``.

attributes.name: determinism_note
attributes.description: Human-readable explanation of why the op is non-deterministic (e.g. ``"uses atomic scatter-add"``).  Included verbatim in the error message raised under ``set_deterministic(True)``.  Empty for deterministic ops.

attributes.name: input_arity
attributes.description: Expected number of input tensors.  ``-1`` means variadic (the op accepts any number of inputs; the op is responsible for its own arity validation).

attributes.name: output_arity
attributes.description: Number of output tensors produced.  Defaults to ``1``; most ops are single-output.

attributes.name: stable_input_indices
attributes.description: Indices into the input list whose values are saved for backward. Autograd's version-counter logic uses these to detect illegal in-place mutations between forward and backward that would corrupt the saved tensors.

attributes.name: internal
attributes.description: ``true`` for ops that are not part of the public Python API (implementation-detail kernels, fused / internal helpers).  The Python binding layer hides these from ``dir(lucid)`` etc.

members.name: OpScope
members.signature: OpScope

methods.name: OpScope
methods.signature: void OpScope(int name, Device device, Dtype dtype, int shape)
methods.summary: Captures the start wall-clock time and memory baseline.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Op name to store in the resulting :class:`OpEvent`.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which the op is executing.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Element dtype of the op's output.

notes:
If no :class:`Profiler` is active at this moment, ``sink_`` is
set to ``nullptr`` immediately and the destructor will skip the
:func:`MemoryTracker` query — keeping un-profiled ops cheap.

methods.name: ~OpScope
methods.signature: void ~OpScope()
methods.summary: Finalises the event with elapsed time and memory delta, then forwards it to :func:`Profiler::record`.
notes:
Short-circuits when ``sink_ == nullptr`` (the no-profiler fast
path).  Otherwise performs a steady-clock subtraction, a
memory-tracker query, and one mutex-guarded vector append on the
sink.

methods.name: operator=
methods.signature: OpScope & operator=(const OpScope &)

methods.name: set_flops
methods.signature: void set_flops(int f)
methods.summary: Records the estimated floating-point operation count for this scope.

parameters.name: f
parameters.description: FLOPs estimate (e.g. ``2 * M * N * K`` for an ``M x N`` matmul with ``K`` inner dim).

notes:
Must be called before the scope exits; otherwise the recorded
event reports ``flops == 0``.  Ops free to omit this if they
do not have a meaningful FLOPs count.

members.summary: RAII scope that records one :class:`OpEvent` into the thread's active :class:`Profiler` on destruction.
members.extended:
Constructing the scope captures the wall-clock start time and the
memory baseline for ``device``; the destructor computes ``time_ns``
and ``memory_delta_bytes`` and calls :func:`Profiler::record`.  When
no profiler is active, ``sink_`` is set to ``nullptr`` at
construction and the destructor short-circuits — the hot path costs
one TLS load and one null check.

**See Also**

:class:`OpScopeFull` — composite that also pushes an error-context
    frame.
examples: Wrap a kernel implementation::
notes:
Ownership: OpScope never owns the Profiler.  Callers of
:func:`set_current_profiler` are responsible for keeping the
pointed-to object alive for the entire scope of any OpScope that
could observe it.

The scope is non-copyable — copying would double-record the same
event into the sink.

attributes.name: sink_
attributes.description: Non-owning pointer to the profiler captured at construction time.  ``nullptr`` indicates "profiling off for this scope"; once set to ``nullptr`` the destructor performs no work.

attributes.name: event_
attributes.description: Partially-filled event accumulated through the scope's lifetime and forwarded to :func:`Profiler::record` on destruction.

attributes.name: start_time_
attributes.description: Wall-clock instant captured at construction.

attributes.name: start_memory_bytes_
attributes.description: :func:`MemoryTracker::current_bytes` snapshot taken at construction; subtracted on destruction to compute ``memory_delta_bytes``.

members.name: OpScopeFull
members.signature: OpScopeFull

methods.name: OpScopeFull
methods.signature: void OpScopeFull(int name, Device device, Dtype dtype, int shape)
methods.summary: Pushes ``name`` onto the error-context stack and starts an :class:`OpScope` for ``(name, device, dtype, shape)``.

parameters.name: name
parameters.description: Op name; used both as the error-context frame label and the :class:`OpEvent::name` field.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which the op is executing.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Output shape; moved into the underlying :class:`OpScope`.

notes:
Both guards are torn down when the composite object goes out of
scope.  The profiler-side teardown happens first; the
error-context teardown second.

methods.name: operator=
methods.signature: OpScopeFull & operator=(const OpScopeFull &)

methods.name: set_flops
methods.signature: void set_flops(int f)
methods.summary: Forwards the FLOPs estimate to the underlying :class:`OpScope`.
methods.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`OpScope::set_flops` — receiver of the forwarded value.

parameters.name: f
parameters.description: Estimated floating-point operations performed by the op body.  Stored on the pending :class:`OpEvent` for later aggregation.

methods.name: set_attr
methods.signature: void set_attr(int key, int value)
methods.summary: Attach a single attribute to the trace's most recently recorded :class:`OpNode` when a Tracer is installed; a no-op otherwise.
methods.extended:
Used by op forwards to thread emitter context (permutation,
axis, stride, padding, eps, …) that the MPSGraph builder would
be unable to recover from input + output shapes alone.

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: Attribute name.  Convention: snake_case, matches the reference-framework keyword argument when one exists.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Payload (variant over int64/vector<int64>/double/bool/string). Moved into the attribute map.

notes:
Outside any ``_tracing()`` scope this is a single TLS load +
null check; no allocations.  Cheap enough to put unconditionally
at every op forward that has shape-invisible parameters.

members.summary: Composite RAII guard activating both error-context annotation and profiler timing for the duration of an op's body.
members.extended:
Holds an :class:`ErrorContextGuard` (constructed first, destroyed
last) and an :class:`OpScope` (constructed second, destroyed first)
as members.  Construction pushes the op name onto the error stack
and starts the profiler timer; destruction first stops the profiler
(recording the event) and then pops the error frame.  This ordering
ensures the error frame is still active if the profiler's
:func:`record` call itself throws.

**See Also**

:class:`OpScope` — timing-only RAII (no error context).
:class:`ErrorContextGuard` — error-context-only RAII (no timing).
examples: Declare once at op entry::
notes:
Non-copyable and non-movable — both member guards hold thread-local
state whose correct LIFO nesting depends on stack-discipline
construction and destruction.  Copying or moving the composite
would either duplicate the error frame or move it out from under
the inner scope.

attributes.name: ctx_
attributes.description: Pushes ``name`` onto the thread-local error call-stack at construction; pops it on destruction.  Declared first so it outlives ``op_``.

attributes.name: op_
attributes.description: Wall-clock and memory-tracking RAII recorder.  Declared second so it is destroyed first, while ``ctx_`` is still on the stack.

members.name: Optimizer
members.signature: Optimizer

methods.name: Optimizer
methods.signature: void Optimizer(int)
methods.summary: Construct an optimizer from a flat list of trainable parameters.

parameters.name: params
parameters.description: Parameters to optimise.  May be empty (a no-op optimizer) and may contain null entries (silently skipped by ``step``).  The caller's vector is moved into the optimizer; subsequent mutations of the caller's copy do not affect this optimizer.

notes:
``state_initialized_`` is sized lazily on the first ``step()``
rather than here, which lets the Python wrapper extend ``params_``
post-construction in the rare param-group reconfiguration case.

methods.name: ~Optimizer
methods.signature: void ~Optimizer()
methods.summary: Virtual destructor — ensures derived ``Adam`` / ``SGD`` / ``RMSProp`` destructors run when held by a base-class pointer.  Defaulted; the per-parameter state buffers are released by their own ``std::vector`` members in the derived classes, and ``params_`` releases its ``shared_ptr`` refs automatically.

methods.name: operator=
methods.signature: Optimizer & operator=(const Optimizer &)

methods.name: step
methods.signature: void step()
methods.summary: Run one optimizer update over all registered parameters.
methods.extended:
For each non-null parameter that has an accumulated gradient,
``step()`` lazily initialises its state slot, invokes the derived
class's ``update_one`` to apply the update rule, then bumps the
parameter's autograd version counter.  Parameters without a
gradient are skipped — this matches the reference framework's
"no grad = no update" semantics and lets a single optimizer
safely cover partially active sub-modules (e.g. frozen layers).
notes:
Called once per training iteration, after ``loss.backward()``.
The accompanying Python wrapper takes care of forcing the
resulting MLX graph to ``eval()`` only when requested via
``AUTO_EVAL_AFTER_STEP``; by default ``step()`` is itself lazy
so that forward → backward → step can fuse into one MLX
submission.

methods.name: zero_grad
methods.signature: void zero_grad()
methods.summary: Zero every parameter's gradient storage.
methods.extended:
Calls ``TensorImpl::zero_grad`` on each non-null parameter.
Should be called before each forward/backward pass so that the
next backward accumulates into a clean buffer rather than into
the previous step's gradient.

methods.name: num_params
methods.signature: int num_params()
methods.summary: Number of parameter slots managed by this optimizer.

returns.description: ``params_.size()`` — includes null entries.

methods.name: set_lr
methods.signature: void set_lr(double lr)
methods.summary: Set the learning rate (called by LR schedulers between steps).

parameters.name: lr
parameters.description: New learning rate.  Must be non-negative.

methods.name: lr
methods.signature: double lr()
methods.summary: Current learning rate.

returns.description: The value last passed to ``set_lr`` (or the constructor).

methods.name: state_dict_id
methods.signature: int state_dict_id()
methods.summary: Short version-tagged identifier baked into checkpoints.

returns.description: A stable short tag like ``"sgd_v1"`` or ``"adam_v1"`` used by the Python loader to detect mismatched optimizer versions in ``load_state_dict``.

methods.name: state_buffers
methods.signature: int state_buffers()
methods.summary: Snapshot the per-parameter mutable state for checkpointing.
methods.extended:
**See Also**

load_state_buffers : the inverse operation.

returns.description: A list of ``(name, tensors)`` pairs.  ``name`` identifies the buffer kind (e.g. ``"momentum_buffer"``); ``tensors`` runs parallel to ``params_`` with one entry per parameter slot (or a null entry if no state was allocated for that slot). Entries are *clones* — callers may keep them after the optimizer is destroyed.  Optimizers with no Python-visible state return an empty vector.

notes:
Implementations typically call ``clone_state_storage`` for each
active slot.  Slots whose state was never allocated (e.g. SGD
with ``momentum == 0``) should contribute a null pointer rather
than a synthetic zero buffer.

methods.name: load_state_buffers
methods.signature: void load_state_buffers(const int & bufs)
methods.summary: Restore the per-parameter state in place from a snapshot.
methods.extended:
**See Also**

state_buffers : the inverse operation.

parameters.name: bufs
parameters.description: Must match the layout returned by ``state_buffers`` for this optimizer: same buffer names, same length, same per-slot shapes / dtypes.  Mismatches raise.

raises.description: If the buffer count, names, shapes, dtypes, or devices do not match the live optimizer state.

notes:
After this returns, every state slot is treated as initialised so
``step()`` will not re-zero it on the next call.  Implementations
typically delegate the byte copy to ``overwrite_state_storage``.

methods.name: step_count
methods.signature: int step_count()
methods.summary: Global step counter used by some optimizers for bias correction.

returns.description: Current step count (Adam bias correction, NAdam momentum schedule, ...).  Optimizers without a global counter return 0.

methods.name: set_step_count
methods.signature: void set_step_count(int)
methods.summary: Override the global step counter (used by ``load_state_dict``).

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: New step count.  Optimizers without a global counter ignore this call.

methods.name: update_one
methods.signature: void update_one(int slot_idx, int & param, const int & grad)
methods.summary: Apply the optimizer's update rule for a single parameter.

parameters.name: slot_idx
parameters.description: Stable index of the parameter in ``params_``.  Used to look up the pre-allocated state buffers (momentum, second moment, parameter average, ...).

parameters.name: param
parameters.description: Parameter tensor to update in place.  Guaranteed non-null and guaranteed to have an accumulated gradient.

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: The parameter's gradient storage for the current step.

notes:
Implementations must mutate ``param``'s storage in place — the
base ``step()`` is responsible for bumping the version counter
after this returns.  Both CPU and GPU paths are typically
dispatched from inside this method based on ``param->device()``.

methods.name: init_state_slot
methods.signature: void init_state_slot(int slot_idx, const int & param)
methods.summary: Allocate and zero-initialise per-parameter state for one slot.

parameters.name: slot_idx
parameters.description: Position in ``params_`` whose state should be materialised.

parameters.name: param
parameters.description: The parameter occupying that slot; provides the shape, dtype, and device for the state buffer(s).

notes:
Called exactly once per slot — on the first ``step()`` for which
that slot has a gradient.  Implementations should size their
``std::vector<Storage>`` state members lazily here rather than in
the constructor so that disabled features (e.g. ``momentum == 0``
in SGD) skip the allocation entirely.

members.summary: Abstract base class owning trainable parameters and the training step.
members.extended:
``Optimizer`` keeps a flat list of ``TensorImpl`` parameters and runs
one update per training iteration.  Derived classes inject their
specific update mathematics via ``update_one`` and lazily allocate
per-parameter state (momentum buffers, moment estimates, parameter
averages, ...) via ``init_state_slot``.  The base class handles the
cross-cutting bookkeeping: skipping parameters without gradients,
allocating state exactly once per slot, and bumping each parameter's
version counter so autograd notices the in-place mutation.

State storage layout
--------------------
Per-parameter state is keyed by an integer ``slot_idx`` that equals
the position of the parameter in ``params_``.  Derived classes hold
one ``std::vector<Storage>`` (or several) parallel to ``params_``;
entry ``i`` is allocated on the first call to ``update_one`` for
slot ``i``.  The parallel ``state_initialized_`` vector tracks
allocation so state is allocated exactly once.

Step contract
-------------
``step()`` is called by the Python wrapper once per training iteration,
*after* the backward pass has populated ``param->grad_storage()``.
It performs three actions per slot:

1. Skip the slot if the parameter pointer is null or its gradient is
   absent (matching the reference framework's "no grad = no update"
   convention).
2. Lazily allocate state via ``init_state_slot`` if this is the slot's
   first observed gradient.
3. Apply the update via ``update_one`` and bump the parameter's
   version counter via ``TensorImpl::bump_version`` so any autograd
   node that captured the parameter before the update sees the change.

``zero_grad()`` clears every parameter's gradient storage so the next
backward pass starts from a fresh zero buffer.

Copy semantics
--------------
Copy is deleted because optimizer state owns raw heap buffers (CPU)
and MLX arrays (GPU) whose semantics under shallow copy would be
ambiguous.  Optimizers are owned by the Python wrapper through a
``std::shared_ptr`` and accessed via pybind11 reference bindings.

Serialisation
-------------
``state_dict_id()`` returns a short version-tagged identifier (e.g.
``"sgd_v1"``) baked into checkpoints so the Python loader can refuse
mismatched versions.  ``state_buffers()`` / ``load_state_buffers()``
expose the per-parameter mutable state as named ``TensorImpl`` lists
for ``torch.save``-style checkpointing — entries are clones, so the
caller is free to keep them after the optimizer is destroyed.

Subclass responsibilities
-------------------------
Concrete optimizers must override ``update_one``, ``init_state_slot``,
``set_lr`` / ``lr``, and ``state_dict_id``.  Optimizers with global
step state (Adam, NAdam, ...) must additionally override
``step_count`` / ``set_step_count``.  Optimizers with Python-visible
per-parameter state must override ``state_buffers`` /
``load_state_buffers``.

**See Also**

SGD, ASGD : plain and averaged stochastic gradient descent.
Adam, AdamW, NAdam, RAdam, Adamax, Adafactor : adaptive moment family.
Adagrad, Adadelta, RMSProp : adaptive learning-rate family.

attributes.name: params_
attributes.description: Flat list of trainable parameters.  Indexed by ``slot_idx`` in the protected hooks.  May contain null entries; nulls are silently skipped by ``step()``.

attributes.name: state_initialized_
attributes.description: Parallel to ``params_``.  Marks whether ``init_state_slot`` has run for each slot; grown lazily by ``step()`` to match ``params_.size()`` on first use.

and peak usage counters tracked by ``MemoryTracker`` for the offending
device, so callers (or end-users reading the Python traceback) can
distinguish OOM caused by absolute size from OOM caused by fragmentation.
Mapped to ``lucid._C.engine.OutOfMemory`` on the Python side.

methods.summary: Eager-mode backward: $\partial L/\partial x = \mathrm{permute}(\partial L/\partial y, p^{-1})$.  ``out_shape_`` is the permuted output shape, which becomes the input shape of the inverse permute call.

methods.name: apply_for_graph
methods.signature: int apply_for_graph(const int & grad_out)
methods.summary: Graph-mode backward used when ``create_graph=True``.  Applies the inverse permutation through ``permute_op`` so the result remains tracked in the autograd graph for second-order differentiation.

methods.name: node_name
methods.signature: int node_name()
methods.summary: Node-name override used by the autograd graph dumper.

members.summary: Autograd node for an arbitrary axis permutation $y_{i_{p_0}, i_{p_1}, \dots} = x_{i_0, i_1, \dots}$ with permutation $p$.
members.extended:
The forward pass is a *view* — it does not copy data.  The output
shares the input's underlying ``Storage`` and only rewrites its
``shape`` and ``stride`` according to ``perm``, paying an $O(\text{ndim})$
bookkeeping cost.  Materialising the permuted layout into contiguous
memory is the caller's responsibility (via a subsequent
``contiguous()`` call), which keeps simple ``.T`` followed by
``matmul`` free of redundant copies.

The backward is the same kind of operation in reverse: apply the
inverse permutation to ``grad_out`` so the gradient is reshuffled
back to the input's axis order.

**Math**

$$y = \mathrm{permute}(x, p),
  \qquad \frac{\partial L}{\partial x}
  = \mathrm{permute}\!\Bigl(\frac{\partial L}{\partial y}, p^{-1}\Bigr)$$

where $p^{-1}[p[i]] = i$ is the inverse permutation.

**Shape**

Output shape is ``[input.shape[perm[0]], input.shape[perm[1]], …]``.
``ndim`` is preserved.

raises.description: If ``perm`` is not a permutation of ``0..ndim-1`` or its length differs from ``ndim``.
If any element of ``perm`` is outside ``[-ndim, ndim)``.

notes:
Composability: a ``permute`` followed by another ``permute`` does not
materialise the intermediate layout; both forward passes rewrite
metadata only, so chains like ``a.T.contiguous()`` pay exactly one
data copy regardless of how many transposes precede the
``contiguous`` call.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"permute"``, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput`` — pure metadata operation, no promotion required.

attributes.name: perm_
attributes.description: Normalised (non-negative) forward permutation saved by ``forward``; used to derive the inverse permutation in ``apply``.

methods.summary: Route the forward computation through the backend's pow primitive.

methods.summary: Compute the gradients for both inputs given the output gradient.

methods.name: grad_formula_impl
methods.signature: int grad_formula_impl(const int & g, const int & a_ptr, const int & b_ptr)
methods.summary: Graph-mode gradient for create_graph=True. dA = b * a^(b-1) * g;   dB = log(a) * a^b * g

members.summary: Autograd node for elementwise tensor-tensor power $y = a^b$ with NumPy broadcasting.
members.extended:
Saves both inputs ``a`` and ``b`` (``kSavesInputs = true`` inherited from
:class:`BinaryKernel`) because both are needed in the backward formulas.
The gradient w.r.t. ``a`` is finite for any real $a$; the gradient w.r.t.
``b`` is undefined when $a \leq 0$ because $\log(a)$ is not real — in that
regime the returned gradient is non-finite and should be masked by the
caller.

**Math**

$$
  y = a^b
$$
$$
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial a} = b \cdot a^{b - 1} \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}
$$
$$
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = \log(a) \cdot a^b \cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y}
$$

**Shape**

Inputs ``a``, ``b`` follow NumPy broadcasting rules; the output ``y`` takes
the broadcast shape.  Both backward branches are sum-reduced back to the
original input shapes by :func:`sum_to_shape` in the apply trampoline.

**See Also**

mul_op : Elementwise multiplication.
log_op : Natural logarithm (used by the $\partial L/\partial b$ branch).
notes:
For tensor-scalar power (constant exponent) prefer the scalar variant in
``ScalarParam.h`` — it skips saving the exponent tensor and avoids the
``log(a) * a^b`` branch entirely.  See :class:`BinaryKernel` for the
broadcasting / save-tensor / reduce-to-shape machinery.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"pow"``, version ``1``, :enum:`AmpPolicy::ForceFP32`, deterministic. FP32 is forced because ``log()`` and fractional exponentiation are numerically unstable in half/bfloat precision.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor (must be non-null; promoted to FP32 by the schema).

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape and (post-promotion) dtype as ``a``; the backward node carries ``exp``.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Computes $\partial L/\partial x = \text{exp} \cdot x^{\text{exp}-1} \cdot g$ from the saved input and the captured ``exp_``.

returns.description: Gradient with respect to ``x`` — same shape, dtype, and device as the saved input.

methods.name: grad_formula_impl
methods.signature: int grad_formula_impl(const int & g, const int & a, const int &)
methods.summary: Graph-mode equivalent of ``grad_formula`` used when higher-order autograd is enabled — produces a fresh ``TensorImpl`` whose own backward chain remains intact.
methods.extended:
**Math**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(x^e) = e \cdot x^{e-1}$$

parameters.name: /* out */
parameters.description: Saved output tensor (unused; preserved for signature parity with other ``grad_formula_impl`` overrides).

returns.description: Tensor representing $\text{exp} \cdot x^{\text{exp}-1} \cdot g$ with a live autograd graph.

members.summary: Autograd node for scalar-exponent power: $y = x^{\text{exp}}$.
members.extended:
The exponent is a Python ``float`` (here promoted to ``double``) captured
on the node at forward time; the *input* tensor is saved so the backward
pass can reconstruct $x^{\text{exp}-1}$ via the same backend kernel.

**Math**

Forward

$$y_i = x_i^{\text{exp}}$$

Backward

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_i} = \text{exp} \cdot x_i^{\text{exp} - 1}
\cdot \frac{\partial L}{\partial y_i}$$

**See Also**

:class:`RPowScalarBackward`, :func:`pow_scalar_op`.
notes:
Capturing only the scalar (rather than baking it into the op name) keeps
the op table small.  ``pow_scalar`` is reused inside ``grad_formula`` to
compute $x^{\text{exp}-1}$ — no separate "decrement-exponent" kernel.

attributes.name: exp_
attributes.description: Scalar exponent captured at forward; reused inside ``grad_formula`` to compute $x^{\text{exp}-1}$ and the leading multiplier.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Op name ``"pow_scalar"`` with ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32`` (integer / half precision are unsafe for arbitrary exponents).

members.signature: ProdBackward

methods.name: cpu_kernel
methods.signature: CpuStorage cpu_kernel(const CpuStorage & a, const int & in_shape, const int & axes, bool keepdims, Dtype dt)
methods.summary: CPU path: iterates axes in descending order (innermost last) to produce a correct sequential multi-axis product using Accelerate primitives.

methods.name: gpu_kernel
methods.signature: GpuStorage gpu_kernel(const GpuStorage & a, const int & in_shape, const int & axes, bool keepdims, Dtype dt)
methods.summary: GPU path: delegates to mlx::core::prod with keepdims.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & grad_out)
methods.summary: Backward — $\partial L/\partial x_i = (y / x_i) \cdot \mathrm{grad\_out}$ (product of all other elements), formed from the saved input and output.

members.summary: Autograd node for the multi-axis reduction product $y = \prod_{i \in \text{axes}} x_i$.
members.extended:
The gradient is the "product of all other elements", which can be
recovered cheaply from the saved output:
$\partial y / \partial x_i = y / x_i$.  Both the forward input
($x$) and the forward output ($y$) are saved so ``grad_formula`` can
reconstruct the ratio without rerunning the reduction.

Because no single ``IBackend`` overload spans every Accelerate /
MLX combination, ``prod`` provides explicit ``cpu_kernel`` and
``gpu_kernel`` overloads instead of a shared ``dispatch``.

**Math**

$$
  y = \prod_{i \in \text{axes}} x_i, \qquad
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} =
  \frac{y}{x_i}\,
  \mathrm{broadcast}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}
  \right).
$$
notes:
CPU path iterates axes in **descending order** so the lower indices
remain valid as dimensions are successively collapsed.  GPU path
delegates to ``mlx::core::prod`` with ``keepdims``.  Empty
reductions return $1$ (multiplicative identity).  Zero elements in
$x$ make the division step undefined; the reference framework
returns NaN there and Lucid follows.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered as ``"prod"`` with ``AmpPolicy::Promote``.

attributes.name: kSavesOutput
attributes.description: ``true``.

methods.name: start
methods.signature: void start()
methods.summary: Enables event collection.
notes:
After this call, every :class:`OpScope` destructor whose
``sink_`` points at this profiler will append its event to
``events_``.  Calling :func:`start` on an already-active
profiler is idempotent.

methods.name: stop
methods.signature: void stop()
methods.summary: Disables event collection without clearing recorded events.
notes:
Events already in ``events_`` are preserved — call
:func:`clear` to drop them.  In-flight :class:`OpScope`
destructors may still record their event if they raced with the
``stop()`` write; :func:`record` re-checks ``active_`` under the
mutex to discard such latecomers.

methods.name: is_active
methods.signature: bool is_active()
methods.summary: Returns whether the profiler is currently recording.

returns.description: ``true`` between :func:`start` and :func:`stop`, ``false`` otherwise.

notes:
Read without acquiring the mutex — the result is a snapshot and
may be stale by the time the caller acts on it.  The internal
``record()`` call re-checks under the lock to avoid races.

methods.name: clear
methods.signature: void clear()
methods.summary: Removes all stored events without changing the active state.
notes:
Resets ``events_.size()`` to zero; the active/stopped state of
the profiler is left untouched, so a running profiler keeps
collecting new events afterwards.

methods.name: events
methods.signature: int events()
methods.summary: Returns a copy of the recorded event list.

returns.description: Snapshot of all events recorded so far, copied under ``mu_``.  Caller can iterate without holding the lock.

notes:
Returns by value to decouple iteration from concurrent writers;
the cost is one ``OpEvent`` vector copy per call.

methods.signature: void record(OpEvent event)

parameters.name: event
parameters.description: The event to store.  Moved into ``events_``.

notes:
Thread-safe.  Called by :class:`OpScope` on destruction.
Re-checks :func:`is_active` under ``mu_`` so events that race
with :func:`stop` are discarded rather than appended.

members.summary: Thread-safe event collector for op profiling.
members.extended:
Holds a vector of :class:`OpEvent` records and a single mutex
protecting both the active flag and the event list.  Multiple
:class:`OpScope` instances on different threads may concurrently
call :func:`record` — the mutex serialises insertions.

**See Also**

:class:`OpScope` — RAII recorder that feeds events into this sink.
:func:`set_current_profiler` — thread-local registration.
notes: Typical lifecycle::

The Python wrapper :class:`lucid.profiler.Profiler` runs this exact
sequence inside ``__enter__`` / ``__exit__``.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Invoke the Python backward callable and collect the resulting gradients.
methods.extended:
Acquires the GIL, wraps ``grad_out`` in a temporary :class:`TensorImpl`
matching :attr:`out_shape` / :attr:`out_dtype` / :attr:`out_device`,
calls ``py_backward_fn(py_ctx, grad_tensor)``, then unpacks the
returned tuple/list/tensor back into a vector of :class:`Storage`
values — one per forward input.

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Upstream gradient delivered by the Engine; assumed to match the output dtype/device of the original forward call.

returns.description: One :class:`Storage` per input edge.  Python ``None`` entries become empty ``CpuStorage{}`` values representing "no gradient for this input".

raises.description: The Python ``backward_fn`` is unset, or it raised an exception during execution (the original message is preserved).

members.summary: Autograd graph node that defers backward computation to a Python callable.
members.extended:
When a user subclasses :class:`lucid.autograd.Function` and invokes
``MySubclass.apply(...)``, the Python layer constructs a
``PythonBackwardNode``, populates :attr:`py_ctx` and :attr:`py_backward_fn`,
and registers the node as the output tensor's ``grad_fn`` via
``_register_python_backward_node``.  The Engine then treats this node like
any other backward node — when :func:`apply` is called during graph
traversal, control crosses back into Python with the GIL held.

**See Also**

:class:`lucid.autograd.Function` — Python base class users subclass.
:class:`FunctionCtx` — context object carrying saved state.
notes:
The public ``py_ctx`` / ``py_backward_fn`` data members are exposed
directly via ``def_readwrite`` so the pybind11 binding can set them
without accessor boilerplate.

Thread safety: none.  The Engine guarantees single-threaded execution of
the backward pass, so :func:`apply` does not lock; calling it concurrently
from multiple threads is undefined.

methods.summary: Compute $\rho_t$ and $r_t$; apply the rectified adaptive update when $\rho_t > 5$, else fall back to a plain momentum update.

methods.name: init_state_slot

methods.summary: Allocate zero-initialised $m$ and $v$ buffers for this slot.

members.summary: RAdam optimiser — Rectified Adam (Liu et al., 2019).
members.extended:
Computes a variance-based rectification factor $r_t$ derived from
the simple moving average length $\rho_t$.  When $\rho_t$ exceeds a
threshold (the paper uses $5$) the adaptive learning rate is
well-conditioned and we apply the standard bias-corrected Adam step
scaled by $r_t$; otherwise we fall back to a plain momentum step
using only $\hat m_t$, which avoids the early-training divergence
caused by an under-sampled second moment.

**Math**

$$
  \rho_\infty = \frac{2}{1 - \beta_2} - 1, \qquad
  \rho_t = \rho_\infty - \frac{2 t\, \beta_2^t}{1 - \beta_2^t}
$$

If $\rho_t > 5$:

$$
  r_t = \sqrt{
    \frac{(\rho_t - 4)(\rho_t - 2)\,\rho_\infty}
         {(\rho_\infty - 4)(\rho_\infty - 2)\,\rho_t}
  }
$$

$$
  \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\, r_t \cdot
      \frac{\hat m_t}{\sqrt{\hat v_t} + \epsilon}
$$

otherwise (fallback to plain momentum):

$$
  \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\, \hat m_t
$$

**References**

Liu, Jiang, He, Chen, Liu, Gao & Han, "On the Variance of the
  Adaptive Learning Rate and Beyond" (ICLR 2020, arXiv 2019).

**See Also**

Adam, AdamW, NAdam.
notes:
$\rho_t$ and $r_t$ depend only on $t$ and $\beta_2$, so they are
computed once per step outside the per-parameter loop.  Weight
decay (if non-zero) is added to $g_t$ in the standard L2 form.

attributes.name: lr_
attributes.description: Step size $\eta$.  Default ``1e-3``.

attributes.name: beta1_, beta2_, eps_, weight_decay_
attributes.description: As in ``Adam``.

attributes.name: m_, v_
attributes.description: Per-parameter first- and second-moment buffers.

attributes.name: step_count_
attributes.description: Global step counter.

members.name: RMSNormBackward
members.signature: RMSNormBackward

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & x, const int & gamma, double eps)
methods.summary: Run the forward pass.
methods.extended:
Reduces ``x`` over its last $D$ dimensions (where $D$ is the rank
of ``gamma``), divides by the RMS, applies the gain, and (when
grad mode is on) registers ``this`` as the ``grad_fn`` of the
returned output.  Caches ``saved_rstd_`` for backward.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape ``(*, normalized_shape)``. Rank must be ``>= rank(gamma)``.

parameters.name: gamma
parameters.description: Per-element scale; its shape must match the trailing dims of ``x``.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Small constant added inside the square root for numerical stability.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

raises.description: If ``gamma.ndim > x.ndim`` or if the trailing dims of ``x`` do not match ``gamma``.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Compute gradients for the two saved inputs.
methods.extended:
Returns ``{dx, d_gamma}`` in the saved-input order.  Closed-form
(per leading slice of length $N$, using the cached
$\text{rstd} = 1/\text{rms}(x)$):

$$
  d\gamma_i = \sum_{\text{slice}}
             \text{grad\_out}_i\,x_i\,\text{rstd}
$$

and the input gradient is the standard RMSNorm backward formula
(the centred grad is replaced by the *un-centred* grad since
RMSNorm has no mean subtraction).

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Incoming gradient, same shape as the forward output.

returns.description: Two-element vector ``{dx, d_gamma}``.

members.summary: Autograd node for RMS Normalization.
members.extended:
Computes the per-slice reciprocal RMS
$\text{rstd} = 1 / \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{N}\sum_i x_i^2 + \epsilon}$ over
the trailing $D$ dimensions of ``x``, scales the input by it, and
applies the per-element gain $\gamma$.  Only ``saved_rstd_`` is
cached for backward — no mean is needed because RMSNorm does not
centre the input.

Inherits ``FuncOp<RMSNormBackward, 2>`` with two saved-input slots —
``x`` and ``gamma`` (RMSNorm has no bias).  ``outer_ * N_`` always
equals ``numel(x)``.

**Math**

For each leading-axis slice of ``x`` (flattened length $N$):

$$
  \text{rms}(x) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 + \epsilon},
  \qquad
  y_i = \frac{x_i}{\text{rms}(x)} \cdot \gamma_i
$$

Equivalently, with the saved $\text{rstd} = 1/\text{rms}(x)$:

$$
  y_i = x_i \cdot \text{rstd} \cdot \gamma_i
$$

**References**

Zhang & Sennrich, "Root Mean Square Layer Normalization"
(NeurIPS 2019, [arXiv:1910.07467](https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.07467)).
notes:
Thread safety: instances are created during forward and consumed
during backward — no concurrent access expected.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (``name="rms_norm"``, ``version=1``, ``amp_policy=ForceFP32``, ``produces_grad=true``).

attributes.name: saved_rstd_
attributes.description: Per-slice reciprocal RMS, shape ``(outer_,)``.

attributes.name: outer_
attributes.description: Product of leading (non-normalised) dimensions of ``x``.

attributes.name: N_
attributes.description: Product of normalised (trailing) dimensions of ``x`` (== ``numel(gamma)``).

notes:
``square_avg_[i]`` is always allocated.  ``grad_avg_[i]`` is
allocated only when ``centered_`` is true and ``moment_buf_[i]``

members.summary: RMSprop optimiser — gradient normalisation by EMA of squared magnitude, with optional centring and Polyak-style momentum.
members.extended:
The plain rule replaces Adagrad's monotonic accumulator with an
exponential moving average, so the effective learning rate stops
shrinking and tracks the *current* gradient regime instead.

**Math**

Uncentred:
$$
  v_{t+1} = \alpha\,v_t + (1 - \alpha)\,g_t^2
$$
$$
  \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\,\frac{g_t}{\sqrt{v_{t+1}} + \epsilon}
$$

Centred — subtract the squared mean to recover the true variance:
$$
  \bar g_{t+1} = \alpha\,\bar g_t + (1 - \alpha)\,g_t
$$
$$
  \tilde v_{t+1} = v_{t+1} - \bar g_{t+1}^2
$$
$$
  \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\,\frac{g_t}{\sqrt{\tilde v_{t+1}} + \epsilon}
$$

With momentum $\mu \ne 0$ the normalised gradient is folded into a
velocity buffer first:
$$
  b_{t+1} = \mu\,b_t + \frac{g_t}{\sqrt{v_{t+1}} + \epsilon}, \qquad
  \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta\,b_{t+1}
$$

**References**

Tieleman & Hinton, "Lecture 6.5 — RMSProp" (Coursera 2012).

**See Also**

:class:`Adadelta`, :class:`Adagrad`, :class:`Rprop`

parameters.name: alpha
parameters.description: EMA decay of the squared-gradient running average.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical stabiliser inside the square root.

parameters.name: momentum
parameters.description: Polyak momentum $\mu$.  When zero the momentum buffer is skipped entirely.

parameters.name: centered
parameters.description: Track $\bar g$ to recover the true variance.  Better-conditioned in non-stationary regimes at the cost of one extra buffer.

notes:
State buffers are allocated **lazily** — code that only ever uses
the plain variant does not pay the memory cost of the centred or
momentum-augmented variants.

attributes.name: square_avg_
attributes.description: EMA of $g^2$ (always allocated).

attributes.name: grad_avg_
attributes.description: EMA of $g$ (allocated only when ``centered == true``).

attributes.name: moment_buf_
attributes.description: Momentum buffer (allocated only when ``momentum != 0``).

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(double base, const int & a)
methods.summary: Forward dispatch with base capture — computes $y = \text{base}^{x}$, saves the output on the backward node, and wires autograd manually (``save_input=false``).

parameters.name: base
parameters.description: Scalar base (must be positive for a real-valued gradient).

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape and (post-promotion) dtype as ``a``; the backward node carries ``base`` and the saved output.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Computes $\partial L/\partial x = \ln(\text{base}) \cdot y \cdot g$ from the saved output and the captured ``base_``.

returns.description: Gradient with respect to ``x``.

members.summary: Autograd node for scalar-base reverse power: $y = \text{base}^{x}$.
members.extended:
The base is captured on the node; the *output* tensor (not the input)
is saved because the gradient formula uses $y$ directly.

**Math**

Forward

$$y_i = \text{base}^{x_i}$$

Backward

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_i} = \ln(\text{base}) \cdot y_i \cdot
\frac{\partial L}{\partial y_i}$$

**See Also**

:class:`PowScalarBackward`, :func:`rpow_scalar_op`.
notes:
Undefined for ``base <= 0`` (``ln`` would not be real).  Use
``pow_scalar_op`` if the exponent — not the base — is the scalar.

attributes.name: base_
attributes.description: Scalar base captured at forward; ``std::log(base_)`` is computed inside ``grad_formula``.

attributes.name: kSavesInput
attributes.description: ``false`` — the input is not needed for the backward pass.

attributes.name: kSavesOutput
attributes.description: ``true`` — the forward output $y = \text{base}^{x}$ is saved.

attributes.description: Op name ``"rpow_scalar"`` with ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32``.

methods.summary: Forward — calls ``IBackend::reciprocal`` to compute $y = 1/x$.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Backward — $\partial y/\partial x = -1/x^2$, scaled by ``grad_out``.

methods.name: grad_formula_impl
methods.signature: int grad_formula_impl(const int & g, const int & x, const int &)
methods.summary: Graph-mode backward: $\partial x = -g / x^2$ kept inside the autograd graph.

members.summary: Autograd node for the element-wise reciprocal $y = 1/x$.
members.extended:
Saves the input ``x`` so the backward pass can form $x^2$ in the
denominator without recomputing the forward result.  The forward and
backward are both undefined at $x = 0$ and the caller is responsible for
avoiding that domain point.

**Math**

$$y = \frac{1}{x}, \qquad
\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = -\frac{1}{x^2}, \qquad
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} =
-\frac{1}{x^2}\,\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}.$$
notes:
Dispatch: Accelerate ``vForce`` reciprocal kernel (CPU) / MLX
element-wise reciprocal (GPU).

attributes.description: Registered as ``"reciprocal"`` with ``AmpPolicy::Promote``.

methods.name: scale_graph_grad
methods.signature: int scale_graph_grad(const int & g)
methods.summary: Graph-mode gradient scaling hook.
methods.extended:
Default implementation passes the gradient through unchanged,
matching the ``sum`` rule (every reduced element contributed with
unit weight).  Derived classes whose rule has a non-trivial scale
— :class:`MeanBackward` divides by $N$, :class:`ProdBackward`
multiplies by $y / x$, the argmax variants apply an indicator
mask — override this method to inject their factor before the
gradient is returned.

parameters.name: g
parameters.description: Broadcast-back gradient already shaped like the original input.  Always non-null.

returns.description: The scaled gradient — either ``g`` itself or a fresh tensor produced by a graph-tracked op.

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: int forward(const int & a, const int & axes_user, bool keepdims)
methods.summary: Forward entry point: run the reduction and wire the backward node.
methods.extended:
Normalises ``axes_user`` (negative indices → positive, dedup),
computes the output shape (respecting ``keepdims``), enforces CPU
contiguity, dispatches to the most specific kernel hook the
``Derived`` class exposes, and — when ``GradMode`` is enabled and
the input requires grad — constructs the backward node with the
extra reduction state (:attr:`reduce_axes_`, :attr:`keepdims_`,
:attr:`full_input_shape_`) populated.

parameters.name: axes_user
parameters.description: Raw axis list as supplied by the caller.  May contain negative indices and duplicates; they are normalised before dispatch.  An empty list reduces over every axis.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true``, every reduced axis appears as a size-1 dim in the output; otherwise it is removed.

returns.description: Output tensor with the reduced shape and the AMP-effective dtype (driven by ``schema_v1.amp_policy``).

raises.description: If ``a`` is null.
If the input lives on GPU and ``Derived`` exposes neither :concept:`HasReduceDispatch` nor :concept:`HasReduceGpuKernel`.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Storage-mode backward: delegate the full broadcast-and-scale to ``Derived::grad_formula``.
methods.extended:
Unlike :class:`UnaryKernel::apply`, this method performs no
``reduce_grad_to_shape`` step — the reduction kernel's
``grad_formula`` is responsible for broadcasting the upstream
gradient back to :attr:`full_input_shape_` using the saved
:attr:`reduce_axes_` and :attr:`keepdims_` fields, and for any
reduction-specific scaling.

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Gradient of the loss with respect to this op's output, shaped like the forward output.

returns.description: Single-element vector containing the input gradient, shaped like :attr:`full_input_shape_`.

methods.name: apply_for_graph
methods.signature: int apply_for_graph(const int & grad_out)
methods.summary: Graph-mode backward: rebuild the broadcast in tracked ops.
methods.extended:
Re-inserts size-1 axes for any dims that were dropped because
``keepdims=False``, runs :func:`broadcast_to_op` to expand the
gradient back to :attr:`full_input_shape_`, then defers to
:meth:`scale_graph_grad` for the reduction-specific factor.  The
entire chain participates in the autograd graph so higher-order
gradients work correctly.

parameters.name: grad_out
parameters.description: Upstream gradient as a live :class:`TensorImpl` (i.e. a graph node), not just a :class:`Storage`.

returns.description: Single-element vector containing the input gradient tensor.

members.summary: CRTP base for single-input multi-axis reduction ops (``sum``, ``mean``, ``prod``, ``max``, ``min``, …).
members.extended:
Concrete reductions inherit as
``class SumBackward : public ReduceKernel<SumBackward>`` and supply
(a) a ``static constexpr OpSchema schema_v1``, (b) one of the kernel
hooks recognised by :concept:`HasReduceDispatch` /
:concept:`HasReduceGpuKernel` (or a plain ``cpu_kernel`` static), and
(c) a ``grad_formula(grad_out) -> Storage`` that converts the upstream
gradient into the input gradient.  The base owns axis normalisation,
output-shape computation, GPU/CPU dispatch, and the extra
reduction-state plumbing that the backward formula needs.

Unlike :class:`UnaryKernel`, the forward output has a *different*
shape than the input, so the backward pass must broadcast the
gradient from the reduced shape back to the full input extent.  That
broadcast is encoded once in :meth:`apply_for_graph` (graph-mode) and
is the responsibility of ``grad_formula`` in storage-mode.

**Math**

$$
  y = \mathrm{reduce}_{i \in \text{axes}}(x), \qquad
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i}
    = \mathrm{scale}_{\text{op}}\!\left(
      \mathrm{broadcast}\!\left(
        \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}
      \right)
    \right).
$$

``scale_op`` is identity for ``sum``, $1/N$ for ``mean``,
$y / x_i$ for ``prod``, an argmax/argmin indicator for ``max``/``min``.

Template Parameters
-------------------
Derived : class
    CRTP self-type.  Must expose ``schema_v1``, a kernel hook
    satisfying one of the reduction concepts (or ``cpu_kernel``), and
    ``grad_formula``.  May override ``scale_graph_grad`` for graph-mode
    scaling (``mean``, ``prod``, …).

**See Also**

:class:`UnaryKernel`, :class:`BinaryKernel`, :class:`NaryKernel` —
sibling CRTP bases for shape-preserving ops.
:class:`SumBackward`, :class:`MeanBackward`, :class:`ProdBackward`,
:class:`MaxBackward`, :class:`MinBackward` — canonical consumers.
notes:
**Dispatch order.**  :meth:`forward` chooses the kernel path with
``if constexpr``:

1. :concept:`HasReduceDispatch` — call ``Derived::dispatch(backend,
   storage, shape, axes, keepdims, dtype)``.
2. GPU + :concept:`HasReduceGpuKernel` — call ``Derived::gpu_kernel``.
3. CPU — call ``Derived::cpu_kernel``.

**Contiguity.**  CPU inputs are routed through :func:`contiguous_op`
before dispatch; GPU inputs are passed through as MLX handles strides
natively.

attributes.name: kSavesInput
attributes.description: ``true`` by default — most reductions need the input activation during backward.  ``SumBackward`` overrides this to ``false``.

attributes.name: kSavesOutput
attributes.description: ``false`` by default.  ``ProdBackward`` / ``MaxBackward`` / ``MinBackward`` override to ``true`` so they can route gradient through the saved output (or its argmax indicator).

attributes.name: kHasGradient
attributes.description: ``true``.  All canonical reductions participate in autograd.

attributes.name: reduce_axes_
attributes.description: Normalised (non-negative, sorted, deduplicated) axis list saved by :meth:`forward` for use in :meth:`apply_for_graph` and in the derived ``grad_formula``.

attributes.name: keepdims_
attributes.description: Whether reduced axes were retained as size-1 dims in the output. Controls whether the backward pass must re-insert those size-1 axes before broadcasting.

attributes.name: full_input_shape_
attributes.description: Shape of the input tensor *before* reduction — the broadcast target during backward.

methods.summary: Construct the plateau detector.

parameters.name: mode

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Minimum lr delta to actually apply a reduction.

methods.name: step
methods.signature: void step(double metric)
methods.summary: Evaluate ``metric``, update counters, and reduce lr if needed.

parameters.name: metric
parameters.description: Latest value of the watched metric (e.g. validation loss).

notes:
No-op while the cooldown counter is positive.  Once ``patience``
bad epochs accumulate, the optimiser's lr is multiplied by
``factor_`` (clamped at ``min_lr_``) and the cooldown begins.

methods.name: last_lr
methods.signature: double last_lr()
methods.summary: Return the lr applied at the most recent :meth:`step`.

returns.description: ``last_lr_``.

methods.name: num_bad_epochs
methods.signature: int num_bad_epochs()
methods.summary: Return the running count of consecutive non-improving epochs.

returns.description: ``num_bad_epochs_``.

methods.name: is_better
methods.signature: bool is_better(double metric)
methods.summary: Test whether ``metric`` improves over ``best_``.

returns.description: True when ``metric`` beats ``best_`` by at least ``threshold_``, interpreted according to ``threshold_mode_``.

members.summary: Metric-driven plateau detector — reduce lr when progress stalls.
members.extended:
**Not** a :class:`LRScheduler` subclass: :meth:`step` takes a scalar
metric instead of an epoch index.  Tracks the best metric seen so
far and counts consecutive epochs that fail to improve beyond a
threshold.  Once ``patience`` bad epochs accumulate the optimiser's
lr is multiplied by ``factor`` (subject to ``min_lr``), after which
a ``cooldown``-step grace period suppresses further reductions to
let the model settle into the new regime.

**See Also**

:class:`CosineAnnealingLR` — deterministic alternative when the loss
curve is too noisy to drive a plateau heuristic.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: ``Min`` to minimise the metric, ``Max`` to maximise.

parameters.name: threshold
parameters.description: Minimum improvement to count as progress (interpretation depends on ``threshold_mode``).

parameters.name: threshold_mode
parameters.description: ``Rel`` — improvement measured as a fraction of ``best_``; ``Abs`` — improvement measured in raw units.

parameters.name: cooldown
parameters.description: Steps to wait after each reduction before resuming bad-epoch counting.

parameters.name: min_lr
parameters.description: Floor of the lr — reductions saturate here.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Guard against reductions whose absolute lr change is below this.

notes:
The improvement test is implemented via :meth:`is_better` which
branches on ``mode_`` and ``threshold_mode_``.

attributes.name: best_
attributes.description: Best metric value observed so far.

attributes.name: num_bad_epochs_
attributes.description: Consecutive non-improving epochs.

attributes.name: cooldown_counter_
attributes.description: Remaining grace steps after the most recent reduction.

attributes.name: last_lr_
attributes.description: lr applied at the last call to :meth:`step`.

methods.summary: Eager-mode backward: applies an in-range $(0, 6)$ mask to the upstream gradient.

members.summary: Autograd node for ReLU6 $y = \mathrm{clamp}(x, 0, 6)$ (Krizhevsky 2010, MobileNet family).
members.extended:
Gradient is the indicator of the open interval $(0, 6)$ — built directly
as an in-range storage mask and multiplied with the upstream gradient,
avoiding a backend round-trip for this very simple piecewise rule.

**Math**

$$y = \mathrm{clamp}(x, 0, 6),
  \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}
  = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 < x < 6 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"relu6"``, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``.

methods.name: dispatch
methods.signature: int dispatch(backend::IBackend & be, const int & a, const int & s, Dtype dt)
methods.summary: Forward — calls ``IBackend::relu`` to compute $y = \max(0, x)$.

methods.name: grad_formula
methods.signature: int grad_formula(const int & g)
methods.summary: Eager-mode backward: $\partial L/\partial x = \mathbb{1}\{x > 0\} \cdot \partial L/\partial y$, computed via a positive-input mask multiplied element-wise against the upstream gradient.

parameters.name: g
parameters.description: Upstream gradient ``dL/dy`` matching the forward output shape.

returns.description: ``dL/dx`` at the input shape and dtype.

methods.name: grad_formula_impl
methods.signature: int grad_formula_impl(const int & g, const int & x, const int &)
methods.summary: Graph-mode backward used when ``create_graph=True``.  Builds the mask as ``sign(relu(x))`` (zero for $x \le 0$, one for $x > 0$) so the operation is itself differentiable for higher-order gradients.

members.summary: Autograd node for the element-wise rectified linear unit $y = \max(0, x)$.
members.extended:
Saves the input so the backward pass can rebuild the positive-input mask
$\mathbb{1}\{x > 0\}$ and gate the upstream gradient through it.  At the
non-differentiable point $x = 0$ the subgradient is taken to be $0$, which
matches the reference framework's convention.

**Math**

$$y = \max(0, x), \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \mathbb{1}\{x > 0\}$$
notes:
MLX exposes a native ``maximum(x, 0)`` kernel on the GPU stream; the CPU
path goes through ``vDSP_vmax`` against a zero buffer.  ``grad_formula_impl``
(used by ``create_graph=True``) builds the mask as ``sign(relu(x))`` so the
inverse-mask multiplication stays inside the autograd graph for second-order
differentiation.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: ``"relu"``, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput`` (ReLU is well-defined on integer dtypes, so the AMP layer leaves the input dtype untouched).

methods.name: error
methods.signature: const ErrorPayload & error()
methods.summary: Accesses the stored error payload (const lvalue overload).

returns.description: Const reference to the error descriptor.  Lifetime is tied to ``*this``.

raises.description: If :cpp:func:`is_ok` is true.

methods.name: value_or_throw
methods.signature: T value_or_throw()
methods.summary: Returns the stored value or throws the error as a :class:`LucidError`.
methods.extended:
The rvalue-ref qualifier means this method consumes the ``Result`` —
callers should write ``std::move(r).value_or_throw()`` (or call it
on a prvalue) to avoid an unnecessary copy of ``T``.

returns.description: The success payload, moved out of the variant.

raises.description: If the error arm is active.  The payload's ``msg`` is forwarded to :cpp:func:`ErrorBuilder::fail`, which prepends ``"Result: "`` and the current :class:`ErrorContext` trace.

members.summary: Value-or-error container that holds either a ``T`` (success) or an :cpp:struct:`ErrorPayload` (failure) in a single ``std::variant``.
members.extended:
Use ``Result<T>`` instead of an exception when a failure must be
propagated across a boundary that cannot safely unwind C++ exceptions:
the pybind11 FFI surface (when ``noexcept`` is required), async callbacks
dispatched onto a worker queue, or any C ABI shim.  Inside the engine
itself, ops typically throw a :class:`LucidError` directly because the
stack-unwinding cost is acceptable and the typed exception carries
richer state.

Invariants
----------
- ``is_ok()``  ⟺  ``data_`` holds a ``T`` (variant index 0).
- ``is_err()`` ⟺  ``data_`` holds an :cpp:struct:`ErrorPayload`
  (variant index 1).

Thread safety
-------------
``Result<T>`` instances are not internally synchronised.  Sharing a
single instance across threads requires external coordination.

**See Also**

:cpp:func:`Ok` : Factory that wraps a value as a successful ``Result``.
:cpp:func:`Err` : Factory that builds an :cpp:struct:`ErrorPayload`,
    implicitly convertible to ``Result<T>`` for any ``T``.

parameters.name: T
parameters.description: Type carried by the success arm.  Must be movable; otherwise no constraints.

methods.summary: Apply RoPE to ``input``.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input of shape $(\ldots, L, D)$ with ``D`` even.  ``L`` is the sequence axis and ``D`` is rotated in pairs.

parameters.name: position_ids_or_null
parameters.description: Optional integer position-index tensor of shape ``(L,)``. When ``null``, the kernel uses ``[0, 1, …, L-1]`` implicitly.

returns.description: Rotated output with the same shape as ``input``.

If ``position_ids_or_null`` lives on a different device than ``input``.

methods.name: apply
methods.signature: int apply(int grad_out)
methods.summary: Apply the conjugate rotation to ``grad_out`` using the saved cos/sin tables.

returns.description: Single-element ``{grad_in}`` of the same shape.

members.summary: Autograd node for Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE, Su et al. 2021).
members.extended:
Rotates pairs of features in the last dimension of ``input`` by
position-dependent angles, encoding absolute position information
in a way that lets dot-product attention recover relative position.
During the forward, the backend returns a triple
``{rotated_input, cos, sin}``; the cos/sin tables are saved into
:member:`saved_cos_` and :member:`saved_sin_` so the backward can
apply the conjugate rotation without recomputing the trigonometry.

:member:`interleaved_` selects the pair layout:

* ``true``  — adjacent pairs ``(d=0, d=1), (d=2, d=3), …``
  * ``false`` — split-half ``(d=0, d=D/2), (d=1, d=D/2+1), …``

The chosen layout must match how the model was trained or attention
scores will be silently corrupted.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Registered schema (name ``"rotary_pos_embedding"``, version 1, ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32``).  Forced to F32 because the rotation math is precision-sensitive at long sequence lengths.

attributes.name: interleaved_
attributes.description: Pair-layout flag (see above).

attributes.name: orig_shape_
attributes.description: Saved input shape; used by backward to size the gradient.

attributes.name: saved_cos_
attributes.description: Cosine table produced by the forward kernel.

attributes.name: saved_sin_
attributes.description: Sine table produced by the forward kernel.

members.summary: Autograd node for element-wise round-to-nearest-even: $y = \operatorname{round}(x)$.
members.extended:
Piecewise-constant; derivative is zero almost everywhere and undefined
at half-integer boundaries.  ``kHasGradient = false`` causes
``UnaryKernel::forward`` to skip autograd wiring entirely.

**Math**

$$y_i = \operatorname{round}(x_i)$$

Ties round to even ("banker's rounding"), matching IEEE-754.

**See Also**

:class:`FloorBackward`, :class:`CeilBackward`.
notes:
CPU dispatch routes through ``backend::IBackend::round``; GPU uses
MLX's ``round`` primitive.  Integer dtypes are a no-op.

attributes.name: kSavesInput
attributes.description: ``false`` — no tensor needs to be saved across the backward pass.

attributes.name: kHasGradient
attributes.description: ``false`` — predicate-style op; the backward node is never invoked in practice.

attributes.name: schema_v1
attributes.description: Op name ``"round"`` with ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput`` (integer inputs pass through unchanged).
