members.name: lapack_cholesky_f32
members.signature: void lapack_cholesky_f32(float * A, int n, bool lower, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision Cholesky decomposition (in place).
members.extended:
Factors a symmetric positive-definite matrix into either $A = L L^T$
(``lower=true``) or $A = U^T U$ (``lower=false``).  After the LAPACK call
the off-triangle entries are zeroed so the result is a clean lower / upper
matrix.

**Math**

$$ A = L L^T \quad\text{or}\quad A = U^T U $$

**References**

LAPACK ``spotrf_``.

parameters.name: A
parameters.description: Row-major $n \times n$ symmetric positive-definite matrix; overwritten by the requested triangular factor.

parameters.name: lower
parameters.description: ``true`` for lower triangular $L$, ``false`` for upper triangular $U$.

parameters.name: info
parameters.description: LAPACK status (``> 0`` means $A$ is not positive definite at column ``info``).

members.name: lapack_cholesky_f64
members.signature: void lapack_cholesky_f64(double * A, int n, bool lower, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision Cholesky decomposition (in place).  Uses ``dpotrf_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dpotrf_``.

parameters.name: A
parameters.description: Row-major SPD matrix; overwritten by the triangular factor.

members.name: lapack_eig_f32
members.signature: void lapack_eig_f32(const float * A, int n, float * wr, float * wi, float * VR, int * info)
members.summary: General (non-symmetric) eigendecomposition (float32).
members.extended:
Computes eigenvalues of a general real matrix; complex eigenvalues are
returned as separate real (``wr``) and imaginary (``wi``) parts.  Right
eigenvectors (columns of $V$) are written to ``VR`` if non-null.

**Math**

$$ A v_j = \lambda_j v_j, \quad \lambda_j = \mathrm{wr}_j + i \, \mathrm{wi}_j $$

**References**

LAPACK ``sgeev_``.

parameters.name: wr, wi
parameters.description: Real / imaginary parts of the eigenvalues, length $n$.

parameters.name: VR
parameters.description: Right-eigenvector matrix (row-major) or ``nullptr`` to skip vectors.

parameters.name: info
parameters.description: LAPACK status (``> 0`` = QR iteration failed to converge).

members.name: lapack_eig_f64
members.signature: void lapack_eig_f64(const double * A, int n, double * wr, double * wi, double * VR, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision general eigendecomposition.  Uses ``dgeev_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dgeev_``.

members.name: lapack_eigh_f32
members.signature: void lapack_eigh_f32(const float * A, int n, float * w, float * V_out, int * info)
members.summary: Symmetric eigendecomposition (float32) via divide-and-conquer.
members.extended:
For a real symmetric input $A = A^T$, computes the spectral decomposition
$A = V \, \mathrm{diag}(w) \, V^T$.  Eigenvalues are produced in ascending
order and eigenvectors are the columns of $V$ (returned row-major after
transposition).

**Math**

$$ A V = V \, \mathrm{diag}(w) $$

**References**

LAPACK ``ssyevd_``.

parameters.name: V_out
parameters.description: Row-major matrix whose columns are the eigenvectors.

members.name: lapack_eigh_f64
members.signature: void lapack_eigh_f64(const double * A, int n, double * w, double * V_out, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision symmetric eigendecomposition.  Uses ``dsyevd_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dsyevd_``.

members.name: lapack_householder_product_f32
members.signature: void lapack_householder_product_f32(const float * H, const float * tau, float * Q_out, int m, int n, int k, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision Householder-product Q reconstruction.
members.extended:
Given the Householder-reflector form of $Q$ produced by ``sgeqrf_``
(matrix $H$ + scalar coefficients $\tau$), materialises the first $k$
columns of $Q$ explicitly into ``Q_out``.

**References**

LAPACK ``sorgqr_``.

members.name: lapack_householder_product_f64
members.signature: void lapack_householder_product_f64(const double * H, const double * tau, double * Q_out, int m, int n, int k, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision Householder-product Q reconstruction.  Uses ``dorgqr_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dorgqr_``.

members.name: lapack_inv_f32
members.signature: void lapack_inv_f32(float * A, int n, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision in-place matrix inversion via LU.
members.extended:
Overwrites $A$ with $A^{-1}$.  Internally runs ``sgetrf_`` (LU with
partial pivoting) followed by ``sgetri_`` (invert using the LU factors).

**Math**

$$ A A^{-1} = I_n $$

**References**

LAPACK ``sgetrf_`` + ``sgetri_``.

parameters.name: A
parameters.description: Row-major $n \times n$ matrix; replaced in place with $A^{-1}$.

parameters.name: info
parameters.description: LAPACK status word.  ``0`` = success; ``< 0`` = illegal argument; ``> 0`` = matrix is exactly singular (no inverse).

members.name: lapack_inv_f64
members.signature: void lapack_inv_f64(double * A, int n, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision in-place matrix inversion via LU.
members.extended:
See ``lapack_inv_f32``; uses ``dgetrf_`` + ``dgetri_``.

**References**

LAPACK ``dgetrf_`` + ``dgetri_``.

members.name: lapack_ldl_factor_f32
members.signature: void lapack_ldl_factor_f32(const float * A, float * A_out, int * ipiv, int n, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision symmetric indefinite LDL^T factorisation.
members.extended:
Bunch-Kaufman factorisation $A = L D L^T$ for a symmetric (possibly
indefinite) matrix.  The packed $L$ + $D$ output is written to ``A_out``
alongside pivot indices in ``ipiv``.

**Math**

$$ A = L \, D \, L^T $$

**References**

LAPACK ``ssytrf_``.

members.name: lapack_ldl_factor_f64
members.signature: void lapack_ldl_factor_f64(const double * A, double * A_out, int * ipiv, int n, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision symmetric indefinite LDL^T factorisation.  Uses ``dsytrf_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dsytrf_``.

members.name: lapack_lstsq_f32
members.signature: void lapack_lstsq_f32(const float * A, float * B, int m, int n, int nrhs, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision least-squares solver via QR.
members.extended:
Computes the minimum-2-norm solution of an overdetermined system:
$$ \min_X \|A X - B\|_2 $$
For tall $A$ (m ≥ n) uses QR; for wide $A$ (m < n) uses LQ.  ``B`` is
overwritten with $X$ in its leading $n$ rows.

**References**

LAPACK ``sgels_``.

members.name: lapack_lstsq_f64
members.signature: void lapack_lstsq_f64(const double * A, double * B, int m, int n, int nrhs, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision least-squares solver.  Uses ``dgels_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dgels_``.

members.name: lapack_lu_f32
members.signature: void lapack_lu_f32(const float * A, int n, int * ipiv, float * L_out, float * U_out, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision LU decomposition split into separate L and U matrices.
members.extended:
Runs ``sgetrf_`` and then explicitly extracts the unit-lower-triangular
$L$ and upper-triangular $U$ from the packed LAPACK output.

**Math**

$$ P A = L U $$ where $P$ is encoded by ``ipiv``.

**References**

LAPACK ``sgetrf_``.

members.name: lapack_lu_f64
members.signature: void lapack_lu_f64(const double * A, int n, int * ipiv, double * L_out, double * U_out, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision LU decomposition split into separate L and U. See ``lapack_lu_f32``; uses ``dgetrf_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dgetrf_``.

members.name: lapack_lu_factor_f32
members.signature: void lapack_lu_factor_f32(const float * A, int n, float * LU_out, int * ipiv_out, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision LU factorisation (packed format).
members.extended:
Writes the LAPACK-native packed LU output (lower triangle = $L$ without its
unit diagonal, upper triangle = $U$) into ``LU_out``, along with the
pivot indices into ``ipiv_out``.  Intended for use with
``lapack_lu_solve_f32`` so the factorisation can be reused across many
right-hand sides.

**References**

LAPACK ``sgetrf_``.

members.name: lapack_lu_factor_f64
members.signature: void lapack_lu_factor_f64(const double * A, int n, double * LU_out, int * ipiv_out, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision packed LU factorisation.  Uses ``dgetrf_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dgetrf_``.

members.name: lapack_lu_solve_f32
members.signature: void lapack_lu_solve_f32(const float * LU, const int * ipiv, float * B, int n, int nrhs, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision LU-based linear solve with pre-computed factors.
members.extended:
Given the packed LU factorisation produced by ``lapack_lu_factor_f32`` and
its pivot indices, solves $A X = B$ for many right-hand sides without
re-factoring.  ``B`` is overwritten with $X$.

**References**

LAPACK ``sgetrs_``.

members.name: lapack_lu_solve_f64
members.signature: void lapack_lu_solve_f64(const double * LU, const int * ipiv, double * B, int n, int nrhs, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision LU-based linear solve with pre-computed factors. Uses ``dgetrs_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dgetrs_``.

members.name: lapack_qr_f32
members.signature: void lapack_qr_f32(const float * A, int m, int n, float * Q, float * R, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision reduced (thin) QR decomposition.
members.extended:
Computes $A = Q R$ where $Q$ is $m \times k$ with orthonormal columns and
$R$ is $k \times n$ upper-triangular, $k = \min(m, n)$.  Internally calls
``sgeqrf_`` to produce Householder reflectors then ``sorgqr_`` to
materialise $Q$.

**Math**

$$ A = Q R, \;\; Q^T Q = I_k $$

**References**

LAPACK ``sgeqrf_`` + ``sorgqr_``.

members.name: lapack_qr_f64
members.signature: void lapack_qr_f64(const double * A, int m, int n, double * Q, double * R, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision reduced QR decomposition.  Uses ``dgeqrf_`` + ``dorgqr_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dgeqrf_`` + ``dorgqr_``.

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Output $m \times k$ matrix with orthonormal columns.

members.name: lapack_solve_f32
members.signature: void lapack_solve_f32(float * A, float * B, int n, int nrhs, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision linear system solver $A X = B$.
members.extended:
Computes the LU factorization of $A$ (with partial pivoting) and solves
for $X$ in place of $B$.  Used for square invertible systems.

**Math**

$$ A X = B \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; X = A^{-1} B $$

**References**

LAPACK ``sgesv_``.

parameters.name: A
parameters.description: Row-major $n \times n$ coefficient matrix; overwritten by its LU.

parameters.name: info
parameters.description: LAPACK status (``> 0`` means $A$ is singular at pivot ``info``).

members.name: lapack_solve_f64
members.signature: void lapack_solve_f64(double * A, double * B, int n, int nrhs, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision linear system solver $A X = B$.  Uses ``dgesv_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dgesv_``.

parameters.name: A
parameters.description: Row-major $n \times n$ matrix; overwritten by its LU.

members.name: lapack_solve_triangular_f32
members.signature: void lapack_solve_triangular_f32(const float * A, float * B, int n, int nrhs, bool upper, bool unit, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision triangular linear solve.
members.extended:
Solves $A X = B$ where $A$ is triangular.  Selects upper or lower triangle
and whether the diagonal is implicit unit.  ``B`` is overwritten with $X$.

**Math**

$$ A X = B \;\;\text{with $A$ triangular} $$

**References**

LAPACK ``strtrs_``.

parameters.name: upper
parameters.description: ``true`` if $A$ is upper triangular, else lower.

parameters.name: unit
parameters.description: ``true`` if the diagonal is implicit one (e.g., from packed LU).

members.name: lapack_solve_triangular_f64
members.signature: void lapack_solve_triangular_f64(const double * A, double * B, int n, int nrhs, bool upper, bool unit, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision triangular linear solve.  Uses ``dtrtrs_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dtrtrs_``.

members.name: lapack_svd_f32
members.signature: void lapack_svd_f32(const float * A, int m, int n, bool full_matrices, float * U, float * S, float * Vt, int * info)
members.summary: Single-precision singular value decomposition via divide-and-conquer.
members.extended:
Computes $A = U \, \mathrm{diag}(S) \, V^T$.  When ``full_matrices`` is
false the thin SVD is returned: $U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times k}$,
$V^T \in \mathbb{R}^{k \times n}$, $k = \min(m, n)$.  When true the full
matrices ($U \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$, $V^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$)
are returned.

**Math**

$$ A = U \, \Sigma \, V^T, \quad \Sigma = \mathrm{diag}(S) $$

**References**

LAPACK ``sgesdd_`` (divide-and-conquer driver).

parameters.name: full_matrices
parameters.description: Controls thin vs full SVD as described above.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: Output singular values in descending order, length $\min(m, n)$.

parameters.name: info
parameters.description: LAPACK status (``> 0`` = SVD did not converge).

members.name: lapack_svd_f64
members.signature: void lapack_svd_f64(const double * A, int m, int n, bool full_matrices, double * U, double * S, double * Vt, int * info)
members.summary: Double-precision SVD via divide-and-conquer.  Uses ``dgesdd_``.
members.extended:
**References**

LAPACK ``dgesdd_``.

members.name: layer_norm_backward_f32
members.signature: void layer_norm_backward_f32(const float * x, const float * gamma, const float * saved_mean, const float * saved_rstd, const float * g, float * dx, float * dgamma, float * dbeta, int outer, int N)
members.summary: Single-precision LayerNorm backward — produces input, weight, and bias gradients from a saved-tensor forward pass.
members.extended:
Uses the closed-form LayerNorm gradient that avoids recomputing the row
statistics.  ``dgamma`` and ``dbeta`` are *accumulated* into the supplied
buffers; callers reducing over multiple sub-batches must zero them once at
the start.  ``dx`` is written fully (not accumulated).

**Math**

Let $\hat{x}_i = (x_i - \mu)\,\text{rstd}$ and
$\widetilde{g}_i = \gamma_i g_i$.  Then
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_i} =
  \frac{\text{rstd}}{N} \!\left(N \widetilde{g}_i - \sum_j \widetilde{g}_j -
    \hat{x}_i \sum_j \widetilde{g}_j \hat{x}_j\right),$$
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \gamma_i} = \sum_{\text{rows}} g_i \hat{x}_i,
  \qquad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \beta_i} = \sum_{\text{rows}} g_i.$$

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Original forward input of shape ``(outer, N)``.

parameters.name: g
parameters.description: Upstream gradient $\partial L / \partial y$ of shape ``(outer, N)``.

parameters.name: dgamma, dbeta
parameters.description: Output gradients w.r.t. the affine parameters; shape ``(N,)``; *accumulated* (caller must zero on first call).

members.name: layer_norm_backward_f64
members.signature: void layer_norm_backward_f64(const double * x, const double * gamma, const double * saved_mean, const double * saved_rstd, const double * g, double * dx, double * dgamma, double * dbeta, int outer, int N)
members.summary: Double-precision counterpart to :cpp:func:`layer_norm_backward_f32`.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Original forward input of shape ``(outer, N)``.

parameters.name: saved_mean, saved_rstd
parameters.description: Per-row statistics from the matching forward call.

parameters.name: dgamma, dbeta
parameters.description: Output gradients of shape ``(N,)``; *accumulated* — caller must zero them before the first invocation.

members.name: layer_norm_forward_f32
members.signature: void layer_norm_forward_f32(const float * x, const float * gamma, const float * beta, float * y, float * saved_mean, float * saved_rstd, int outer, int N, double eps)
members.summary: Single-precision LayerNorm forward — normalises every row of length ``N`` using its empirical mean and variance, then applies a per-feature affine transform $\gamma \hat{x} + \beta$ in the same pass.
members.extended:
The f32 path is a vDSP fast path: it computes the row mean with
``vmean_f32``, centres the row into a scratch buffer with ``vsadd_f32``,
derives the variance as a self-dot-product via ``vdotpr_f32``, then fuses
the affine into a ``vmadd_f32`` call so the data is touched twice rather
than three times.  Per-row ``mean`` and ``rstd = 1/sqrt(var + eps)`` are
stashed so the backward kernel can skip the recomputation.

**Math**

$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \varepsilon}}, \qquad
  y_i = \gamma_i \hat{x}_i + \beta_i,$$
with $\mu, \sigma^2$ the row mean and variance of ``x``.

**References**

[Ba, Kiros & Hinton, "Layer Normalization" ([arXiv:1607.06450](https://arxiv.org/abs/1607.06450), 2016)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1607.06450).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input buffer of shape ``(outer, N)`` in row-major order.

parameters.name: gamma, beta
parameters.description: Per-feature affine parameters of shape ``(N,)``.  Both required.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Output buffer of shape ``(outer, N)``; written densely.

parameters.name: saved_mean, saved_rstd
parameters.description: Per-row scratch outputs of shape ``(outer,)``.  Consumed by :cpp:func:`layer_norm_backward_f32`.

parameters.name: outer
parameters.description: Number of independent rows (product of all leading dimensions).

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Variance stabiliser; cast to ``float`` before being added to ``var``.

members.name: layer_norm_forward_f64
members.signature: void layer_norm_forward_f64(const double * x, const double * gamma, const double * beta, double * y, double * saved_mean, double * saved_rstd, int outer, int N, double eps)
members.summary: Double-precision LayerNorm forward — generic template implementation that computes mean and variance with a two-pass loop (no vDSP equivalent exists for ``double`` at the same fusion level).
members.extended:
**Math**

$$y_i = \gamma_i \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sqrt{\sigma^2 + \varepsilon}} + \beta_i.$$

parameters.name: saved_mean, saved_rstd
parameters.description: Per-row mean and reciprocal standard deviation, both shape ``(outer,)``.

parameters.name: outer, N
parameters.description: Number of rows and length of the normalised axis.

members.name: max_axis_f32
members.signature: void max_axis_f32(const float * in, float * out, int outer, int reduce_dim, int inner)
members.summary: Reduces a single axis by elementwise maximum into a packed ``(outer, inner)`` output.
members.extended:
The kernel always uses the generic ``axis_reduce`` template — Accelerate
does not expose a stride-aware vDSP equivalent for ``max`` with the same
semantics, so vectorisation is left to the compiler.  Initial accumulator
is ``-inf`` which means an empty reduce dimension yields ``-inf`` per cell.

**Math**

$$\text{out}[o, i] = \max_{r} \text{in}[o, r, i].$$

parameters.name: in
parameters.description: Source buffer of shape ``(outer, reduce_dim, inner)``.

notes:
NaN propagation follows the C++ ``a > b`` semantics: a NaN on either side
of the comparison loses, so a NaN element will not necessarily survive the
reduction.  Downstream code that requires NaN-poisoning must guard explicitly.

members.name: max_axis_f64
members.signature: void max_axis_f64(const double * in, double * out, int outer, int reduce_dim, int inner)
members.summary: Double-precision counterpart to :cpp:func:`max_axis_f32`.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$\text{out}[o, i] = \max_{r} \text{in}[o, r, i].$$

parameters.name: in
parameters.description: Source buffer of shape ``(outer, reduce_dim, inner)``.

members.summary: Single-precision 1-D max-pool backward — scatters each upstream gradient onto the input slot that won its pool window.
members.extended:
The caller must zero ``dx`` first because windows may overlap (when
``stride_l < KL``) and the kernel uses ``+=`` to accumulate contributions.

parameters.name: argmax
parameters.description: Argmax indices from the matching forward call; shape ``(B, C, OL)``.

parameters.name: dx
parameters.description: Output gradient w.r.t. the original input; shape ``(B, C, L)``; *accumulated* — caller must zero before invocation.

members.signature: void max_pool1d_backward_f64(const double * g, const int * argmax, double * dx, int B, int C, int L, int OL)
members.summary: Double-precision counterpart to :cpp:func:`max_pool1d_backward_f32`.

parameters.name: g
parameters.description: Upstream gradient of shape ``(B, C, OL)``.

parameters.name: argmax

members.name: max_pool1d_forward_f32
members.signature: void max_pool1d_forward_f32(const float * x, float * y, int * argmax, int B, int C, int L, int KL, int OL, int stride_l, int pad_l)
members.summary: Single-precision 1-D max-pool forward over an NCL-laid-out input.
members.extended:
For each output element the kernel scans the corresponding ``KL``-wide
window and records both the maximum value into ``y`` and the *flat*
position within the ``L`` dimension of the input row into ``argmax``.
Out-of-bounds (pad) positions contribute ``-inf`` and so never win unless
the whole window is padding (in which case the first pad slot wins).

**Shape**

$\text{OL} = \lfloor (L + 2 \cdot \text{pad\_l} - \text{KL}) /
\text{stride\_l}\rfloor + 1$.

**See Also**

max_pool1d_backward_f32 : Reverse pass that consumes ``argmax``.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape ``(B, C, L)``.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Output tensor of shape ``(B, C, OL)``.

parameters.name: argmax
parameters.description: Companion buffer of shape ``(B, C, OL)`` holding the winning ``L``-axis index of each output element; consumed by the backward kernel.

parameters.name: B, C, L
parameters.description: Batch, channel, and spatial extents of ``x``.

parameters.name: OL
parameters.description: Spatial extent of ``y``; caller computes it as ``(L + 2*pad_l - KL) / stride_l + 1``.

parameters.name: stride_l, pad_l
parameters.description: Window stride and symmetric zero-pad on the ``L`` axis.

members.summary: Double-precision counterpart to :cpp:func:`max_pool1d_forward_f32`.

parameters.name: B, C, L, KL, OL, stride_l, pad_l

members.name: permute_copy_f32
members.signature: void permute_copy_f32(const float * in, float * out, const int & in_shape, const int & perm)
members.summary: Copies a single-precision tensor into a permuted, densely-packed output buffer using the supplied axis permutation.
members.extended:
The implementation iterates the output in flat row-major order and, for
each output flat index, back-computes its N-D coordinate and reads from
the matching input position via the inverse permutation expressed through
the input's C-order strides.  No intermediate allocation beyond two stride
vectors of size ``ndim`` is performed.  This is an out-of-place layout
transform — there is no in-place fast path.

**Shape**

``out_shape[d] == in_shape[perm[d]]`` for every ``d``.

**See Also**

permute_copy_f64, permute_copy_i32, permute_copy_i64 : Same operation for
    other element types.

parameters.name: in
parameters.description: Source buffer laid out densely (C-order) according to ``in_shape``.

parameters.name: out
parameters.description: Destination buffer.  Caller must pre-allocate ``numel(in_shape) * sizeof(float)`` bytes.  May not alias ``in``.

parameters.name: in_shape
parameters.description: Shape of the source tensor.  An empty vector is treated as a 0-D scalar and produces a single-element copy.

parameters.name: perm
parameters.description: Axis permutation of length ``in_shape.size()``.  ``perm[d]`` names the source axis whose extent becomes output axis ``d``.  Must be a valid permutation of ``[0, ndim)``.

examples:
``perm = {2, 0, 1}`` over ``in_shape = {H, W, C}`` yields a CHW output.
``perm = {0, 2, 3, 1}`` over ``in_shape = {N, C, H, W}`` performs an NHWC
re-layout.
notes:
Cost is ``O(numel * ndim)`` due to per-element coordinate back-projection.
For frequently used permutations (e.g. matrix transpose) consider a
dimension-specialised kernel; this routine is the generic fallback used by
:cpp:class:`CpuBackend` and the GPU tensordot pre-layout path.

parameters.name: perm
parameters.description: Axis permutation; ``perm[d]`` names the source axis whose extent becomes output axis ``d``.

members.name: prod_axis_f32
members.signature: void prod_axis_f32(const float * in, float * out, int outer, int reduce_dim, int inner)
members.summary: Reduces a single axis by multiplicative product into a packed ``(outer, inner)`` output.
members.extended:
Identity element is ``1``; an empty reduce dimension yields ``1`` per cell.
Uses the generic ``axis_reduce`` template — there is no vDSP equivalent.
Note that floating-point products are not associative, so the result is
deterministic only for a fixed loop order (the kernel iterates ``r`` from
``0`` to ``reduce_dim - 1``).

**Math**

$$\text{out}[o, i] = \prod_{r=0}^{\text{reduce\_dim}-1} \text{in}[o, r, i].$$

parameters.name: in
parameters.description: Source buffer of shape ``(outer, reduce_dim, inner)``.

notes:
Overflow and underflow are unchecked.  Callers that require ``log-sum-exp``
style stability must compute the product in log space themselves.

members.signature: void prod_axis_f64(const double * in, double * out, int outer, int reduce_dim, int inner)
members.summary: Double-precision counterpart to :cpp:func:`prod_axis_f32`.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$\text{out}[o, i] = \prod_{r=0}^{\text{reduce\_dim}-1} \text{in}[o, r, i].$$

parameters.name: in
parameters.description: Source buffer of shape ``(outer, reduce_dim, inner)``.

members.name: rms_norm_backward_f32
members.signature: void rms_norm_backward_f32(const float * x, const float * gamma, const float * saved_rstd, const float * g, float * dx, float * dgamma, int outer, int N)
members.summary: Single-precision RMSNorm backward — produces input and weight gradients.
members.extended:
Reuses the saved per-row ``rstd`` to avoid recomputing the row's RMS.
``dgamma`` is *accumulated*; callers reducing over multiple sub-batches
must zero it once at the start.  ``dx`` is written fully (not accumulated).

**Math**

With $r = \text{rstd}$ and $c = \sum_j \gamma_j g_j x_j$,
$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_i} = r \!\left(\gamma_i g_i - \frac{x_i r^2 c}{N}\right),
  \qquad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \gamma_i} = \sum_{\text{rows}} g_i x_i r.$$

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Original forward input of shape ``(outer, N)``.

parameters.name: saved_rstd
parameters.description: Per-row reciprocal RMS from the matching forward call.

members.name: rms_norm_backward_f64
members.signature: void rms_norm_backward_f64(const double * x, const double * gamma, const double * saved_rstd, const double * g, double * dx, double * dgamma, int outer, int N)
members.summary: Double-precision counterpart to :cpp:func:`rms_norm_backward_f32`.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Original forward input of shape ``(outer, N)``.

parameters.name: saved_rstd
parameters.description: Per-row reciprocal RMS from the matching forward call.

parameters.name: dgamma
parameters.description: Output gradient w.r.t. ``gamma``; *accumulated* — caller must zero before the first invocation.

members.name: rms_norm_forward_f32
members.signature: void rms_norm_forward_f32(const float * x, const float * gamma, float * y, float * saved_rstd, int outer, int N, double eps)
members.summary: Single-precision RMSNorm forward — divides each row by its root-mean-square magnitude and scales by a per-feature gain.
members.extended:
Unlike LayerNorm there is no mean subtraction and no bias parameter, so the
kernel only stores ``rstd = 1/sqrt(mean(x^2) + eps)`` per row.  This makes
RMSNorm faster and is the form used in many modern transformer variants.

**Math**

$$y_i = \gamma_i \frac{x_i}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_j x_j^2 + \varepsilon}}.$$

**References**

Zhang & Sennrich, "Root Mean Square Layer Normalization" (NeurIPS 2019).

parameters.name: saved_rstd
parameters.description: Per-row reciprocal RMS, shape ``(outer,)``; consumed by :cpp:func:`rms_norm_backward_f32`.

parameters.name: outer, N
parameters.description: Number of rows and normalised-axis length.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Stabiliser added to ``mean(x^2)`` before the square root; cast to ``float``.

members.name: rms_norm_forward_f64
members.signature: void rms_norm_forward_f64(const double * x, const double * gamma, double * y, double * saved_rstd, int outer, int N, double eps)
members.summary: Double-precision counterpart to :cpp:func:`rms_norm_forward_f32`.

members.name: sgemm
members.signature: void sgemm(bool transA, bool transB, int M, int N, int K, float alpha, const float * A, int lda, const float * B, int ldb, float beta, float * C, int ldc)
members.summary: Single-precision general matrix multiply (GEMM).
members.extended:
Computes $C \leftarrow \alpha (A B) + \beta C$ in row-major layout using
Accelerate's ``cblas_sgemm``.  Each transpose flag is mapped to the
corresponding ``CBLAS_TRANSPOSE`` enum (``CblasNoTrans`` / ``CblasTrans``)
at zero copy cost — the BLAS kernel selects an alternate inner loop for
transposed operands.

**Math**

$$ C_{ij} \leftarrow \alpha \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} A_{ik} B_{kj} + \beta C_{ij} $$

**References**

BLAS Reference (Netlib), Accelerate.framework ``cblas_sgemm``.

parameters.name: transA, transB
parameters.description: Whether $A$ or $B$ should be transposed before the multiply.

parameters.name: M, N, K
parameters.description: Output is $M \times N$; the contracted dimension is $K$, so $A \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times K}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times N}$ (before optional transposition).

parameters.name: alpha, beta
parameters.description: Linear-combination coefficients.  Use $\alpha = 1, \beta = 0$ for a pure multiply; nonzero $\beta$ enables fused accumulate-into-C.

parameters.name: C
parameters.description: Row-major output buffer; updated in place when $\beta \neq 0$.

parameters.name: lda, ldb, ldc
parameters.description: Leading dimensions (row stride in elements) of $A$, $B$, $C$.

notes:
Single-threaded on Apple Silicon for small/medium sizes; Accelerate
dispatches to AMX (matrix coprocessor) for large GEMMs automatically.

members.name: sgemv
members.signature: void sgemv(bool transA, int M, int N, float alpha, const float * A, int lda, const float * x, int incx, float beta, float * y, int incy)
members.summary: Single-precision general matrix-vector multiply (GEMV).
members.extended:
Computes $y \leftarrow \alpha (A x) + \beta y$ in row-major layout via
Accelerate's ``cblas_sgemv``.  Significantly cheaper than ``sgemm`` for
vector right-hand sides because the inner loop stays in cache.

**Math**

$$ y_i \leftarrow \alpha \sum_{j=0}^{N-1} A_{ij} x_j + \beta y_i $$

**References**

Accelerate.framework ``cblas_sgemv``.

parameters.name: transA
parameters.description: If true, compute $y \leftarrow \alpha A^T x + \beta y$ instead.

parameters.name: M, N
parameters.description: $A \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N}$.  When ``transA`` is false, $x$ has $N$ elements and $y$ has $M$; reversed when ``transA`` is true.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Output vector buffer (stride ``incy``); updated in place.

parameters.name: incx, incy
parameters.description: Element stride within the input/output vectors.

members.name: sum_axis_f32
members.signature: void sum_axis_f32(const float * in, float * out, int outer, int reduce_dim, int inner)
members.summary: Reduces a single axis by summation into a packed ``(outer, inner)`` output.
members.extended:
For each ``(o, i)`` pair the kernel walks the reduce dimension contiguously
when ``inner == 1`` and dispatches to Accelerate's ``vDSP_sve`` for a
vectorised compensated-sum implementation; in the strided case it falls
back to the scalar ``axis_reduce`` template with identity ``0``.

**Math**

$$\text{out}[o, i] = \sum_{r=0}^{\text{reduce\_dim}-1} \text{in}[o, r, i].$$

**See Also**

max_axis_f32, min_axis_f32, prod_axis_f32 : Companion reductions sharing the
    same ``(outer, reduce_dim, inner)`` layout convention.

parameters.name: in
parameters.description: Source buffer laid out as ``(outer, reduce_dim, inner)`` in row-major order.

parameters.name: out
parameters.description: Destination buffer of shape ``(outer, inner)``; written densely.

parameters.name: outer
parameters.description: Product of all dimensions strictly before the reduce axis.  May be ``0``.

parameters.name: reduce_dim
parameters.description: Size of the axis being summed.  A value of ``0`` yields ``0`` per cell (the additive identity).

parameters.name: inner
parameters.description: Product of all dimensions strictly after the reduce axis.

notes:
The contiguous fast path uses ``vDSP_sve`` which performs a vectorised
single-pass summation on Apple Silicon NEON.  Numerical results may differ
from the scalar loop by a few ULP for very long reductions but are typically
stabler.

members.name: sum_axis_f64
members.signature: void sum_axis_f64(const double * in, double * out, int outer, int reduce_dim, int inner)
members.summary: Double-precision counterpart to :cpp:func:`sum_axis_f32`.
members.extended:
Uses ``vDSP_sveD`` on the contiguous (``inner == 1``) path and the generic
``axis_reduce`` template otherwise.

**Math**

$$\text{out}[o, i] = \sum_{r=0}^{\text{reduce\_dim}-1} \text{in}[o, r, i].$$

parameters.name: in
parameters.description: Source buffer of shape ``(outer, reduce_dim, inner)``.

members.name: vzconj_c64
members.signature: void vzconj_c64(const float * a, float * out, int n)
members.summary: Complex C64 conjugation on interleaved storage.
members.extended:
Returns the complex conjugate of an interleaved complex64 vector by
negating every imaginary component (every odd-indexed float) and leaving
the real components untouched.  Implemented as a ``vDSP_vneg`` over the
stride-2 imaginary view.

**Math**

$$ \text{out}_i = \overline{a_i} = \mathrm{Re}(a_i) - i \, \mathrm{Im}(a_i) $$

**References**

Accelerate.framework ``vDSP_vneg``.

parameters.name: out
parameters.description: Interleaved complex output, length $2n$ floats (may alias ``a``).

members.name: vzmul_c64
members.signature: void vzmul_c64(const float * a, const float * b, float * out, int n)
members.summary: Complex C64 elementwise multiplication on interleaved storage.
members.extended:
Multiplies two complex64 vectors stored as interleaved ``[re, im, re, im,
...]`` floats.  Internally rebinds the buffers as ``DSPSplitComplex`` views
(via Accelerate's ``DSPComplex *`` cast) and delegates to ``vDSP_zvmul``
with ``flag = 1`` (no conjugation).

**Math**

$$ \text{out}_i = a_i \cdot b_i, \quad a_i, b_i \in \mathbb{C} $$

**References**

Accelerate.framework ``vDSP_zvmul``.

parameters.name: out
parameters.description: Interleaved complex output buffer, length $2n$ floats.

members.name: batch_norm1d_op
members.signature: int batch_norm1d_op(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, double eps, const int & running_mean, const int & running_var, double momentum)
members.summary: Public entry point for 1-D Batch Normalization.
members.extended:
Applies training-mode batch normalization over the batch and length
axes of a sequence input.  This is the symbol the pybind11 binding
layer forwards to from :func:`lucid.nn.functional.batch_norm` when
the input has rank 3.

**See Also**

BatchNorm1dBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.
batch_norm_eval_op  : Inference-mode counterpart using running stats.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape ``(B, C, L)``.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: batch_norm3d_op
members.signature: int batch_norm3d_op(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, double eps, const int & running_mean, const int & running_var, double momentum)
members.summary: Public entry point for 3-D Batch Normalization.
members.extended:
Applies training-mode batch normalization over the batch and three
spatial axes — used by 3-D CNNs for volumetric data (video,
medical imaging).

**See Also**

BatchNorm3dBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape ``(B, C, D, H, W)``.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: batch_norm_eval_op
members.signature: int batch_norm_eval_op(const int & x, const int & mean, const int & var, const int & gamma, const int & beta, double eps)
members.summary: Public free-function entry point for inference-mode Batch Normalization.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that delegates to :func:`BatchNormEvalBackward::forward`.
This is the symbol the pybind11 binding layer forwards to from
:func:`lucid.nn.functional.batch_norm` when ``training=False``.

**See Also**

BatchNormEvalBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.
batch_norm_op         : Training-mode counterpart that uses batch stats.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: batch_norm_op
members.signature: int batch_norm_op(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, double eps, const int & running_mean, const int & running_var, double momentum)
members.summary: Public entry point for 2-D Batch Normalization.
members.extended:
Applies training-mode batch normalization over the batch and spatial
(height, width) axes.  This is the most common batch-norm variant —
used by virtually every CNN.

**See Also**

BatchNorm2dBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape ``(B, C, H, W)``.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: bernoulli_mask_storage
members.signature: int bernoulli_mask_storage(double keep_prob, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator & gen)
members.summary: Generate a Bernoulli dropout mask as a flat 1-D storage.
members.extended:
Each element is independently set to ``1.0`` with probability
``keep_prob`` and to ``0.0`` otherwise.  The mask is generated on CPU
using ``gen`` and then transferred to ``device`` via
``Dispatcher::from_cpu`` to keep the random number stream deterministic
regardless of where the consuming op runs.

parameters.name: keep_prob
parameters.description: Probability of keeping each element (``1 - dropout_rate``).

parameters.name: gen
parameters.description: Random-number generator owning the PRNG state.

returns.description: New 1-D storage holding the dropout mask.

members.name: bernoulli_mask_storage_shape
members.signature: int bernoulli_mask_storage_shape(double keep_prob, const int & shape, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator & gen)
members.summary: Generate a Bernoulli dropout mask matching an explicit N-D shape.
members.extended:
Same semantics as :func:`bernoulli_mask_storage` but the returned
storage carries ``shape`` directly instead of a flat 1-D shape.

returns.description: New storage of shape ``shape`` holding the mask.

members.name: bernoulli_op
members.signature: int bernoulli_op(const int & shape, double p, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator * gen)
members.summary: Allocates a tensor of independent Bernoulli$(p)$ samples.
members.extended:
Each element is independently drawn as ``1`` with probability $p$
and ``0`` with probability $1 - p$.

**Math**

$$x_i \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(p), \quad
  \Pr(x_i = 1) = p, \quad \Pr(x_i = 0) = 1 - p$$

**See Also**

:class:`lucid.distributions.Bernoulli` — higher-level
    distribution object built on top of this op.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Output dtype.  Both floating-point (``F32``/``F64`` storing ``0.0``/``1.0``) and integer (``U8``/``I32`` storing ``0``/``1``) are accepted.

returns.description: Newly allocated leaf tensor (``requires_grad = false``).

members.name: bilinear_layer_op
members.signature: int bilinear_layer_op(const int & x1, const int & x2, const int & weight, const int & bias)
members.summary: Public bilinear-layer entry point.
members.extended: Thin wrapper that delegates to ``BilinearLayerBackward::forward``.

parameters.name: x1
parameters.description: First operand of the bilinear form (last dim $= D_1$).

parameters.name: x2
parameters.description: Second operand of the bilinear form (last dim $= D_2$).

parameters.name: weight
parameters.description: Weight tensor of shape ``(D_out, D_1, D_2)``.

parameters.name: bias
parameters.description: Optional bias vector of shape ``(D_out,)``; pass ``nullptr`` to omit.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape ``(*, D_out)``.

members.name: bind_unary_extra
members.signature: void bind_unary_extra()
members.summary: Registers a unary op that accepts extra keyword arguments beyond the mandatory ``"a"`` tensor operand.
members.extended:
Variadic in the extra-argument pack so that arbitrary
:func:`py::arg`-style descriptors with default values can be
forwarded unchanged.  Typical use cases are activation functions
with hyperparameters (``leaky_relu(slope=0.01)``,
``elu(alpha=1.0)``).  The callable template parameter ``Fn`` is
intentionally unconstrained so that both plain function pointers
and lambdas wrapping the C++ op can be passed.

**See Also**

:func:`bind_unary` — without extra kwargs.

parameters.name: fn
parameters.description: Callable implementing the op.  Function pointer or lambda; the argument arity must match ``"a"`` plus the forwarded extra descriptors.

parameters.name: extra_args
parameters.description: Zero or more pybind11 argument descriptors (:func:`py::arg("name") = default`) appended after the implicit ``py::arg("a")``.

examples: Binding leaky ReLU with a slope hyperparameter::

bind_unary_extra<LeakyReluBackward>(m, &leaky_relu_op,
                                        py::arg("slope") = 0.01);

members.name: bitwise_and_op
members.signature: int bitwise_and_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise bitwise AND.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = a[i] & b[i]`` for every element.  Defined on
integer (``I8``/``I16``/``I32``/``I64``) and ``Bool`` dtypes; on Bool
inputs the result is equivalent to logical AND.

Non-differentiable: bitwise operations have no meaningful gradient on
integer manifolds, so no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = a[i] \,\wedge_{\text{bit}}\, b[i].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.bitwise_and``.

**See Also**

bitwise_or_op, bitwise_xor_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of identical shape, integer-or-Bool dtype, and device.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as the inputs.

raises.description: If shapes/devices differ, or if the input dtype is not integer or Bool.

notes:
* No broadcasting — shapes must match exactly.
* Floating-point inputs are rejected at the dispatch layer.

members.name: bitwise_left_shift_op
members.signature: int bitwise_left_shift_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise left shift.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = a[i] << b[i]`` for every element.  Defined only on
signed integer dtypes (``I8``/``I16``/``I32``/``I64``); ``Bool`` is
rejected because shifting a bit has no defined meaning.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = a[i] \cdot 2^{b[i]}.
$$
Out-of-range shift amounts follow the underlying C++ ``operator<<``
semantics for signed integers; callers should clamp ``b`` to
``[0, bitwidth)`` to stay portable.

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.left_shift``.

**See Also**

bitwise_right_shift_op

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Value to shift.  Signed integer dtype.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Shift amount per element.  Must match ``a`` in shape, dtype, and device.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as the inputs.

raises.description: If shapes/devices differ, or if the input dtype is not one of ``I8``/``I16``/``I32``/``I64``.

notes:
* Floating-point and Bool inputs are rejected at the dispatch layer.
* No broadcasting — shapes must match exactly.

members.name: bitwise_or_op
members.signature: int bitwise_or_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise bitwise OR.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = a[i] | b[i]`` for every element.  Defined on
integer and ``Bool`` dtypes; on Bool inputs this is equivalent to
logical OR.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = a[i] \,\vee_{\text{bit}}\, b[i].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.bitwise_or``.

**See Also**

bitwise_and_op, bitwise_xor_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of identical shape, integer-or-Bool dtype, and device.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as the inputs.

raises.description: If shapes/devices differ, or if the input dtype is not integer or Bool.

members.name: bitwise_right_shift_op
members.signature: int bitwise_right_shift_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise right shift.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = a[i] >> b[i]`` for every element.  Defined only on
signed integer dtypes (``I8``/``I16``/``I32``/``I64``); ``Bool`` is
rejected.  On signed inputs the shift is arithmetic (sign-extending),
matching the C++ ``operator>>`` convention used by the backend.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = \left\lfloor a[i] / 2^{b[i]} \right\rfloor.
$$
For negative ``a[i]`` the result is the floor of the true division,
i.e. the standard arithmetic-shift result.

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.right_shift``.

**See Also**

bitwise_left_shift_op

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Value to shift.  Signed integer dtype.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Shift amount per element.  Must match ``a`` in shape, dtype, and device.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as the inputs.

raises.description: If shapes/devices differ, or if the input dtype is not one of ``I8``/``I16``/``I32``/``I64``.

notes:
* Floating-point and Bool inputs are rejected at the dispatch layer.
* No broadcasting — shapes must match exactly.

members.name: bitwise_xor_op
members.signature: int bitwise_xor_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise bitwise XOR.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = a[i] ^ b[i]`` for every element.  Defined on
integer and ``Bool`` dtypes; on Bool inputs this is equivalent to
logical exclusive-or.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = a[i] \,\oplus_{\text{bit}}\, b[i].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.bitwise_xor``.

**See Also**

bitwise_and_op, bitwise_or_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of identical shape, integer-or-Bool dtype, and device.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as the inputs.

raises.description: If shapes/devices differ, or if the input dtype is not integer or Bool.

members.name: bmm_op
members.signature: int bmm_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Strict 3-D batched matrix multiply: $C_b = A_b \cdot B_b$.
members.extended:
Composite — rank-checked alias of :func:`matmul_op`.  Rejects inputs of
rank $\ne 3$ and demands matching leading batch dimensions so the error
path is crisp.  Gradient flows through ``MatmulBackward``.

**Math**

For every batch index $b \in [0, B)$:
$$
  C_{b, i, j} = \sum_{k} A_{b, i, k} \, B_{b, k, j}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`mm_op`, :func:`matmul_op`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand of shape ``(B, M, K)``.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand of shape ``(B, K, N)``.  Leading batch ``B`` must match ``a``.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``(B, M, N)``.

raises.description: If either input is null, not exactly 3-D, or batch dimensions disagree.

members.name: broadcast_back_for_reduce
members.signature: int broadcast_back_for_reduce(const int & grad, const int & grad_shape, const int & input_shape, const int & axes, bool keepdims, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Broadcast ``grad`` back to ``input_shape`` after a forward reduction.
members.extended:
The backward pass for any reduction op (``sum``, ``mean``, ``var``,
...) receives a gradient at the reduced shape and must re-expand it
across the squeezed axes so that the result matches the original input
shape.  This helper performs that expansion, taking the same
``axes`` / ``keepdims`` parameters as the forward call to know which
dimensions to broadcast back along.

**See Also**

:func:`reduce_output_shape` — the inverse direction.

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: Incoming gradient at the reduced shape.

parameters.name: grad_shape
parameters.description: Shape of ``grad``; must equal ``reduce_output_shape(input_shape, axes, keepdims)``.

parameters.name: input_shape
parameters.description: Shape to broadcast back to (the original forward input shape).

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axis list used in the forward reduction.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: Same value as passed to the forward reduction.

returns.description: New storage of shape ``input_shape`` carrying the expanded gradient.

raises.description: ``grad_shape`` does not equal the expected reduced shape.

members.name: broadcast_to_op
members.signature: int broadcast_to_op(const int & a, const int & shape)
members.summary: Broadcast a tensor to a larger shape following NumPy rules.
members.extended:
The input shape is right-aligned with ``shape``; size-1 dimensions are
replicated and newly prepended axes are inserted.  If ``a`` is not
contiguous a copy is made before the broadcast operation runs.

**See Also**

:func:`expand_op` (alias), :func:`BroadcastBackward`.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Target shape.  Must be NumPy-broadcast-compatible with ``a.shape``.

returns.description: A tensor of shape ``shape`` whose values are replicated from ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a.shape`` cannot be broadcast to ``shape`` (any mismatching axis where neither side is 1).

examples:
``broadcast_to_op([1, 2, 3], (4, 3))`` produces a 4x3 tensor where every
row equals ``[1, 2, 3]``.

members.name: bucketize_op
members.signature: int bucketize_op(const int & values, const int & boundaries, bool right)
members.summary: Bucket-index lookup: alias of :func:`searchsorted_op` with the operand order flipped to match the bucket-first convention.
members.extended:
Composite — delegates directly to :func:`searchsorted_op(boundaries,
values, right)`.  Not differentiable.

**Math**

$$
  y_{\ldots, j} = \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}\!\left[ b_i \prec v_{\ldots, j} \right]
$$
with $b$ = ``boundaries`` and the comparison $\prec$ selected by
``right`` per :func:`searchsorted_op`.

**See Also**

:func:`searchsorted_op`.

parameters.name: right
parameters.description: If ``true``, queries equal to a boundary land in the higher bucket.

returns.description: ``int64`` tensor of the same shape as ``values`` whose entries are bucket indices in $[0, \mathrm{len}(\mathrm{boundaries})]$.

members.name: cartesian_prod_op
members.signature: int cartesian_prod_op(const int & tensors)
members.summary: Enumerate every combination across a list of 1-D tensors.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`meshgrid_op` (with ``ij`` indexing) +
:func:`reshape_op` + :func:`stack_op`.  Builds the n-D grid, flattens
each component, and stacks the flattened columns along a new trailing
axis to produce a ``(N, D)`` matrix whose rows enumerate the full
Cartesian product in row-major (ij) order.  Gradient flows through
``MeshgridBackward``, ``ReshapeBackward``, and ``StackBackward``.

**Math**

For input tensors $t^{(0)}, \ldots, t^{(D-1)}$ of lengths
$n_0, \ldots, n_{D-1}$, the output rows are
$$
  \left( t^{(0)}_{i_0}, t^{(1)}_{i_1}, \ldots, t^{(D-1)}_{i_{D-1}} \right),
  \qquad (i_0, \ldots, i_{D-1}) \in \prod_d [0, n_d)
$$
in row-major (lex) order with $i_0$ varying slowest.

**See Also**

:func:`meshgrid_op`, :func:`stack_op`.

parameters.name: tensors
parameters.description: Non-empty list of 1-D tensors.  Lengths may differ.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``(N, D)`` where ``N = prod(lengths)`` and ``D`` is the number of inputs.  Dtype matches the inputs (must agree).

raises.description: If the input list is empty, any element is null, or any element is not 1-D.

members.name: ceil_inplace_op
members.signature: int ceil_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place ceiling: writes $\lceil x \rceil$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \lceil a_i \rceil$$

**See Also**

:func:`ceil_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to ceil in place.  Must be non-null.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: ceil_op
members.signature: int ceil_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise ceiling — returns the smallest integer-valued tensor not less than ``a``, with the same dtype as ``a``.
members.extended:
Not differentiable: output is detached from the autograd graph.

**Math**

$$y_i = \lceil x_i \rceil$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`floor_op`, :func:`round_op`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor.  Integer dtypes are returned unchanged.

returns.description: New tensor with ceiled values; same shape, dtype, and device as ``a``.

members.name: check_schema_determinism
members.signature: void check_schema_determinism(const OpSchema & schema)
members.summary: Determinism-only gate for a schema.
members.extended:
Equivalent to the first half of :class:`SchemaGuard`'s constructor.
Exists so callers that do not need AMP dtype resolution can perform
just the determinism check without paying for the (small) extra work.

parameters.name: schema
parameters.description: The op's schema record.  Only the ``deterministic``, ``determinism_note``, and ``name`` fields are read.

raises.description: Thrown when :func:`Determinism::is_enabled` is true and ``schema.deterministic`` is false.  The message starts with ``"non-deterministic op called under set_deterministic(True)"`` and appends ``schema.determinism_note`` in parentheses when non-empty.

notes:
When determinism is not globally enabled, this function is a no-op
and adds only a single relaxed atomic load to the call site.

members.name: check_version_match
members.signature: void check_version_match(const int & live, int saved_version, int op_name, int input_idx)
members.summary: Verify that a saved tensor's version counter has not advanced.
members.extended:
Compares the current ``version()`` of the tensor referenced by ``live``
against ``saved_version``.  When they differ the saved data has been
mutated in place since it was stashed for backward, which would silently
corrupt the gradient — so a :class:`VersionMismatch` is raised naming
the op and input that owns the broken contract.

parameters.name: live
parameters.description: Weak reference to the still-live tensor.  When the referent has been destroyed the check is skipped (no harm if it cannot be mutated after the fact).

parameters.name: saved_version
parameters.description: Version counter value captured when the tensor was saved.

parameters.name: op_name
parameters.description: Name of the op for diagnostic messages (e.g. ``"matmul"``).

parameters.name: input_idx
parameters.description: Zero-based index of the offending input within ``op_name``.

raises.description: The live counter differs from ``saved_version`` and the safety override (:func:`is_mutation_on_saved_allowed`) is false.

members.name: cholesky_op
members.signature: int cholesky_op(const int & a, bool upper)
members.summary: Compute the Cholesky factor of a symmetric positive-definite matrix.
members.extended:
Returns the lower-triangular $L$ (default) or upper-triangular $U$ such
that $A = LL^\top$ or $A = U^\top U$ respectively.  Forward dispatches to
LAPACK ``*potrf`` (CPU) or ``mlx::core::linalg::cholesky`` (GPU).  Batched
inputs are supported: every dimension except the trailing two is treated
as a batch axis and the factorisation is run independently on each
$N \times N$ slice.

**Math**

$$
  A = LL^\top \quad\text{or}\quad A = U^\top U.
$$
The corresponding reverse-mode rule (Murray 2016) is
$$
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial A} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, L^{-\top}
    \Phi\!\bigl(L^\top \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial L}\bigr) L^{-1},
$$
where $\Phi(M) = \mathrm{tril}(M) + \mathrm{tril}(M, -1)^\top$ copies the
strict lower triangle into the strict upper.  This formula is realised in
the Python wrapper via two triangular solves, not in this engine op.

**Shape**

- ``a``: ``(..., N, N)``.
- return: ``(..., N, N)``.

**See Also**

- ``solve_triangular_op`` — triangular back/forward substitution used in
  the Python-side Cholesky backward.
- ``ldl_factor_op`` — analogous factorisation for symmetric indefinite
  matrices.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Square symmetric positive-definite matrix of shape ``(..., N, N)``, dtype ``F32`` or ``F64``.

parameters.name: upper
parameters.description: If ``true`` return the upper factor $U$; otherwise (default) return the lower factor $L$.

returns.description: Triangular Cholesky factor with the same shape and dtype as ``a``. The opposite triangle is filled with zeros.

raises.description: If ``a`` is not at least 2-D, not square, or has a non-float dtype.
If LAPACK ``info > 0`` — a non-positive pivot was encountered, i.e. ``a`` is not positive definite (within working precision).

notes:
No autograd node is wired; the returned ``TensorImpl`` has
``requires_grad = false``.  Gradient-aware Cholesky is provided one layer
up by ``lucid.linalg.cholesky``.

members.name: chunk_op
members.signature: int chunk_op(const int & a, int chunks, int axis)
members.summary: Split a tensor into a fixed number of equal chunks along an axis.
members.extended:
Alias for :func:`split_op` with ``num_splits = chunks``.  Provided for
API symmetry with reference framework's ``chunk``.

**See Also**

:func:`split_op`.

parameters.name: chunks
parameters.description: Number of chunks; must divide ``a.shape[axis]`` exactly.

returns.description: ``chunks`` equal-sized tensors.

members.name: clip_inplace_op
members.signature: int clip_inplace_op(const int & a, double lo, double hi)
members.summary: In-place clip / clamp: writes $\mathrm{clip}(x, \mathit{lo}, \mathit{hi})$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \min(\mathit{hi}, \max(\mathit{lo}, a_i))$$

**See Also**

:func:`clip_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to clip in place.  Must be non-null.

parameters.name: lo
parameters.description: Lower bound; values below ``lo`` are set to ``lo``.

parameters.name: hi
parameters.description: Upper bound; values above ``hi`` are set to ``hi``.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

notes:
Implemented directly (without going through the generic
``inplace_unary`` helper) because the two scalar parameters
``lo`` / ``hi`` cannot be threaded through the zero-argument
function-pointer template the other entry points use.

members.name: clip_op
members.signature: int clip_op(const int & a, double min_v, double max_v)
members.summary: Element-wise clamp — returns ``a`` clipped to the range ``[min_v, max_v]`` with a fully wired autograd node.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$y_i = \max\bigl(\text{min\_v},\ \min(\text{max\_v},\ x_i)\bigr)$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:class:`ClipBackward`.

returns.description: Clamped output tensor with the same shape, dtype, and device as ``a``.  Gradient is ``g`` where ``min_v < a < max_v`` and zero where ``a`` saturates either bound.

notes:
Equivalent to reference framework's ``clamp`` (Lucid uses the name
``clip`` for the canonical op).  Saturated positions act as gradient
stoppers — useful for hard-tanh-style activations.

members.name: clone_state_storage
members.signature: int clone_state_storage(const int & src, const int & shape, Dtype dtype, Device device)
members.summary: Deep-copy an optimizer state buffer into a new owning TensorImpl.
members.extended:
Snapshots a state ``Storage`` (CPU or GPU) into a freshly allocated
``TensorImpl`` whose backing buffer is independent of the original.
This is the primitive used by derived optimizers when implementing
``state_buffers()`` so that the returned tensors survive any future
in-place updates by ``step()``.

**See Also**

overwrite_state_storage : in-place inverse used by
    ``load_state_buffers``.

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: Source state buffer.  Must be either ``CpuStorage`` or ``GpuStorage``.  For ``GpuStorage`` the underlying MLX array is materialised via ``eval()`` before copying.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Logical shape to attach to the returned ``TensorImpl``.  Must match the element count implied by ``src``.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: Dtype tag to record on the new ``TensorImpl``.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device tag to record on the new ``TensorImpl``.

returns.description: A non-leaf tensor whose storage is a deep copy of ``src``.

raises.description: If ``src`` is neither a ``CpuStorage`` nor a ``GpuStorage``.

notes:
CPU copies use ``allocate_aligned_bytes`` followed by ``memcpy``;
GPU copies build an independent MLX array.  In both cases the
resulting buffer can be mutated freely without affecting the source
optimizer state.

members.name: clone_storage
members.signature: int clone_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Allocate a fresh copy of ``s`` (deep clone).

returns.description: Newly allocated buffer with the same contents as ``s``.

members.name: compile_trace
members.signature: CompiledExecutable * compile_trace(const TraceGraph & graph, const int & external_feeds, int * error_msg)
members.summary: Lower ``graph`` into an MPSGraph executable.

parameters.name: external_feeds
parameters.description: The trace's external feeds (model parameters + user inputs), mapped by the :type:`TensorId` minted during :func:`Tracer::on_op_io`.  Each entry becomes one MPSGraph placeholder, and the corresponding ``TensorImplPtr`` carries the runtime data that will be bound to that placeholder.

parameters.name: error_msg
parameters.description: If non-null, populated with a human-readable reason whenever the build aborts (unsupported op, mixed-device trace, dtype mismatch, …).

returns.description: Heap-allocated executable handle on success, or ``nullptr`` on any abort condition (caller falls back to eager dispatch). Caller owns the returned pointer.

notes:
Phase 1.2 step 1 supports only Linear forward in the emitter
registry; everything else returns ``nullptr`` cleanly.  The op gap
protocol (per-op spike → expand coverage incrementally) is the
canonical way new families come online.

members.name: current_tracer
members.signature: Tracer * current_tracer()
members.summary: Returns the :class:`Tracer` currently installed on the calling thread.

returns.description: Non-owning pointer to the active tracer, or ``nullptr`` if no tracer is installed.

notes:
The pointer lives in ``thread_local`` storage; each thread reads its
own value.  Ownership of the pointed-to object remains with the
caller of :func:`set_current_tracer`.

members.name: destroy_executable
members.signature: void destroy_executable(CompiledExecutable * exe)
members.summary: Release a previously-built executable.  Safe to call with ``nullptr``.  Defined in :file:`CompiledExecutable.mm`; releases the ARC-strong reference held on the wrapped ``MPSGraphExecutable*``.

members.name: executable_input_ids
members.signature: int executable_input_ids(const CompiledExecutable * exe)
members.summary: Feed-order :type:`TensorId` list (returns a copy so callers can keep the result alive past the executable's lifetime).  Empty when ``exe`` is ``nullptr``.

members.name: executable_num_inputs
members.signature: int executable_num_inputs(const CompiledExecutable * exe)
members.summary: Number of inputs the executable expects, in feed order.
members.extended:
Returns ``0`` when ``exe`` is ``nullptr``.  Useful for binding-layer
validation before invoking :func:`run_executable`.

members.name: executable_num_outputs
members.signature: int executable_num_outputs(const CompiledExecutable * exe)
members.summary: Number of outputs the executable produces, in target order.
members.extended: Returns ``0`` when ``exe`` is ``nullptr``.

members.name: executable_output_ids
members.signature: int executable_output_ids(const CompiledExecutable * exe)
members.summary: Target-order :type:`TensorId` list — see :func:`executable_input_ids`.

members.name: find_emitter
members.signature: OpEmitter * find_emitter(int op_name)
members.summary: Look up the emitter for ``op_name``; returns ``nullptr`` if no emitter is registered.  Used by :class:`MpsBuilder` to decide between emit and eager-fallback.

members.name: make_cache_key
members.signature: CacheKey make_cache_key(const TraceGraph & graph)
members.summary: Build a :class:`CacheKey` from a :class:`TraceGraph`.
members.extended:
Walks ``graph.ops`` once, copying the name + first-output meta from
each node.  Device is taken from the most recent non-empty output;
it is the caller's responsibility to ensure the trace is
single-device (the MpsBuilder rejects mixed-device traces before
hashing).

members.name: run_executable
members.signature: int run_executable(CompiledExecutable * exe, const int & input_feeds)
members.summary: Run a compiled executable.

parameters.name: exe
parameters.description: Executable returned by :func:`compile_trace`; must be non-null.

parameters.name: input_feeds
parameters.description: Inputs in the executable's ``input_ids`` order.  Each feed must live on the same device as the executable; the GPU path materialises the underlying ``MTLBuffer`` via the MLX bridge and binds it as an ``MPSGraphTensorData``.

returns.description: Freshly-allocated output tensors in ``output_ids`` order.  Each output's ``GpuStorage`` wraps a new ``MTLBuffer`` allocated by the executable run — no copy back to MLX.

notes:
Implementation lives in :file:`CompiledExecutable.mm` (Objective-C++)
so all MPSGraph types stay in one translation unit.

members.name: set_current_tracer
members.signature: void set_current_tracer(Tracer * t)
members.summary: Installs ``t`` as the active :class:`Tracer` for the calling thread.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`current_tracer` — reader for the same TLS slot.

parameters.name: t
parameters.description: Tracer to install, or ``nullptr`` to detach the active tracer without destroying the object.

notes:
Ownership is not transferred — the caller keeps the Tracer alive
for as long as any :class:`OpScopeFull` could observe the pointer.
The Python binding holds the underlying ``shared_ptr`` for exactly
this reason (matches :func:`lucid::set_current_profiler`).

members.name: complex_op
members.signature: int complex_op(const int & re, const int & im)
members.summary: Combine two real tensors into a complex tensor.
members.extended:
The result has dtype ``C64`` and the same shape and device as the inputs.
Both inputs must be real-floating (``F16`` / ``F32`` / ``F64``); the
resulting interleaved storage holds $a + b\,i$ element-wise.

**Math**

$$
  z_k = a_k + b_k\,i
$$

**See Also**

real_op, imag_op, conj_op

parameters.name: re
parameters.description: Real part.  Must be a real-floating dtype.

parameters.name: im
parameters.description: Imaginary part.  Same shape, device, and dtype family as ``re``.

returns.description: Complex (C64) tensor with the same shape and device as the inputs.

raises.description: If either input is not a real-floating dtype.
If ``re`` and ``im`` have different shapes.
If ``re`` and ``im`` live on different devices.

notes:
Backward is supplied by the Python autograd layer via ``real`` / ``imag``
extraction of the incoming complex gradient; this entry point only
implements the forward.

members.name: concatenate_op
members.signature: int concatenate_op(const int & xs, int axis)
members.summary: Concatenate a sequence of tensors along an existing axis.
members.extended:
All inputs must share dtype, device, and rank; every dimension except
``axis`` must agree exactly.  The output reuses freshly allocated storage —
no input buffer is aliased — and the autograd node saves only per-input
shapes so backward can split the gradient back into one slice per input.

**Math**

$$ y_{[\ldots,\, k,\, \ldots]} = x^{(i)}_{[\ldots,\, k - \sum_{j<i} N_j,\, \ldots]} $$
where $i$ is the unique input index satisfying
$\sum_{j<i} N_j \le k < \sum_{j \le i} N_j$.

**Shape**

Inputs of shape ``(D_0, ..., D_{axis-1}, N_i, D_{axis+1}, ..., D_{ndim-1})``
produce output of shape
``(D_0, ..., D_{axis-1}, \sum_i N_i, D_{axis+1}, ..., D_{ndim-1})``.

**See Also**

:func:`stack_op`, :func:`split_op`, :func:`hstack_op`, :func:`vstack_op`.

parameters.name: xs
parameters.description: Non-empty list of input tensors with matching dtype, device, and rank. All dimensions except ``axis`` must be identical across inputs.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis along which to concatenate.  Supports negative indexing (wrapped to ``axis + ndim``).  Must satisfy ``-ndim <= axis < ndim``.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape ``(..., \sum_i N_i, ...)`` where the ``axis`` dimension is the sum of each input's ``axis`` dimension.

raises.description: If inputs disagree on dtype.
If inputs reside on different devices.
If inputs disagree on any non-concat dimension, or differ in rank.

notes:
Backward slices the incoming gradient back into per-input pieces using the
saved size of each input along ``axis``; the schema is
``"concatenate"`` with ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``.

members.name: conj_op
members.signature: int conj_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Return the element-wise complex conjugate of a tensor.
members.extended:
For ``C64`` input, produces a fresh C64 tensor whose imaginary part is
negated.  For any real dtype the operator is a no-op (the conjugate of a
real number equals itself) and the backend short-circuits the dispatch.

**Math**

$$
  y_k = \overline{z_k}
$$

**See Also**

real_op, imag_op, complex_op

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor.  May be any dtype; only ``C64`` produces a non-trivial transformation.

returns.description: Tensor with the same shape, dtype, and device as ``a``.

notes:
Backward is performed at the Python autograd layer as another ``conj`` on
the incoming gradient, consistent with the Wirtinger calculus convention
adopted by the reference framework.

members.name: contiguous_op
members.signature: int contiguous_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Return a dense, row-major copy of ``a`` whenever its strides diverge from the canonical contiguous layout; return ``a`` unchanged when it is already contiguous.
members.extended:
This is the public entry point every CRTP kernel ``forward()`` calls
on CPU inputs before handing storage to a backend compute routine.
Slices and transposes produce non-unit strides without copying data,
but the Accelerate (CPU) kernels assume stride-1 row-major access, so
materialising a contiguous copy is required to avoid garbage reads.

**See Also**

:class:`ContiguousBackward` — the underlying op implementation.
:class:`UnaryKernel`, :class:`BinaryKernel`, :class:`ReduceKernel` —
CRTP bases that invoke this helper during forward.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Source tensor.  Must be non-null.  May live on either device.

returns.description: Either ``a`` itself (when ``a->is_contiguous()`` is true) or a freshly allocated :class:`TensorImpl` with the same shape, dtype, and device but a contiguous storage whose elements are copied from ``a`` honouring its strides and ``storage_offset``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is null.

notes:
**Autograd.**  Implemented via ``ContiguousBackward`` so the operation
participates in the autograd graph; the backward is the identity on
the incoming gradient (a clone to give the upstream node an owning
buffer).  See ``ops/utils/Contiguous.cpp``.

**GPU path.**  On :data:`Device::GPU` the underlying MLX backend
handles non-contiguous storage natively via lazy evaluation, so the
dispatched ``contiguous`` is typically a cheap pass-through.  Kernel
``forward()`` helpers therefore guard the call with a
``device() == CPU && !is_contiguous()`` predicate.

**Aliasing.**  The returned tensor never aliases ``a``'s storage when
a copy was performed; consumers may freely mutate it without
violating the version counter on ``a``.

members.name: contiguous_stride
members.signature: int contiguous_stride(const int & shape, int elem_size)
members.summary: Computes the row-major (C-contiguous) byte-stride vector for a tensor with the given shape and element size.
members.extended:
The stride is built back-to-front: the innermost (last) axis has stride
``elem_size``, and each outer axis's stride is the product of the inner
stride and the inner dimension size.  This matches NumPy's default
``'C'`` order and is the layout expected by Apple Accelerate BLAS / vDSP
routines.

**Math**

$$\text{stride}[i] = \text{elem\_size} \cdot \prod_{j > i} \text{shape}[j]$$

**See Also**

:func:`TensorMeta::is_contiguous_for`
    Reverse check — verifies a stored stride against the canonical one.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Target shape.  Empty shape yields an empty stride.

parameters.name: elem_size
parameters.description: Element size in bytes — typically obtained from :func:`dtype_size`.

returns.description: Vector of the same length as ``shape``.  When the result is fed back into :class:`TensorMeta`, :func:`TensorImpl::is_contiguous` returns ``true``.

examples:
contiguous_stride({2, 3, 4}, 4)    // {48, 16, 4}  (float32)
    contiguous_stride({}, 4)           // {}           (scalar)

members.name: conv1d_op
members.signature: int conv1d_op(const int & x, const int & W, const int & b, int stride_l, int pad_l, int dilation_l, int groups)
members.summary: One-dimensional cross-correlation over a batch of signals.
members.extended:
Computes the autograd-aware op
$y[b, c_o, l] = \sum_{c_i, k} x[b, c_i, s\,l + d\,k] \cdot W[c_o, c_i, k] + b[c_o]$
where ``s`` is the stride and ``d`` is the dilation.

parameters.name: W
parameters.description: Filters of shape ``(C_out, C_in / groups, KL)``.

parameters.name: stride_l
parameters.description: Stride along the length axis.  Default: ``1``.

parameters.name: pad_l
parameters.description: Zero-padding added to both ends of ``L``.  Default: ``0``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C_out, L_out)`` where $L_\text{out} = \lfloor (L + 2 p - d(KL - 1) - 1)/s + 1 \rfloor$.

members.name: conv2d_op
members.signature: int conv2d_op(const int & x, const int & W, const int & b, int stride_h, int stride_w, int pad_h, int pad_w, int dilation_h, int dilation_w, int groups)
members.summary: Two-dimensional cross-correlation over a batch of images / feature maps.
members.extended:
Computes
$$
y[b, c_o, h, w] = \sum_{c_i, k_h, k_w}
  x[b, c_i, s_h h + d_h k_h, s_w w + d_w k_w]
  \cdot W[c_o, c_i, k_h, k_w] + b[c_o].
$$

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape ``(B, C_in, H, W)``.

parameters.name: W
parameters.description: Filters of shape ``(C_out, C_in / groups, KH, KW)``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C_out, H_out, W_out)`` with $H_\text{out} = \lfloor (H + 2 p_h - d_h(KH - 1) - 1)/s_h + 1 \rfloor$ and analogously for $W_\text{out}$.

members.name: conv3d_op
members.signature: int conv3d_op(const int & x, const int & W, const int & b, int stride_d, int stride_h, int stride_w, int pad_d, int pad_h, int pad_w, int dilation_d, int dilation_h, int dilation_w, int groups)
members.summary: Three-dimensional cross-correlation over a batch of volumes.
members.extended:
Performs the natural extension of 2-D convolution to the additional
depth axis ``D``, summing the kernel response across
$(k_d, k_h, k_w)$.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape ``(B, C_in, D, H, W)``.

parameters.name: W
parameters.description: Filters of shape ``(C_out, C_in / groups, KD, KH, KW)``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C_out, D_out, H_out, W_out)`` where each output extent follows $X_\text{out} = \lfloor (X + 2 p_x - d_x(K_X - 1) - 1)/s_x + 1 \rfloor$ for $X \in \{D, H, W\}$.

members.name: conv_transpose1d_op
members.signature: int conv_transpose1d_op(const int & x, const int & W, const int & b, int stride_l, int pad_l, int opad_l)
members.summary: One-dimensional transposed convolution (fractionally-strided conv).
members.extended:
Upsamples a 1-D batch by inserting ``stride - 1`` implicit zeros
between consecutive input positions, then applies a regular
cross-correlation against the kernel.  This is the primitive used in
decoder / generator architectures to lengthen sequences.

parameters.name: pad_l
parameters.description: Length-axis padding subtracted from the output extent. Default: ``0``.

parameters.name: opad_l
parameters.description: Length-axis output padding (one-sided).  Must be ``< stride_l``. Default: ``0``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C_out, L_out)`` where $L_\text{out} = (L - 1)\, s - 2 p + KL + p^\text{out}$.

members.name: conv_transpose2d_op
members.signature: int conv_transpose2d_op(const int & x, const int & W, const int & b, int stride_h, int stride_w, int pad_h, int pad_w, int opad_h, int opad_w)
members.summary: Two-dimensional transposed convolution (fractionally-strided conv).
members.extended:
The 2-D upsampling primitive used by VAE / GAN decoders, U-Net
expansion paths, and super-resolution heads.  Equivalent to the
gradient of a 2-D convolution w.r.t. its input.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape ``(B, C_in, H, W)``.

parameters.name: W
parameters.description: Filters of shape ``(C_in, C_out, KH, KW)``.

parameters.name: pad_h, pad_w
parameters.description: Per-axis padding subtracted from the output extent. Default: ``0``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C_out, H_out, W_out)`` with $H_\text{out} = (H - 1) s_h - 2 p_h + KH + p^\text{out}_h$ and analogously for $W_\text{out}$.

notes:
When ``stride > 1`` and ``kernel_size`` is not divisible by
``stride`` the output can exhibit characteristic *checkerboard*
artefacts.  A common mitigation is to choose
``kernel_size = stride * n`` or to substitute bilinear upsampling
followed by a regular convolution.

members.name: conv_transpose3d_op
members.signature: int conv_transpose3d_op(const int & x, const int & W, const int & b, int stride_d, int stride_h, int stride_w, int pad_d, int pad_h, int pad_w, int opad_d, int opad_h, int opad_w)
members.summary: Three-dimensional transposed convolution.
members.extended:
The 3-D extension used in volumetric decoders, video generators and
medical-image synthesis.  All three spatial axes are upsampled
simultaneously.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape ``(B, C_in, D, H, W)``.

parameters.name: W
parameters.description: Filters of shape ``(C_in, C_out, KD, KH, KW)``.

parameters.name: pad_d, pad_h, pad_w
parameters.description: Per-axis padding subtracted from each output extent. Default: ``0``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C_out, D_out, H_out, W_out)`` with each output extent following $X_\text{out} = (X - 1) s_x - 2 p_x + K_X + p^\text{out}_x$ for $X \in \{D, H, W\}$.
