members.name: cross_entropy_op
members.signature: int cross_entropy_op(const int & input, const int & target, const int & weight_or_null, int reduction, double eps, int ignore_index)
members.summary: Public cross-entropy (fused log-softmax + NLL) entry point.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Logits of shape ``(N, C)`` or ``(N, C, d_1, ...)``.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Integer class labels of shape ``(N,)`` or ``(N, d_1, ...)``.

parameters.name: weight_or_null
parameters.description: Optional per-class weight vector of length $C$; pass null when unused.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical clamp passed through to the backend.

parameters.name: ignore_index
parameters.description: Class index excluded from the loss and gradient.

returns.description: Scalar (Mean / Sum) or per-sample (None) loss tensor.

members.name: ctc_loss_op
members.signature: int ctc_loss_op(const int & log_probs, const int & targets, const int & input_lengths, const int & target_lengths, int blank, bool zero_infinity)
members.summary: Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss.
members.extended:
Computes the negative log-probability of all valid alignments between an
input log-probability sequence and a flat target label sequence using the
forward algorithm (Graves et al., 2006).  The blank symbol allows the model
to emit "no character" at any time step, and the loss is summed over all
valid label expansions:
$$
  \mathcal{L} = -\log \sum_{\pi \in \mathcal{B}^{-1}(y)}
    \prod_{t=1}^{T} p_{\pi_t}^{(t)}
$$
where $\mathcal{B}$ is the standard CTC collapse operation.  No reduction
is applied here — the caller is expected to apply ``mean`` / ``sum`` in
Python.

**References**

Graves et al., "Connectionist Temporal Classification: Labelling
Unsegmented Sequence Data with Recurrent Neural Networks" (ICML 2006).

parameters.name: log_probs
parameters.description: Log-probability tensor of shape ``(T, N, C)``.

parameters.name: targets
parameters.description: Flat ``int32`` label tensor of shape ``(N * S,)`` where $S$ is the maximum target length.

parameters.name: input_lengths
parameters.description: ``int32`` vector of shape ``(N,)`` giving the valid time-step count per sample.

parameters.name: target_lengths
parameters.description: ``int32`` vector of shape ``(N,)`` giving the true label length per sample.

parameters.name: blank
parameters.description: Index of the CTC blank class.

parameters.name: zero_infinity
parameters.description: When ``true``, infinite loss values (alignments with zero probability) are clamped to zero — both forward and backward.

returns.description: Per-sample loss tensor of shape ``(N,)``.

members.name: cube_inplace_op
members.signature: int cube_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place cube: writes $x^3$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow a_i^3$$

**See Also**

:func:`cube_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to cube in place.  Must be non-null.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: cube_op
members.signature: int cube_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = x^3$ element-wise.
members.extended:
Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and
delegates to :class:`CubeBackward::forward`.

**See Also**

:class:`CubeBackward` — backward node.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype.

members.name: cube_root_op
members.signature: int cube_root_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise cube root — returns $\sqrt[3]{a}$ with a fully wired autograd node.
members.extended:
Defined for all real inputs (positive, zero, and negative); the
negative branch is the real cube root, not the complex principal value.

**Math**

$$y_i = \sqrt[3]{x_i}, \qquad
\frac{\partial y_i}{\partial x_i} = \frac{1}{3 y_i^{2}}$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:class:`CubeRootBackward`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor (promoted to FP32 by the op schema).

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``; gradient is $g / (3 y^{2})$.

examples:
``cube_root_op(-8.0) == -2.0``  (real cube root of a negative number).
``cube_root_op(27.0) == 3.0``.
notes:
CPU implementation uses Accelerate's ``vvcbrtf``; GPU uses MLX's
power op with exponent $1/3$.  The gradient is singular at zero —
avoid backpropagating through inputs that touch the origin.

members.name: cummax_op
members.signature: int cummax_op(const int & a, int axis)
members.summary: Inclusive cumulative maximum along a single axis, $y_k = \max_{j \le k} x_j$.
members.extended:
Output is non-decreasing along ``axis``.  Backward credits the
upstream gradient $dy_k$ to the position that **first** achieved the
running maximum up to $k$ (i.e. the unique $i \le k$ at which the
running max changed value); positions that merely tie with the
running max receive no gradient.

**Math**

$$
  y_k = \max_{j \le k} x_j, \qquad
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} =
  \sum_{k \ge i} dy_k \cdot \mathbb{1}[\,i = \arg\max_{j \le k} x_j\,].
$$

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any non-scalar shape; only F32 / F64 are supported by the backward pass.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis along which to accumulate (negative wraps).

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

notes:
Backward is computed by a right-to-left segmented accumulation on
CPU (or evaluated on CPU after a GPU round-trip).  Saves only the
forward output; the input is not needed.

members.name: cummin_op
members.signature: int cummin_op(const int & a, int axis)
members.summary: Inclusive cumulative minimum along a single axis, $y_k = \min_{j \le k} x_j$.
members.extended:
Symmetric to :func:`cummax_op`: gradient flows only to the position
that first achieved the running minimum at each $k$.

**Math**

$$
  y_k = \min_{j \le k} x_j, \qquad
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} =
  \sum_{k \ge i} dy_k \cdot \mathbb{1}[\,i = \arg\min_{j \le k} x_j\,].
$$

**See Also**

:func:`cummax_op` — symmetric maximum scan.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any non-scalar shape; backward supports F32 / F64.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis along which to accumulate (negative wraps).

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

members.name: cumprod_op
members.signature: int cumprod_op(const int & a, int axis)
members.summary: Inclusive cumulative product along a single axis, $y_k = \prod_{j \le k} x_j$.
members.extended:
The output has the same shape and dtype as the input.  Backward
reuses the reverse-cumsum primitive against the saved cumprod
output and divides by the saved input:
$dx = \mathrm{reverse}(\mathrm{cumsum}(\mathrm{reverse}(dy \cdot y)))
/ x$.

**Math**

$$
  y_k = \prod_{j \le k} x_j, \qquad
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} =
  \sum_{j \ge i} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_j}
  \cdot \frac{y_j}{x_i}.
$$

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any non-scalar shape and real dtype.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis along which to accumulate (negative wraps).

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

notes:
Backward is undefined where $x_i = 0$ because the formula divides
by $x$.  Forward saves both $x$ and $y$ on the backward node.

members.name: cumsum_op
members.signature: int cumsum_op(const int & a, int axis)
members.summary: Inclusive cumulative sum along a single axis, $y_k = \sum_{j \le k} x_j$.
members.extended:
The output has the same shape and dtype as the input.  Backward
uses the reverse-cumsum trick to avoid an explicit $O(n^2)$
summation: reverse the upstream gradient along the axis, take its
cumsum, then reverse again.

**Math**

$$
  y_k = \sum_{j \le k} x_j, \qquad
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} =
  \sum_{j \ge i} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y_j}.
$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals ``a.shape``.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any non-scalar shape and real dtype.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis along which to accumulate.  May be negative; in that case it is interpreted as ``axis + ndim``.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is a scalar (``ndim == 0``) or ``axis`` is out of range.

notes:
Dispatch: Accelerate strided cumsum (CPU) / MLX ``cumsum`` (GPU).
Backward is `O(n)` per axis-stride.

members.name: current_profiler
members.signature: Profiler * current_profiler()
members.summary: Returns the :class:`Profiler` currently registered for the calling thread.

returns.description: Non-owning pointer to the active profiler, or ``nullptr`` if no profiler is registered on this thread.

notes:
The pointer is stored in ``thread_local`` storage, so each thread
sees its own value independently.  Ownership of the pointed-to
:class:`Profiler` remains with the caller.

members.name: default_generator
members.signature: Generator & default_generator()
members.summary: Returns the process-wide default :class:`Generator` instance.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`lucid.manual_seed` — Python convenience wrapper.

returns.description: Reference to the global singleton, lazily constructed on first access with ``seed = 0``.  Lifetime persists for the duration of the process.

examples: Re-seed every distribution-op call across the whole process::

default_generator().set_seed(42);
    // every subsequent randn / uniform / bernoulli sees seed=42
notes:
All random ops that are not given an explicit ``generator=`` kwarg
route through this singleton.  Python's
:func:`lucid.manual_seed(seed)` is implemented by calling
:func:`Generator::set_seed` on the object returned here.

members.name: det_op
members.signature: int det_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $\det(A)$ for a square float tensor.
members.extended:
Validates that ``a`` is at least 2-D, square in the trailing two
dimensions, and float-typed; dispatches to the backend; and wires
``DetBackward`` into the autograd graph (saving both $A$ and the
scalar output) when grad mode is active.

**Shape**

- ``a``: ``(..., N, N)``.
- Output: ``(...,)`` (rank decreases by 2; a single 2-D input
  yields a 0-D scalar).

**See Also**

inv_op : Matrix inverse, used in the backward.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input matrix of shape ``(..., N, N)``.

returns.description: Scalar-per-batch determinant of shape ``(...,)`` (the trailing two matrix dimensions are removed).

raises.description: If ``a`` is fewer than 2 dimensions, non-square, or non-float.
(At backend level.) If $A$ is singular — LU returns a zero pivot.

notes:
For ill-conditioned $A$ prefer ``slogdet`` (sign + log) when it
becomes available; the magnitude of ``det`` can over/underflow long
before $A$ is numerically singular.

members.name: device_name
members.signature: int device_name(Device d)
members.summary: Returns the canonical lower-case name for a device tag.
members.extended:
The string form is what surfaces in error messages, ``repr(tensor)``
output, and the Python :class:`Device` wrapper's ``__str__``.

returns.description: ``"cpu"`` for :attr:`Device::CPU`, ``"gpu"`` for :attr:`Device::GPU`. The returned view points into a static string literal — safe to store beyond the call site.

raises.description: The enum was extended without updating this switch.  Indicates a missing recompile of all translation units.

examples: const auto name = device_name(Device::GPU);   // "gpu"

members.name: diag_op
members.signature: int diag_op(const int & v, int k)
members.summary: Extract a diagonal from a 2-D matrix or construct a 2-D matrix from a 1-D diagonal vector.
members.extended:
Follows ``numpy.diag`` semantics:
  - If ``v`` is 1-D of length $N$, output is 2-D of shape
    $(N + |k|, N + |k|)$ with ``v`` on diagonal $k$ and zeros elsewhere.
  - If ``v`` is 2-D, output is 1-D containing entries
    ``v[i, i + k]`` for valid $i$.

returns.description: 1-D or 2-D output (the opposite rank from ``v``).

raises.description: If ``v`` is neither 1-D nor 2-D.

members.name: diagonal_op
members.signature: int diagonal_op(const int & a, int offset, int axis1, int axis2)
members.summary: Extract a single diagonal as a final-axis vector.
members.extended:
Returns the elements of ``a`` lying on the diagonal of the
``(axis1, axis2)`` plane offset by ``offset``: positive offsets shift
the diagonal toward the upper-right, negative offsets toward the
lower-left.  The two selected axes are removed and a new trailing axis
of length ``L`` is appended.

parameters.name: axis2
parameters.description: Second axis defining the plane.  Must differ from ``axis1``; the two are canonicalised to ``a1 < a2``.

returns.description: Tensor whose shape is ``a.shape`` with axes ``axis1`` and ``axis2`` removed and a trailing dimension of length $L = \max(0, \min(M - r_0, N - c_0))$ appended, where $M, N$ are the sizes of the two selected axes and $r_0, c_0$ are the starting row / column for the given offset.

notes:
Backward scatters the diagonal gradient into a zero tensor of the
original input shape, leaving off-diagonal positions zero.

members.name: div_inplace_op
members.signature: int div_inplace_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: In-place elementwise division: writes $a / b$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \frac{a_i}{b_i}$$

**See Also**

:func:`div_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand and in-place target.  Must be non-null and must not share storage with a view.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand.  Must be non-null and broadcastable into ``a->shape()``.  Division by zero follows the backend's IEEE-754 convention (typically $\pm\infty$ or NaN).

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``, now holding the quotient storage.

raises.description: If the broadcast output would change ``a``'s shape.
If ``a`` shares storage with a view.

members.name: div_op
members.signature: int div_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Public entry point — element-wise division with autograd support.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$ y = \frac{a}{b} $$

**See Also**

add_op, sub_op, mul_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of any rank.  Shapes must be NumPy-broadcast-compatible.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape ``broadcast_shapes(a.shape, b.shape)`` and promoted dtype.  Participates in autograd via :class:`DivBackward`.

raises.description: If ``a`` and ``b`` have incompatible shapes under NumPy broadcasting rules, or if their devices differ.

examples: >>> auto c = div_op(a, b);    // c = a / b

warns.annotation: No explicit zero-divisor guard.  Division by zero yields IEEE-754
$\pm\infty$ / NaN in both the forward output and gradient.

members.name: divide_storages
members.signature: int divide_storages(const int & a, const int & b, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise division: returns ``a / b``.

returns.description: New storage holding the element-wise quotient.

members.name: dot_op
members.signature: int dot_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Compute the dot product of two vectors or the matrix product of two matrices, with autograd support for both cases.
members.extended:
Dispatches on rank:

- **1-D × 1-D** — inner product
    $c = \sum_i a_i b_i$ producing a scalar (shape ``{}``).
    Backed by Accelerate's ``cblas_*dot`` via the element-wise multiply
    and ``reduce_sum`` backend primitives.
    Backward node: ``Dot1DBackward`` (saves both operands).
  - **2-D × 2-D** — matrix product $C = A B$.
    Backed by ``cblas_*gemm`` (Accelerate) on CPU, ``mlx::matmul`` on GPU.
    Backward node: ``Dot2DBackward`` (saves both operands).

**Math**

1-D case:
$$
  c = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} a_i b_i, \qquad
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial a_i} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial c}\, b_i,
  \qquad
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial b_i} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial c}\, a_i
$$
2-D case:
$$
  C = A B, \qquad
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial A} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial C}\, B^\top,
  \qquad
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial B} = A^\top\, \frac{\partial L}{\partial C}
$$

**Shape**

- 1-D: ``a.shape[0] == b.shape[0]``; output is rank-0.
- 2-D: ``a.shape[1] == b.shape[0]``; output is $(M, N)$.

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.dot`` semantics for the 1-D and 2-D cases.

**See Also**

matmul_op : N-D matmul with NumPy broadcasting on batch dims.
inner_op : Last-axis contraction (NumPy ``inner``).
outer_op : 1-D × 1-D outer product.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Second operand.  Must have the same rank as ``a``.

returns.description: - 1-D inputs → scalar tensor with shape ``{}``. - 2-D inputs of shape $(M, K)$ and $(K, N)$ → matrix of shape $(M, N)$.

raises.description: If the contracted dimensions disagree (1-D length mismatch, or 2-D ``a.shape[1] != b.shape[0]``).
If either input has rank other than 1 or 2.

notes:
Unlike :func:`matmul_op` this entry point uses ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``
— integer dot products are valid and must not be promoted silently.

members.name: drop_block_op

members.name: dtype_name
members.signature: int dtype_name(Dtype dt)
members.summary: Returns the canonical string name of a dtype.
members.extended:
This is the form used in error messages, ``repr(tensor)`` rendering, and
the Python :class:`lucid.dtype` wrapper's ``__repr__``.

returns.description: One of ``"bool"``, ``"int8"``, ``"int16"``, ``"int32"``, ``"int64"``, ``"float16"``, ``"float32"``, ``"float64"``, ``"complex64"``.  The view points into a static literal — safe to store across calls.

members.name: dtype_size
members.signature: int dtype_size(Dtype dt)
members.summary: Returns the size in bytes of a single element of the given dtype.

returns.description: Element size in bytes.  ``1`` for :attr:`Bool` / :attr:`I8`, ``2`` for :attr:`I16` / :attr:`F16`, ``4`` for :attr:`I32` / :attr:`F32`, ``8`` for :attr:`I64` / :attr:`F64` / :attr:`C64`.

notes:
``constexpr`` so that ``nbytes = numel * dtype_size(dt)`` folds away at

members.name: eig_op
members.signature: int eig_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute eigenvalues and right eigenvectors of a square float matrix.
members.extended:
**Math**

Returns $(w, V)$ such that $A V = V \mathrm{diag}(w)$, equivalently
$A v_i = w_i v_i$ for each column $v_i$ of $V$.

**Shape**

- Input ``a``         : ``(..., n, n)``
- Output ``w``        : ``(..., n)``
- Output ``V``        : ``(..., n, n)``

**See Also**

[[eigh_op]] : Symmetric / Hermitian specialisation (preferred when
              applicable — faster, real outputs, autograd-friendly).

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Square float tensor of shape ``(..., n, n)``.  Leading dimensions are treated as independent batch axes — one decomposition per batch element.  Must be at least 2-D and floating-point.

returns.description: Two-element vector ``{w, V}`` where: - ``w`` has shape ``(..., n)`` — one eigenvalue per column of $A$ (real part only; see Notes on complex spectra). - ``V`` has shape ``(..., n, n)`` — columns are the right eigenvectors, normalised to unit Euclidean length.

raises.description: When ``a`` is not at least 2-D, not square, or not float-typed. Also if LAPACK fails to converge (rare; reported via ``info > 0``).

examples:
In Python (via [[lucid.linalg.eig]]):
>>> A = lucid.tensor([[2.0, 0.0], [0.0, 3.0]])
>>> w, V = lucid.linalg.eig(A)
>>> w
Tensor([2., 3.])
notes:
Outputs are leaf nodes — autograd is **not** wired.  See header-level
Notes for the deferred backward formula and its caveats.

members.name: eigh_op
members.signature: int eigh_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a real symmetric square matrix.
members.extended:
**Math**

$A = V \mathrm{diag}(w) V^\top$ with $V^\top V = I$ and
$w_1 \le \cdots \le w_n$.

**Shape**

- Input ``a``  : ``(..., n, n)``, symmetric
- Output ``w`` : ``(..., n)``, real, ascending
- Output ``V`` : ``(..., n, n)``, orthonormal columns

**See Also**

[[eig_op]] : General non-symmetric eigendecomposition (use only
             when the symmetric structure cannot be exploited).

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Square float tensor of shape ``(..., n, n)``, interpreted as symmetric.  Only the lower triangle is read; the strict upper triangle is ignored.  Leading dimensions are independent batch axes.  Must be at least 2-D and floating-point.

returns.description: Two-element vector ``{w, V}`` where: - ``w`` has shape ``(..., n)`` — real eigenvalues in **ascending** order. - ``V`` has shape ``(..., n, n)`` — columns are the orthonormal eigenvectors corresponding to ``w``.

raises.description: When ``a`` is not at least 2-D, not square, or not float-typed. Also when LAPACK ``*syevd`` / ``*heevd`` fails to converge (reported via ``info > 0``).

examples:
In Python (via [[lucid.linalg.eigh]]):
>>> A = lucid.tensor([[2.0, 1.0], [1.0, 2.0]])
>>> w, V = lucid.linalg.eigh(A)
>>> w
Tensor([1., 3.])
notes:
Outputs are leaf nodes — autograd is **not** wired.  See header-level
Notes for the closed-form backward and its caveats around sign
ambiguity / spectral degeneracy.

members.name: einops_rearrange_op
members.signature: int einops_rearrange_op(const int & a, const int & pattern, const int & axes_lengths)
members.summary: Rearrange (permute and/or reshape) a tensor according to an einops pattern.
members.extended:
The pattern must have the form ``"lhs -> rhs"``.  Both sides must contain
exactly the same set of named axes; ``rhs`` may group or split them with
parentheses.  ``axes_lengths`` provides sizes for any axis that cannot be
inferred from the input shape (typically the components of a decomposed
group).  No reduction is performed — element count is preserved.

**Math**

Pure index permutation and reshape:
$$
  y_{\sigma(i_1, \dots, i_n)} = x_{i_1, \dots, i_n}
$$
where $\sigma$ is determined by aligning ``lhs`` and ``rhs`` flat axis lists.

**See Also**

einops_reduce_op, einops_repeat_op, einsum_op

parameters.name: axes_lengths
parameters.description: Named sizes for axes whose size cannot be inferred from ``a.shape``.

returns.description: Rearranged tensor.  Same element count and dtype as ``a``; shape is determined by ``rhs`` and ``axes_lengths``.

raises.description: If the pattern is malformed, axes are inconsistent across sides, or a required size is missing from ``axes_lengths``.

examples:
Pattern ``"b c h w -> b (h w) c"`` flattens spatial dims into a token
sequence (NCHW → NLC) suitable for transformer-style backbones.

members.name: einops_reduce_op
members.signature: int einops_reduce_op(const int & a, const int & pattern, int reduction, const int & axes_lengths)
members.summary: Reduce a tensor over one or more axes specified by an einops pattern.
members.extended:
Axes present on the ``lhs`` but absent from the ``rhs`` are collapsed using
the given reduction code.  ``axes_lengths`` is forwarded to the internal
rearrange step that aligns axes before the reduction.

**Math**

For ``reduction = sum``:
$$
  y_{j_1, \dots, j_m} = \sum_{k_1, \dots, k_r} x_{i_1, \dots, i_n}
$$
where $\{k_\bullet\}$ are the axes dropped between ``lhs`` and ``rhs``.
The mean / max / min / prod variants substitute the corresponding monoid.

**See Also**

einops_rearrange_op, einops_repeat_op, einsum_op

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: Einops pattern ``"lhs -> rhs"`` with at least one axis dropped on the ``rhs``.

parameters.name: reduction
parameters.description: Reduction code: ``1=mean``, ``2=sum``, ``3=max``, ``4=min``, ``5=prod``. Must match the Python-side enum.

parameters.name: axes_lengths
parameters.description: Named sizes for axes whose size cannot be inferred from ``a.shape``.

returns.description: Reduced tensor of the shape implied by ``rhs``.

raises.description: If the pattern is malformed or no axis is dropped (use ``rearrange`` instead).
If ``reduction`` is outside the accepted code range.

members.name: einops_repeat_op
members.signature: int einops_repeat_op(const int & a, const int & pattern, const int & axes_lengths)
members.summary: Tile / broadcast a tensor to introduce new axes specified by an einops pattern.
members.extended:
Axes present in ``rhs`` but absent from ``lhs`` are new and their sizes
must be provided in ``axes_lengths``.  Existing axes may additionally be
reordered and merged exactly as in ``einops_rearrange_op``.  Internally
the op uses ``unsqueeze`` + ``broadcast_to`` so it avoids data copies
whenever possible — repeat is materialised only when subsequent ops force
a contiguous layout.

**Math**

For each new axis $k$ of size $n_k$:
$$
  y_{\dots, k, \dots} = x_{\dots},
  \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, n_k - 1
$$

**See Also**

einops_rearrange_op, einops_reduce_op, einsum_op

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: Einops pattern ``"lhs -> rhs"`` with at least one axis introduced on the ``rhs``.

parameters.name: axes_lengths
parameters.description: Sizes for the new axes introduced on the ``rhs``.  Must contain every axis name that does not appear on the ``lhs``.

returns.description: Tensor with new axes broadcast/repeated to the requested sizes.

raises.description: If a new axis on the ``rhs`` is missing from ``axes_lengths``.

members.name: einsum_op
members.signature: int einsum_op(const int & pattern, const int & operands)
members.summary: General tensor contraction via an Einstein summation equation.
members.extended:
The equation uses the same syntax as NumPy's ``einsum``: single-character
labels separated by commas for each input, optionally followed by ``->``
and the output labels.  If ``->`` is omitted, the output labels are the
sorted set of labels that appear exactly once across all inputs (implicit
form).  Repeated labels in a single operand take the diagonal; labels
shared across operands trigger contraction; labels dropped from the output
are summed out.

**Math**

$$
  y_{\ell_\text{out}} = \sum_{\ell_\text{contract}}
      \prod_{k} x^{(k)}_{\ell^{(k)}}
$$

**See Also**

einops_rearrange_op, einops_reduce_op, einops_repeat_op

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: Einsum equation, e.g. ``"ij,jk->ik"`` for matrix multiplication or ``"bij,bjk->bik"`` for batched matmul.

parameters.name: operands
parameters.description: The input tensors, one per comma-separated label group.  All operands must share the same device and dtype.

returns.description: Contracted tensor of the shape implied by the output labels.

raises.description: If the equation is malformed, label counts do not match operand dimensions, or contracted dimensions disagree in size.
If operands live on different devices.
If operands have different dtypes.

examples:
``"ij,jk->ik"``    — matrix multiplication.
``"bhij,bhjk->bhik"`` — multi-head batched attention contraction.
notes:
Ellipsis (``...``) is not yet supported.  Use explicit labels for batch
dimensions in the meantime.

members.name: elu_op
members.signature: int elu_op(const int & a, double alpha)
members.summary: Exponential Linear Unit $y = x$ if $x \ge 0$ else $\alpha\,(e^x - 1)$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

EluBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

members.name: embedding_bag_op
members.signature: int embedding_bag_op(const int & weight, const int & indices, const int & offsets, int mode, int padding_idx, bool include_last_offset)
members.summary: Pooled embedding lookup with per-bag reduction.
members.extended:
Fuses a row gather and a reduction over bags into a single backend
call, avoiding the intermediate $(B, L, D)$ tensor that
``embedding`` followed by ``sum``/``mean``/``max`` would materialize.
Variable-length bags are encoded as a flat ``indices`` tensor plus
an ``offsets`` tensor that marks the start of each bag — bag $b$
consists of ``indices[offsets[b] : offsets[b+1]]`` (the last bag
runs to the end of ``indices`` when ``include_last_offset=false``).

No autograd node is currently wired for this op (sparse gradients
are not yet supported on the Lucid backends), so the returned
tensor is not differentiable with respect to ``weight``.

**Math**

$$y_b =
  \begin{cases}
    \displaystyle\sum_{j\in\mathcal{B}_b} W[j] & \text{mode}=0\\[6pt]
    \displaystyle\frac{1}{|\mathcal{B}_b|}\sum_{j\in\mathcal{B}_b} W[j]
      & \text{mode}=1\\[6pt]
    \displaystyle\max_{j\in\mathcal{B}_b} W[j] & \text{mode}=2
  \end{cases}$$

**See Also**

embedding_op : Per-token lookup without reduction.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description: Flat 1-D integer tensor of all bag contents concatenated.

parameters.name: offsets
parameters.description: 1-D integer tensor of shape ``(B,)`` marking the start of each bag.

parameters.name: padding_idx
parameters.description: Index that contributes zero to the reduction; negative disables.

parameters.name: include_last_offset
parameters.description: If ``true``, ``offsets`` is treated as ``(B+1,)`` so the last bag also has an explicit upper bound.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, D)`` — one pooled embedding per bag.

members.name: embedding_op
members.signature: int embedding_op(const int & weight, const int & indices, int padding_idx)
members.summary: Integer-index embedding lookup — free-function entry point.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that delegates to :func:`EmbeddingBackward::forward`.
Used by the pybind11 binding layer and by other C++ call sites.

parameters.name: padding_idx
parameters.description: Index to mask; negative disables masking.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(*indices.shape, embed_dim)``.

members.name: empty_like_op
members.signature: int empty_like_op(const int & a, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create an uninitialised tensor matching ``a`` in shape, dtype, and device.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: New leaf tensor; content must not be relied upon (see ``empty_op``).

members.name: empty_op
members.signature: int empty_op(const int & shape, Dtype dt, Device device, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create an uninitialised tensor of the given shape.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: Leaf tensor with unspecified content.

notes:
The current implementation zero-fills (via ``make_zero_storage``) to
avoid UB on uninitialised reads.  The ``empty`` name signals that
callers must not rely on the values — future implementations may
switch to a true uninitialised allocator.

members.name: equal_op
members.signature: int equal_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise equality comparison.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = (a[i] == b[i])`` for every element and returns the
result as a Bool tensor.  The comparison is exact, so for floating-point
inputs it follows IEEE 754 equality (``NaN == NaN`` is false).

Non-differentiable: the comparison function has zero gradient almost
everywhere and undefined gradient on the decision boundary, so no
autograd node is registered and the output never carries grad.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = \mathbf{1}\!\left[a[i] = b[i]\right].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.equal``.

**See Also**

not_equal_op, less_op, greater_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands.  Must have identical shape, dtype, and device — this op does not broadcast.

returns.description: Bool tensor with the same shape as ``a`` and ``b``.

raises.description: If the operand shapes, dtypes, or devices differ.

notes:
* Output dtype is always ``Bool`` regardless of input dtype.
* No broadcasting — callers must align shapes upstream.

members.name: erf_op
members.signature: int erf_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = \mathrm{erf}(x)$ element-wise.  Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and delegates to :class:`ErfBackward::forward`.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`ErfBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.  Domain is all of $\mathbb{R}$.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype, values in $(-1, 1)$.

members.name: erfinv_op
members.signature: int erfinv_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = \mathrm{erfinv}(x)$ element-wise.  Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and delegates to :class:`ErfinvBackward::forward`.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`ErfinvBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape with values in $(-1, 1)$.  Values at $\pm 1$ map to $\pm\infty$; out-of-domain values produce NaN.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype.

members.name: exp2_op
members.signature: int exp2_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Base-2 exponential: $y = 2^{x} = \exp(x \ln 2)$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`mul_op` + :func:`exp_op`.  Multiplying against a
constant tensor keeps the gradient chain visible to ``MulBackward`` and
``ExpBackward``.

**Math**

$$
  y = \exp(x \cdot \ln 2),
  \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = (\ln 2) \cdot 2^{x}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`exp_op`.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

members.name: exp_inplace_op
members.signature: int exp_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place natural exponential: writes $e^x$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \exp(a_i)$$

**See Also**

:func:`exp_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to exponentiate in place.  Must be non-null.  AMP may promote integer inputs to float before dispatch; the promoted dtype is written back to ``a``.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: exp_op
members.signature: int exp_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = e^x$ element-wise.  Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and delegates to :class:`ExpBackward::forward`.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`ExpBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.  Half-precision inputs are upcast to F32 before computation, per ``AmpPolicy::ForceFP32``.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype.  Large positive values overflow to ``+inf``; large negative values flush to ``0``.

members.name: exp_storage
members.signature: int exp_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise exponential: returns :math:`e^{s}`.

returns.description: New storage holding ``exp(s)``.

members.name: expand_op
members.signature: int expand_op(const int & a, const int & shape)
members.summary: Alias for :func:`broadcast_to_op` with reference framework's expand semantics.
members.extended:
Provided so callers porting from reference framework can keep the call site
unchanged.  Behaviour is identical to :func:`broadcast_to_op`.

**See Also**

:func:`broadcast_to_op`.

returns.description: Broadcast tensor.

members.name: eye_op
members.signature: int eye_op(int N, int M, int k, Dtype dt, Device device, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create an $N \times M$ identity-like matrix with ones on diagonal $k$.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$
  \text{out}[i, j] = \begin{cases} 1 & j - i = k \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

parameters.name: M
parameters.description: Number of columns.  If ``M <= 0``, ``M`` is set to ``N``.

parameters.name: k
parameters.description: Diagonal index.  ``k = 0`` is the main diagonal; positive ``k`` is above, negative ``k`` is below.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: 2-D tensor of shape $(N, M)$.

members.name: fftn_op
members.signature: int fftn_op(const int & a, const int & n, const int & axes)
members.summary: N-dimensional complex-to-complex DFT (forward).
members.extended:
Computes the discrete Fourier transform of $x$ along the requested
``axes``.  This is the entry point invoked by ``lucid.fft.fftn`` and
is consumed by Python-side autograd machinery; this function itself
performs no gradient wiring.

**Math**

For a $d$-dimensional transform over axes $a_1, \dots, a_d$ of sizes
$N_1, \dots, N_d$,

$$
  X[k_1, \dots, k_d] = \sum_{n_1=0}^{N_1-1} \cdots \sum_{n_d=0}^{N_d-1}
    x[n_1, \dots, n_d] \,
    \exp\!\left( -2\pi i \sum_{j=1}^{d} \frac{k_j n_j}{N_j} \right) .
$$

The ``"backward"`` normalisation convention is used (no scaling on
forward; division by $\prod_j N_j$ on inverse).

**Shape**

``out.shape[axes[i]] == n[i]`` when ``n`` is supplied; otherwise
``out.shape == a.shape``.  All other axes retain their input size.

**References**

Cooley & Tukey, "An Algorithm for the Machine Calculation of Complex
Fourier Series" (Math. Comp., 1965).

**See Also**

ifftn_op : Inverse N-D DFT (backward of ``fftn``).
rfftn_op : Real-input N-D DFT.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor.  Real (F16/F32) inputs are accepted and promoted to C64; complex (C64) inputs are passed through.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: Per-axis transform length.  When empty, the corresponding input dimensions are used.  When non-empty, ``len(n) == len(axes)`` must hold; each input axis is cropped or zero-padded to ``n[i]``.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to transform.  Negative axes are normalised against the input rank.  Empty means "transform all axes".

returns.description: C64 tensor with shape equal to ``a.shape()`` except that each ``axes[i]`` is replaced by ``n[i]`` (or unchanged when ``n`` is empty).

raises.description: If ``a`` is null, rank-0, has an unsupported dtype, or if ``len(n) != len(axes)`` when ``n`` is non-empty.

notes:
FFT bypasses the IBackend dispatcher and calls MLX directly from
both CPU and GPU device paths (see ``_Detail.h``).  On Device::CPU
the result is downloaded to host memory after the transform.

members.name: flatten_op
members.signature: int flatten_op(const int & a, int start_axis, int end_axis)
members.summary: Collapse a contiguous range of axes into a single dimension.
members.extended:
Folds dimensions ``[start_axis, end_axis]`` (inclusive on both ends) into
one axis whose size is the product of the collapsed dimensions.  Delegates
to :func:`reshape_op` after computing the flattened size, so the input
must be contiguous along the affected range.

**Shape**

``(D_0, ..., D_{s-1}, D_s, ..., D_e, D_{e+1}, ..., D_{n-1})`` becomes
``(D_0, ..., D_{s-1}, \prod_{d=s}^{e} D_d, D_{e+1}, ..., D_{n-1})``
with ``s = start_axis`` and ``e = end_axis``.

**See Also**

:func:`reshape_op`, :func:`squeeze_all_op`.

parameters.name: start_axis
parameters.description: First axis in the range to collapse.  Supports negative indexing.

parameters.name: end_axis
parameters.description: Last axis in the range (inclusive).  Supports negative indexing. Must satisfy ``start_axis <= end_axis`` after normalisation.

returns.description: Tensor with rank ``ndim - (end_axis - start_axis)`` and a flattened dimension of size ``\prod_{d=start_axis}^{end_axis} a.shape[d]`` at position ``start_axis``.

raises.description: If the collapsed range cannot be reshape-merged because of non-contiguous strides.

members.name: flip_op
members.signature: int flip_op(const int & a, int dims)
members.summary: Reverse a tensor along one or more axes.
members.extended:
Equivalent to NumPy's ``np.flip``: along each listed axis the index
ordering is inverted.  Unlike :func:`roll_op` there is no wrap-around.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` with the requested axes reversed.

notes:
Backward applies ``flip`` with the same ``dims`` to the incoming
gradient — flipping is self-inverse.

members.name: floor_inplace_op
members.signature: int floor_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place floor: writes $\lfloor x \rfloor$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \lfloor a_i \rfloor$$

**See Also**

:func:`floor_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to floor in place.  Must be non-null.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: floor_op
members.signature: int floor_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise floor — returns the largest integer-valued tensor not greater than ``a``, with the same dtype as ``a``.
members.extended:
Not differentiable: output is detached from the autograd graph.

**Math**

$$y_i = \lfloor x_i \rfloor$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`ceil_op`, :func:`round_op`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor.  Integer dtypes are returned unchanged.

returns.description: New tensor with floored values; same shape, dtype, and device as ``a``.

examples: ``floor_op(-1.2) == -2.0`` (not ``-1.0`` — that would be ``trunc``).

members.name: floordiv_op
members.signature: int floordiv_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Compute the elementwise floor division $y = \lfloor a / b \rfloor$.
members.extended:
Floor division is **non-differentiable** (piecewise constant with jumps at
every integer crossing), so no autograd node is registered: the returned
tensor never carries a ``grad_fn`` and the result effectively detaches its
inputs from the graph.

**Math**

$$
  y_i = \left\lfloor \frac{a_i}{b_i} \right\rfloor
$$

**Shape**

``a.shape == b.shape``.  The result is the same shape; the dtype is always
I64.

**See Also**

div_op : True (real-valued) elementwise division, fully differentiable.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Denominator tensor.  Must have the **same shape and dtype** as ``a`` — no broadcasting and no implicit dtype promotion is performed.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``a.shape`` and dtype :enum:`Dtype::I64` holding $\lfloor a / b \rfloor$.  The cast to I64 is unconditional regardless of the input dtype, matching Python's ``//`` operator semantics.

raises.description: If ``a.shape != b.shape`` or ``a.dtype != b.dtype``.

notes:
Behaviour on division by zero is delegated to the backend primitive
(``backend.floordiv``).  Because the output is forced to I64, any
floating-point ``NaN`` / ``Inf`` produced by the backend is reinterpreted
to a platform-defined integer value.

members.name: fmod_op
members.signature: int fmod_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: C-style floating modulo: $y = a - \mathrm{trunc}(a/b) \cdot b$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`div_op` + :func:`trunc_op` + :func:`mul_op` +
:func:`sub_op`.  Result has the sign of ``a`` because ``trunc`` rounds
toward zero.  Gradient flows through the primitives.

**Math**

$$
  y = a - \mathrm{trunc}(a/b) \cdot b
$$

**See Also**

:func:`remainder_op` — Python-style sign convention.

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Broadcastable operands.  ``b`` must be non-zero where evaluated.

returns.description: Tensor on the broadcast shape with the sign of ``a``.

members.name: fold_op
members.signature: int fold_op(const int & x, const int & output_size, const int & kernel_size, const int & stride, const int & padding, const int & dilation)
members.summary: Fold (col2im) — the inverse of Unfold for 2-D inputs.
members.extended:
Scatters each column back into its source location in the spatial
grid; values at overlapping positions are summed (this is the formal
adjoint of Unfold, not a true inverse when stride permits overlap).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Column matrix of shape ``(N, C * kH * kW, L)`` produced by ``unfold_op`` or any equivalent column layout.

parameters.name: kernel_size
parameters.description: Kernel size per spatial axis used during the matching unfold.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(N, C, outH, outW)`` with overlapping contributions summed.

members.name: frac_op
members.signature: int frac_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Fractional part: $y = x - \mathrm{trunc}(x)$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`trunc_op` + :func:`sub_op`.  Because the ``trunc``
branch contributes zero gradient, the gradient w.r.t. ``a`` is exactly
the upstream gradient (identity).

**Math**

$$
  y = x - \mathrm{trunc}(x),
  \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = 1
$$

**See Also**

:func:`trunc_op`.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``, with $y \in (-1, 1)$ and sign matching $x$.

members.name: full_like_op
members.signature: int full_like_op(const int & a, double fill_value, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create a constant-filled tensor matching ``a`` in shape, dtype, and device.

parameters.name: fill_value
parameters.description: Scalar value cast to ``a.dtype()`` before filling.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: New leaf tensor with constant content.

members.name: full_op
members.signature: int full_op(const int & shape, double fill_value, Dtype dt, Device device, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create a tensor of the given shape filled with a constant scalar.

parameters.name: fill_value
parameters.description: Scalar value cast to ``dt`` before filling.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: Leaf tensor whose every element equals ``fill_value`` (cast).

members.name: gather_op
members.signature: int gather_op(const int & a, const int & indices, int axis)
members.summary: Gather elements from ``a`` at index positions along a single axis.
members.extended:
For each location in the output, looks up the value of ``a`` at the
index given by ``indices`` along ``axis``, holding all other axes fixed.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description: Integer index tensor of the same rank as ``a``.  Along every axis other than ``axis``, the shape must match ``a``.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``indices.shape`` and dtype of ``a``.

notes:
Backward is a scatter-add: the incoming gradient is added back into a
zero tensor of the input shape at the positions specified by
``indices``.  Duplicate indices accumulate, matching NumPy / reference
framework semantics.

members.name: ge_mask_storage
members.signature: int ge_mask_storage(const int & a, const int & b, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise greater-or-equal mask: ``(a >= b) ? 1 : 0``.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Output dtype the mask is cast to.

returns.description: New storage holding the gated mask.

notes:
Used by max / clamp-style backward passes to route the gradient only
through the "winning" element of each comparison.

members.name: gelu_exact_op
members.signature: int gelu_exact_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Exact GeLU $y = 0.5\,x\,(1 + \mathrm{erf}(x/\sqrt{2}))$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

gelu_op : Tanh-approximation variant (default).
GeluExactBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

members.name: gelu_op
members.signature: int gelu_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Tanh-approximation GeLU $y = 0.5\,x\,(1 + \tanh(\sqrt{2/\pi}\,(x + 0.044715\,x^3)))$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

gelu_exact_op : Exact erf-based variant.
GeluBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

members.name: global_response_norm_op
members.signature: int global_response_norm_op(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, double eps)
members.summary: Public free-function entry point for Global Response Normalization.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that delegates to
:func:`GlobalResponseNormBackward::forward`.  This is the symbol the
pybind11 binding layer forwards to from the ConvNeXt-V2 building
block in :mod:`lucid.nn.functional`.

**See Also**

GlobalResponseNormBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: 4-D input tensor of shape ``(N, C, H, W)``.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: allocate_shared
members.signature: MetalBuffer allocate_shared(int nbytes)
members.summary: Allocate a new ``MTLResourceStorageModeShared`` buffer of size ``nbytes``.
members.extended:
The returned buffer is backed by unified-memory pages: the CPU pointer
and the Metal buffer handle reference the same physical bytes, with no
transfer required between domains.

**See Also**

:func:`make_metal_shared` — ref-counted variant.

parameters.name: nbytes
parameters.description: Allocation size in bytes.  Zero-sized allocations are legal and return a buffer with ``cpu_ptr == nullptr``.

returns.description: A populated descriptor on success; a zero-initialised value on allocation failure (e.g. out-of-memory).  Callers should check ``mtl_handle != nullptr`` before use.

notes:
The returned buffer must be released with :func:`deallocate_shared`
to avoid leaking the underlying Metal allocation.

members.name: compile_metal_kernel
members.signature: MetalKernel compile_metal_kernel(const int & source, const int & function_name)
members.summary: Compile (or fetch from the global cache) a Metal compute pipeline.
members.extended:
Hashes ``(source, function_name)`` to form a cache key, then on
miss compiles ``source`` via ``newLibraryWithSource:``, looks up
``function_name`` inside the resulting library, and creates a
``MTLComputePipelineState`` + dedicated ``MTLCommandQueue``.

**See Also**

:func:`run_metal_kernel` — invokes the compiled pipeline.

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: Complete MSL source text containing at least one ``kernel`` function named ``function_name``.

parameters.name: function_name
parameters.description: The MSL kernel function symbol to bind.

returns.description: A valid kernel handle on success.  An invalid handle (``is_valid() == false``) is returned when Metal is unavailable (no GPU device on the host).

raises.description: On MSL compilation failure or pipeline-state creation error.

notes:
The pipeline cache is protected by an internal mutex, so concurrent
callers safely share compiled kernels.  Cached entries live for
the lifetime of the process.

members.name: deallocate_shared
members.signature: void deallocate_shared(MetalBuffer & buf)
members.summary: Release the Metal buffer held in ``buf`` and zero all fields.
members.extended:
Drops the ``CFRetain`` on ``buf.mtl_handle`` and clears the
descriptor in place so callers cannot reuse the dangling pointer
by accident.  Calling this on a zero-initialised ``MetalBuffer``
is a no-op (safe).

parameters.name: buf
parameters.description: The descriptor to release.  Mutated to the zero state.

notes:
Marked ``noexcept`` — failures from the underlying Obj-C release
are silently swallowed because there is no recoverable action.

members.name: download_gpu_to_cpu
members.signature: CpuStorage download_gpu_to_cpu(const GpuStorage & gpu, const int & shape)
members.summary: Download a GPU array to a freshly allocated CPU buffer.
members.extended:
Calls ``arr.eval()`` to force materialisation of any pending lazy
MLX kernels, then ``memcpy``s the contiguous data into a new
:class:`CpuStorage` of the requested shape.

parameters.name: gpu
parameters.description: Source MLX-backed storage on the GPU stream.

returns.description: A fresh CPU buffer.  Disjoint from the GPU source — mutations do not propagate either way.

notes:
Always synchronous from the caller's perspective.  The cost is
dominated by the GPU graph completion (the actual ``memcpy`` is
at ~100 GB/s).

members.name: from_mlx_dtype
members.signature: Dtype from_mlx_dtype(int dt)
members.summary: Map an ``mlx::core::Dtype`` back to a Lucid :enum:`Dtype`.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`to_mlx_dtype` — inverse direction.

returns.description: The corresponding Lucid dtype.

raises.description: For unsigned integer types (``uint8/16/32/64``) and ``bfloat16`` — Lucid does not currently expose Python-visible equivalents.

members.name: make_metal_shared
members.signature: OwnedMetalBuffer make_metal_shared(int nbytes)
members.summary: Allocate a shared Metal buffer wrapped in an RAII owner.
members.extended:
Combines :func:`allocate_shared` with a ``shared_ptr`` whose
destructor releases the allocation.  Preferred entry point for
``Storage`` construction code paths that need automatic cleanup.

**See Also**

:func:`allocate_shared` — non-owning variant.

returns.description: Bundle whose ``owner`` keeps ``buf`` alive for as long as any copy of the ``shared_ptr`` survives.

examples: Construct a 1 KiB shared buffer::

auto owned = lucid::gpu::make_metal_shared(1024);
    // owned.buf.cpu_ptr ↔ owned.buf.mtl_handle alias same pages.

members.name: mlx_scalar
members.signature: ::mlx::core::array mlx_scalar(double v, int dt)
members.summary: Build a 0-D MLX array holding ``v`` cast to ``dt``.
members.extended:
Used as a broadcastable RHS in arithmetic helpers (e.g. ``x + 1``)
to avoid materialising a same-shape constant tensor.  The literal
flows into the kernel inline, paying zero allocation cost.

parameters.name: v
parameters.description: Scalar value to embed.  Cast to ``float`` before MLX construction.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Final MLX dtype the scalar should be cast to.

returns.description: A rank-0 MLX array of dtype ``dt`` holding ``v``.

notes:
The intermediate float cast loses precision for very large
double-precision values; callers needing exact int64 broadcasts
should construct the array directly.

members.name: mlx_shape_to_lucid
members.signature: int mlx_shape_to_lucid(const int & shape)
members.summary: Convert an ``mlx::core::Shape`` to a Lucid :class:`Shape`.
members.extended:
Widens the int32 dimensions to int64 for Lucid's canonical
representation.

returns.description: Lucid ``std::vector<int64_t>`` shape.

members.name: array_to_buffer
members.signature: BufferView array_to_buffer(const ::mlx::core::array & arr)
members.summary: Materialise an MLX array and view its backing ``MTLBuffer``.
members.extended:
Forces ``arr`` to evaluate (running any pending MLX graph nodes
that produced it) and blocks until the producing command buffer
reaches the ``available`` status, then returns a non-owning
view of the buffer + offset + size.

parameters.name: arr
parameters.description: The MLX array whose buffer the caller wishes to bind into an MPSGraph dispatch.

returns.description: Triple of (buffer handle, byte offset, byte length).  All fields are valid for the lifetime of ``arr``.

notes:
Used by every MPS kernel in :doc:`MpsKernels` to obtain Obj-C
``MTLBuffer`` handles for ``MPSGraphTensorData`` construction
without copying.

members.name: batch_norm_train_backward
members.signature: BatchNormBackwardOut batch_norm_train_backward(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & saved_mean, const int & saved_rstd, const int & grad, int channels, int ndim, const int & x_shape, Dtype dt, double eps)
members.summary: BatchNorm training-mode backward as one fused MPSGraph executable.
members.extended:
Computes ``(dx, dgamma, dbeta)`` in a single dispatch using the
saved mean/rstd from the forward.

**See Also**

:func:`batch_norm_train_forward` — corresponding forward.

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: Upstream gradient w.r.t. the BN output.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical-stability epsilon used in the forward.  Needed here for symbolic consistency; the gradient itself uses ``saved_rstd``.

returns.description: The three gradients.

members.name: batch_norm_train_forward
members.signature: BatchNormForwardOut batch_norm_train_forward(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, int channels, int ndim, double eps, const int & x_shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: BatchNorm training-mode forward as one fused MPSGraph executable.
members.extended:
Produces ``(y, saved_mean, saved_rstd)`` in a single dispatch.
MLX has no fused BN primitive, so this kernel exists primarily to
close the gap on large activations (see
:func:`should_dispatch_batch_norm_train`).

**Math**

$$\mu_c = \mathbb{E}[x_{n,c,\ldots}], \quad
  \mathrm{rstd}_c = (\operatorname{Var}[x_{n,c,\ldots}] + \varepsilon)^{-1/2}$$
$$y_{n,c,\ldots} = \gamma_c \cdot (x_{n,c,\ldots} - \mu_c) \cdot \mathrm{rstd}_c + \beta_c$$
Reduction axes: $0, 2, 3, \ldots, 2 + \mathrm{ndim} - 1$.

**See Also**

:func:`batch_norm_train_backward` — corresponding backward.
:func:`should_dispatch_batch_norm_train` — dispatch gate.

parameters.name: eps
parameters.description: Numerical stability epsilon added inside the rsqrt.

returns.description: Output activation + saved mean + saved rstd.

members.name: bridge_available
members.signature: bool bridge_available()
members.summary: Report whether the MPSGraph bridge is usable on this host.
members.extended:
Returns ``true`` iff Metal Performance Shaders Graph initialised
successfully (i.e. the device + queue are valid and the framework
loaded).  When ``false``, every :func:`should_dispatch_*` heuristic
in :doc:`MpsDispatch` must short-circuit and the MLX path runs.

returns.description: ``true`` iff MPSGraph is available.

members.name: buffer_to_array
members.signature: ::mlx::core::array buffer_to_array(void * mtl_buffer, int shape, Dtype dt, int offset_bytes)
members.summary: Wrap a caller-allocated ``MTLBuffer`` as a fresh leaf MLX array.
members.extended:
The caller must hold exactly one strong reference to
``mtl_buffer`` going in; this function **transfers** that
reference into the returned ``mlx::core::array`` (and any copies
of it).  When the final copy dies, the buffer is released exactly
once via the array's custom deleter.

parameters.name: mtl_buffer
parameters.description: Opaque ``id<MTLBuffer>`` allocated by the caller, typically via MPSGraph's executable returning a fresh output buffer.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Logical MLX shape (int32 dimensions).  The buffer must hold at least ``prod(shape) * sizeof(dt)`` bytes starting at ``offset_bytes``.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Lucid dtype of the elements.  Translated to MLX's dtype enum internally.

parameters.name: offset_bytes
parameters.description: Byte offset into ``mtl_buffer`` where the array's data begins. Defaults to 0.

returns.description: A leaf array (no upstream graph dependency) backed by ``mtl_buffer``.

warns.annotation: Calling this with a buffer that the caller still intends to
release elsewhere causes a double-free.  Hand off ownership

members.name: debug_enabled
members.signature: bool debug_enabled()
members.summary: Return whether MPS dispatch decisions should be logged to ``stderr``.
members.extended:
Reads ``LUCID_MPS_DEBUG`` from the environment once.  When ``true``,
each ``should_dispatch_*`` call emits a line indicating whether the
MPS or MLX path was selected (and why, for shape-gated ops).

returns.description: ``true`` iff MPS debug logging is enabled.

members.name: enabled
members.signature: bool enabled()
members.summary: Return whether MPS dispatch is globally enabled this process.
members.extended:
Reads ``LUCID_MPS_DISABLE`` from the environment exactly once on
first call.  When set (to any non-empty value), every
:func:`should_dispatch_*` heuristic short-circuits to ``false`` and
all ops fall back to MLX.  Defaults to enabled.

returns.description: ``true`` iff MPS dispatch is enabled.

notes: Thread-safe; the env-read is guarded by a one-shot flag.

members.name: gelu_backward
members.signature: int gelu_backward(const int & x, const int & grad, const int & shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: GELU backward (tanh approximation).
members.extended:
Fused MPSGraph evaluation of the derivative of the tanh
formulation, given the input ``x`` and upstream gradient ``grad``.

**Math**

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = 0.5(1 + t) + 0.5 \, x \, (1 - t^2) \, d_{\text{inner}}$$
where $t = \tanh\!\bigl[c_1(x + c_2 x^3)\bigr]$ and
$d_{\text{inner}} = c_1 (1 + 3 c_2 x^2)$.

**See Also**

:func:`gelu_forward` — corresponding forward.

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: Upstream gradient w.r.t. the GELU output.

returns.description: Gradient w.r.t. ``x``.

members.name: gelu_exact_backward
members.signature: int gelu_exact_backward(const int & x, const int & grad, const int & shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: GELU exact (Gaussian-CDF) backward via fused MPSGraph kernel.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \Phi(x) + x \, \phi(x)$$
where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF and $\phi$ its PDF.

returns.description: Gradient w.r.t. ``x``.

members.name: gelu_exact_forward
members.signature: int gelu_exact_forward(const int & x, const int & shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: GELU exact (Gaussian-CDF) forward via fused MPSGraph kernel.
members.extended:
Replaces the 10-op MLX composition of the erf-approximation
(``0.5 * x * (1 + erf(x/√2))``) with a single fused MPSGraph node.

**Math**

$$y = 0.5 \, x \, \left(1 + \operatorname{erf}\!\Bigl(\tfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\Bigr)\right)$$

**See Also**

:func:`gelu_exact_backward` — corresponding backward.
:func:`gelu_forward` — tanh-approx variant.

returns.description: Fresh GPU result of identical shape + dtype.

members.name: gelu_forward
members.signature: int gelu_forward(const int & x, const int & shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: GELU forward (tanh approximation) via a single MPSGraph activation node.
members.extended:
Replaces the 7-op MLX expression inside :meth:`GpuBackend::gelu` with
the equivalent fused MPSGraph node, eliminating the intermediate
memory traffic.

**Math**

$$y = 0.5 \, x \, \left(1 + \tanh\!\bigl[c_1\,(x + c_2 x^3)\bigr]\right)$$
where $c_1 = \sqrt{2/\pi}$, $c_2 = 0.044715$.

**See Also**

:func:`gelu_backward` — corresponding backward.
:func:`gelu_exact_forward` — Gaussian-CDF variant.

returns.description: Fresh GPU-resident result of identical shape + dtype.

members.name: layer_norm_backward
members.signature: LayerNormBackwardOut layer_norm_backward(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & saved_mean, const int & saved_rstd, const int & grad, int outer, int normalized_size, const int & x_shape, const int & gamma_shape, const int & beta_shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: LayerNorm backward as a single fused MPSGraph executable.
members.extended:
Computes ``(dx, dgamma, dbeta)`` in one MPSGraph dispatch using
the saved mean / rstd from the forward.  Faster than the MLX
composition for large ``normalized_size`` (see
:func:`should_dispatch_layer_norm_backward`).

**Math**

$$\hat{x} = (x - \mu) \cdot \mathrm{rstd}, \quad y = \gamma \hat{x} + \beta$$
$$d_\gamma = \sum_{\text{outer}} \mathrm{grad} \cdot \hat{x}, \quad
  d_\beta  = \sum_{\text{outer}} \mathrm{grad}$$

**See Also**

:func:`should_dispatch_layer_norm_backward` — dispatch gate.

parameters.name: saved_mean
parameters.description: Per-row mean from the forward, shape ``(outer, 1)``.

parameters.name: saved_rstd
parameters.description: Per-row reciprocal-stddev from the forward, shape ``(outer, 1)``.

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: Upstream gradient w.r.t. the layer output.

parameters.name: normalized_size
parameters.description: Size of the trailing normalised dimension.

parameters.name: beta_shape
parameters.description: Logical shape of ``beta``.  Used for the output ``dbeta``.

returns.description: The three gradients.

members.name: shared_mtl_device
members.signature: void * shared_mtl_device()
members.summary: Return the process-wide ``MTLDevice`` used by both MLX and MPSGraph.
members.extended:
Lazily initialised on first call.  Reuses MLX's device pointer
(``mlx::core::metal::device``) so MPS kernels and MLX kernels
dispatch onto the same hardware queue family.

returns.description: Opaque ``id<MTLDevice>``.  Cast via ``(__bridge id<MTLDevice>)`` inside ``.mm`` callers.  Never null on a supported host.

notes: Thread-safe; the underlying init is guarded by a one-shot flag.

members.name: shared_mtl_queue
members.signature: void * shared_mtl_queue()
members.summary: Return the process-wide ``MTLCommandQueue`` used by MPS kernels.
members.extended:
Distinct from MLX's queue so MPS dispatches and MLX dispatches do
not contend on the same encoder.  Lazily initialised.

returns.description: Opaque ``id<MTLCommandQueue>``.  Cast via ``(__bridge id<MTLCommandQueue>)`` inside ``.mm`` callers.

notes: Thread-safe.

members.name: should_dispatch_batch_norm_train
members.signature: bool should_dispatch_batch_norm_train(int numel, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Dispatch BatchNorm train (fwd + bwd) through MPSGraph for large activations.
members.extended:
MLX has no fused BatchNorm primitive; for very large activations
(e.g. ImageNet-scale 16×64×112×112 ≈ 12.8M elements) the
MPSGraph ``normalizationWithTensor:`` kernel is the only way to
close the parity gap.  ResNet-scale shapes (≤ ~2M numel) hit
MLX's per-axis reduction kernels at roughly reference-framework
parity, so they keep the MLX path.  Phase 0 measured 5.5× the
reference on ``large_acts``, 1× on ResNet shapes.

**See Also**

:func:`batch_norm_train_forward`, :func:`batch_norm_train_backward`.

parameters.name: numel
parameters.description: Total element count of the activation tensor.  The gate.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Element dtype.  Reserved for future dtype-specific tuning.

returns.description: ``true`` iff MPS dispatch is enabled and ``numel`` clears the large-activation threshold.

members.name: should_dispatch_gelu
members.signature: bool should_dispatch_gelu(int numel, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Always dispatch GELU (tanh approximation) through MPSGraph.
members.extended:
The MLX path for tanh-approximation GELU is a 7-op composite that
is memory-bound on every measured shape.  MPSGraph fuses these
into a single activation node — universal win.

**See Also**

:func:`should_dispatch_gelu_exact` — Gaussian-CDF variant.

parameters.name: numel
parameters.description: Total element count of the input.  Currently unused (universal dispatch) but kept in the signature for future gating.

returns.description: ``true`` if MPS dispatch is enabled at process scope.

members.name: should_dispatch_gelu_exact
members.signature: bool should_dispatch_gelu_exact(int numel, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Always dispatch GELU exact (Gaussian-CDF) through MPSGraph.
members.extended:
The MLX path composes erf via a 10-op approximation chain; MPSGraph
has a single fused node.  Universal win.

parameters.name: numel
parameters.description: Total element count of the input.  Currently unused.

returns.description: ``true`` if MPS dispatch is enabled at process scope.

members.name: should_dispatch_layer_norm_backward
members.signature: bool should_dispatch_layer_norm_backward(int outer, int normalized_size, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Dispatch LayerNorm backward through MPSGraph for large normalize sizes.
members.extended:
The fused MPSGraph executable producing ``(dx, dgamma, dbeta)``
from saved tensors is large enough that its launch overhead
dominates for small ``normalized_size`` (e.g. transformer
``Q/K/V`` projections), so the heuristic shape-gates on that
dimension.  Phase-4 tuning will lock in the exact threshold.

**See Also**

:func:`layer_norm_backward` — the kernel that gets dispatched.

parameters.name: normalized_size
parameters.description: Size of the trailing (normalised) dimension.  The gate.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Element dtype.  Reserved for future dtype-specific tuning.

returns.description: ``true`` iff MPS dispatch is enabled and ``normalized_size`` is large enough to amortise the dispatch cost.

members.name: should_dispatch_silu_backward
members.signature: bool should_dispatch_silu_backward(int numel, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Always dispatch SiLU backward through MPSGraph.
members.extended:
The MLX backward composes ``sigmoid + multiply + add + multiply
+ ...`` (~7 ops) with the same memory-bound profile as GELU; the
MPSGraph fusion is a universal win across all measured shapes.

**See Also**

:func:`silu_backward` — the kernel that gets dispatched.

parameters.name: numel
parameters.description: Total element count of the input.  Currently unused.

returns.description: ``true`` if MPS dispatch is enabled at process scope.

members.name: should_dispatch_softmax_backward
members.signature: bool should_dispatch_softmax_backward(int axis_size, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Dispatch Softmax backward through MPSGraph for large reduction axes.
members.extended:
The MLX path is a 4-op chain (``gz / sum / diff / result``) that
is memory-bound on large axes (e.g. cross-entropy at GPT-2's
vocab=50257 allocates ~800 MB of intermediate tensors).  Phase 0
measured 3× the reference on ``ce_gpt2_logits`` and ~1× on
attention-shape softmaxes.  The heuristic gates at
``axis_size >= 1024`` to skip small / attention shapes.

**See Also**

:func:`softmax_backward` — the kernel that gets dispatched.

parameters.name: axis_size
parameters.description: Size of the softmax reduction axis.  The gate.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Element dtype.  Reserved for future dtype-specific tuning.

returns.description: ``true`` iff MPS dispatch is enabled and ``axis_size`` clears the threshold.

members.name: silu_backward
members.signature: int silu_backward(const int & x, const int & grad, const int & shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: SiLU backward through a fused MPSGraph kernel.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \sigma(x) \, \bigl(1 + x \, (1 - \sigma(x))\bigr) \cdot \text{grad}$$
where $\sigma$ is the logistic sigmoid.

**See Also**

:func:`should_dispatch_silu_backward` — dispatch gate.

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: Upstream gradient w.r.t. the SiLU output.

returns.description: Gradient w.r.t. ``x``.

members.name: softmax_backward
members.signature: int softmax_backward(const int & z, const int & grad, int axis, const int & shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Softmax backward as a fused MPSGraph kernel.
members.extended:
Evaluates the canonical formula ``z * (grad - sum(z*grad, axis))``
in a single dispatch, where ``z`` is the **saved softmax output**
(not the input) and ``axis`` has already been normalised to a
non-negative index by the caller.

**Math**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x_i} = z_i \left(g_i - \sum_j z_j g_j\right)$$
reducing along ``axis``.

**See Also**

:func:`should_dispatch_softmax_backward` — dispatch gate.

parameters.name: z
parameters.description: Saved softmax output from the forward pass.

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: Upstream gradient w.r.t. the softmax output.

returns.description: Gradient w.r.t. the softmax input.

members.name: wait_all
members.signature: void wait_all()
members.summary: Block until every in-flight MPS command buffer has completed.
members.extended:
Provided primarily for the test suite + callers that need
CPU-visible results from a kernel before the next op enqueues.
Routine code paths normally do not need this — MLX consumers of
an MPS-produced array automatically sync via :func:`array_to_buffer`.
notes: Synchronous from the caller's perspective.

members.name: run_metal_kernel
members.signature: int run_metal_kernel(const MetalKernel & kernel, const int & inputs, const int & output_shape, Dtype output_dtype, const KernelLaunchConfig & config, const int & constants)
members.summary: Encode + dispatch a compiled kernel; block until it completes.
members.extended:
Allocates an output ``SharedStorage`` of ``output_shape`` /
``output_dtype``, binds ``inputs`` at indices ``0..n-1``,
``constants`` at ``n..n+k-1``, and the output buffer at ``n+k``,
then dispatches the threadgroup grid given by ``config`` and waits
synchronously for the GPU to finish.

**See Also**

:func:`compile_metal_kernel` — produces ``kernel``.
:func:`make_metal_shared` — backs the returned storage.

parameters.name: kernel
parameters.description: Compiled pipeline produced by :func:`compile_metal_kernel`. Must satisfy ``kernel.is_valid() == true``.

parameters.name: inputs
parameters.description: Input tensors.  ``SharedStorage`` is bridged in place; ``GpuStorage`` is materialised (``eval()``) and copied into a fresh shared buffer; ``CpuStorage`` is copied verbatim.

parameters.name: output_shape
parameters.description: Logical shape of the output tensor.

parameters.name: output_dtype
parameters.description: Element dtype of the output tensor.

parameters.name: constants
parameters.description: Scalar constants bound after the input buffers.  Defaults to an empty list.

returns.description: ``SharedStorage`` backed by a unified-memory buffer; the result is immediately CPU-readable on return (the call waits for completion).

raises.description: If the kernel is invalid, an input type cannot be resolved, or the GPU dispatch fails.

notes:
Returning ``SharedStorage`` (rather than ``GpuStorage``) trades
some bandwidth for caller flexibility — the result can flow into
either CPU or GPU consumers without an extra copy.

members.name: shared_storage_to_gpu
members.signature: GpuStorage shared_storage_to_gpu(const SharedStorage & sh, const int & shape)
members.summary: Adopt a unified-memory ``SharedStorage`` as a ``GpuStorage`` view.
members.extended:
Wraps the underlying ``MTLResourceStorageModeShared`` buffer as a
fresh MLX array without copying.  The result is readable + writable
from both CPU and GPU (subject to Metal's memory-model rules).

parameters.name: sh
parameters.description: Source storage backed by a shared Metal buffer.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Logical shape to view the buffer with.

returns.description: A non-copying view participating in MemoryTracker accounting.

notes:
This is the canonical fast path for results produced by
:func:`run_metal_kernel` that flow back into MLX-based ops.

members.name: to_mlx_dtype
members.signature: int to_mlx_dtype(Dtype dt)
members.summary: Map a Lucid :enum:`Dtype` to the matching ``mlx::core::Dtype``.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`from_mlx_dtype` — inverse direction.

returns.description: The corresponding MLX dtype enumerator.

raises.description: If ``dt == Dtype::F64`` — Apple's Metal stack has no native 64-bit floating-point path, so float64 tensors cannot live on the GPU.  Callers must downcast to float32 first or keep the tensor on the CPU.

members.name: to_mlx_shape
members.signature: int to_mlx_shape(const int & shape)
members.summary: Convert a Lucid :class:`Shape` (int64) to an ``mlx::core::Shape`` (int32).

returns.description: ``std::vector<int32_t>`` view of the same dimensions.

raises.description: If any dimension exceeds ``INT32_MAX`` — MLX is currently int32-indexed and cannot represent larger extents.

members.name: upload_cpu_to_gpu
members.signature: GpuStorage upload_cpu_to_gpu(const CpuStorage & cpu, const int & shape)
members.summary: Upload a CPU buffer to GPU-private memory via ``mlx::core::copy``.
members.extended:
MLX arrays own GPU-private pages that **cannot alias** a CPU
pointer (touching the raw ``data()`` from the CPU side raises
SIGBUS on M-series hardware).  This routine allocates a new MLX
array of the requested shape and dispatches a lazy
``mlx::core::copy`` from the CPU buffer.  The ``cpu`` storage is
kept alive — via a custom deleter on the source MLX view — until
the asynchronous copy completes.

**See Also**

:func:`download_gpu_to_cpu` — inverse direction.
:func:`shared_storage_to_gpu` — zero-copy variant for shared buffers.

parameters.name: cpu
parameters.description: Source buffer in Accelerate-allocated CPU memory.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Logical shape of the array.  Must match ``cpu`` element count.

returns.description: A GPU-resident array participating in MemoryTracker accounting.

notes:
Copy bandwidth on M-series unified memory is ~100 GB/s, so the
transfer is rarely the bottleneck — but the call still pays for a
fresh GPU allocation.  Prefer batching uploads.

members.name: wrap_existing
members.signature: MetalBuffer wrap_existing(void * cpu_ptr, int nbytes)
members.summary: Wrap an externally-owned, page-aligned CPU pointer in a Metal buffer.
members.extended:
Calls ``newBufferWithBytesNoCopy:`` on the shared MTLDevice, which
requires the source memory to be page-aligned and at least
page-size bytes.  No copy is performed — GPU kernels read/write the
caller's buffer in place.

**See Also**

:func:`allocate_shared` — fresh allocation, no aliasing.

parameters.name: cpu_ptr
parameters.description: CPU pointer to wrap.  Must be page-aligned (typically obtained from :func:`posix_memalign` or another Metal allocation).

parameters.name: nbytes
parameters.description: Byte length of the region.  Must be ≥ system page size.

returns.description: Descriptor referring to ``cpu_ptr`` with ``mtl_handle`` set to the freshly created ``id<MTLBuffer>``.

raises.description: If Metal rejects the wrap (e.g. unaligned pointer, missing MTLDevice).

warns.annotation: Do **not** pass MLX GPU-private array data here — MLX arrays are

members.name: wrap_mlx_array
members.signature: GpuStorage wrap_mlx_array(::mlx::core::array && arr, Dtype dtype)
members.summary: Wrap a caller-owned ``mlx::core::array`` into a tracked ``GpuStorage``.
members.extended:
Takes ownership of ``arr`` (moves it) and packages it as a
``GpuStorage`` registered with the MemoryTracker.  Used by every
op method in :class:`GpuBackend` to return MLX results back to
Lucid.

parameters.name: arr
parameters.description: The MLX array to wrap.  Moved-from on return.

parameters.name: dtype
parameters.description: The Lucid dtype tag to associate with the result (must match ``arr``'s MLX dtype via :func:`from_mlx_dtype`).

returns.description: The wrapped array tracked by Python-side memory accounting.

members.name: greater_equal_op
members.signature: int greater_equal_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise greater-than-or-equal comparison.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = (a[i] >= b[i])`` and returns the result as a Bool
tensor.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = \mathbf{1}\!\left[a[i] \geq b[i]\right].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.greater_equal``.

**See Also**

greater_op, less_equal_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of identical shape, dtype, and device.

returns.description: Bool tensor with the same shape as the inputs.

raises.description: If the operand shapes, dtypes, or devices differ.

members.name: greater_op
members.signature: int greater_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise strict greater-than comparison.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = (a[i] > b[i])`` and returns the result as a Bool
tensor.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = \mathbf{1}\!\left[a[i] > b[i]\right].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.greater``.

**See Also**

greater_equal_op, less_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of identical shape, dtype, and device.

returns.description: Bool tensor with the same shape as the inputs.

raises.description: If the operand shapes, dtypes, or devices differ.

members.name: grid_sample_op
members.signature: int grid_sample_op(const int & input, const int & grid, int mode, int padding_mode, bool align_corners)
members.summary: Public grid-sample entry point.
members.extended: Thin wrapper that delegates to ``GridSampleBackward::forward``.

parameters.name: grid
parameters.description: Sampling grid ``(N, H_out, W_out, 2)`` with coordinates in $[-1, 1]$.

returns.description: Resampled tensor of shape ``(N, C, H_out, W_out)``.
