members.name: group_norm_op
members.signature: int group_norm_op(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, int num_groups, double eps)
members.summary: Public free-function entry point for Group Normalization.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that delegates to :func:`GroupNormBackward::forward`.
This is the symbol the pybind11 binding layer forwards to from
:func:`lucid.nn.functional.group_norm`.

**See Also**

GroupNormBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.
layer_norm_op     : Equivalent when ``num_groups == 1``.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of shape ``(B, C, S_0, ...)`` with rank ``>= 2``.

parameters.name: num_groups
parameters.description: Number of groups; must divide ``C`` evenly.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: hard_sigmoid_op
members.signature: int hard_sigmoid_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Hard sigmoid $y = \mathrm{clamp}((x + 3)/6, 0, 1)$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

HardSigmoidBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with values in $[0, 1]$.

members.name: hard_swish_op
members.signature: int hard_swish_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Hard swish $y = x \cdot \mathrm{hard\_sigmoid}(x)$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

HardSwishBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

members.name: allocate_cpu
members.signature: CpuStorage allocate_cpu(const int & shape, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Allocates a zero-initialised CPU buffer sized for ``shape`` elements of dtype ``dt`` and wraps it as a :class:`CpuStorage`.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`allocate_aligned_bytes` — the underlying aligned allocator.
:func:`helpers::fresh` — wraps a Storage into a TensorImpl.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Logical shape of the tensor that will use the buffer.  Only the element count (:func:`shape_numel`) influences allocation size.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Element dtype.  Determines per-element size via :func:`dtype_size`.

returns.description: Newly allocated storage with ``dtype = dt``, ``nbytes = shape_numel(shape) * dtype_size(dt)``, and a 64-byte aligned (``kCpuAlignment``) pointer to a zeroed buffer. ``nbytes == 0`` (empty shape or any dim of 0) yields a storage whose ``ptr`` is null — callers must check for this case.

examples: Allocate a ``(3, 4)`` ``float32`` buffer::
notes:
The buffer is zeroed via :func:`std::memset`; allocation uses
:func:`allocate_aligned_bytes` so the pointer satisfies SIMD-friendly
alignment requirements expected by Apple Accelerate kernels.

Zero-byte allocations short-circuit the memset and return a storage
with a null ``ptr`` and ``nbytes == 0``.  This matches the
convention used elsewhere in the engine for empty tensors.

members.name: fresh
members.signature: int fresh(int && s, int shape, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Wraps an existing :class:`Storage` and tensor geometry into a new :class:`TensorImpl` with autograd disabled.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`helpers::allocate_cpu` — typical Storage producer for the
    first argument.
:class:`TensorImpl` — concrete wrapper type returned.

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: Storage holding the tensor data.  Moved into the new :class:`TensorImpl` — no copy of the underlying buffer occurs. May be CPU, GPU, or Shared storage.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Logical shape of the resulting tensor.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Element dtype.  Must be consistent with the storage's element layout.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which the storage resides.  Must match the storage's actual location.

returns.description: ``std::shared_ptr<TensorImpl>`` to a freshly constructed tensor. The stride is initialised to the C-contiguous default by the :class:`TensorImpl` constructor; ``requires_grad`` is ``false``.

examples: Build a kernel output tensor in one statement::
notes:
"Fresh" here means newly produced by an op kernel — neither a view
onto an existing tensor nor an autograd leaf that needs gradient
tracking.  Callers that want a gradient-tracking output must set
the autograd metadata after construction.

Storage is moved (rvalue reference), so the input is consumed.
This matches the kernel pattern where storage and tensor are
produced in adjacent statements and the storage handle is no
longer needed after wrapping.

members.name: histc_op
members.signature: int histc_op(const int & a, int bins, double lo, double hi)
members.summary: Histogram counts over $[\mathrm{lo}, \mathrm{hi})$ with ``bins`` uniform buckets.
members.extended:
Composite — counts-only wrapper around :func:`histogram_op`.  When
``lo == hi`` the range is auto-derived from the input via :func:`min_op`
and :func:`max_op`, with the degenerate ``min == max`` case bumped by
$+1$ so the bins remain well-defined (matches reference framework
behaviour).  Not differentiable — integer-valued output.

**Math**

Let $w = (\mathrm{hi} - \mathrm{lo}) / \mathrm{bins}$.  Then for each
bin index $k \in [0, \mathrm{bins})$,
$$
  y_k = \#\!\left\{\, i : \mathrm{lo} + k w \le a_i < \mathrm{lo} + (k+1) w \,\right\}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`histogram_op` — full version that also returns bin edges.

parameters.name: bins
parameters.description: Number of histogram bins.  Must be positive.

parameters.name: lo
parameters.description: Inclusive lower edge.  If ``lo == hi``, auto-range from ``min(a)`` to ``max(a)``.

returns.description: 1-D tensor of length ``bins`` holding integer counts.

raises.description: If ``a`` is null.

notes:
Values outside ``[lo, hi)`` are dropped silently by the underlying
:func:`histogram_op` — no overflow / underflow bins are returned.

members.name: histogram2d_op
members.signature: int histogram2d_op(const int & a, const int & b, int bins_a, int bins_b, double lo_a, double hi_a, double lo_b, double hi_b, bool density)
members.summary: Compute a 2-D histogram from two equal-length 1-D tensors.
members.extended:
Treats paired observations ``(a_k, b_k)`` as samples on an
$\mathrm{bins\_a} \times \mathrm{bins\_b}$ grid of uniform rectangular
cells and counts the number of pairs falling in each cell.  When
``density`` is true each cell value is divided by
``(step_a * step_b * N)``.

**See Also**

histogram_op : 1-D variant.
histogramdd_op : General N-dimensional variant.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: 1-D tensor of y-coordinates.  Must match ``a`` in length, dtype, and device.

parameters.name: bins_a
parameters.description: Number of bins along the x-axis.

parameters.name: bins_b
parameters.description: Number of bins along the y-axis.

parameters.name: density
parameters.description: If true, normalise to a probability density.

returns.description: ``[counts, edges]`` where ``counts`` has shape ``(bins_a, bins_b)`` and ``edges`` has shape ``(bins_a + 1 + bins_b + 1,)`` — the x-edges followed by the y-edges concatenated.  Both dtype F64.

notes:
Non-differentiable.  Executes on the CPU stream regardless of input
device.

members.name: histogram_op
members.signature: int histogram_op(const int & a, int bins, double lo, double hi, bool density)
members.summary: Compute a 1-D histogram of values in ``a`` over uniform bins.
members.extended:
Partitions the half-open interval $[lo, hi)$ into ``bins`` equal-width
sub-intervals and counts the number of elements of ``a`` falling in each
one.  When ``density`` is true the counts are normalised so that the
histogram integrates to one over the support: counts are divided by
``(bin_width * N)`` where ``N`` is the total number of input elements.

**Math**

$$counts_i = \#\{k : lo + i \Delta \le a_k < lo + (i+1)\Delta\}, \quad
\Delta = (hi - lo) / bins$$

**See Also**

histogram2d_op : Joint 2-D histogram of paired observations.
histogramdd_op : Histogram of (N, D) row-major samples in D dimensions.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape; flattened internally.

parameters.name: bins
parameters.description: Number of equal-width bins.  Must be positive.

parameters.name: lo
parameters.description: Left edge of the lowest bin (inclusive).

parameters.name: hi
parameters.description: Right edge of the highest bin (exclusive, except for the last bin which is closed on the right).

parameters.name: density
parameters.description: If true, return a probability density; otherwise return raw counts.

returns.description: ``[counts, edges]`` where ``counts`` has shape ``(bins,)`` and ``edges`` has shape ``(bins + 1,)``.  Both have dtype F64.

notes:
Non-differentiable: counts are piecewise-constant in the input.  Always
executes on the CPU stream; GPU inputs are copied across.

members.name: histogramdd_op
members.signature: int histogramdd_op(const int & a, int bins, int)
members.summary: Compute an N-dimensional histogram from a 2-D sample matrix.
members.extended:
Given an input tensor of shape ``(N, D)`` whose rows are D-dimensional
observations, partitions each dimension into the corresponding number
of bins and counts samples falling in each D-dimensional cell.  When
``density`` is true each cell is divided by ``(cell_volume * N)``.

**See Also**

histogram_op, histogram2d_op : Lower-dimensional specialisations.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: 2-D tensor of shape ``(N, D)``; row ``k`` is the ``k``-th sample.

parameters.name: bins
parameters.description: Length-D vector giving the bin count along each dimension.

parameters.name: ranges
parameters.description: Length-D vector of ``(lo, hi)`` pairs, one range per dimension.

parameters.name: density
parameters.description: If true, normalise to a probability density.

returns.description: ``[counts, edges]`` where ``counts`` is a D-dimensional tensor whose shape equals ``bins``, and ``edges`` is a 1-D tensor of length ``sum(bins[d] + 1)`` containing the per-dimension edge arrays concatenated in order.  Both dtype F64.

notes: Non-differentiable.  Always executes on the CPU stream.

members.name: householder_product_op
members.signature: int householder_product_op(const int & H, const int & tau)
members.summary: Materialise the orthogonal $Q$ from packed Householder reflectors.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$
  Q = H_1 H_2 \cdots H_k, \qquad k = \min(m, n)
$$

**Shape**

- Input ``H``    : ``(m, n)``
- Input ``tau``  : ``(k,)`` with $k = \min(m, n)$
- Output ``Q``   : ``(m, k)``

**References**

LAPACK ``*orgqr``; Golub & Van Loan §5.1.2.

**See Also**

[[eig_op]]  : Produces packed reflectors for general eigenvector
              reconstruction.
[[eigh_op]] : Symmetric eigendecomp; reflectors come from a
              tridiagonal reduction step.

parameters.name: H
parameters.description: Packed reflector matrix of shape ``(m, n)`` produced by a QR-type factorisation.  Column $i$ stores reflector $v_i$ in the strict lower triangle below the diagonal (the diagonal entry of $v_i$ is implicitly $1$ and the slot is reused for $R$).  Must be float-typed and non-null.

parameters.name: tau
parameters.description: 1-D scaling factors of length $k = \min(m, n)$.  ``tau[i]`` is the $\tau_i$ scalar of the $i$-th reflector $H_i = I - \tau_i v_i v_i^\top$.  Must be float-typed and non-null.

returns.description: Explicit orthogonal matrix $Q$ of shape ``(m, k)`` with the same dtype as ``H``.  Columns are orthonormal ($Q^\top Q = I_k$).

raises.description: When ``H`` or ``tau`` is null / not float-typed.

notes:
Output is a leaf node — autograd is **not** wired.  Gradients flow
through the calling QR / eig op rather than through this unpack
step.

members.name: hstack_op
members.signature: int hstack_op(const int & xs)
members.summary: Horizontally stack a sequence of tensors.
members.extended:
Equivalent to NumPy ``hstack``: for 1-D inputs the result concatenates
along axis 0; for tensors with rank >= 2 the concatenation occurs along
axis 1.  Tensors must agree on every non-horizontal dimension.

**See Also**

:func:`vstack_op`, :func:`concatenate_op`.

parameters.name: xs
parameters.description: Non-empty list of tensors with the same rank, dtype, and device.

returns.description: Horizontally concatenated tensor.

examples:
1-D inputs ``[a, b, c]`` and ``[d, e]`` produce ``[a, b, c, d, e]``.
2-D inputs of shapes ``(R, C_i)`` produce shape ``(R, \sum_i C_i)``.

members.name: huber_loss_op
members.signature: int huber_loss_op(const int & input, const int & target, double delta, int reduction)
members.summary: Public Huber (smooth L1) loss entry point.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Ground-truth tensor of the same shape.

parameters.name: delta
parameters.description: Quadratic / linear transition threshold; must be ``> 0``.

returns.description: Reduced or per-element loss tensor.

members.name: hypot_op
members.signature: int hypot_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Pythagorean length: $y = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`square_op` + :func:`add_op` + :func:`sqrt_op`.
**Naive** formulation — does not perform IEEE-754 overflow-safe scaling
(i.e. no $\max(|a|, |b|) \cdot \sqrt{1 + (\min / \max)^2}$ trick),
matching the engine's ``square_op`` precision.

**Math**

$$
  y = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}
$$

returns.description: Tensor on the broadcast shape; values $\ge 0$.

notes:
Inputs near the float dtype's overflow threshold may lose precision —
prefer a dedicated ``hypot`` kernel for safety-critical paths.

members.name: ifftn_op
members.signature: int ifftn_op(const int & a, const int & n, const int & axes)
members.summary: N-dimensional inverse complex-to-complex DFT (forward of the inverse).
members.extended:
Inverts an N-D DFT.  Uses the ``"backward"`` normalisation
convention: forward has no scaling, this op divides by $\prod_j N_j$
where $N_j$ is the transform length along each axis.

**Math**

$$
  x[n_1, \dots, n_d] = \frac{1}{\prod_j N_j}
    \sum_{k_1=0}^{N_1-1} \cdots \sum_{k_d=0}^{N_d-1}
    X[k_1, \dots, k_d] \,
    \exp\!\left( +2\pi i \sum_{j=1}^{d} \frac{k_j n_j}{N_j} \right) .
$$

**See Also**

fftn_op : Forward N-D DFT.
irfftn_op : Inverse of the real-input DFT.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor.  C64 expected; F16/F32 are accepted and promoted.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: Per-axis transform length.  Empty means "use input sizes". When supplied, ``len(n) == len(axes)`` is required.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to transform.  Negative values are normalised against the input rank.  Empty means "transform all axes".

returns.description: C64 tensor of the same shape as ``a`` (with ``axes[i]`` replaced by ``n[i]`` when ``n`` is supplied).

raises.description: If ``a`` is null, rank-0, has an unsupported dtype, or if ``len(n) != len(axes)`` when ``n`` is non-empty.

notes:
FFT bypasses IBackend dispatch and calls MLX directly from both CPU
and GPU device paths (see ``_Detail.h``).

members.name: imag_op
members.signature: int imag_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Extract the imaginary part of a complex tensor as a real tensor.
members.extended:
The result dtype is the corresponding real dtype (``C64`` → ``F32``); the
shape and device are unchanged.

**Math**

$$
  y_k = \Im(z_k)
$$

**See Also**

real_op, complex_op, conj_op

returns.description: Real-dtype output (``F32``) of the same shape and device as ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is not a complex dtype.

members.name: in_range_mask_storage
members.signature: int in_range_mask_storage(const int & s, double lo, double hi, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Mask elements that fall inside the closed range ``[lo, hi]``.
members.extended:
Returns ``1`` where ``lo <= s <= hi`` and ``0`` everywhere else, used
by clamp / hardtanh backward passes to gate the gradient through the
active region.

returns.description: New storage holding the {0, 1} gate.

members.name: index_select_op
members.signature: int index_select_op(const int & a, int dim, const int & indices)
members.summary: Pick ``indices.size`` slices along ``dim`` of ``a``.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`reshape_op` + :func:`expand_op` + :func:`gather_op`.
The 1-D index list is reshaped to rank ``a.ndim`` (size $k$ along ``dim``,
1 elsewhere) and expanded to the source shape so ``gather_op``'s
same-rank contract holds.

**Math**

For each output coordinate $(\ldots, j, \ldots)$ along ``dim``:
$$
  y_{\ldots, j, \ldots} = a_{\ldots, \mathrm{idx}_j, \ldots}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`take_op` — flat indexing; :func:`narrow_op` — contiguous window.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Axis to index along.  Negative values wrap modulo ``a.ndim``.

returns.description: Tensor with the same shape as ``a`` except size $k$ along ``dim``.

raises.description: If ``dim`` is out of range.
If ``indices`` is not 1-D or not an integer dtype.

members.name: inner_op
members.signature: int inner_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Compute the generalised inner product of $A$ and $B$ by contracting their last axes (NumPy ``inner`` semantics).
members.extended:
For rank-$n_A$ and rank-$n_B$ inputs whose last dims agree in size, the
output preserves all leading-of-A dims followed by all leading-of-B
dims, with the shared last axis summed out.

**Math**

General contraction over the trailing axis ($K = a_{n_A-1} = b_{n_B-1}$):
$$
  \mathrm{out}[i_0, \ldots, i_{n_A-2}, j_0, \ldots, j_{n_B-2}]
    = \sum_{k=0}^{K-1}
        A[i_0, \ldots, i_{n_A-2}, k]\,
        B[j_0, \ldots, j_{n_B-2}, k]
$$
2-D × 2-D specialisation:
$$
  \mathrm{out}[i, j] = \sum_k A[i, k]\, B[j, k]
$$

**Shape**

- ``a.shape[-1] == b.shape[-1]`` is required.
- Output shape concatenates the all-but-last dims of each input.

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.inner`` (which Lucid mirrors).

**See Also**

dot_op : 1-D × 1-D / 2-D × 2-D specialised dispatch.
matmul_op : Standard matmul with NumPy batch broadcasting.
outer_op : 1-D outer product (the rank-1 "anti-inner").
einsum_op : The lowering target for the gradient-tracked path.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: First operand, rank $\ge 1$.  Last dim is the contraction axis.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Second operand, rank $\ge 1$.  Last dim must match ``a.shape[-1]``.

returns.description: Output with shape ``a.shape[:-1] + b.shape[:-1]``.  When both inputs are 1-D the result is a scalar (shape ``{}``).

raises.description: If either input is rank-0 or the last dims disagree.

notes:
When gradient tracking is enabled this op is lowered to ``einsum_op``
with a pattern produced by ``inner_einsum_pattern`` (free axes of $A$
use letters ``a..o``, free axes of $B$ use ``p..y``, contracted axis
is ``z``).  The einsum backward pass handles autograd, so this op has
no dedicated backward node.

On the GPU stream the output shape is read back from the MLX array
after the kernel returns to absorb any scalar-squeeze MLX may perform.

members.name: interpolate_bilinear_op
members.signature: int interpolate_bilinear_op(const int & input, int H_out, int W_out, bool align_corners)
members.summary: Public 2-D bilinear resize with autograd.
members.extended: Thin wrapper that delegates to ``InterpolateBilinearBackward::forward``.

returns.description: Bilinearly resampled tensor of shape ``(N, C, H_out, W_out)``.

members.name: interpolate_nearest_2d_op
members.signature: int interpolate_nearest_2d_op(const int & input, int H_out, int W_out)
members.summary: Public 2-D nearest-neighbour resize (no autograd).
members.extended:
Each output pixel receives the value of the nearest input pixel:
$$
  y[i, j] = x\!\left[\lfloor (i + 0.5) \cdot H_{\text{in}} / H_{\text{out}} \rfloor,\;
                      \lfloor (j + 0.5) \cdot W_{\text{in}} / W_{\text{out}} \rfloor\right]
$$
No backward node is attached — the operation is inference-only.

returns.description: Resampled tensor of shape ``(N, C, H_out, W_out)``.

members.name: interpolate_nearest_3d_op
members.signature: int interpolate_nearest_3d_op(const int & input, int D_out, int H_out, int W_out)
members.summary: Public 3-D nearest-neighbour resize (no autograd).
members.extended:
Volumetric counterpart of ``interpolate_nearest_2d_op``; produces a
``(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)`` output by floor-rounding source voxel
coordinates.  No backward node is attached.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: 5-D input tensor ``(N, C, D_in, H_in, W_in)``.

returns.description: Resampled tensor of shape ``(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)``.

members.name: interpolate_trilinear_op
members.signature: int interpolate_trilinear_op(const int & input, int D_out, int H_out, int W_out, bool align_corners)
members.summary: Public 3-D trilinear resize with autograd.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: 5-D input tensor ``(N, C, D_in, H_in, W_in)``.

returns.description: Trilinearly resampled tensor of shape ``(N, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)``.

members.name: inv_op
members.signature: int inv_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $A^{-1}$ for a square float tensor.
members.extended:
Validates that ``a`` is at least 2-D, square in the trailing two
axes, and float-typed; dispatches to the backend; and wires
``InvBackward`` into the autograd graph (saving only the output)
when grad mode is active.

**Shape**

- ``a``: ``(..., N, N)``.
- Output: same shape as ``a``.

**See Also**

solve_op : Often a better alternative — solves $AX = B$ without
    forming $A^{-1}$ explicitly, with better conditioning.
det_op : Determinant, which uses ``inv_op`` in its backward.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input matrix of shape ``(..., N, N)``.

returns.description: Inverse $A^{-1}$ of identical shape to ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is not square, fewer than 2-D, or non-float.
(At backend level.) If $A$ is singular (LU produces a zero pivot).

members.name: invert_op
members.signature: int invert_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise bitwise NOT — returns the bit-flipped tensor with the same integer dtype as ``a``.
members.extended:
Not differentiable: output is detached from the autograd graph.

**Math**

$$y_i = \mathtt{\sim} x_i$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of integer dtype (``Bool``, ``Int8``, ``Int16``, ``Int32``, ``Int64``, or unsigned variants).  Floating-point dtypes raise an error.

returns.description: New tensor with the bitwise complement; same shape, dtype, and device as ``a``.

raises.annotation: Errors if ``a`` has a floating-point dtype — bitwise NOT is only

notes:
Exposed in Python as both ``invert`` and ``bitwise_not`` (reference
framework alias).

members.name: irfftn_op
members.signature: int irfftn_op(const int & a, const int & n, const int & axes)
members.summary: N-dimensional inverse real DFT (complex Hermitian-half to real).
members.extended:
Reconstructs the real signal from a Hermitian-half spectrum produced
by ``rfftn``.  The last transformed axis is expanded from the stored
length $n_{\text{last}}/2 + 1$ back to $n_{\text{last}}$.  Uses the
``"backward"`` normalisation convention (divides by $\prod_j N_j$).

**Math**

$$
  x[n_1, \dots, n_d] = \frac{1}{\prod_j N_j}
    \sum_{k_1=0}^{N_1-1} \cdots \sum_{k_d=0}^{N_d-1}
    X[k_1, \dots, k_d] \,
    \exp\!\left( +2\pi i \sum_{j=1}^{d} \frac{k_j n_j}{N_j} \right) ,
$$

reconstructed assuming $X$ is Hermitian-symmetric along the last
transformed axis ($X[\dots, N_d - k_d] = \overline{X[\dots, k_d]}$).

**Shape**

``out.shape[axes[i]] == n[i]`` when ``n`` is supplied; otherwise
``out.shape[axes.back()] == 2 * (a.shape[axes.back()] - 1)`` and
other transformed axes are unchanged.

**See Also**

rfftn_op : Forward real-input DFT.
ifftn_op : Inverse of full complex-to-complex DFT.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Complex input (C64), shaped as the output of ``rfftn``.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: Per-axis transform length.  The last entry specifies the original real-input length along ``axes.back()``; when ``n`` is empty, the default ``2 * (a.shape[axes.back()] - 1)`` is used.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to transform.  Must be non-empty.  Negative values are normalised against the input rank.

returns.description: F32 tensor.  Shape follows ``a`` except that ``axes[i]`` is replaced by ``n[i]`` (with the default applied to the last axis when ``n`` is empty).

raises.description: If ``a`` is null, not C64, ``axes`` is empty, or ``len(n) != len(axes)`` when ``n`` is non-empty.

notes:
FFT bypasses IBackend dispatch and calls MLX directly (see
``_Detail.h``).  When the original real length was odd, ``n.back()``
must be supplied explicitly — the default formula assumes even length.

members.name: is_floating_point
members.signature: bool is_floating_point(Dtype dt)
members.summary: Predicate: is the dtype a real floating-point type?
members.extended:
:attr:`Dtype::C64` is **not** floating point under this predicate even
though its lanes are float32 — complex tensors take a separate dispatch
path in most kernels.

returns.description: ``true`` for :attr:`F16`, :attr:`F32`, :attr:`F64`; ``false`` otherwise.

notes:
Must be kept in sync with the :class:`Dtype` enum: if BF16 is added,
it should be included here.

members.name: is_integral
members.signature: bool is_integral(Dtype dt)
members.summary: Predicate: is the dtype a signed integer type?
members.extended:
:attr:`Dtype::Bool` is intentionally **excluded** — booleans are stored
as 1-byte integers but have no meaningful arithmetic interpretation in
the integer sense, so promotion / index validation paths should not
treat them as integers.

returns.description: ``true`` for :attr:`I8`, :attr:`I16`, :attr:`I32`, :attr:`I64`; ``false`` for everything else (including :attr:`Bool`).

members.name: is_mutation_on_saved_allowed
members.signature: bool is_mutation_on_saved_allowed()
members.summary: Read the process-wide "allow mutation on saved tensors" flag.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`set_mutation_on_saved_allowed`
:func:`lucid.autograd.graph.allow_mutation_on_saved_tensors`

returns.description: Current state of the override.  When ``true``, :func:`check_version_match` becomes a no-op.

members.name: isclose_op
members.signature: int isclose_op(const int & a, const int & b, double rtol, double atol)
members.summary: Elementwise tolerance check: $|a - b| \le \mathrm{atol} + \mathrm{rtol} \cdot |b|$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`sub_op` + :func:`abs_op` + :func:`mul_op` +
:func:`add_op` + :func:`less_equal_op` — no dedicated kernel required.
``rtol`` and ``atol`` are materialised as same-shape constant tensors so
the multiply does not depend on any scalar-broadcast support in the
binary ops.  Returns a bool tensor; not differentiable.

**Math**

$$
  y_i = \mathbb{1}\!\left[ |a_i - b_i| \le \mathrm{atol} + \mathrm{rtol} \cdot |b_i| \right]
$$

returns.description: Bool tensor on the broadcast shape of ``a`` and ``b``.

notes:
The asymmetry in ``b`` matches the reference framework's convention —
swap operands if the symmetric formulation is needed.

members.name: isfinite_op
members.signature: int isfinite_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise test for finite values — returns a boolean tensor that is ``True`` at every position whose input is neither ``NaN`` nor $\pm\infty$.
members.extended:
Not differentiable: no autograd node is wired and the result is always
detached.

**Math**

$$y_i = \neg \operatorname{isnan}(x_i) \wedge \neg \operatorname{isinf}(x_i)$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`isnan_op`, :func:`isinf_op`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any floating-point dtype.  Integer inputs always produce ``True`` since every integer value is finite.

returns.description: Boolean tensor with the same shape and device as ``a``; dtype is ``Dtype::Bool``.

notes:
Equivalent to ``~(isnan(a) | isinf(a))`` but dispatched as a single
backend kernel for efficiency.

members.name: isinf_op
members.signature: int isinf_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise test for $\pm\infty$ — returns a boolean tensor that is ``True`` at every position whose input is positive or negative infinity.
members.extended:
Not differentiable: this op does not register an autograd node, so the
returned tensor is detached even if ``a`` has ``requires_grad=True``.

**Math**

$$y_i = \begin{cases} \text{True} & x_i = +\infty \text{ or } x_i = -\infty \\
                      \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`isnan_op`, :func:`isfinite_op`, :func:`nan_to_num_op`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any floating-point dtype.  Integer dtypes always produce ``False`` at every position (no value can represent infinity).

returns.description: Boolean tensor with the same shape and device as ``a``.  Dtype is ``Dtype::Bool`` regardless of ``a->dtype()``.

notes:
CPU dispatch uses Accelerate's vector isinf primitive; GPU dispatch
uses MLX's ``isinf``.  NaN inputs yield ``False`` (NaN is neither
finite nor infinite).

members.name: isnan_op
members.signature: int isnan_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise test for ``NaN`` values — returns a boolean tensor that is ``True`` at every position whose input is ``NaN``.
members.extended:
Not differentiable: returns a detached boolean tensor with no autograd
node, regardless of ``a->requires_grad()``.

**Math**

$$y_i = (x_i \ne x_i)$$

The IEEE-754 property "NaN does not equal itself" defines the predicate.

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`isinf_op`, :func:`isfinite_op`, :func:`nan_to_num_op`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any floating-point dtype.  Integer inputs always produce ``False`` since no integer encodes NaN.

returns.description: Boolean tensor with the same shape and device as ``a``; dtype is ``Dtype::Bool``.

notes:
Useful as a guard inside composite reductions (``nanmean``, ``nansum``)
where NaN-tainted slots must be replaced before accumulation.

members.name: cpu_matmul_nd
members.signature: CpuStorage cpu_matmul_nd(const CpuStorage & a, const CpuStorage & b, const NdMatmulInfo & info, bool transA, bool transB, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Execute a batched N-D matrix multiplication on the CPU.
members.extended:
Iterates over the flattened batch dimension described by ``info`` and
dispatches one ``sgemm`` (or ``dgemm``) per batch slice via the
Accelerate BLAS wrappers in ``backend/cpu/Blas.h``.  Optional transpose
flags reuse the same kernel for the matmul backward pass:
$dA = dOut \cdot B^T$ uses ``transB = true`` and
$dB = A^T \cdot dOut$ uses ``transA = true``.

**Math**

For each batch index $\mathbf{b}$ in ``info.out_shape[:-2]``:
$$
  C[\mathbf{b}] = \mathrm{op}_A(A[\mathbf{b}]) \cdot \mathrm{op}_B(B[\mathbf{b}])
$$
where $\mathrm{op}_X(X) = X^T$ if the corresponding transpose flag is
set, else $X$.

**See Also**

backend::cpu::sgemm : Single-precision Accelerate GEMM wrapper.
backend::cpu::dgemm : Double-precision Accelerate GEMM wrapper.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand; logical shape ``info.a_bcast_shape``.  Assumed to be pre-broadcast and contiguous in row-major layout.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand; logical shape ``info.b_bcast_shape``.  Assumed to be pre-broadcast and contiguous in row-major layout.

parameters.name: transA
parameters.description: If true, treat each $A$ slice as $K \times M$ (transposed) before the GEMM call — adjusts the leading dimension accordingly.

parameters.name: transB
parameters.description: If true, treat each $B$ slice as $N \times K$ (transposed) before the GEMM call.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Element dtype; only :data:`Dtype::F32` and :data:`Dtype::F64` are supported.

returns.description: Newly allocated, aligned output buffer of shape ``info.out_shape`` (flattened, row-major).  All-zero contraction dims ($M$, $N$, or $K$ equal to 0) yield a zero-filled output.

raises.description: If ``dt`` is not one of :data:`Dtype::F32` or :data:`Dtype::F64`.

notes:
Leading dimensions are set based on the transpose flags: the
untransposed $M \times K$ slice has ``lda = K``, while a transposed
$K \times M$ view has ``lda = M``.  The output leading dimension is
always $N$ (untransposed output).

members.name: gather_nd_cpu
members.signature: void gather_nd_cpu()
members.summary: Gather elements from a source buffer at precomputed flat positions.
members.extended:
Reads ``n_out`` values from ``src_data`` at the row-major offsets in
``flat_indices`` and writes them sequentially into ``dst_data``.  This
is the inner loop of every index-select / embedding-lookup operation:
callers pre-compute the flat index map once and reuse it across the
gather plus the corresponding scatter in the backward pass.

**Math**

For every output position $i$:
$$
  \text{dst}[i] = \text{src}[\text{flat\_indices}[i]]
$$

**See Also**

scatter_add_cpu : The adjoint operation used in the backward pass.

parameters.name: src_data
parameters.description: Pointer to the contiguous source buffer.

parameters.name: dst_data
parameters.description: Pointer to the contiguous destination buffer with at least ``n_out`` writable elements.

parameters.name: flat_indices
parameters.description: Row-major flat offsets into ``src_data``; one entry per output element.  Size must equal ``n_out``.

notes:
The caller is responsible for ensuring every index is in-bounds.  The
function performs no bounds checking on release builds — only an
assertion that the index vector length matches ``n_out``.

members.name: make_nearest_scale_index
members.signature: int make_nearest_scale_index(int out_idx, int in_dim, int out_dim)
members.summary: Map an output spatial index to the corresponding nearest input index.
members.extended:
Implements the nearest-neighbor resampling rule used by spatial
upsampling / downsampling primitives: an integer division-style
rescaling that floors to the input grid and clamps at the boundary
to prevent off-by-one writes at the last pixel.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{idx} = \mathrm{clamp}\!\left(
    \left\lfloor \frac{o \cdot D_\text{in}}{D_\text{out}} \right\rfloor,
    \, 0,\, D_\text{in} - 1\right)
$$

parameters.name: in_dim
parameters.description: Length of the input spatial axis ($D_\text{in}$).

parameters.name: out_dim
parameters.description: Length of the output spatial axis ($D_\text{out}$).

returns.description: Corresponding input-space coordinate in $[0, D_\text{in} - 1]$.

notes:
Computation uses ``double`` intermediate division to avoid integer
truncation surprises near boundaries (e.g., $o \cdot D_\text{in}$
overflowing 32-bit when both factors are large).

members.name: make_zero_cpu_storage
members.signature: CpuStorage make_zero_cpu_storage(int numel, Dtype dt)
members.summary: Allocate a new zero-filled CpuStorage.
members.extended:
Convenience constructor used when building gradient accumulation
buffers: allocates an aligned byte buffer sized for ``numel`` elements
of dtype ``dt`` and clears it to zero so that subsequent scatter-add
calls accumulate against a clean slate.

**See Also**

zero_cpu_storage : In-place re-zero of an existing storage.
scatter_add_cpu  : Primary consumer of the zero-initialised buffer.

parameters.name: numel
parameters.description: Number of elements (not bytes) to allocate.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Element dtype; determines the per-element byte size via :func:`dtype_size`.

returns.description: Newly allocated, aligned, fully zero-filled storage with ``nbytes = numel * dtype_size(dt)``.

members.name: plan_nd_matmul
members.signature: NdMatmulInfo plan_nd_matmul(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Plan an N-D matmul from two operand shapes.
members.extended:
Validates that both operands are at least 2-D, that the inner
contraction dimensions agree, and that the leading batch dimensions
broadcast against each other under NumPy rules.  Returns a fully
populated :struct:`NdMatmulInfo` describing the GEMM loop bounds.

**Math**

Splits each operand into a leading batch shape and a trailing matrix
shape, then broadcasts the batch shapes:
$$
  A \in \mathbb{R}^{B_a \times M \times K}, \quad
  B \in \mathbb{R}^{B_b \times K \times N}, \quad
  B_\text{out} = \mathrm{broadcast}(B_a, B_b)
$$
so that the output has shape ``out_shape = B_out + [M, N]``.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Shape of the left operand; must satisfy ``a.size() >= 2`` with trailing dims ``[..., M, K]``.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Shape of the right operand; must satisfy ``b.size() >= 2`` with trailing dims ``[..., K, N]``.

returns.description: Plan describing the output shape, per-operand broadcast shapes, contraction dims, and total batch count.

raises.description: If either operand has rank below 2, the inner contraction dimensions disagree, or the leading batch dimensions cannot be broadcast under NumPy rules (a leading dim of 1 expands to match the other operand's value).

notes:
Either operand may have an empty batch shape — in that case the other
operand's batch dims become the output's batch dims unchanged.

members.name: scatter_add_cpu
members.signature: void scatter_add_cpu()
members.summary: Weighted scatter-add into a destination buffer at scattered positions.
members.extended:
Adjoint of :func:`gather_nd_cpu`: every value of ``src`` is multiplied
by the corresponding ``weights`` entry and added into ``dst`` at the
flat position ``flat_dst_indices[i]``.  Duplicate indices accumulate
by summation — the mathematically correct gradient transform whenever
the forward gather read the same source position more than once
(e.g., shared embedding rows, overlapping upsample windows).

**Math**

For each contribution index $i$:
$$
  \text{dst}[\,\text{flat\_dst\_indices}[i]\,]
  \mathrel{+}= \text{src}[i] \cdot \text{weights}[i]
$$
Multiple $i$ mapping to the same destination index sum together.

**See Also**

gather_nd_cpu : The forward gather whose adjoint this implements.
make_zero_cpu_storage : Convenience allocator for a pre-zeroed
    gradient buffer.

parameters.name: dst
parameters.description: Destination buffer.  Must be pre-zeroed by the caller when used to build a fresh gradient — this routine only accumulates.

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: Source values to scatter; one entry per contribution.

parameters.name: flat_dst_indices
parameters.description: Row-major flat offsets into ``dst``.  Size must be at least ``n_contrib``.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Per-contribution scalar weight (e.g., 1.0 for plain scatter, or interpolation coefficients for bilinear backwards).

parameters.name: n_contrib
parameters.description: Number of (src, index, weight) triplets to apply.

notes:
CPU-only routine — single-threaded, no atomics.  The GPU equivalent
uses MLX's native scatter primitive which performs atomic
accumulation across threads.  ``dst`` ownership remains with the
caller; this function does not allocate.

members.name: zero_cpu_storage
members.signature: void zero_cpu_storage(CpuStorage & s)
members.summary: Zero all bytes of a CpuStorage in-place.
members.extended:
Convenience wrapper around ``std::memset`` that handles the
edge cases of a null pointer or zero-sized buffer.  Used to reset
gradient accumulation buffers between mini-batches or before a
scatter-add pass.

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: Storage to zero.  No-op if ``s.ptr`` is null or ``s.nbytes == 0``.

notes:
Operates on the raw byte buffer regardless of dtype; safe for every
numeric :data:`Dtype` since IEEE-754 zero and integer zero both have
all-zero byte representations.

members.name: kron_op
members.signature: int kron_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Kronecker (block tensor) product over matching ranks.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`reshape_op` + :func:`mul_op` + :func:`reshape_op`.
Interleaves size-1 placeholders so the multiply broadcasts to a
$2 \cdot \mathrm{ndim}$-rank block tensor; the trailing reshape
collapses each ``(sa[i], sb[i])`` pair into a single ``sa[i] * sb[i]``
axis.  Gradient flows through ``ReshapeBackward`` and ``MulBackward``.

**Math**

For rank-2 operands $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and
$B \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times q}$:
$$
  (A \otimes B)_{ip + r,\, jq + s} = A_{ij} \, B_{rs}
$$
generalised elementwise to arbitrary equal ranks.

**See Also**

:func:`mul_op`, :func:`reshape_op`.

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands sharing the same rank ``ndim``.  Shapes need not match dimension-by-dimension.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``(sa[0] * sb[0], ..., sa[-1] * sb[-1])``.

raises.description: If either input is null or the ranks differ.

examples: ``kron`` of two ``(2, 3)`` matrices yields a ``(4, 9)`` matrix.

members.name: kthvalue_op
members.signature: int kthvalue_op(const int & a, int k, int dim, bool keepdim)
members.summary: Pluck the $k$-th smallest element along ``dim``.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`sort_op` + :func:`gather_op` (+ optional
:func:`squeeze_op`).  After sorting ``a`` ascending along ``dim``, we
gather the slice at position ``k - 1`` to obtain the $k$-th smallest
value.  Not differentiable — the integer-valued index breaks the chain;
gradient is conventionally treated as zero.

**Math**

Let $a^{\uparrow}$ be ``a`` sorted ascending along ``dim``.  Then
$$
  y_{\ldots} = a^{\uparrow}_{\ldots, k - 1, \ldots}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`sort_op` — produces the full sorted view.

parameters.name: k
parameters.description: 1-based rank of the value to return.  Must satisfy ``1 <= k <= a.shape[dim]``.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Axis to reduce along.  Negative values wrap modulo ``a.ndim``.

parameters.name: keepdim
parameters.description: If ``true``, retain a size-1 axis at ``dim``; otherwise the axis is squeezed off.

returns.description: Tensor with the same shape as ``a`` except size 1 (or removed) at ``dim``.  Dtype matches ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is null or ``k`` is outside ``[1, a.shape[dim]]``.
If ``dim`` is out of range.

notes:
Unlike the reference framework's API, this entry point returns only the
value — the matching index can be recovered via :func:`argsort_op` if
required.

members.name: layer_norm_op
members.signature: int layer_norm_op(const int & x, const int & gamma, const int & beta, double eps)
members.summary: Public free-function entry point for Layer Normalization.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that delegates to :func:`LayerNormBackward::forward`.
This is the symbol the pybind11 binding layer forwards to from
:func:`lucid.nn.functional.layer_norm`.

**See Also**

LayerNormBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.
rms_norm_op       : Variant that omits mean subtraction.

parameters.name: gamma
parameters.description: Per-element scale; must match the trailing dims of ``x``.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: ldl_factor_op
members.signature: int ldl_factor_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute the LDL$^\top$ factorisation of a symmetric matrix.
members.extended:
Returns the packed factor and the pivot index tensor.  Both follow the
LAPACK ``*sytrf`` storage convention so they are consumable by
``lucid.linalg.ldl_solve`` (which composes triangular solves).  Batched
inputs are supported: leading dimensions are treated as independent
matrices and the factorisation is run on each $N \times N$ slice.

**Math**

$$
  P A P^\top = L D L^\top,
$$
where $P$ is the permutation implied by the pivots, $L$ is unit
lower-triangular, and $D$ is block-diagonal with 1$\times$1 and
2$\times$2 blocks.  The 2$\times$2 blocks are necessary when no
scalar pivot can be chosen without losing numerical stability — this is
what allows the factorisation to handle indefinite $A$.

**Shape**

- ``a``           : ``(..., N, N)``.
- return ``[0]``  : ``(..., N, N)``.
- return ``[1]``  : ``(..., N)``.

**See Also**

- ``cholesky_op`` — cheaper alternative when $A$ is SPD.
- ``solve_triangular_op`` — building block for ``ldl_solve``.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Square symmetric matrix of shape ``(..., N, N)`` with dtype ``F32`` or ``F64``.  The matrix need not be positive definite; only the lower triangle is read.

returns.description: - ``[0]`` packed factor ``LD`` of shape ``(..., N, N)``, same dtype as ``a``.  Strict lower triangle stores $L$ (unit diagonal implicit); diagonal and sub-diagonal entries jointly encode $D$ including 2$\times$2 pivot blocks. - ``[1]`` pivot tensor of shape ``(..., N)``, dtype ``I32``, following the LAPACK ``ipiv`` convention (positive: 1$\times$1 pivot; pair of equal negatives: 2$\times$2 pivot block).

raises.description: If ``a`` is not at least 2-D, not square, or has a non-float dtype.
If LAPACK ``info > 0`` — $D$ is exactly singular, so $A$ is rank deficient and the factorisation cannot be completed.

notes:
- No autograd node is registered; the outputs are leaves.
- The current ``ldl_solve`` wrapper in ``lucid.linalg`` only supports
  1$\times$1 pivots (no 2$\times$2 Bunch-Kaufman block solving yet).

members.name: leaky_mask_storage
members.signature: int leaky_mask_storage(const int & s, double slope, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: LeakyReLU gradient mask: returns ``1`` where ``s >= 0`` else ``slope``.

members.name: leaky_relu_op
members.signature: int leaky_relu_op(const int & a, double slope)
members.summary: Leaky ReLU $y = x$ if $x > 0$ else $\text{slope} \cdot x$.
members.extended:

LeakyReluBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

members.name: less_equal_op
members.signature: int less_equal_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise less-than-or-equal comparison.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = (a[i] <= b[i])`` and returns the result as a Bool
tensor.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = \mathbf{1}\!\left[a[i] \leq b[i]\right].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.less_equal``.

**See Also**

less_op, greater_equal_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of identical shape, dtype, and device.

returns.description: Bool tensor with the same shape as the inputs.

raises.description: If the operand shapes, dtypes, or devices differ.

members.name: less_op
members.signature: int less_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise strict less-than comparison.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = (a[i] < b[i])`` and returns the result as a Bool
tensor.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = \mathbf{1}\!\left[a[i] < b[i]\right].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.less``.

**See Also**

less_equal_op, greater_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of identical shape, dtype, and device.

returns.description: Bool tensor with the same shape as the inputs.

raises.description: If the operand shapes, dtypes, or devices differ.

members.name: linear_op
members.signature: int linear_op(const int & x, const int & W, const int & b)
members.summary: Public free-function entry point for the linear op.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that delegates to :func:`LinearBackward::forward`.  This
is the symbol the pybind11 binding layer (``bind_nn.cpp``) and other
C++ call sites use; calling it directly is equivalent to using the
node's static forward.

**See Also**

LinearBackward : Autograd node implementing the actual forward + backward.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Bias of shape ``(N,)`` (zero-length tensor when bias is disabled).

returns.description: Output tensor of shape $(\ast, N)$.

members.name: linspace_op
members.signature: int linspace_op(double start, double stop, int num, Dtype dt, Device device, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create a 1-D tensor with ``num`` linearly spaced values from ``start`` to ``stop`` inclusive.
members.extended:
**Math**

Element $i$ equals
$\text{start} + i \cdot (\text{stop} - \text{start}) / (\text{num} - 1)$;
the final element is pinned to ``stop`` exactly to avoid floating-point
drift.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: 1-D tensor of length ``num``.

raises.description: If ``num < 0``.

members.name: log10_op
members.signature: int log10_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Base-10 logarithm: $y = \log_{10}(x) = \ln x / \ln 10$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`log_op` + :func:`div_op`.  Materialises $\ln 10$
as a same-shape constant so the divide does not need scalar broadcasting.
Gradient flows through ``LogBackward`` and ``DivBackward``.

**Math**

$$
  y = \frac{\ln x}{\ln 10}, \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{x \ln 10}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`log_op`, :func:`log1p_op`.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

notes:
Inherits the precision of the underlying ``log_op``; no specialised
base-10 kernel is used.

members.name: log1p_op
members.signature: int log1p_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Natural log of 1 + x: $y = \ln(1 + x)$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`add_op` + :func:`log_op`.  Inherits the underlying
``log_op`` precision — does **not** provide the high-accuracy small-$x$
behaviour of a dedicated ``log1p`` kernel.  Gradient flows through
``LogBackward`` and ``AddBackward``.

**Math**

$$
  y = \ln(1 + x), \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{1 + x}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`log_op`, :func:`log10_op`.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

notes:
For $|x| \ll 1$ accuracy degrades relative to ``libm``'s ``log1p`` —
catastrophic cancellation happens before the ``log`` is applied.

members.name: log2_inplace_op
members.signature: int log2_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place base-2 logarithm: writes $\log_2 x$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \log_2 a_i$$

**See Also**

:func:`log2_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to log in place.  Must be non-null.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: log2_op
members.signature: int log2_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = \log_2(x)$ element-wise.  Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and delegates to :class:`Log2Backward::forward`.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`Log2Backward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.  Must be strictly positive.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype.

members.name: log_inplace_op
members.signature: int log_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place natural logarithm: writes $\ln(x)$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \ln(a_i)$$

**See Also**

:func:`log_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to log in place.  Must be non-null.  Inputs with $a_i \le 0$ follow the backend's convention (typically NaN / $-\infty$).

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: log_op
members.signature: int log_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = \ln(x)$ element-wise.  Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and delegates to :class:`LogBackward::forward`.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`LogBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.  Must be strictly positive for finite output; non-positive values produce NaN / $-\infty$.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype.

members.name: log_softmax_op
members.signature: int log_softmax_op(const int & a, int axis)
members.summary: Numerically stable log-softmax $y = x - \mathrm{logsumexp}(x, \text{axis})$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

softmax_op : Probability-domain variant.
LogSoftmaxBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis along which to normalise.  Negative values count from the end.

returns.description: Log-softmax output with the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

notes:
Prefer ``log_softmax_op`` followed by ``nll_loss`` over
``log(softmax_op(...))`` followed by ``nll_loss`` — the former is
numerically stable for very confident logits, the latter is not.

members.name: log_storage
members.signature: int log_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise natural logarithm: returns :math:`\ln(s)`.

returns.description: New storage holding ``ln(s)`` element-wise.

members.name: logaddexp_op
members.signature: int logaddexp_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Numerically-stable $\log(\exp(a) + \exp(b))$ via the max-shift trick.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`maximum_op` + :func:`sub_op` + :func:`exp_op` +
:func:`add_op` + :func:`log_op`.  Factoring out the per-pair max keeps
the exponent's argument $\le 0$ so the underflow side bears the rounding
error rather than the overflow side.

**Math**

$$
  y = m + \ln\!\left( e^{a - m} + e^{b - m} \right),
  \qquad m = \max(a, b)
$$

**See Also**

:func:`logsumexp_op` — generalised reduction over axes.

returns.description: Tensor on the broadcast shape.

members.name: logical_and_op
members.signature: int logical_and_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Elementwise logical AND: $y_i = [a_i \ne 0] \land [b_i \ne 0]$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`not_equal_op` (to coerce each operand to a bool
mask) + :func:`bitwise_and_op`.  Not differentiable — the output is
integral and ``bitwise_*`` has no backward.

**Math**

$$
  y_i = \mathbb{1}\!\left[ a_i \ne 0 \right] \cdot
        \mathbb{1}\!\left[ b_i \ne 0 \right]
$$

**See Also**

:func:`bitwise_and_op` — bit-level variant on integer inputs.

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of broadcastable shapes and arbitrary numeric dtype.

returns.description: Bool tensor on the broadcast shape of ``a`` and ``b``.

members.name: logical_not_op
members.signature: int logical_not_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Elementwise logical NOT: $y_i = [a_i = 0]$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`equal_op` against zero — strictly cheaper than the
double-flip ``bitwise_not(not_equal(a, 0))`` pattern.  Not differentiable.

**Math**

$$
  y_i = \mathbb{1}\!\left[ a_i = 0 \right]
$$

**See Also**

:func:`logical_and_op`, :func:`logical_or_op`.

returns.description: Bool tensor of the same shape as ``a``.

members.name: logical_or_op
members.signature: int logical_or_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Elementwise logical OR: $y_i = [a_i \ne 0] \lor [b_i \ne 0]$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`not_equal_op` + :func:`bitwise_or_op`.  Not
differentiable.

**Math**

$$
  y_i = \mathbb{1}\!\left[ a_i \ne 0 \,\lor\, b_i \ne 0 \right]
$$

**See Also**

:func:`bitwise_or_op` — bit-level variant on integer inputs.

returns.description: Bool tensor on the broadcast shape.

members.name: logical_xor_op
members.signature: int logical_xor_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Elementwise logical XOR: $y_i = [a_i \ne 0] \oplus [b_i \ne 0]$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`not_equal_op` + :func:`bitwise_xor_op`.  Not
differentiable.

**Math**

$$
  y_i = \mathbb{1}\!\left[ (a_i \ne 0) \ne (b_i \ne 0) \right]
$$

**See Also**

:func:`bitwise_xor_op` — bit-level variant.

returns.description: Bool tensor on the broadcast shape.

members.name: logspace_op
members.signature: int logspace_op(double start, double stop, int num, double base, Dtype dt, Device device, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create a 1-D tensor of ``num`` values logarithmically spaced.
members.extended:
**Math**

Element $i$ equals $\text{base}^{\,t_i}$ where $t_i$ are the values
produced by ``linspace(start, stop, num)``.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: 1-D tensor of length ``num``.

members.name: logsumexp_op
members.signature: int logsumexp_op(const int & a, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Numerically-stable $\log \sum_i \exp(x_i)$ via the max-shift trick.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`max_op` + :func:`sub_op` + :func:`exp_op` +
:func:`sum_op` + :func:`log_op` + :func:`add_op` (+ :func:`squeeze_op`).
Subtracting the per-reduction-group max keeps the largest exponent
argument at $0$ so the rest underflow rather than overflow; the offset
is added back outside the log.  Gradient flows through ``MaxBackward``,
``SubBackward``, ``ExpBackward``, ``SumBackward``, ``LogBackward``, and
``AddBackward`` automatically — no dedicated backward node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  y = m + \ln \!\left( \sum_{i \in \mathrm{axes}} \exp(x_i - m) \right),
  \qquad m = \max_{i \in \mathrm{axes}} x_i
$$

**See Also**

:func:`logaddexp_op` — two-operand elementwise variant.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to reduce.  Negative values wrap modulo ``a.ndim``.  An empty list reduces over all axes per the primitive's convention.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true``, retain size-1 placeholders at the reduced positions. Otherwise, the reduced axes are squeezed off (in descending order so each remaining index stays valid through the sequential squeeze loop).

returns.description: Reduced tensor.  Dtype matches ``a``.

notes:
The intermediate :func:`max_op` is computed with ``keepdims=true`` so
the broadcast subtract is well defined; the optional axis collapse is
done at the end on the keep-dim result.

members.name: lp_normalize_op
members.signature: int lp_normalize_op(const int & x, double ord, int axis, double eps)
members.summary: Public free-function entry point for Lp normalisation.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that delegates to :func:`LpNormalizeBackward::forward`.
This is the symbol the pybind11 binding layer forwards to from
:func:`lucid.nn.functional.normalize`.

**See Also**

LpNormalizeBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis along which to compute the norm (negative allowed).

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: lstm_op
members.signature: int lstm_op(const int & input, const int & h0, const int & c0, const int & weights, const backend::IBackend::LstmOpts & opts)
members.summary: Run a single-layer LSTM forward pass and return ``{output, hn, cn}``.
members.extended:
Thin public entry point that delegates to ``LstmBackward::forward``
and is bound into Python as ``lucid._C.engine.nn.lstm_forward``.  The
Python LSTM module in :mod:`lucid.nn.modules.rnn` calls this once per
layer per direction and composes multi-layer, bidirectional and
inter-layer dropout behaviour itself.

**Math**

Per-time-step gating (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber 1997):
$$
  \begin{aligned}
    i_t &= \sigma(W_{ii}\,x_t + W_{hi}\,h_{t-1} + b_i) \\
    f_t &= \sigma(W_{if}\,x_t + W_{hf}\,h_{t-1} + b_f) \\
    g_t &= \tanh(W_{ig}\,x_t + W_{hg}\,h_{t-1} + b_g) \\
    o_t &= \sigma(W_{io}\,x_t + W_{ho}\,h_{t-1} + b_o) \\
    c_t &= f_t \odot c_{t-1} + i_t \odot g_t,\qquad h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(c_t)
  \end{aligned}
$$

**References**

Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, "Long Short-Term Memory" (Neural Computation
1997).

**See Also**

:class:`lucid.nn.LSTM` — Python multi-layer / bidirectional /
    projection wrapper that composes single-layer engine calls.
:class:`lucid.nn.LSTMCell` — single-time-step variant.

parameters.name: h0
parameters.description: Initial hidden state shaped ``(1, B, H_rec)`` (zero-filled by the caller if no explicit initial state is supplied).

parameters.name: c0
parameters.description: Initial cell state shaped ``(1, B, hidden_size)``.

parameters.name: weights
parameters.description: Layer weights ``{W_ih, W_hh, b_ih, b_hh}`` (plus optional ``W_hr`` when projection is enabled).  Gate matrices are stacked in ``[i, f, g, o]`` order along the leading axis.

returns.description: ``{output, hn, cn}`` — output sequence ``(T, B, H_out)``, final hidden state ``(1, B, H_out)``, final cell state ``(1, B, hidden_size)``.

notes:
Only ``output`` participates in autograd; ``hn`` and ``cn`` are
returned as detached tensors so the Python wrapper can feed them as
initial states to a stacked next-layer call without leaking gradient
edges between layers.

members.name: lstsq_op
members.signature: int lstsq_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Solve the linear least-squares problem $\min_X \|A X - B\|_2$.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Coefficient matrix of shape ``(..., m, n)`` with leading batch dims. Must be at least 2-D and have a floating-point dtype.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right-hand side of shape ``(..., m, k)`` (multiple RHS) or ``(..., m)`` (single RHS).  Must share dtype with ``a``.

returns.description: A one-element vector ``{solution}`` where ``solution`` has shape ``(n, k)`` when ``nrhs > 1`` and ``(n,)`` when ``nrhs == 1``.  The vector wrapping anticipates a future expansion to ``{solution, residuals, rank, singular_values}`` matching the NumPy/reference-framework signature.

raises.description: If either input is null or has a non-floating-point dtype.

examples:
>>> // Solve A x = b in the least-squares sense (tall A, single RHS):
>>> auto x_impl = lstsq_op(a_impl, b_impl)[0];
notes:
The current backend uses ``*gels`` (QR driver), which assumes $A$ is
full rank.  Rank-deficient inputs may produce silently inaccurate
solutions until ``*gelsd`` / ``*gelsy`` drivers are exposed.

members.name: lt_mask_storage
members.signature: int lt_mask_storage(const int & a, const int & b, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise less-than mask: ``(a < b) ? 1 : 0``.

parameters.name: dt
parameters.description: Output dtype the mask is cast to.

returns.description: New storage holding the gated mask.

members.name: lu_factor_op
members.signature: int lu_factor_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute the LU factorisation $PA = LU$ of a square matrix.
members.extended:
**Shape**

- Input ``a``: ``(..., n, n)``.
- Output ``LU_packed``: ``(..., n, n)``.
- Output ``pivots``: ``(..., n)`` with ``Dtype::I32``.

**References**

LAPACK ``sgetrf``/``dgetrf``; *LAPACK Users' Guide* §2.5.

**See Also**

[[lu_solve_op]] : Apply the factors to solve $Ax = b$.
[[solve_op]]    : End-to-end differentiable linear solve.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Square float tensor of shape ``(..., n, n)``.  Leading dimensions are treated as independent batch axes — one factorisation is produced per batch element.

returns.description: A two-element vector ``{LU_packed, pivots}``: - ``LU_packed`` has the same shape and dtype as ``a``; its upper triangle holds $U$ and its strict lower triangle holds the off-diagonal entries of $L$ (unit diagonal is implicit). - ``pivots`` has shape ``(..., n)`` and dtype ``I32``; entries are 1-based row indices matching the LAPACK convention.

raises.description: When ``a`` is not square, not float-typed, or the LAPACK call reports a structural failure.

members.name: lu_solve_op
members.signature: int lu_solve_op(const int & LU, const int & pivots, const int & b)
members.summary: Solve $Ax = b$ given the packed LU factors and pivot vector.
members.extended:
**Shape**

- ``LU``: ``(..., n, n)``.
- ``pivots``: ``(..., n)``.
- ``b``: ``(..., n)`` or ``(..., n, k)``.
- Output: same shape as ``b``.

**References**

LAPACK ``sgetrs``/``dgetrs``; *LAPACK Users' Guide* §2.5.1.

**See Also**

[[lu_factor_op]] : Produce the packed factors consumed here.
[[solve_op]]     : Differentiable one-shot solve (factor + apply).

parameters.name: LU
parameters.description: Packed LU matrix of shape ``(..., n, n)`` as returned by [[lu_factor_op]] — upper triangle holds $U$, strict lower triangle holds the off-diagonal entries of $L$.

parameters.name: pivots
parameters.description: 1-based pivot indices of shape ``(..., n)`` and dtype ``I32``.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right-hand side of shape ``(..., n)`` for a single RHS or ``(..., n, k)`` for $k$ simultaneous RHS columns.

returns.description: Solution tensor with the same shape and dtype as ``b``.

raises.description: When ``LU`` or ``b`` is not a float dtype, when any argument is null, or when shapes are inconsistent with the batched solve.

members.name: mT_op
members.signature: int mT_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Batch matrix-transpose — swap the last two axes only, leaving all leading batch axes intact.  Backing function for the ``.mT`` tensor property and standard for batched matmul transposes.

returns.description: View of ``a`` with the final two axes swapped.

raises.description: If ``a.ndim < 2``.

members.name: make_ones_storage
members.signature: int make_ones_storage(const int & shape, Dtype dtype, Device device)
members.summary: Allocate a ones-filled :class:`Storage` on the requested device.
members.extended:
Used by :func:`Engine::backward` as the default seed gradient when the
caller does not supply an explicit ``grad`` argument.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: N-D shape of the resulting storage.

returns.description: Storage with every element equal to one.

members.name: make_zero_storage
members.signature: int make_zero_storage(const int & shape, Dtype dtype, Device device)
members.summary: Allocate a zero-filled :class:`Storage` on the requested device.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: N-D shape of the resulting storage.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Target device (``CPU`` uses Accelerate; ``GPU`` allocates an MLX array).

returns.description: Newly allocated storage with every element equal to zero.

members.name: masked_fill_op
members.signature: int masked_fill_op(const int & a, const int & mask, double value)
members.summary: Replace masked positions with a scalar value.
members.extended:
Returns a tensor equal to ``a`` except at positions where ``mask`` is
true, which are filled with ``value``.

parameters.name: mask
parameters.description: Boolean mask of the same shape as ``a``.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Fill value (cast to the dtype of ``a``).

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

notes:
Backward routes ``grad_out`` through positions where ``mask`` is false
(the values that survived the fill); positions where ``mask`` is true
receive zero gradient.  Neither ``mask`` nor ``value`` is differentiable.

members.name: masked_select_op
members.signature: int masked_select_op(const int & a, const int & mask)
members.summary: Extract elements where a boolean mask is true.
members.extended:
Returns a 1-D tensor containing the elements of ``a`` at the positions
where ``mask`` is true, in row-major (C) order.  The output length is
data-dependent.

returns.description: 1-D tensor of length equal to the number of true entries in ``mask``; dtype matches ``a``.

notes:
Because the output size depends on the values of ``mask``, this op
performs a CPU round-trip on the GPU stream to materialise the count
before allocation.  Non-differentiable in ``mask``; the gradient
w.r.t. ``a`` is a scatter into a zero tensor at the selected
positions.

members.name: matmul_op
members.signature: int matmul_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Compute $A B$ with full NumPy-style batch broadcasting and autograd.
members.extended:
Thin public wrapper that delegates to :func:`MatmulBackward::forward`.

**Math**

$$
  C[\ldots, i, j] = \sum_{k=0}^{K-1} A[\ldots, i, k]\, B[\ldots, k, j]
$$

**See Also**

dot_op : Rank-1 / rank-2 specialised dispatch.
MatmulBackward : Autograd node implementing the backward pass.

returns.description: Result tensor $C = A B$ with broadcast batch shape and trailing $(M, N)$.

examples:
// C++: 2-D × 2-D plain GEMM.
    auto C = matmul_op(A, B);  // A: [M,K], B: [K,N]  → C: [M,N]
notes:
FLOPs per output element ≈ $2K$; total $\approx 2 \cdot B \cdot M \cdot
N \cdot K$ where $B$ is the broadcast batch size.

members.name: matrix_power_op
members.signature: int matrix_power_op(const int & a, int n)
members.summary: Compute $A^n$ for a square float matrix and integer exponent.
members.extended:
Validates that ``a`` is at least 2-D, square in the trailing two
axes, and float-typed; dispatches to the backend.  The output
shape always matches ``a``'s shape (matrix powers preserve shape).
Autograd is not wired — the result is a leaf in the gradient
graph.

**Math**

$$
  A^n = \begin{cases}
    I & n = 0, \\
    \underbrace{A \cdot A \cdots A}_{n\;\text{times}} & n > 0, \\
    (A^{-1})^{|n|} & n < 0.
  \end{cases}
$$
Repeated squaring decomposes $n = \sum_i b_i 2^i$ (its binary
representation) and accumulates the result in
$O(\log |n|)$ matrix multiplies.

**Shape**

- ``a``: ``(..., N, N)``.
- Output: same shape as ``a``.

**References**

Knuth, *The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 2* §4.6.3
(right-to-left binary exponentiation).
Higham, *Functions of Matrices* (2008), §1.2.

**See Also**

inv_op : Used internally when ``n < 0``.
matmul_op : Single-step underlying primitive.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input matrix of shape ``(..., N, N)``.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: Integer exponent.  Named ``n`` for parity with the reference framework's ``matrix_power(a, n)`` signature; the algorithm refers to it as $p$ internally.  May be zero, positive, or negative; negative values require $A$ to be invertible.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``(..., N, N)`` holding $A^n$ (and the identity matrix if ``n == 0``).

raises.description: If ``a`` is not square, fewer than 2-D, or non-float.
(At backend level, when ``n < 0``.) If $A$ is singular.

notes:
- No backward is registered; ``matrix_power`` outputs are leaves.
- For graph neural networks $A$ is often an adjacency or
  normalised Laplacian — large ``n`` is fine because cost grows
  only logarithmically.

members.name: max_op
members.signature: int max_op(const int & a, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Reduce ``a`` by maximum along ``axes``.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`MaxBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to reduce.  Negative indices wrap.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true``, retains reduced dims as size-1.

returns.description: Output tensor of the reduced shape.

members.name: max_pool1d_op
members.signature: int max_pool1d_op(const int & x, int KL, int stride_l, int pad_l)
members.summary: One-dimensional max pooling over a batch of sequences.
members.extended:
Emits the maximum of each sliding window of length ``KL`` along the
last axis of the input.

parameters.name: stride_l
parameters.description: Stride.  ``0`` is a sentinel meaning "use ``KL``" (non-overlapping windows).  Default: ``0``.

parameters.name: pad_l
parameters.description: Zero-padding added to both ends.  Default: ``0``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C, L_out)`` where $L_\text{out} = \lfloor (L + 2 p - KL)/s + 1 \rfloor$.

members.name: max_pool2d_op
members.signature: int max_pool2d_op(const int & x, int KH, int KW, int stride_h, int stride_w, int pad_h, int pad_w)
members.summary: Two-dimensional max pooling over a batch of feature maps.
members.extended:
Emits the maximum of each $KH \times KW$ sliding window.  Provides
local translation invariance and is the canonical downsampling
primitive in convolutional classification networks.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape ``(B, C, H, W)``.

parameters.name: stride_h, stride_w
parameters.description: Per-axis stride.  ``0`` is a sentinel meaning "use ``K``". Default: ``0``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C, H_out, W_out)`` with $H_\text{out} = \lfloor (H + 2 p_h - KH)/s_h + 1 \rfloor$ and analogously for $W_\text{out}$.

members.name: max_pool3d_op
members.signature: int max_pool3d_op(const int & x, int KD, int KH, int KW, int stride_d, int stride_h, int stride_w, int pad_d, int pad_h, int pad_w)
members.summary: Three-dimensional max pooling over a batch of volumes.
members.extended:
Emits the maximum of each $KD \times KH \times KW$ window.  Used in
video classification and volumetric (CT / MRI) models.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape ``(B, C, D, H, W)``.

parameters.name: stride_d, stride_h, stride_w
parameters.description: Per-axis stride.  ``0`` is a sentinel for non-overlapping windows.  Default: ``0``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``(B, C, D_out, H_out, W_out)``.

members.name: maximum_inplace_op
members.signature: int maximum_inplace_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: In-place elementwise maximum: writes $\max(a, b)$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \max(a_i, b_i)$$

**See Also**

:func:`maximum_op` — out-of-place counterpart.
:func:`minimum_inplace_op` — elementwise minimum sibling.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand and in-place target.  Must be non-null and must not share storage with a view.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand.  Must be non-null and broadcastable into ``a->shape()``.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``, now holding the elementwise maxima.

raises.description: If the broadcast output would change ``a``'s shape.
If ``a`` shares storage with a view.

members.name: maximum_op
members.signature: int maximum_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Compute the elementwise maximum $y = \max(a, b)$ with broadcasting and autograd support.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``broadcast(a.shape, b.shape)`` holding the elementwise maximum.

examples: >>> auto y = maximum_op(a, b);  // y[i] = max(a[i], b[i]) with broadcasting
notes:
At ties ($a_i = b_i$) the gradient is routed entirely to ``a``.  When
grad-tracking is on, registers a :class:`MaximumBackward` node which saves
both inputs.

members.name: mean_op
members.signature: int mean_op(const int & a, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Reduce ``a`` by averaging along ``axes``.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`MeanBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to reduce.  Empty means "all axes".  Negative indices wrap.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true``, retains reduced dims as size-1.

returns.description: Output tensor of the broadcast-compatible reduced shape.

members.name: meshgrid_op
members.signature: int meshgrid_op(const int & xs, bool indexing_xy)
members.summary: Build N broadcast coordinate grids from N 1-D input tensors.
members.extended:
Given inputs $x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{N-1}$ with lengths
$L_0, L_1, \ldots, L_{N-1}$, produces N output tensors all of the same
rank-N shape; the $i$-th output broadcasts $x_i$ across every axis
except its "carry" axis, where it varies.  This is the
multi-dimensional analogue of NumPy's ``np.meshgrid``.

**Math**

For ``ij`` indexing:
$$\mathrm{out}_i[k_0, k_1, \ldots, k_{N-1}] = x_i[k_i]$$

**See Also**

Concat : Stack the resulting grids along a new leading axis.

parameters.name: xs
parameters.description: One or more 1-D input tensors.  All must share the same dtype and device.

parameters.name: indexing_xy
parameters.description: Axis convention for the first two inputs: * ``false`` — ``"ij"`` (matrix) indexing.  Output ``i`` varies along axis ``i``; output shape is ``(L_0, L_1, ..., L_{N-1})``. * ``true`` — ``"xy"`` (Cartesian) indexing.  Output 0 varies along axis 1 (x) and output 1 varies along axis 0 (y); outputs ``i >= 2`` follow the ``ij`` convention.  Output shape is ``(L_1, L_0, L_2, ..., L_{N-1})``.

returns.description: N coordinate grids, one per input, each of rank N.

examples:
>>> X, Y = meshgrid([x, y], indexing_xy=true)  // 'xy' convention
>>> X.shape == (len(y), len(x))
True
notes:
Backward sums each output gradient over every axis except the carry
axis to recover the gradient w.r.t. the corresponding 1-D input.  All
inputs must be 1-D; mismatched ranks raise.

members.name: min_op
members.signature: int min_op(const int & a, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Reduce ``a`` by minimum along ``axes``.

returns.description: Output tensor of the reduced shape.

members.name: minimum_inplace_op
members.signature: int minimum_inplace_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: In-place elementwise minimum: writes $\min(a, b)$ back into ``a``.

**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \min(a_i, b_i)$$

**See Also**

:func:`minimum_op` — out-of-place counterpart.
:func:`maximum_inplace_op` — elementwise maximum sibling.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand and in-place target.  Must be non-null and must not share storage with a view.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand.  Must be non-null and broadcastable into ``a->shape()``.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``, now holding the elementwise minima.

raises.description: If the broadcast output would change ``a``'s shape.
If ``a`` shares storage with a view.

members.name: minimum_op
members.signature: int minimum_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Compute the elementwise minimum $y = \min(a, b)$ with broadcasting and autograd support.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``broadcast(a.shape, b.shape)`` holding the elementwise minimum.

examples: >>> auto y = minimum_op(a, b);  // y[i] = min(a[i], b[i]) with broadcasting
notes:
At ties ($a_i = b_i$) the gradient is routed entirely to ``a``.  When
grad-tracking is on, registers a :class:`MinimumBackward` node which saves
