members.name: mish_op
members.signature: int mish_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Mish activation $y = x \cdot \tanh(\mathrm{softplus}(x))$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

MishBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

members.name: mm_op
members.signature: int mm_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Strict 2-D matrix multiply: $C = A \cdot B$.
members.extended:
Composite — rank-checked alias of :func:`matmul_op` that rejects inputs
of rank $\ne 2$ up front so the error message names ``mm`` rather than
the planner.  Gradient flows through ``MatmulBackward``.

**Math**

$$
  C_{ij} = \sum_{k} A_{ik} \, B_{kj}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`matmul_op` — generalised version with broadcasting and 1-D
vector promotion; :func:`bmm_op` — strict batched variant.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``(M, N)``.

raises.description: If either input is null or not exactly 2-D.

members.name: movedim_op
members.signature: int movedim_op(const int & a, const int & source, const int & destination)
members.summary: Move dimensions from ``source`` to ``destination`` positions.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`permute_op`.  Builds an explicit permutation in
which every axis listed in ``source`` is placed at the matching index in
``destination``; axes not listed preserve their original relative order
and fill the remaining slots left to right.  No new backward node —
gradient flows through ``PermuteBackward``.

**Math**

If $\pi$ is the constructed permutation of $[0, n)$, then
$$
  y_{i_0, \ldots, i_{n-1}} = a_{i_{\pi(0)}, \ldots, i_{\pi(n-1)}}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`permute_op` — underlying primitive.

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: Axes of ``a`` to move.  Negative values wrap modulo ``a.ndim``. Must be unique.

parameters.name: destination
parameters.description: Target positions for the moved axes, parallel to ``source``. Negative values wrap.  Must be unique and have the same length as ``source``.

returns.description: A view (or contiguous copy, per the primitive's policy) of ``a`` with the requested axis order.

raises.description: If ``a`` is null, the lists differ in length, or either list contains duplicates.
If any axis is out of range.

examples:
``movedim_op(a, {0}, {-1})`` moves the leading axis to the trailing
position — equivalent to a cyclic left rotation of axes.

members.name: mse_loss_op
members.signature: int mse_loss_op(const int & input, const int & target, int reduction)
members.summary: Public mean-squared-error entry point.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that casts ``reduction`` to ``Reduction`` and delegates to
``MseLossBackward::forward``.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: Ground-truth tensor of the same shape and dtype.

returns.description: Scalar (Mean / Sum) or per-element (None) loss.

members.name: mul_inplace_op
members.signature: int mul_inplace_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: In-place elementwise multiplication: writes $a \cdot b$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow a_i \cdot b_i$$

**See Also**

:func:`mul_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand and in-place target.  Must be non-null and must not share storage with a view.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand.  Must be non-null and broadcastable into ``a->shape()``.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``, now holding the product storage.

raises.description: If the broadcast output would change ``a``'s shape.
If ``a`` shares storage with a view.

notes:
Common usage: scaling gradients (``grad_.mul_(scale)``) or applying
element-wise masks (``out_.mul_(mask)``) without allocating a new
buffer per step.

members.name: mul_op
members.signature: int mul_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Public entry point — element-wise multiplication with autograd support.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$ y = a \cdot b $$

**See Also**

add_op, sub_op, div_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of any rank.  Shapes must be NumPy-broadcast-compatible.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape ``broadcast_shapes(a.shape, b.shape)`` and promoted dtype.  Participates in autograd via :class:`MulBackward`.

raises.description: If ``a`` and ``b`` have incompatible shapes under NumPy broadcasting rules, or if their devices differ.

examples: >>> auto c = mul_op(a, b);    // c = a * b (Hadamard / elementwise)

members.name: mul_scalar_storage
members.signature: int mul_scalar_storage(const int & s, double scalar, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Broadcast-multiply every element by a scalar: returns ``s * scalar``.

returns.description: New storage holding ``s * scalar`` element-wise.

members.name: multiply_storages
members.signature: int multiply_storages(const int & a, const int & b, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise multiplication: returns ``a * b``.

returns.description: New storage holding the element-wise product.

members.name: nan_to_num_op
members.signature: int nan_to_num_op(const int & a, double nan_val, double posinf_val, double neginf_val)
members.summary: Replace ``NaN`` and $\pm\infty$ values with user-specified finite substitutes, preserving dtype and shape.
members.extended:
Not differentiable: returns a detached tensor with no backward node.
Use only in inference / data-cleaning paths.

**Math**

$$y_i = \begin{cases}
  \text{nan\_val}    & x_i \text{ is NaN} \\
  \text{posinf\_val} & x_i = +\infty \\
  \text{neginf\_val} & x_i = -\infty \\
  x_i                & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`isnan_op`, :func:`isinf_op`, :func:`isfinite_op`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any floating-point dtype.  Integer inputs are passed through unchanged (no non-finite values to replace).

parameters.name: posinf_val
parameters.description: Replacement for $+\infty$ positions.  Default is the maximum finite IEEE-754 ``float32`` value (~``3.4028e+38``).

parameters.name: neginf_val
parameters.description: Replacement for $-\infty$ positions.  Default is the minimum finite IEEE-754 ``float32`` value (~``-3.4028e+38``).

returns.description: New tensor with the same shape, dtype, and device as ``a``; non-finite slots are replaced according to the three scalars.

notes:
The default ``posinf_val`` / ``neginf_val`` clamp to ``float32`` range
even when ``a`` is ``float64``.  Pass ``std::numeric_limits<double>::max``
explicitly to preserve double-precision extremes.

members.name: narrow_op
members.signature: int narrow_op(const int & a, int dim, int start, int length)
members.summary: Slice a contiguous window ``[start, start + length)`` along ``dim``.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`split_at_op`.  Returns the input unchanged when
the window covers the full axis (zero-cost fast path).  Gradient flows
through ``SplitSliceBackward``.

**Math**

$$
  y_{\ldots, j, \ldots} = a_{\ldots, j + \mathrm{start}, \ldots},
  \qquad 0 \le j < \mathrm{length}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`split_at_op` — underlying primitive.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Axis to slice along.  Negative values wrap modulo ``a.ndim``.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: Inclusive start index along ``dim``.  Must be non-negative.

parameters.name: length
parameters.description: Window length.  Must satisfy ``start + length <= a.shape[dim]``.

returns.description: Tensor with the same shape as ``a`` except size ``length`` along ``dim``.  When the window covers the full axis, the input is returned by identity.

raises.description: If ``dim`` is out of range or the window falls outside ``a``.

members.name: neg_inplace_op
members.signature: int neg_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place element-wise negation: writes $-x$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
Equivalent to ``a = neg_op(a)`` but reuses ``a``'s storage and bumps
its version counter.  Useful for negating large activations without
allocating a second buffer.

**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow -a_i$$

**See Also**

:func:`neg_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to negate in place.  Must be non-null.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``, now holding the negated storage.

raises.description: If :func:`neg_op` returns a tensor whose shape differs from ``a->shape()`` (should never happen under the standard schema).

members.name: neg_op
members.signature: int neg_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = -x$ element-wise.
members.extended:
Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and routes
the forward pass through :class:`NegBackward`, which also installs the
autograd edge.

**See Also**

:class:`NegBackward` — backward node.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype.

members.name: negate_storage
members.signature: int negate_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise negation: returns ``-s``.

parameters.name: numel
parameters.description: Number of elements (used to build a 1-D Shape for the dispatcher).

returns.description: New storage holding ``-s``.

members.name: next_sequence_nr
members.signature: int next_sequence_nr()
members.summary: Return the next globally-unique node sequence number and advance the shared counter.

returns.description: A value strictly greater than any previously returned.

notes:
Thread-safe via relaxed atomic increment.  Relaxed ordering is sufficient
because the engine only requires that any two nodes' sequence numbers be
distinct and totally ordered — not that the ordering match the global
memory order across threads.

members.name: nextafter_op
members.signature: int nextafter_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Return the next representable floating-point value after ``a`` toward ``b``.
members.extended:
Computes element-wise IEEE-754 ``nextafter``: for each element pair
``(a_k, b_k)`` returns the float bit-adjacent to ``a_k`` in the
direction of ``b_k``.  If ``a_k == b_k`` the result is ``b_k``.  NaN
propagates.

**Math**

$$\mathrm{out}_k = \begin{cases}
    a_k & \text{if } a_k = b_k \\
    \mathrm{succ}(a_k) & \text{if } a_k < b_k \\
    \mathrm{pred}(a_k) & \text{if } a_k > b_k
\end{cases}$$
where $\mathrm{succ}$ / $\mathrm{pred}$ are the IEEE-754 next- / previous-
representable functions.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Source values.  Floating-point dtype (F32 or F64).

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Direction values.  Must match ``a`` in dtype and broadcast-compatible shape.

returns.description: Tensor of the same dtype and broadcast shape as the inputs.

raises.description: If ``a`` and ``b`` differ in dtype, or the dtype is not F32 / F64.

notes:
Non-differentiable: the output is piecewise constant in ``a`` (the step
is always one ULP regardless of magnitude).  No backward is registered.

On the GPU stream, the F32 fast path operates entirely on int32 views of
the floating bits (sign-magnitude adjusted to two's complement) and avoids
a CPU round-trip.  F64 inputs always traverse the CPU stream because MLX
does not provide F64 buffers.

members.name: nll_loss_op
members.signature: int nll_loss_op(const int & input, const int & target, const int & weight_or_null, int reduction, int ignore_index)
members.summary: Public negative log-likelihood entry point.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Log-probabilities of shape ``(N, C)`` or ``(N, C, d_1, ...)``.

parameters.name: weight_or_null
parameters.description: Optional per-class weight vector of length $C$; pass null when unused.

parameters.name: ignore_index
parameters.description: Class index excluded from the loss / gradient.

returns.description: Reduced or per-sample loss tensor.

members.name: nonzero_op
members.signature: int nonzero_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Coordinates of all non-zero elements.
members.extended:
Returns a 2-D tensor of shape ``(count, ndim)`` listing the multi-axis
coordinates of every non-zero element of ``a`` in row-major order.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any rank and dtype.

returns.description: Integer tensor of shape ``(count, a.ndim)`` and dtype I64. ``count`` is the number of non-zero entries.

notes:
The output shape depends on the input values, so this op always
materialises the result on the CPU stream and copies it to the
requested device on demand.  Non-differentiable.

members.name: norm_op
members.signature: int norm_op(const int & a, double ord, int axis, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Compute the p-norm of a float tensor over the specified axes.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$
  \|A\|_p = \Bigl(\sum_i |a_i|^p\Bigr)^{1/p}.
$$
For $p = 2$ on a matrix this is the Frobenius norm; for $p = 1$ on
a matrix this is the entrywise L1 norm (not the operator 1-norm).

**Shape**

- ``a``: arbitrary float shape.
- Output:
    * ``axis`` empty, ``keepdims=false`` $\to$ scalar (rank 0).
    * ``axis`` empty, ``keepdims=true`` $\to$ all-ones shape of
      the same rank as ``a``.
    * Otherwise: rank of ``a`` minus the number of reduced axes
      (``keepdims=false``) or rank preserved with those axes set
      to 1 (``keepdims=true``).

**See Also**

SVD.h : Underpins operator norms (spectral, nuclear) once exposed.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor (any float dtype, any shape).

parameters.name: ord
parameters.description: Norm order.  ``2.0`` selects the Frobenius / Euclidean norm $\|A\|_F = \sqrt{\sum a_{ij}^2}$; ``1.0`` selects the entrywise L1 norm $\|A\|_1 = \sum |a_{ij}|$.  Other values may be forwarded to the backend in the forward pass but have no registered backward.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axes to reduce.  Empty means a global reduction over every element; negative indices are accepted and normalised.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true`` the reduced axes are retained as size-1 stubs in the output; otherwise they are removed.

returns.description: Reduced norm tensor.

raises.description: If ``a`` is non-float.
(At backward time.) If ``ord`` is not 1.0 or 2.0 and a gradient is requested.

notes:
Operator norms (spectral, nuclear, induced 1-/inf-norm) are not yet
dispatched here — they would require SVD and a separate backward.
This op currently covers only the entrywise reductions.

members.name: normal_op
members.signature: int normal_op(const int & shape, double mean, double std, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator * gen)
members.summary: Allocates a tensor filled with Gaussian samples $\mathcal{N}(\mathrm{mean}, \mathrm{std}^2)$.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathrm{mean}, \mathrm{std}^2)$$

**See Also**

:func:`randn_op` — shortcut for $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.

parameters.name: std
parameters.description: Scale parameter $\sigma$.  Must be non-negative; a value of ``0`` produces a constant tensor equal to ``mean``.

returns.description: Newly allocated leaf tensor (``requires_grad = false``).

raises.description: If ``std < 0``.

members.name: normalize_axes
members.signature: int normalize_axes(const int & axes, int ndim)
members.summary: Canonicalise a list of axis indices for an ``ndim``-rank tensor.
members.extended:
Wraps each negative axis to its positive counterpart, deduplicates the
result, sorts ascending, and returns the canonical list.  When ``axes``
is empty the function treats the call as "reduce over everything" and
returns ``[0, ndim)``.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Raw axis list; may contain negatives and duplicates.

parameters.name: ndim
parameters.description: Rank of the tensor the axes refer to.

returns.description: Sorted, deduplicated, all-positive axis list.

raises.description: Some entry of ``axes`` falls outside ``[-ndim, ndim)``.

members.name: not_equal_op
members.signature: int not_equal_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Element-wise inequality comparison.
members.extended:
Computes ``out[i] = (a[i] != b[i])`` and returns the result as a Bool
tensor.  Logical complement of ``equal_op``.

Non-differentiable: no autograd node is registered.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{out}[i] = \mathbf{1}\!\left[a[i] \neq b[i]\right].
$$

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.not_equal``.

**See Also**

equal_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of identical shape, dtype, and device.

returns.description: Bool tensor with the same shape as the inputs.

raises.description: If the operand shapes, dtypes, or devices differ.

members.name: one_hot_op
members.signature: int one_hot_op(const int & input, int num_classes, Dtype out_dtype)
members.summary: Encode integer indices into one-hot float vectors.
members.extended:
Returns a tensor of shape ``(*input.shape(), num_classes)`` where the last
axis is the one-hot indicator
$$
  y_{\ldots, c} = \mathbf{1}\bigl[c = \text{input}_{\ldots}\bigr], \quad
  c \in \{0, 1, \ldots, \text{num\_classes} - 1\}.
$$
The output dtype is ``out_dtype`` (typically a floating-point type so the
result can feed directly into a loss function).  Indices outside the
$[0, \text{num\_classes})$ range produce all-zero rows.  No autograd node
is attached — this op is inference-only.

returns.description: One-hot tensor of shape ``(*input.shape(), num_classes)`` and dtype ``out_dtype``.

members.name: ones_like_op
members.signature: int ones_like_op(const int & a, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create a one-filled tensor matching ``a`` in shape, dtype, and device.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: New leaf tensor with the same shape/dtype/device as ``a``.

members.name: ones_op
members.signature: int ones_op(const int & shape, Dtype dt, Device device, bool requires_grad)
members.summary: Create a tensor of the given shape filled with ones.

parameters.name: requires_grad
parameters.description: Attach a fresh autograd leaf when true.

returns.description: Leaf tensor of the specified shape filled with $1$.

members.name: outer_op
members.signature: int outer_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Compute the outer (tensor) product of two 1-D vectors, yielding a 2-D matrix.
members.extended:
The implementation evaluates the outer product as a degenerate GEMM
between reshaped operands: ``a[:, None] @ b[None, :]`` — i.e. an
$(M, 1) \times (1, N)$ matmul producing an $(M, N)$ result.  On
Accelerate this maps onto ``cblas_sger`` (rank-1 update) or
``cblas_*gemm``; on the GPU stream it routes through MLX's matmul
primitive.  Autograd is wired through the package-private
``OuterBackward`` node, which saves both operands.

**Math**

Forward:
$$
  C_{ij} = a_i\, b_j
$$
Backward (with $G = \partial L/\partial C$):
$$
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial a_i} = \sum_j G_{ij}\, b_j
    = (G\, b)_i, \qquad
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial b_j} = \sum_i G_{ij}\, a_i
    = (a^\top G)_j
$$

**Shape**

- ``a.shape == {M}`` and ``b.shape == {N}``.
- Output shape ``{M, N}``.

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.outer`` (which Lucid mirrors for the 1-D case).

**See Also**

dot_op : 1-D × 1-D inner product (the "dual" of outer).
inner_op : Last-axis contraction.
matmul_op : Generalised matmul with batch broadcasting.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand.  Must be 1-D with length $M$.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand.  Must be 1-D with length $N$.

returns.description: Output matrix of shape $(M, N)$ with $\mathrm{out}[i, j] = a_i\, b_j$.

raises.description: If either input has rank other than 1.

notes:
Schema uses ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput`` — outer products on integer
vectors are valid and must not be promoted.

members.name: overwrite_state_storage
members.signature: void overwrite_state_storage(int & dst, const int & src)
members.summary: Overwrite an existing state buffer in place from a saved snapshot.
members.extended:
Used by ``Optimizer::load_state_buffers`` to restore a checkpoint
into the optimizer's live state without reallocating or rebinding
any storage objects.  ``dst`` keeps its identity (pointer, shape,
dtype, device) — only the underlying bytes / MLX array are replaced.

**See Also**

clone_state_storage : produces the snapshots consumed here.

parameters.name: dst
parameters.description: Live optimizer state buffer to update in place.

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: Snapshot whose contents should be written into ``dst``.  Must be on the same device as ``dst`` and have the same byte size.

raises.description: If ``dst`` and ``src`` are on different devices, or if their CPU byte sizes disagree.

members.name: pad_op
members.signature: int pad_op(const int & a, int)
members.summary: Pad a tensor on each side of each dimension with a constant fill value.
members.extended:
``pad_width`` is a per-dimension list of ``(before, after)`` widths whose
length must equal ``a.rank()``.  The output preserves the input's dtype and
device; newly added elements are filled with ``constant``.

**Math**

$$ y_{[i_0, \ldots, i_{n-1}]} =
       \begin{cases}
           x_{[i_0 - b_0, \ldots, i_{n-1} - b_{n-1}]}
               & \text{if } b_d \le i_d < b_d + D_d \text{ for all } d, \\
           c   & \text{otherwise},
       \end{cases} $$
where $b_d$ is the leading pad width, $D_d$ is the input size at axis
$d$, and $c$ is ``constant``.

**Shape**

For each dimension $d$:
$$ \text{out.shape}[d] = \text{a.shape}[d] + \text{pad\_width}[d].\text{first}
                         + \text{pad\_width}[d].\text{second}. $$

**See Also**

:func:`concatenate_op` — pad is conceptually concat with constant tensors.

parameters.name: pad_width
parameters.description: One ``(before, after)`` pair per dimension of ``a``.  Both widths must be non-negative.

parameters.name: constant
parameters.description: Fill value for the padded region.  Cast to the input's dtype before storing.

returns.description: Padded tensor with shape ``out.shape[d] = a.shape[d] + before_d + after_d``.

raises.description: If ``pad_width.size() != a.rank()`` or any width is negative.

examples:
Padding a ``(2, 2)`` tensor with ``pad_width = {(1, 0), (0, 2)}`` and
``constant = 0`` yields a ``(3, 4)`` output whose first row and last two
columns are zeros.
notes:
Backward is implemented in the .cpp via ``PadBackward`` (schema name
``"pad"``, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``).  It slices the gradient sequentially
per dimension, discarding ``pad_width[d].first`` leading and
``pad_width[d].second`` trailing elements to recover the original input
region.  Only the constant-fill mode is supported here; reflect / replicate
modes live in higher-level Python wrappers.

members.name: permute_op
members.signature: int permute_op(const int & a, const int & perm)
members.summary: Permute all axes of ``a`` according to ``perm``.
members.extended:
**See Also**

transpose_op : Full-reversal permutation.
swapaxes_op : Swap two specific axes.
PermuteBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

parameters.name: perm
parameters.description: Permutation of ``0..ndim-1``.  Negative indices count from the end.

returns.description: Output tensor sharing storage with ``a`` but with permuted shape and stride (a view; no data copy).

raises.description: If ``perm`` is not a valid permutation of ``0..ndim-1``.
If any element of ``perm`` is outside ``[-ndim, ndim)``.

members.name: pinv_op
members.signature: int pinv_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse $A^+$ of a rectangular matrix.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input matrix of shape ``(..., m, n)`` with leading batch dims.  Need not be square or full rank.  Must be at least 2-D and have a floating-point dtype.

returns.description: The pseudoinverse $A^+$ of shape ``(..., n, m)`` — the last two dimensions are swapped relative to the input.

raises.description: If ``a`` is null, has a non-floating-point dtype, or has fewer than two dimensions.

examples:
>>> // Solve a tall least-squares system via the pseudoinverse:
>>> auto x_impl = matmul_op(pinv_op(a_impl), b_impl);
notes:
Internally a full reduced SVD is computed; if the caller already has the
SVD on hand, forming $A^+ = V \Sigma^+ U^\top$ manually avoids the
redundant factorisation.

members.name: positive_mask_storage
members.signature: int positive_mask_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Mask elements that are strictly positive: ``(s > 0) ? 1 : 0``.
members.extended:
Used by the ReLU backward pass to gate the gradient through positive
activations.

parameters.name: s
parameters.description: Input buffer (typically the ReLU's saved input).

returns.description: New storage holding the {0, 1} gate.

members.name: pow_inplace_op
members.signature: int pow_inplace_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: In-place elementwise exponentiation: writes $a^b$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow a_i^{b_i}$$

**See Also**

:func:`pow_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Base / in-place target.  Must be non-null and must not share storage with a view.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Exponent.  Must be non-null and broadcastable into ``a->shape()``.  Domain restrictions (e.g. $0^0$, negative base with fractional exponent) follow the backend's convention.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``, now holding the powered storage.

raises.description: If the broadcast output would change ``a``'s shape.
If ``a`` shares storage with a view.

members.name: pow_op
members.signature: int pow_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Compute the elementwise tensor-tensor power $y = a^b$ with broadcasting and autograd support.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``broadcast(a.shape, b.shape)`` holding $a^b$.

examples: >>> auto y = pow_op(a, b);  // y[i] = a[i] ** b[i] with broadcasting
notes:
Always computed in FP32 (see :class:`PowBackward` schema).  When grad-tracking
is on, registers a :class:`PowBackward` node which saves both inputs.

members.name: pow_scalar_op
members.signature: int pow_scalar_op(const int & a, double exp)
members.summary: Element-wise scalar-exponent power — returns $a^{\text{exp}}$ with a fully wired autograd node.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$y_i = x_i^{\text{exp}}, \qquad
\frac{\partial y_i}{\partial x_i} = \text{exp} \cdot x_i^{\text{exp} - 1}$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`rpow_scalar_op`, :class:`PowScalarBackward`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor (promoted to FP32 by the op schema).

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``; gradient is $\text{exp} \cdot x^{\text{exp}-1} \cdot g$.

members.name: pow_storage
members.signature: int pow_storage(const int & base, const int & expo, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise power: returns ``base ** expo``.

parameters.name: base, expo
parameters.description: Base and exponent buffers.  Both must have ``numel`` elements.

returns.description: New storage holding :math:`\text{base}^{\text{expo}}`.

members.name: prod_op
members.signature: int prod_op(const int & a, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Reduce ``a`` by multiplication along ``axes``.
members.extended:
Promotes narrow integer dtypes to ``int64`` (same headroom logic as
:func:`sum_op`).

**See Also**

:class:`ProdBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to reduce.  Negative indices wrap.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true``, retains reduced dims as size-1.

returns.description: Output tensor.  Empty reductions yield $1$.

members.name: qr_op
members.signature: int qr_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute the reduced QR factorisation of a float matrix.
members.extended:
Forward calls LAPACK ``*geqrf`` + ``*orgqr`` on the CPU stream or
``mlx::core::linalg::qr`` on the GPU stream and returns the two
factors as a length-2 vector ``{Q, R}``.  Autograd is not wired —
both outputs are leaves in the gradient graph.

**Math**

$$
  A = Q R, \qquad Q^\top Q = I_k, \qquad R = \mathrm{triu}(R).
$$

**Shape**

- ``a``: ``(..., m, n)``.
- ``Q``: ``(..., m, k)``, ``R``: ``(..., k, n)`` with
  $k = \min(m, n)$.

**References**

Golub & Van Loan, *Matrix Computations* (4th ed.), Algorithm 5.2.1
(Householder QR).
Walter & Lehmann, "Algorithmic Differentiation of Linear Algebra
Functions" (2010), §3.
Bettale, "Differentiating the QR Decomposition" (2013).

**See Also**

inv_op : Matrix inverse, often composed with QR for least squares.
Lstsq.h : Direct least-squares solver built on top of QR.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input matrix of shape ``(..., m, n)``.  Must be at least 2-D and have a floating-point dtype.  Leading dimensions are treated as independent batch dimensions.

returns.description: Length-2 vector ``{Q, R}`` where - ``Q`` has shape ``(..., m, k)`` with $Q^\top Q = I_k$, - ``R`` has shape ``(..., k, n)`` and is upper-triangular, and $k = \min(m, n)$.

raises.description: If ``a`` is fewer than 2 dimensions or has a non-float dtype.

notes:
- Only the reduced ("thin") factorisation is produced; ``mode='complete'``
  parity with the reference framework is not currently exposed.
- No backward is registered.  Wrapping ``qr_op`` inside a graph
  where ``Q`` or ``R`` requires gradients will yield leaf tensors.

members.name: rand_op
members.signature: int rand_op(const int & shape, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator * gen)
members.summary: Allocates a tensor filled with uniform samples on $[0, 1)$.
members.extended:
Convenience wrapper around :func:`uniform_op` with
``low = 0`` / ``high = 1``.

**Math**

$$x_i \sim \mathcal{U}(0, 1)$$

**See Also**

:func:`uniform_op` — arbitrary $[low, high)$ interval.
:func:`randn_op` — Gaussian variant.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Memory domain of the output (``CPU`` or ``GPU``).

parameters.name: gen
parameters.description: Bit-stream state to draw from.  When ``nullptr`` (the default), uses :func:`default_generator`.

returns.description: Newly allocated leaf tensor (``requires_grad = false``) of the requested shape / dtype / device.

members.name: randint_op
members.signature: int randint_op(const int & shape, int low, int high, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator * gen)
members.summary: Allocates an integer tensor filled with samples drawn uniformly from the integer interval $[\mathrm{low}, \mathrm{high})$.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$x_i \sim \mathcal{U}\{\mathrm{low}, \mathrm{low}+1, \dots,
  \mathrm{high}-1\}$$

parameters.name: high
parameters.description: Exclusive upper bound; must be strictly greater than ``low``.

returns.description: Newly allocated leaf integer tensor (``requires_grad = false``).

notes:
The integer dtype must be wide enough to represent every value in
$[\mathrm{low}, \mathrm{high})$ losslessly; the underlying Storage
helper does **not** mask or wrap on overflow.

members.name: randn_op
members.signature: int randn_op(const int & shape, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator * gen)
members.summary: Allocates a tensor filled with standard-normal samples $\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$.
members.extended:
Convenience wrapper around :func:`normal_op` with ``mean = 0`` /
``std = 1``.

**Math**

$$x_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$$

**See Also**

:func:`normal_op` — arbitrary mean / std.
:func:`rand_op` — uniform variant.

returns.description: Newly allocated leaf tensor (``requires_grad = false``).

members.name: random_bernoulli_storage
members.signature: int random_bernoulli_storage(const int & shape, double p, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator & gen)
members.summary: Generate Bernoulli samples with success probability ``p``.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Success probability for value ``1``; failure value is ``0``.

returns.description: New storage of shape ``shape`` with Bernoulli samples.

members.name: random_normal_storage
members.signature: int random_normal_storage(const int & shape, double mean, double std, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator & gen)
members.summary: Generate normally distributed samples using the Box-Muller transform.

parameters.name: mean
parameters.description: Mean of the resulting normal distribution.

returns.description: New storage of shape ``shape`` with i.i.d. :math:`\mathcal{N}(\text{mean}, \text{std}^2)` samples.

members.name: random_randint_storage
members.signature: int random_randint_storage(const int & shape, int low, int high, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator & gen)
members.summary: Generate uniform random integers in the half-open interval ``[low, high)``.
members.extended:
Uses batched ``uint32`` generation from ``gen`` for throughput, then
folds the raw bytes into the requested integer range.

returns.description: New storage of shape ``shape`` with integer samples.

members.name: random_uniform_storage
members.signature: int random_uniform_storage(const int & shape, double lo, double hi, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator & gen)
members.summary: Generate uniform random samples in the half-open interval ``[lo, hi)``.
members.extended:
Samples are produced on the CPU using ``gen`` and transferred to
``device`` to make the stream deterministic and reproducible.

returns.description: New storage of shape ``shape`` with uniform samples.

members.name: real_op
members.signature: int real_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Extract the real part of a complex tensor as a real tensor.
members.extended:
The result dtype is the corresponding real dtype (``C64`` → ``F32``); the
shape and device are unchanged.

**Math**

$$
  y_k = \Re(z_k)
$$

**See Also**

imag_op, complex_op, conj_op

returns.description: Real-dtype output (``F32``) of the same shape and device as ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is not a complex dtype.

members.name: reciprocal_inplace_op
members.signature: int reciprocal_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place reciprocal: writes $1/x$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \frac{1}{a_i}$$

**See Also**

:func:`reciprocal_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to invert in place.  Must be non-null.  Behaviour at $a_i = 0$ follows the dispatching backend (typically $\pm\infty$).

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: reciprocal_op
members.signature: int reciprocal_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = 1 / x$ element-wise.
members.extended:
Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and
delegates to :class:`ReciprocalBackward::forward`.

**See Also**

:class:`ReciprocalBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.  Behaviour is undefined at $x = 0$.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype.

members.name: reciprocal_storage
members.signature: int reciprocal_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise reciprocal: returns :math:`1 / s`.

returns.description: New storage holding ``1 / s``.

members.name: reduce_grad_to_shape
members.signature: int reduce_grad_to_shape(const int & grad, const int & grad_shape, const int & target_shape, Dtype dtype, Device device)
members.summary: Reduce ``grad`` back to ``target_shape`` by summing over broadcast axes.
members.extended:
Used in the backward pass of any binary op that broadcast one operand
against the other.  The gradient initially has the broadcast (output)
shape; this helper walks the trailing dimensions and sums along any
axis where ``target_shape`` is size 1 or absent, so the result matches
the original input shape ready to be accumulated into ``.grad``.

**Math**

For each output index :math:`i`,

$$
\text{out}[i] = \sum_{j \in \text{broadcast}(i)} \text{grad}[j].
$$

parameters.name: grad
parameters.description: Incoming gradient at the broadcast shape.

parameters.name: target_shape
parameters.description: Original (pre-broadcast) input shape to reduce back to.

returns.description: Storage of shape ``target_shape`` with broadcast axes summed.

notes: No-op when ``grad_shape == target_shape``.

members.name: reduce_output_shape
members.signature: int reduce_output_shape(const int & input_shape, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Compute the output shape of a reduction over selected axes.
members.extended:
Reduced dimensions are removed by default; passing ``keepdims=true``
leaves them in place as size-1 axes (matching reference-framework
semantics) so the result broadcasts cleanly against the input.

parameters.name: input_shape
parameters.description: Shape of the tensor being reduced.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: Whether to retain reduced axes as size 1.

returns.description: Resulting shape after the reduction.

members.name: register_custom_function
members.signature: void register_custom_function(int & m)
members.summary: Register the custom-function bindings into a pybind11 module.
members.extended:
Exposes three Python-visible names under the receiving module:

- ``FunctionCtx`` — opaque shared-pointer class with
    ``save_for_backward(*tensors)``, the ``saved_tensors`` property,
    and permissive ``__setattr__`` / ``__getattr__`` for extras.
  - ``_PythonBackwardNode`` — internal :class:`Node` subclass with
    read/write ``ctx`` and ``backward_fn`` fields.
  - ``_register_python_backward_node(output, node, inputs)`` — wires
    ``node`` into the autograd graph as ``output``'s ``grad_fn``,
    materialising :class:`AccumulateGrad` nodes for any leaves that
    need them and recording version counters for in-place mutation
    checks.

**See Also**

:mod:`lucid.autograd._python_node` — Python-side caller of
``_register_python_backward_node``.

parameters.name: m
parameters.description: The pybind11 module to register into.  Called once during engine module initialisation.

members.name: register_module_hook_nodes
members.signature: void register_module_hook_nodes(int & m)
members.summary: Register :class:`ModuleBackwardHookState` and the wrapper helpers (``_create_module_backward_hook_state``, ``_wrap_module_backward_inputs``, ``_wrap_module_backward_outputs``) on a pybind11 module.

parameters.name: m
parameters.description: Target pybind11 module — typically the ``engine`` extension module loaded via ``from lucid._C import engine as _C_engine``.

members.name: relu6_op
members.signature: int relu6_op(const int & a)
members.summary: ReLU6 $y = \mathrm{clamp}(x, 0, 6)$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

Relu6Backward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with values in $[0, 6]$.

members.name: relu_inplace_op
members.signature: int relu_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place rectified linear unit: writes $\max(0, x)$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \max(0, a_i)$$

**See Also**

:func:`relu_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to apply ReLU to in place.  Must be non-null.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

notes:
The most common in-place op in practice: ResNet-style residual
blocks save a per-layer activation by writing ReLU back over the
convolution output.  Safe under autograd as long as the convolution
was the only saved-for-backward dependency.

members.name: relu_op
members.signature: int relu_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise rectified linear unit $y = \max(0, x)$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

ReluBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any real dtype.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape and dtype as ``a``; autograd-aware when ``a.requires_grad`` is true.

members.name: remainder_op
members.signature: int remainder_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Python-style modulo: $y = a - \lfloor a/b \rfloor \cdot b$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`div_op` + :func:`floor_op` + :func:`mul_op` +
:func:`sub_op`.  Result has the sign of ``b`` because ``floor`` rounds
toward $-\infty$.

**Math**

$$
  y = a - \lfloor a/b \rfloor \cdot b
$$

**See Also**

:func:`fmod_op` — C-style sign convention.

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Broadcastable operands.  ``b`` must be non-zero where evaluated.

returns.description: Tensor on the broadcast shape with the sign of ``b``.

members.name: repeat_op
members.signature: int repeat_op(const int & a, int repeats, int axis)
members.summary: Repeat the contents of a tensor along a single axis.
members.extended:
Produces an output whose ``axis`` dimension is ``repeats`` times larger
than the input's; every other dimension is unchanged.  Each element along
``axis`` is replicated as a contiguous block of ``repeats`` copies in the
output (this is interleaved repetition, distinct from :func:`tile_op`).

**Math**

$$ y_{[\ldots, i_a, \ldots]} = x_{[\ldots, \lfloor i_a / r \rfloor, \ldots]} $$
where $r = $ ``repeats`` and $i_a$ is the index along ``axis``.

**Shape**

``out.shape[axis] = a.shape[axis] * repeats``; all other dims unchanged.

**See Also**

:func:`tile_op` — multi-axis tiling; different element ordering.

parameters.name: repeats
parameters.description: Number of times to repeat along ``axis``.  Must be > 0.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis along which to repeat.  Supports negative indexing.

returns.description: Tensor of shape ``a.shape`` with the ``axis`` dimension scaled by ``repeats``.

notes:
Backward sums the repeated gradient blocks back into the original shape via
``Dispatcher::repeat_backward``.  Schema name ``"repeat"``,
``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``.

members.name: reshape_op
members.signature: int reshape_op(const int & a, const int & new_shape)
members.summary: Reinterpret a tensor under a new shape (no data copy).
members.extended:
The total element count must be preserved.  Exactly one entry of
``new_shape`` may be ``-1``, in which case that dimension is inferred from
the remaining sizes.  The input must be contiguous; pass non-contiguous
inputs through :func:`contiguous_op` first.

**Shape**

$\mathrm{numel}(a) = \prod_d \text{new\_shape}[d]$, after resolving any
``-1`` entry.

**See Also**

:func:`flatten_op`, :func:`squeeze_op`, :func:`contiguous_op`.

parameters.name: new_shape
parameters.description: Target shape.  At most one entry may be ``-1``.  All other entries must be non-negative.  Since ``Shape == vector<int64_t>``, this overload also accepts ``Shape`` directly.

returns.description: A view tensor with shape ``new_shape`` (with ``-1`` resolved) sharing storage with ``a``.

raises.description: If element counts do not match, multiple ``-1`` entries are given, or the input is non-contiguous.

examples:
``reshape_op(t_2x3, {6})`` flattens a 2x3 to a 1-D length-6 view.
``reshape_op(t_2x6, {-1, 3})`` infers the first dim as ``4``.

members.name: rfftn_op
members.signature: int rfftn_op(const int & a, const int & n, const int & axes)
members.summary: N-dimensional real-to-complex DFT.
members.extended:
Computes the DFT of a real signal and stores only the non-redundant
half along the last transformed axis (the Hermitian half).  The
remaining frequencies can be reconstructed by complex conjugation;
this storage convention matches ``numpy.fft.rfftn``.

**Math**

For real input $x$,

$$
  X[k_1, \dots, k_d] = \sum_{n_1=0}^{N_1-1} \cdots \sum_{n_d=0}^{N_d-1}
    x[n_1, \dots, n_d] \,
    \exp\!\left( -2\pi i \sum_{j=1}^{d} \frac{k_j n_j}{N_j} \right) ,
$$

stored over $k_d \in \{0, 1, \dots, N_d/2\}$ only (the last axis is
trimmed by Hermitian symmetry; all other axes are stored in full).

**Shape**

Let ``L = axes.back()``.  Then ``out.shape[L] == n.back() / 2 + 1``
when ``n`` is supplied, else ``a.shape[L] / 2 + 1``.  All other
transformed axes follow ``n`` (or the input shape).

**See Also**

irfftn_op : Inverse real DFT.
fftn_op : Full complex-to-complex DFT.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: Per-axis transform length.  Empty means "use input sizes". When supplied, ``len(n) == len(axes)`` is required, and the last transformed axis is stored at ``n.back() / 2 + 1``.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to transform.  Must be non-empty (rfftn requires at least one axis).  Negative axes are normalised against the input rank.

returns.description: C64 tensor.  Shape matches the input except for the last entry of ``axes``, which becomes ``(n_last) / 2 + 1`` where ``n_last`` is ``n.back()`` (or ``a.shape[axes.back()]`` when ``n`` is empty).

raises.description: If ``a`` is null, has a non-real dtype, ``axes`` is empty, or ``len(n) != len(axes)`` when ``n`` is non-empty.

notes:
FFT bypasses IBackend dispatch and calls MLX directly (see
``_Detail.h``).  The Python wrapper must remember the original last
axis size to disambiguate the inverse.

members.name: rms_norm_op
members.signature: int rms_norm_op(const int & x, const int & gamma, double eps)
members.summary: Public free-function entry point for RMS Normalization.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper that delegates to :func:`RMSNormBackward::forward`.
This is the symbol the pybind11 binding layer forwards to from
:func:`lucid.nn.functional.rms_norm`.

**See Also**

RMSNormBackward : Autograd node implementing the forward + backward.
layer_norm_op   : Variant that also subtracts the mean and adds a bias.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape with rank ``>= rank(gamma)``.

parameters.name: gamma
parameters.description: Per-element scale; must match the trailing dims of ``x``.

returns.description: Normalised output, same shape as ``x``.

members.name: roll_op
members.signature: int roll_op(const int & a, int shifts, int axes)
members.summary: Circularly shift elements along one or more axes.
members.extended:
For each ``(shift, axis)`` pair, rotates the tensor along that axis by
``shift`` positions; elements that fall off one end wrap around to the
other.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to shift.  Must match ``shifts`` in length.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

notes:
Backward applies the inverse circular shift (negated ``shifts``) to the
incoming gradient, which exactly undoes the forward rotation.

members.name: rotary_pos_embedding_op
members.signature: int rotary_pos_embedding_op(const int & input, const int & position_ids_or_null, bool interleaved)
members.summary: Apply Rotary Position Embedding to ``input`` — free-function entry point.
members.extended: Thin wrapper that delegates to :func:`RotaryPosEmbeddingBackward::forward`.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: Input of shape $(\ldots, L, D)$ with ``D`` even.

parameters.name: position_ids_or_null
parameters.description: Optional integer position-index tensor of shape ``(L,)``; pass ``null`` to use the implicit ``[0, 1, …, L-1]``.

returns.description: Rotated output with the same shape as ``input``.

members.name: rotate_op
members.signature: int rotate_op(const int & input, double angle_deg, double cy, double cx)
members.summary: Rotate a 4-D image batch around an arbitrary centre.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: 4-D image batch of shape ``(N, C, H, W)``.

returns.description: Rotated image batch of shape ``(N, C, H, W)``.

members.name: round_inplace_op
members.signature: int round_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place banker's rounding: writes $\mathrm{round}(x)$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \mathrm{round}(a_i)$$

**See Also**

:func:`round_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to round in place.  Must be non-null.  Half-to-even ("banker's") rounding follows the backend default.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: round_op
members.signature: int round_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise round-to-nearest-even — returns a new tensor whose values are ``round(a)`` with the same shape and dtype as ``a``.
members.extended:
Not differentiable: ``a`` is detached from the autograd graph.

**Math**

$$y_i = \operatorname{round}(x_i)$$  (round-half-to-even / banker's rounding).

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`floor_op`, :func:`ceil_op`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor.  Integer dtypes are returned unchanged.

returns.description: New tensor with rounded values; same shape, dtype, and device as ``a``.

members.name: rpow_scalar_op
members.signature: int rpow_scalar_op(double base, const int & a)
members.summary: Element-wise scalar-base reverse power — returns $\text{base}^{a}$ with a fully wired autograd node.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$y_i = \text{base}^{x_i}, \qquad
\frac{\partial y_i}{\partial x_i} = \ln(\text{base}) \cdot y_i$$

**Shape**

Output shape equals input shape (elementwise).

**See Also**

:func:`pow_scalar_op`, :class:`RPowScalarBackward`.

parameters.name: base
parameters.description: Scalar base (must be positive for a real-valued gradient).

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor — the exponent (promoted to FP32 by the op schema).

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``; gradient is $\ln(\text{base}) \cdot y \cdot g$.

raises.annotation: Gradient is undefined / non-real for ``base <= 0``; callers should
guard the base if they intend to backpropagate.

members.name: rsqrt_op
members.signature: int rsqrt_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = 1/\sqrt{x}$ element-wise.  Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and delegates to :class:`RsqrtBackward::forward`.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:class:`RsqrtBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape.  Must be strictly positive.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype.

members.name: run_fusion_pass
members.signature: int run_fusion_pass(Node * root_node)
members.summary: Convenience entry point invoked by ``Engine::backward()`` before traversal.
members.extended:
Builds a topological ordering of the backward graph rooted at
``root_node`` using an iterative post-order DFS over raw ``Node*``
(mirroring ``Engine::topo_order`` but avoiding ``shared_ptr`` reference-
counting overhead during the scan), then constructs a :class:`FusionPass`
and invokes :func:`FusionPass::run` on the result.

**See Also**

:class:`FusionPass` — the underlying pattern-matching pass.

parameters.name: root_node
parameters.description: Root of the backward graph (usually the ``grad_fn`` of the loss tensor).  ``nullptr`` is tolerated and short-circuits to ``0``.

returns.description: Total number of fusions applied, or ``0`` when ``root_node`` is null or the resulting graph has fewer than two nodes.

members.name: scaled_dot_product_attention_op
members.signature: int scaled_dot_product_attention_op(const int & q, const int & k, const int & v, const int & attn_mask_or_null, double scale, bool is_causal)
members.summary: Run scaled dot-product attention and return only the output tensor.
members.extended:
Public entry point that wires Lucid's autograd system to the SDPA
kernel.  Internally calls ``ScaledDotProductAttentionBackward::forward``
and, when ``GradMode::is_enabled()`` and any of ``q``, ``k``, ``v``
require a gradient, attaches a fresh ``ScaledDotProductAttentionBackward``
node so the output participates in BPTT.

**Math**

$\text{Attn}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M\right)V$.

**References**

Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need" (NeurIPS 2017).

**See Also**

scaled_dot_product_attention_with_weights_op : Variant that also
    returns the post-softmax attention weight matrix ``W``.

parameters.name: q
parameters.description: Query tensor of shape ``(..., L_q, d_k)``.

parameters.name: k
parameters.description: Key tensor of shape ``(..., L_k, d_k)``.

parameters.name: v
parameters.description: Value tensor of shape ``(..., L_k, d_v)``.

parameters.name: attn_mask_or_null
parameters.description: Optional additive mask broadcastable to ``(..., L_q, L_k)``; ``nullptr`` skips the additive term entirely.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Multiplicative factor applied to $QK^\top$ before the softmax.

parameters.name: is_causal
parameters.description: If ``true``, an engine-side upper-triangular ``-inf`` mask is added so each query position attends only to earlier keys.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape ``(..., L_q, d_v)``.

members.name: scaled_dot_product_attention_with_weights_op
members.signature: int scaled_dot_product_attention_with_weights_op(const int & q, const int & k, const int & v, const int & attn_mask_or_null, double scale, bool is_causal)
members.summary: Run scaled dot-product attention and return ``{output, weights}``.
members.extended:
Identical numerics to ``scaled_dot_product_attention_op`` but exposes
the post-softmax attention weight matrix
$W = \text{softmax}(QK^\top \cdot s + M)$ shaped ``(..., L_q, L_k)``
as a second return value.  Useful for visualisation, head analysis,
or any caller that needs to inspect or further process attention
scores.

When autograd is required, two ``ScaledDotProductAttentionBackward``
nodes are wired — one for the output tensor (the normal SDPA
backward) and a second, independent node attached to the weights
tensor so callers may use the weights in downstream loss terms
without conflating gradients between the two outputs.

**See Also**

scaled_dot_product_attention_op : Lighter variant returning only the
    contracted output, when attention weights are not needed.

parameters.name: q
parameters.description: Query tensor of shape ``(..., L_q, d_k)``.

parameters.name: k
parameters.description: Key tensor of shape ``(..., L_k, d_k)``.

parameters.name: v
parameters.description: Value tensor of shape ``(..., L_k, d_v)``.

parameters.name: attn_mask_or_null
parameters.description: Optional additive mask broadcastable to ``(..., L_q, L_k)``.

parameters.name: scale
parameters.description: Multiplicative factor applied to $QK^\top$ before the softmax.

parameters.name: is_causal
parameters.description: If ``true``, an upper-triangular ``-inf`` mask is applied engine-side.

returns.description: A two-element vector ``{output, weights}`` where ``output`` has shape ``(..., L_q, d_v)`` and ``weights`` has shape ``(..., L_q, L_k)``.

notes:
On the MLX (GPU) path the fused kernel does not produce a dense
weight matrix; this entry point therefore recomputes
$\text{softmax}(QK^\top \cdot s + M)$ to materialise the weights for
the caller.  On the Accelerate (CPU) path the weight matrix is a
natural byproduct of the manual softmax and is returned directly.

members.name: scatter_add_op
members.signature: int scatter_add_op(const int & base, const int & indices, const int & src, int dim)
members.summary: User-facing scatter-add: ``out = base`` with ``src`` accumulated at positions given by ``indices`` along ``dim``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$
  \text{out}[i_0, \dots, i_{d-1}, \text{indices}[j], i_{d+1}, \dots]
  \mathrel{+}= \text{src}[i_0, \dots, j, \dots]
$$

parameters.name: base
parameters.description: Tensor providing the initial values; same shape as the output.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description: Integer tensor indexing into ``base`` along ``dim``.  Shape matches ``src``.

returns.description: Same shape and dtype as ``base``.

notes:
Full autograd support.  Gradients: $\partial / \partial \text{base} = \text{grad\_out}$,
$\partial / \partial \text{src} = \text{gather}(\text{grad\_out}, \text{dim}, \text{indices})$.

members.name: scatter_amax_op
members.signature: int scatter_amax_op(const int & base, const int & indices, const int & src, int dim)
members.summary: Scatter with element-wise ``max`` reduction at duplicate indices.
members.extended:
**Math**

At each output slot $i$, the result is the maximum among ``base[i]``
and all ``src[j]`` with ``indices[j] == i``.

**See Also**

scatter_amin_op : Same pattern with ``min`` reduction.

returns.description: Reduced tensor, same shape and dtype as ``base``.

notes:
Backward flows only through "winning" elements:
  $\partial / \partial \text{src}[j] = \text{grad}[\text{idx}[j]]$ when $\text{src}[j] = \text{out}[\text{idx}[j]]$, else $0$;
  $\partial / \partial \text{base}[i] = \text{grad}[i]$ when $\text{base}[i] = \text{out}[i]$, else $0$.
Ties distribute the gradient equally to all winners (matches the
reference framework).

members.name: scatter_amin_op
members.signature: int scatter_amin_op(const int & base, const int & indices, const int & src, int dim)
members.summary: Scatter with element-wise ``min`` reduction at duplicate indices.
members.extended:
**Math**

At each output slot $i$, the result is the minimum among ``base[i]``
and all ``src[j]`` with ``indices[j] == i``.

**See Also**

scatter_amax_op : Companion ``max`` variant.

returns.description: Reduced tensor, same shape and dtype as ``base``.

notes:
Backward identical to ``scatter_amax_op`` with the winner predicate
flipped to "matches the output minimum".

members.name: scatter_op
members.signature: int scatter_op(const int & base, int dim, const int & indices, const int & src)
members.summary: Overwrite-semantics scatter: $\mathrm{out}[..., \mathrm{idx}, ...] = \mathrm{src}$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`gather_op` + :func:`sub_op` + :func:`scatter_add_op`.
The overwrite is encoded as a scatter-add of the *delta*
$\mathrm{src} - \mathrm{base}[\mathrm{idx}]$, so the result is identical
to assignment but reuses the existing additive scatter kernel.  Gradient
flows through ``ScatterAddBackward``, ``SubBackward``, and
``GatherBackward``.

**Math**

$$
  y_i = \begin{cases}
    \mathrm{src}_j & \text{if } i = \mathrm{idx}_j \text{ for some } j \\
    \mathrm{base}_i & \text{otherwise}
  \end{cases}
$$

**See Also**

:func:`scatter_add_op` — additive variant; canonical primitive.

parameters.name: base
parameters.description: Tensor to scatter into.  Provides the output shape and dtype.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description: Integer tensor (``int32`` or ``int64``) with the same shape as ``src``.

parameters.name: src
parameters.description: Source values written into ``base`` at the positions given by ``indices``.

returns.description: A new tensor with the same shape and dtype as ``base``.

raises.description: If any input is null or ``indices`` is not an integer dtype.
If ``dim`` is out of range.

notes:
Duplicate indices yield undefined ordering — the *last write wins*
guarantee of true scatter is not preserved here because the underlying
primitive is additive.  Use disjoint ``indices`` for deterministic
behaviour.

members.name: scatter_prod_op
members.signature: int scatter_prod_op(const int & base, const int & indices, const int & src, int dim)
members.summary: Scatter with multiplicative reduction.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$
  \text{out}[i] = \text{base}[i] \cdot \prod_{j : \text{idx}[j] = i} \text{src}[j] .
$$

returns.description: Same shape and dtype as ``base``.

notes:
Gradients use the product rule:
  $\partial / \partial \text{src}[j] = \text{grad}[\text{idx}[j]] \cdot \text{out}[\text{idx}[j]] / \text{src}[j]$,
  $\partial / \partial \text{base}[i] = \text{grad}[i] \cdot \text{out}[i] / \text{base}[i]$
(the latter requires ``base[i] != 0``).

members.name: schema_hash
members.signature: int schema_hash(const OpSchema & s)
members.summary: Computes a 64-bit FNV-1a fingerprint of an op's behavioural schema.
members.extended:
The hash mixes the schema's ``name``, ``version``, ``amp_policy``, and
``deterministic`` flag — fields that together define how the op
behaves.  ``stable_input_indices``, ``internal``, and arity counts are
excluded because they describe shape rather than semantics.

Used for change-detection: cached graph compilations, profiler entries,
and checkpoint metadata embed this hash so a schema bump (or any
behaviour-relevant change) invalidates the cache automatically.

**Math**

$$ h_0 = \text{0xcbf29ce484222325}, \qquad
   h_{i+1} = (h_i \oplus b_i) \cdot \text{0x100000001b3} \pmod{2^{64}}, $$

where $b_i$ ranges over the bytes of ``name``, a null separator,
the little-endian bytes of ``version``, the ``amp_policy`` byte, and
finally the ``deterministic`` flag (0 or 1).

returns.description: A 64-bit FNV-1a digest.  Stable across processes for the same schema fields; not cryptographic.

notes:
The null byte between ``name`` and ``version`` prevents length-
extension collisions of the form ``"ab"`` vs ``"a" + 'b'``.

members.name: searchsorted_op
members.signature: int searchsorted_op(const int & sorted_1d, const int & values, bool right)
members.summary: Per-query insertion point into a sorted 1-D reference.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`reshape_op` + :func:`broadcast_to_op` +
(:func:`less_op` | :func:`less_equal_op`) + :func:`astype_op` +
:func:`sum_op`.  The 1-D reference is broadcast to
``[1, ..., 1, N]`` and the queries to ``S + [1]``; both expand to the
full ``S + [N]`` grid, the comparison is reduced over the trailing axis,
and the bool $\to$ F32 $\to$ I64 cast chain produces the standard
``searchsorted`` dtype.  Not differentiable — integer output.

**Math**

Let $s$ be the sorted reference and $v$ the query tensor.  Then
$$
  y_{\ldots, j} = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1}
  \mathbb{1}\!\left[ s_i \prec v_{\ldots, j} \right]
$$
with $\prec$ being $<$ when ``right = false`` (leftmost insertion) and
$\le$ when ``right = true`` (rightmost insertion).

**See Also**

:func:`bucketize_op` — same operation with the argument order flipped.

parameters.name: right
parameters.description: If ``true``, returns the rightmost (post-equal) insertion point; otherwise the leftmost.

returns.description: ``int64`` tensor of shape ``S`` whose entries lie in $[0, N]$.

raises.description: If either input is null or ``sorted_1d`` is not 1-D.

notes:
The reduction kernel only supports float dtypes; the bool comparison is
cast to F32 for the sum, then back to I64 to match the canonical
``searchsorted`` dtype.

members.name: selu_op
members.signature: int selu_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Scaled ELU (SELU) with fixed self-normalising constants $\alpha \approx 1.6732632$ and $\lambda \approx 1.0507010$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

SeluBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

members.name: set_current_profiler
members.signature: void set_current_profiler(Profiler * p)
members.summary: Registers ``p`` as the active :class:`Profiler` for the calling thread.
members.extended:
**See Also**

:func:`current_profiler` — reader for the same TLS slot.

parameters.name: p
parameters.description: Profiler to register, or ``nullptr`` to deactivate profiling on this thread without destroying the existing object.

notes:
Does not transfer ownership — the caller must keep ``p`` alive for
as long as any :class:`OpScope` could observe it as the current
profiler.  The Python wrapper holds the binding object's
``shared_ptr`` for exactly this reason.

members.name: set_mutation_on_saved_allowed
members.signature: void set_mutation_on_saved_allowed(bool v)
members.summary: Toggle the process-wide "allow mutation on saved tensors" flag.
members.extended:
Used by ``lucid.autograd.graph.allow_mutation_on_saved_tensors`` to let
users opt into the unsafe contract that they will not mutate a saved
tensor in a way that would corrupt the gradient.

parameters.name: v
parameters.description: New state of the override.  ``true`` disables version-counter checks; ``false`` re-enables them.

notes:
The flag is global to the process — there is no per-thread or
per-graph scoping.  The Python context manager is responsible for
restoring the previous value on exit.

members.name: shape_numel
members.signature: int shape_numel(const int & shape)
members.summary: Returns the total number of elements implied by a shape.
members.extended:
The product is computed in :type:`std::size_t` to avoid sign issues when
the caller wants to use the result for allocation sizing.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Shape vector to reduce.  May be empty (scalar) or contain negative sentinel values.

returns.description: Product of all dimensions.  ``1`` for an empty shape (scalar convention); ``0`` as soon as any dimension is negative — the caller is responsible for not allocating storage in that case.

examples:
shape_numel({})            // 1   (scalar)
    shape_numel({3, 4})        // 12
    shape_numel({-1, 8})       // 0   (unresolved leading dim)
notes:
The early-return-zero behaviour for negative dims is deliberate: it lets
dynamic-shape pipelines pass an unresolved :type:`Shape` through generic
code paths without manually filtering for sentinels.

members.name: sigmoid_inplace_op
members.signature: int sigmoid_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place logistic sigmoid: writes $\sigma(x) = 1/(1 + e^{-x})$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \sigma(a_i) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-a_i}}$$

**See Also**

:func:`sigmoid_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to apply sigmoid to in place.  Must be non-null.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

notes:
Primarily useful for inference paths where the pre-activation buffer
can be safely reused.  Avoid in training when ``a`` is saved for the
backward of a downstream op.

members.name: sigmoid_op
members.signature: int sigmoid_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Logistic sigmoid $y = 1 / (1 + e^{-x})$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

SigmoidBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any real dtype.

returns.description: Output tensor with values in $(0, 1)$.

members.name: sigmoid_storage
members.signature: int sigmoid_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise sigmoid: returns :math:`1 / (1 + e^{-s})`.

returns.description: New storage holding the logistic sigmoid of ``s``.

members.name: sign_inplace_op
members.signature: int sign_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place sign function: writes $\mathrm{sign}(x) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \mathrm{sign}(a_i)$$

**See Also**

:func:`sign_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Tensor to apply sign to in place.  Must be non-null.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``.

members.name: sign_op
members.signature: int sign_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute $y = \mathrm{sign}(x) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ element-wise.
members.extended:
Allocates a fresh output of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` and
delegates to :class:`SignBackward::forward`.  No autograd edge is
recorded because ``sign`` has a zero gradient almost everywhere.

**See Also**

:class:`SignBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any real dtype, including integer.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype, with values in $\{-1, 0, +1\}$.

members.name: sign_storage
members.signature: int sign_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise sign function: returns -1, 0, or +1.

returns.description: New storage with values in {-1, 0, +1} matching the sign of ``s``.

members.name: silu_op
members.signature: int silu_op(const int & a)
members.summary: SiLU / Swish activation $y = x \cdot \sigma(x)$.
members.extended:
**See Also**

SiluBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

returns.description: Output tensor with the same shape as ``a``.

members.name: sin_inplace_op
members.signature: int sin_inplace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: In-place sine: writes $\sin x$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow \sin(a_i)$$

members.name: sinusoidal_pos_embedding_op
members.signature: int sinusoidal_pos_embedding_op(int seq_len, int embed_dim, Dtype dtype, Device device)
members.summary: Generate the fixed sinusoidal position-encoding matrix from Vaswani et al. 2017, "Attention Is All You Need".
members.extended:
Returns the non-trainable matrix

$$\text{PE}(p, 2i) = \sin\!\left(\frac{p}{10000^{2i/D}}\right),
  \quad
  \text{PE}(p, 2i+1) = \cos\!\left(\frac{p}{10000^{2i/D}}\right),$$

where $p \in [0, L)$ indexes positions and $i \in [0, D/2)$ indexes
frequency bands.  No autograd node is created — the result is a
constant tensor used as an additive bias on token embeddings.

parameters.name: embed_dim
parameters.description: Embedding dimension ``D``.  Must be strictly positive.

parameters.name: device
parameters.description: Device on which to allocate the output.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape ``(seq_len, embed_dim)``.

raises.description: If ``seq_len < 0`` or ``embed_dim <= 0``.

log_softmax_op : Numerically advantageous log-domain variant for
    downstream NLL / cross-entropy losses.
SoftmaxBackward : Autograd node implementing the gradient rule.

parameters.name: axis

returns.description: Output probabilities with the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

members.name: solve_op
members.signature: int solve_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Solve the linear system $AX = B$ for $X$.
members.extended:
**Shape**

- ``a``: ``(..., N, N)``.
- ``b``: ``(..., N)`` or ``(..., N, K)``.
- Output: same shape as ``b``.

**References**

[Giles, "Collected Matrix Derivative Results" (Oxford, 2008)](https://people.maths.ox.ac.uk/gilesm/files/NA-08-01.pdf), §2.4.
LAPACK ``sgesv``/``dgesv``; *LAPACK Users' Guide* §2.5.

**See Also**

[[lu_factor_op]] : Standalone LU factorisation (no autograd).
[[lu_solve_op]]  : Apply pre-computed LU factors (no autograd).
[[matmul_op]]    : Used in the backward outer product $-dB\, X^\top$.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Square float tensor of shape ``(..., N, N)``.  Must share dtype and device with ``b``.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right-hand side of shape ``(..., N)`` or ``(..., N, K)``.

returns.description: Solution tensor $X = A^{-1} B$ with the same shape as ``b``. When grad mode is active, the result has [[SolveBackward]] wired into the autograd graph with $A$ saved as input and $X$ saved as output.

raises.description: When ``a`` is not square, not float-typed, when dtypes/devices mismatch between ``a`` and ``b``, or when $A$ is singular (zero pivot detected by LU).

examples: Single RHS solve::
auto x = solve_op(a, b);  // a: (n, n), b: (n,) → x: (n,)
Batched multi-RHS solve::
auto x = solve_op(a, b);  // a: (B, n, n), b: (B, n, k)

members.name: solve_triangular_op
members.signature: int solve_triangular_op(const int & a, const int & b, bool upper, bool unitriangular)
members.summary: Solve $A X = B$ for $X$ where $A$ is triangular.
members.extended:
Performs a single substitution sweep through $A$; no factorisation is
required.  $A$ and $B$ must share the same dtype and device.  Batched
inputs are supported on both arguments: the trailing two dimensions are
treated as the matrix axes and the broadcast batch loop runs LAPACK once
per slice.

**Math**

Solves
$$
  A X = B \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad X = A^{-1} B,
$$
without ever forming $A^{-1}$.  When ``unitriangular`` is set, $A$ is
regarded as $\widetilde{A}$ with $\widetilde{A}_{ii} = 1$ for all $i$.
The corresponding reverse-mode rule is
$$
  \frac{\partial B}{\partial L} = A^{-\top} \frac{\partial X}{\partial L},
  \qquad
  \frac{\partial A}{\partial L} = -\,\frac{\partial B}{\partial L}\,X^\top,
$$
implemented in the Python wrapper.

**Shape**

- ``a`` : ``(..., N, N)``.
- ``b`` : ``(..., N, K)`` or ``(..., N)``.
- return: same shape as ``b``.

**See Also**

- ``solve_op`` — full LU-based dense solve; use when $A$ is not known to
  be triangular.
- ``cholesky_op`` / ``ldl_factor_op`` — produce the triangular factors
  this op back-substitutes through.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Triangular coefficient matrix of shape ``(..., N, N)`` with dtype ``F32`` or ``F64``.  Only the half indicated by ``upper`` is read.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right-hand side of shape ``(..., N, K)`` (or ``(..., N)`` for a single RHS), same dtype and device as ``a``.

parameters.name: upper
parameters.description: If ``true`` (default), ``a`` is interpreted as upper-triangular and back-substitution is used.  If ``false``, ``a`` is lower-triangular and forward-substitution is used.

parameters.name: unitriangular
parameters.description: If ``true``, the diagonal of ``a`` is treated as all-ones regardless of its stored values.  Useful for the unit-lower factor produced by LDL$^\top$ and for Householder products.  Default is ``false``.

returns.description: Solution $X$ with the same shape and dtype as ``b``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is not square, if ``a`` and ``b`` have mismatched dtype or device, or if either tensor has a non-float dtype.
If LAPACK reports a singular triangular system (zero on the diagonal when ``unitriangular`` is false).

notes: No autograd node is wired; the output ``TensorImpl`` is a leaf.

members.name: sort_op
members.signature: int sort_op(const int & a, int axis)
members.summary: Sort a tensor along a single axis in ascending order.
members.extended:
Returns the sorted values; the permutation indices are computed
internally and consumed by the backward pass but are not exposed here.
See :func:`argsort_op` for the indices alone, or :func:`topk_op` for a
(values, indices) pair.

**Math**

Let $\sigma$ be the permutation that sorts ``a`` along ``axis``.  Then
$$y_i = a_{\sigma(i)}, \qquad
\frac{\partial L}{\partial a_i} =
    \left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial y}\right)_{\sigma^{-1}(i)}.$$

returns.description: Sorted tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

notes:
Differentiable for F32 / F64 inputs only via ``IndexScatterBackward``,
which scatters the incoming gradient back to the original positions.
Integer dtypes return a detached output.

members.name: split_at_op
members.signature: int split_at_op(const int & a, int indices, int axis)
members.summary: Split a tensor at user-specified indices along an axis.
members.extended:
Produces ``indices.size() + 1`` pieces whose sizes along ``axis`` are the
consecutive differences between ``0``, the sorted ``indices``, and
``a.shape[axis]``.  Each piece carries its own ``SplitSliceBackward`` node.

**See Also**

:func:`split_op`.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description: Strictly increasing split positions in the range ``[0, a.shape[axis]]``.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis to split along.  Supports negative indexing.

returns.description: ``indices.size() + 1`` tensors whose ``axis`` dimensions partition ``a.shape[axis]``.

members.name: split_op
members.signature: int split_op(const int & a, int num_splits, int axis)
members.summary: Split a tensor into a fixed number of equal pieces along an axis.
members.extended:
The size of ``a`` along ``axis`` must be divisible by ``num_splits``.
Each output piece is wired with a ``SplitSliceBackward`` node that
scatters its gradient back into the correct slice of the input gradient
via ``insert_axis_slice``.

**See Also**

:func:`split_at_op`, :func:`chunk_op`, :func:`unbind_op`.

parameters.name: num_splits
parameters.description: Number of equal pieces.  Must divide ``a.shape[axis]`` exactly.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis to split along.  Supports negative indexing.

returns.description: ``num_splits`` tensors, each with shape identical to ``a`` except the ``axis`` dimension equals ``a.shape[axis] / num_splits``.

raises.description: If ``a.shape[axis]`` is not divisible by ``num_splits``.

members.name: squeeze_all_op
members.signature: int squeeze_all_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Remove every size-1 dimension from a tensor.
members.extended:
Equivalent to repeated application of :func:`squeeze_op` on each size-1
axis.  If no such axes exist, returns a view with the same shape.

**See Also**

:func:`squeeze_op`.

returns.description: A view with all size-1 dimensions removed.  Rank may equal the input's rank if no size-1 axes were present.

members.name: squeeze_op
members.signature: int squeeze_op(const int & a, int dim)
members.summary: Remove a single size-1 dimension.
members.extended:
Drops the axis at position ``dim``, which must currently have size 1.
Negative ``dim`` wraps relative to the input rank.

**See Also**

:func:`squeeze_all_op`, :func:`unsqueeze_op`.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Axis to remove.  Must satisfy ``-ndim <= dim < ndim`` and ``a.shape[dim] == 1``.

returns.description: A view with rank ``ndim - 1``.

raises.description: If ``dim`` is out of range.
If the targeted dimension is not of size 1.

members.name: stack_op
members.signature: int stack_op(const int & xs, int axis)
members.summary: Stack a list of same-shape tensors along a new axis.
members.extended:
Inserts a fresh size-``len(xs)`` dimension at position ``axis`` of the
output, increasing the rank by one.  All inputs must have identical shape,
dtype, and device.

**Shape**

Inputs of shape ``(D_0, ..., D_{ndim-1})`` stacked along ``axis = k`` yield
output shape ``(D_0, ..., D_{k-1}, len(xs), D_k, ..., D_{ndim-1})``.

**See Also**

:func:`concatenate_op`, :func:`unbind_op`.

parameters.name: xs
parameters.description: Non-empty list of tensors with identical shape, dtype, and device.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Insertion position for the new dimension.  Supports negative indexing against the output rank ``ndim + 1`` (so ``axis = -1`` appends).

returns.description: Output tensor with rank ``ndim + 1`` and a new dimension of size ``len(xs)`` inserted at ``axis``.

raises.description: On any disagreement between inputs.

notes:
Backward slices each size-1 hyperplane along the new axis and squeezes it
to recover the gradient for the corresponding input.  Schema name
``"stack"``, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``.

members.name: std_op
members.signature: int std_op(const int & a, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Compute the (biased) standard deviation along ``axes``.
members.extended:
Defined as $\mathrm{std}(x) = \sqrt{\mathrm{var}(x, \text{axes},
\text{keepdims})}$, where the underlying variance uses the **biased**
estimator $\frac{1}{N}\sum (x - \bar{x})^2$.  No new backward node
is needed: the gradient flows naturally through ``SqrtBackward`` and
``VarBackward`` via the chain rule.

**Math**

$$
  \mathrm{std}(x) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i \in \text{axes}}
  (x_i - \bar{x})^2}.
$$

**See Also**

:func:`var_op` — variance the standard deviation is built on.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to reduce.  Negative indices wrap.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true``, retains reduced dims as size-1.

returns.description: Output tensor of the reduced shape.
