members.name: sub_inplace_op
members.signature: int sub_inplace_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: In-place elementwise subtraction: writes $a - b$ back into ``a``.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$a_i \leftarrow a_i - b_i$$

**See Also**

:func:`sub_op` — out-of-place counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand and in-place target.  Must be non-null and must not share storage with a view.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand.  Must be non-null and broadcastable into ``a->shape()``.

returns.description: The same pointer as ``a``, now holding the difference storage.

raises.description: If the broadcast output would change ``a``'s shape.
If ``a`` shares storage with a view.

members.name: sub_op
members.signature: int sub_op(const int & a, const int & b)
members.summary: Public entry point — element-wise subtraction with autograd support.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$ y = a - b $$

**See Also**

add_op, mul_op, div_op

parameters.name: a, b
parameters.description: Operands of any rank.  Shapes must be NumPy-broadcast-compatible.

returns.description: Output tensor of shape ``broadcast_shapes(a.shape, b.shape)`` and promoted dtype.  Participates in autograd via :class:`SubBackward`.

raises.description: If ``a`` and ``b`` have incompatible shapes under NumPy broadcasting rules, or if their devices differ.

examples: >>> auto c = sub_op(a, b);    // c = a - b

members.name: subtract_storages
members.signature: int subtract_storages(const int & a, const int & b, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise subtraction: returns ``a - b``.

returns.description: New storage holding the element-wise difference.

members.name: sum_op
members.signature: int sum_op(const int & a, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Reduce ``a`` by addition along ``axes``.
members.extended:
Promotes ``bool`` / ``int8`` / ``int16`` / ``int32`` inputs to
``int64`` before dispatch so the sum has overflow headroom matching
the reference framework.  Routes the forward through
:class:`SumBackward` which also installs the autograd edge when
``a->requires_grad()`` is true.

**See Also**

:class:`SumBackward` — backward node.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any shape and any real dtype.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to reduce.  May be empty to reduce all axes.  Negative indices wrap around ``ndim``.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true``, the reduced dimensions are kept as size-1 entries; otherwise they are collapsed.

returns.description: Output tensor.  Shape is the input shape with the reduced axes either removed (default) or set to $1$ (``keepdims=true``).

members.name: svd_op
members.signature: int svd_op(const int & a, bool compute_uv)
members.summary: Compute the reduced singular value decomposition of a rectangular matrix.
members.extended:
**Shape**

- Input ``a``: ``(..., m, n)``.
- ``U``:  ``(..., m, k)`` — left singular vectors as columns.
- ``S``:  ``(..., k)``    — singular values in descending order.
- ``Vh``: ``(..., k, n)`` — right singular vectors as rows (note: ``Vh``
  is $V^\top$, not $V$, matching NumPy/reference-framework convention).

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input matrix of shape ``(..., m, n)`` with leading batch dims.  Must be at least 2-D and have a floating-point dtype.

parameters.name: compute_uv
parameters.description: When ``true`` (default) returns the full reduced factorisation $\{U, \Sigma, V^\top\}$.  When ``false`` returns $\{\Sigma\}$ only, which lets the backend skip assembly of the singular vectors — roughly 2x faster and used by ``svdvals`` / ``matrix_rank`` / ``cond``.

returns.description: If ``compute_uv=true``: ``{U, S, Vh}`` with $U$ shape ``(..., m, k)``, $S$ shape ``(..., k)``, $V^\top$ shape ``(..., k, n)``, $k = \min(m, n)$. If ``compute_uv=false``: ``{S}`` only.

raises.description: If ``a`` is null, has dtype that is not floating point, or has fewer than two dimensions.

notes:
Singular values are guaranteed non-negative and sorted in descending
order on output.  Outputs are leaf nodes in the autograd graph at the
C++ level — the Python wrapper attaches the gradient functions.

members.name: swapaxes_op
members.signature: int swapaxes_op(const int & a, int axis1, int axis2)
members.summary: Swap two named axes of ``a``, wrapping negative indices.

parameters.name: axis1, axis2
parameters.description: Axes to swap.  Negative values count from the end.

returns.description: View of ``a`` with the two requested axes swapped and all other axes left in place.

raises.description: If either axis falls outside ``[-ndim, ndim)`` after normalisation.

members.name: take_op
members.signature: int take_op(const int & a, const int & indices)
members.summary: Index ``a`` with a flat index list: $y_j = \mathrm{flatten}(a)_{i_j}$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`reshape_op` + :func:`gather_op`.  The input is
flattened to a 1-D view first so the index dimension is unambiguous;
gradient flows back through ``GatherBackward`` (scatter-add) and
``ReshapeBackward``.

**Math**

$$
  y_j = a_{\mathrm{unravel}(i_j)}
$$
where $\mathrm{unravel}$ maps the linear index into ``a``'s row-major
coordinates.

**See Also**

:func:`index_select_op` — keeps the original rank instead of flattening.

parameters.name: indices
parameters.description: Integer tensor (``int32`` or ``int64``) of arbitrary shape; each element is a flat-index into ``a``.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape as ``indices`` and dtype of ``a``.

raises.description: If either input is null, or if ``indices`` is not an integer dtype.

notes:
Out-of-bounds indices are forwarded verbatim to ``gather_op`` — behaviour
matches the underlying primitive's bounds policy.

returns.description: Output tensor of the same shape and dtype, values in $(-1, 1)$.

members.name: tanh_storage
members.signature: int tanh_storage(const int & s, int numel, Dtype dt, Device device)
members.summary: Element-wise hyperbolic tangent: returns :math:`\tanh(s)`.

returns.description: New storage holding ``tanh(s)``.

members.name: tensordot_op
members.signature: int tensordot_op(const int & a, const int & b, int axes_a, int axes_b)
members.summary: General multi-axis tensor contraction (NumPy ``tensordot``).
members.extended:
Sums the product of ``a`` and ``b`` over the axes named pairwise by
``axes_a`` and ``axes_b``, leaving every other axis as an outer-product
dimension in the output.  This is the multi-axis generalisation of
matrix multiplication: ``tensordot(a, b, [k], [0])`` over rank-2 inputs
is exactly ``a @ b``.

When gradient tracking is active the contraction is lowered to an
equivalent ``einsum`` string and the einsum backward node handles the
gradient computation, so this op itself does not register a dedicated
backward node.  On the CPU inference path the call is fused into a
permute-reshape-then-scalar-GEMM kernel; on GPU it dispatches to the
MLX ``tensordot`` primitive.

**Math**

With ``axes = (axes_a, axes_b)`` and ``a``, ``b`` reshaped into
matrices $A' \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times K}$ and $B' \in \mathbb{R}^{K \times N}$
(free dims flattened to $M$ and $N$, contracted dims flattened to $K$),
$$
  C' = A' B', \qquad C = \mathrm{reshape}(C', \text{free}_a + \text{free}_b).
$$
Gradients flow back as two ``matmul``-equivalent contractions:
$$
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial A'} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial C'} {B'}^{\top}, \qquad
  \frac{\partial L}{\partial B'} = {A'}^{\top} \frac{\partial L}{\partial C'}.
$$

**Shape**

Let ``N = len(axes_a) = len(axes_b)``.  Then
$$
  \mathrm{ndim}(\text{out}) = \mathrm{ndim}(a) + \mathrm{ndim}(b) - 2N.
$$
The contracted-dim sizes must match pairwise:
``a.shape[axes_a[i]] == b.shape[axes_b[i]]`` for all ``i``.

**References**

NumPy ``numpy.tensordot`` semantics.

**See Also**

einsum_op, matmul_op

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Left operand.  Any rank, any floating dtype.  ``a->dtype()`` defines the result dtype.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Right operand.  Must share dtype and device with ``a``.

parameters.name: axes_a
parameters.description: Axis indices of ``a`` to contract over.  Negative indices count from the right and are normalised to ``[0, a.ndim)``.

parameters.name: axes_b
parameters.description: Axis indices of ``b`` to contract over, paired element-wise with ``axes_a``.  Must have the same length as ``axes_a``.

returns.description: Output of shape ``a.shape[free_a] ++ b.shape[free_b]`` where ``free_*`` denotes the axes of each operand that are NOT contracted. The output ``ndim`` equals ``a.ndim + b.ndim - 2 * len(axes_a)``.

raises.description: If ``axes_a.size() != axes_b.size()``, if any axis is out of range after normalisation, if a paired pair of axes has mismatched sizes, or if the CPU dtype is neither ``F32`` nor ``F64``.

examples:
Contract the trailing axis of ``a`` with the leading axis of ``b``
(a rank-3 by rank-3 contraction equivalent to ``a @ b`` over the last
two axes when both are 2-D):

auto a = randn({4, 5, 6});  // shape (4, 5, 6)
    auto b = randn({6, 7, 8});  // shape (6, 7, 8)
    auto c = tensordot_op(a, b, {2}, {0});  // shape (4, 5, 7, 8)
Two-axis contraction (full inner product over the trailing pair):

auto a = randn({3, 4, 5});           // shape (3, 4, 5)
    auto b = randn({4, 5, 7});           // shape (4, 5, 7)
    auto c = tensordot_op(a, b, {1, 2}, {0, 1});  // shape (3, 7)
notes:
* ``axes_a`` and ``axes_b`` are passed by value so the implementation
  may normalise negative indices in place.
* Inputs need not be contiguous; the CPU path materialises a permuted
  contiguous copy before the GEMM step.
* Saves both inputs and the axes specification (under the einsum
  lowering) for backward.

attributes.name: schema
attributes.description: ``"tensordot"``, ``AmpPolicy::Promote``.  Lowers to ``einsum`` when autograd is active.

members.name: cpu_available
members.signature: bool cpu_available()
members.summary: Whether the CPU backend is available — always ``true`` in tests.
members.extended:
Provided as a counterpart to :func:`gpu_available` so dtype-parameterised
fixtures can branch on device availability uniformly.  The CPU backend
(Accelerate / vDSP) is always present on the supported Apple Silicon
platforms targeted by Lucid, so this function unconditionally returns
``true``.

**See Also**

:func:`gpu_available`.

returns.description: Always ``true``.

members.name: cpu_eye
members.signature: int cpu_eye(int n, Dtype dtype)
members.summary: Create an :math:`N \times N` identity matrix on CPU.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper over :func:`eye_op` that produces a square identity with
the main diagonal set to ``1`` and all off-diagonal entries set to
``0``.  Used in linear-algebra tests as a multiplicative identity
(``A @ I == A``) or as a reference matrix.

**Math**

$$ I_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & i = j \\ 0 & i \ne j \end{cases} $$

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: Side length of the square identity matrix.  The resulting tensor has shape ``(n, n)``.

returns.description: Newly allocated ``(n, n)`` CPU tensor with ``1`` on the main diagonal and ``0`` elsewhere.

members.name: cpu_full
members.signature: int cpu_full(const int & shape, double value, Dtype dtype)
members.summary: Create a CPU tensor filled with a constant scalar value.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper over :func:`full_op` pinned to :attr:`Device::CPU` and
``requires_grad = false``.  The fill value is accepted as ``double``
and cast down to :paramref:`dtype` by the backend kernel.

**See Also**

:func:`cpu_zeros`, :func:`cpu_ones`.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: Scalar value broadcast to every element.  Cast to :paramref:`dtype` during fill.

returns.description: Newly allocated CPU tensor with all elements equal to :paramref:`value` (after dtype cast).

examples: .. code-block:: cpp

auto x = cpu_full({4}, 2.0);          // [2, 2, 2, 2] F32
    auto y = cpu_full({3}, -1.0, Dtype::F64);

members.name: cpu_ones
members.signature: int cpu_ones(const int & shape, Dtype dtype)
members.summary: Create a CPU tensor filled with ones.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper over :func:`ones_op` pinned to :attr:`Device::CPU` and
``requires_grad = false``.  Useful as a multiplicative identity input
or for tests that need a non-zero baseline.

**See Also**

:func:`cpu_zeros`, :func:`cpu_full`.

returns.description: Newly allocated CPU tensor with all elements set to ``1``.

members.name: cpu_zeros
members.signature: int cpu_zeros(const int & shape, Dtype dtype)
members.summary: Create a CPU tensor filled with zeros.
members.extended:
Thin wrapper over :func:`zeros_op` pinned to :attr:`Device::CPU` and
``requires_grad = false`` — the canonical "blank tensor" used as
fixture input for unit tests.

**See Also**

:func:`cpu_ones`, :func:`cpu_full`.

parameters.name: shape
parameters.description: Target tensor shape.  Empty shape produces a 0-dim scalar.

returns.description: Newly allocated CPU tensor of the given shape, with all elements set to ``0``.

examples: .. code-block:: cpp

auto x = cpu_zeros({3, 4});                  // F32 by default
    auto y = cpu_zeros({2}, Dtype::F64);         // double precision

members.name: gpu_available
members.signature: bool gpu_available()
members.summary: Whether the GPU backend (MLX / Metal) is available on the current host.
members.extended:
Probes :func:`backend::Dispatcher::for_device` with
:attr:`Device::GPU` and reports success / failure as a boolean.  A
runtime probe (rather than a compile-time constant) is required
because Lucid tests may be cross-compiled or run on machines without
a functioning Metal device.

**See Also**

:func:`cpu_available`.

returns.description: ``true`` if a GPU dispatcher can be constructed, ``false`` otherwise (no Metal device, missing MLX initialisation, etc.).

notes:
Catches every exception raised by the dispatcher lookup; treat the
negative result as advisory and skip GPU-only tests via
``GTEST_SKIP()`` when this returns ``false``.

members.name: grad_numel
members.signature: int grad_numel(const int & t)
members.summary: Number of elements stored in the tensor's gradient buffer.
members.extended:
Derived from :func:`CpuStorage::nbytes` divided by
:func:`dtype_size(t->dtype())`.  Returns ``0`` when no gradient has
been accumulated — same sentinel as a genuinely empty gradient, so
pair with :func:`has_grad` when the distinction matters.

**See Also**

:func:`has_grad`, :func:`grad_to_float_vec`.

parameters.name: t
parameters.description: The tensor whose gradient size is queried.

returns.description: Element count of the gradient buffer (``nbytes / dtype_size``), or ``0`` if the tensor has no gradient.

members.name: grad_to_float_vec
members.signature: int grad_to_float_vec(const int & t)
members.summary: Extract a tensor's accumulated gradient as a flat ``std::vector<float>``.
members.extended:
Mirror of :func:`to_float_vec` but reads from
:func:`TensorImpl::grad_storage` instead of :func:`TensorImpl::storage`.
Returns an empty vector when no gradient has been accumulated — the
caller is responsible for distinguishing that from a genuinely
zero-length gradient via :func:`has_grad` if needed.

**See Also**

:func:`has_grad`, :func:`grad_numel`, :func:`to_float_vec`.

parameters.name: t
parameters.description: The tensor whose gradient is read.  Must have already been touched by :func:`Engine::backward` (verify with :func:`has_grad`).

returns.description: Flat ``float`` view of the gradient buffer in row-major order. ``F64`` gradient storage is cast down element-wise.  Empty vector if the tensor has no gradient yet.

notes:
Assumes the gradient lives on the CPU (the normal :func:`Engine::backward`
path).  GPU-resident gradients would need an explicit
:func:`IBackend::to_cpu` transfer that this helper does *not* perform
— extend the helper if your test exercises that path.

members.name: has_grad
members.signature: bool has_grad(const int & t)
members.summary: Whether the tensor currently holds a populated gradient buffer.
members.extended:
Thin predicate over :func:`TensorImpl::grad_storage` — returns
``true`` once :func:`Engine::backward` has accumulated into ``t``,
``false`` for any tensor that has never received a backward signal
(including freshly constructed ``requires_grad = true`` tensors
before the first backward pass).

**See Also**

:func:`grad_to_float_vec`, :func:`grad_numel`.

parameters.name: t
parameters.description: Any tensor.  Need not have ``requires_grad = true``.

returns.description: ``true`` if ``t->grad_storage().has_value()``, ``false`` otherwise.

members.name: to_float_vec
members.signature: int to_float_vec(const int & t)
members.summary: Extract a :class:`TensorImpl` 's raw bytes as a flat ``std::vector<float>`` for numeric inspection in unit tests.
members.extended:
For CPU tensors, reinterprets the :class:`CpuStorage` buffer directly
— zero copy.  For GPU tensors, performs a one-shot
:func:`IBackend::to_cpu` transfer then reads.  Always returns the
"as-if F32" view, casting from the tensor's native dtype as needed
so the caller can compare without dtype branching.

**See Also**

:func:`grad_to_float_vec`, :func:`EXPECT_TENSOR_NEAR`.

parameters.name: t
parameters.description: The tensor to flatten.  Any shape, any device, dtype ``F32`` or ``F64``.

returns.description: Length equals ``t->numel()``.  Elements are in row-major order matching the tensor's logical layout.  ``F64`` storage is cast down element-wise to ``float``.

notes:
Test-only — production code never round-trips GPU→CPU just to read
values.  Use :class:`Storage` accessors or the public
:func:`Tensor::numpy` bridge instead.

The F64 → F32 narrowing is lossy by design: the matcher tolerance
(``tol`` parameters on the assertion macros) is expected to absorb
the rounding noise.

members.name: tile_op
members.signature: int tile_op(const int & a, int reps)
members.summary: Tile a tensor by repeating it along multiple axes.
members.extended:
``reps[d]`` specifies how many times to repeat along dimension ``d``.  If
``reps`` is longer than ``a.rank()``, the input is conceptually left-padded
with size-1 dimensions before tiling, so the output rank equals
``reps.size()``.

**Math**

$$ y_{[i_0, \ldots, i_{r-1}]} = x_{[i_0 \bmod D_0, \ldots, i_{r-1} \bmod D_{r-1}]} $$
where $D_d$ is the input's effective size along dimension $d$.

**Shape**

Let ``r = reps.size()`` and ``n = a.rank()``.  After left-padding ``a`` with
``max(r - n, 0)`` size-1 dims:
``out.shape[d] = a_padded.shape[d] * reps[d]`` for ``d in [0, max(r, n))``.

**See Also**

:func:`repeat_op` — single-axis, interleaved variant.

parameters.name: reps
parameters.description: Per-dimension repetition counts.  Each entry must be >= 1.  If ``reps.size() > a.rank()``, leading size-1 axes are virtually prepended to the input.

returns.description: Tiled tensor with rank ``max(reps.size(), a.rank())`` and ``out.shape[d] = a_padded.shape[d] * reps[d]``.

notes:
Backward sums the tiled gradient blocks via ``Dispatcher::tile_backward``,
which uses the padded input shape and the repetition counts to locate each
contribution.  Schema name ``"tile"``, ``AmpPolicy::KeepInput``.

members.name: topk_op
members.signature: int topk_op(const int & a, int k, int axis)
members.summary: Top-k values and their indices along an axis.
members.extended:
Selects the ``k`` largest elements along ``axis`` and returns them
together with their positions in the original tensor.

**See Also**

sort_op : Full sort (no truncation).
argsort_op : Sort indices only.

parameters.name: k
parameters.description: Number of top elements to return.  Must satisfy ``0 < k <= a.shape[axis]``.

returns.description: ``[values, indices]`` where both have shape equal to ``a.shape`` with ``axis`` reduced to ``k``.  ``values`` has the dtype of ``a``; ``indices`` has dtype I32.

notes:
``values`` is differentiable for F32 / F64 inputs via
``IndexScatterBackward``, which scatters the incoming gradient back to
the selected positions in the original tensor.  ``indices`` is never
differentiable.

members.name: trace_op
members.signature: int trace_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Compute the trace (sum of main-diagonal elements) of a matrix or a batch of matrices.
members.extended:
For a 2-D input ``a`` of shape ``[m, n]`` the result is a scalar
$\mathrm{tr}(a) = \sum_i a_{ii}$ where $i$ ranges over
$\min(m, n)$.  For a higher-rank input of shape
``[b_0, ..., b_{k-1}, m, n]`` the last two dimensions are
contracted: each $[m, n]$ slice produces one scalar, and the result
has shape ``[b_0, ..., b_{k-1}]``.

Backward (2-D only) scatters $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial y$ to
each diagonal position of a zero-filled matrix of the same shape
as ``a``:
$\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial a_{ij} =
\mathbb{1}[i = j]\cdot \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial y$.
For batched inputs (``ndim > 2``) the output is returned without an
autograd edge — attempts to backpropagate through batched
:func:`trace_op` will silently produce no gradient.

**Math**

2-D forward and backward:
$$
  y = \sum_{i} a_{ii}, \qquad
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial a_{ij}} =
  \mathbb{1}[i = j]\cdot
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial y}.
$$

Batched forward (no offset, no autograd):
$$
  y_{b_0 \dots b_{k-1}} = \sum_{i}
  a_{b_0 \dots b_{k-1}, i, i}.
$$

**Shape**

- Input  ``[m, n]``                          → output ``[]``
- Input  ``[b_0, ..., b_{k-1}, m, n]``       → output
  ``[b_0, ..., b_{k-1}]``

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of any dtype, with ``ndim >= 2``.  Non-null.

returns.description: Scalar (``[]``) for 2-D inputs, or a tensor of shape ``a.shape[2:]`` for batched inputs.

raises.description: If ``a`` is null or has ``ndim < 2``.

notes:
Diagonal offset (analogous to NumPy's ``trace(offset=...)``) is not
currently exposed; only the main diagonal is summed.  Autograd is
wired only when ``ndim == 2`` — the batched path returns a plain
non-differentiable tensor by design.

members.name: transpose_op
members.signature: int transpose_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Reverse all axes of ``a``: equivalent to ``permute(a, [ndim-1, …, 1, 0])``.
members.extended:
**See Also**

T_op : Alias used to implement the ``.T`` property.
mT_op : Batch-matrix-transpose variant that only swaps the last two
    axes.

returns.description: View of ``a`` with reversed axis order.

members.name: tril_op
members.signature: int tril_op(const int & a, int k)
members.summary: Lower-triangular part of a matrix (or batch of matrices).
members.extended:
Zeros every element strictly above the ``k``-th diagonal of the last
two axes of ``a``.  The main diagonal is ``k = 0``; positive ``k``
shifts the diagonal toward the upper-right (keeping more elements);
negative ``k`` toward the lower-left (keeping fewer).

**Math**

$$\mathrm{out}_{\ldots,i,j} = \begin{cases}
    a_{\ldots,i,j} & \text{if } j - i \le k \\
    0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

**See Also**

triu_op : Upper-triangular counterpart.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of rank $\ge 2$.  The mask is applied to the trailing two axes; leading axes are treated as batch dimensions.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` with the upper triangle (relative to ``k``) zeroed.

notes:
Backward applies ``tril`` with the same ``k`` to the incoming gradient,
preserving only the gradient at positions retained in the forward pass.

members.name: triu_op
members.signature: int triu_op(const int & a, int k)
members.summary: Upper-triangular part of a matrix (or batch of matrices).
members.extended:
Zeros every element strictly below the ``k``-th diagonal of the last
two axes of ``a``.  Diagonal offset convention matches :func:`tril_op`.

**Math**

$$\mathrm{out}_{\ldots,i,j} = \begin{cases}
    a_{\ldots,i,j} & \text{if } j - i \ge k \\
    0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

**See Also**

tril_op : Lower-triangular counterpart.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a`` with the lower triangle (relative to ``k``) zeroed.

notes: Backward applies ``triu`` with the same ``k`` to the incoming gradient.

members.name: trunc_op
members.signature: int trunc_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Round toward zero: $y = \mathrm{trunc}(x) = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \lfloor |x| \rfloor$.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`greater_equal_op` + :func:`where_op` +
:func:`floor_op` + :func:`ceil_op`.  Picks floor for non-negative
inputs and ceil for negatives.  Both branches are piecewise constant,
so the gradient contribution is **zero** (matches the reference
framework's convention).

**Math**

$$
  y = \begin{cases}
    \lfloor x \rfloor & x \ge 0 \\
    \lceil x \rceil & x < 0
  \end{cases}, \qquad \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} = 0
$$

**See Also**

:func:`floor_op`, :func:`ceil_op`, :func:`frac_op`.

returns.description: Tensor of the same shape and dtype as ``a``.

members.name: unbind_op
members.signature: int unbind_op(const int & a, int axis)
members.summary: Unbind a tensor into a list of slices along an axis, dropping that axis.
members.extended:
Produces ``a.shape[axis]`` tensors, each with rank ``ndim - 1``.  The
k-th output is ``a[..., k, ...]`` along ``axis`` with the split dimension
squeezed away.

**See Also**

:func:`stack_op` (the inverse), :func:`split_op`.

parameters.name: axis
parameters.description: Axis to unbind along.  Supports negative indexing.

returns.description: ``a.shape[axis]`` tensors of rank ``ndim - 1``.

notes:
Backward inserts the gradient slice back at offset ``k`` along ``axis``
(after unsqueezing) before scattering into the input gradient.

members.name: unflatten_op
members.signature: int unflatten_op(const int & a, int dim, const int & sizes)
members.summary: Inverse of ``flatten`` — split ``dim`` into ``sizes``.
members.extended:
Composite over :func:`reshape_op`.  The product of ``sizes`` must equal
``a.shape[dim]``; the surrounding axes are preserved verbatim.  Pure
view operation when the input is contiguous; gradient flows through
``ReshapeBackward``.

**Math**

Let ``a`` have shape $(s_0, \ldots, s_{d-1}, s_d, s_{d+1}, \ldots)$ and
``sizes`` $= (m_0, \ldots, m_{k-1})$ with $\prod_i m_i = s_d$.  Then the
output has shape
$$
  (s_0, \ldots, s_{d-1}, m_0, \ldots, m_{k-1}, s_{d+1}, \ldots)
$$

**See Also**

:func:`reshape_op` — the underlying primitive operates on the full shape.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Axis to expand.  Negative values wrap modulo ``a.ndim``.

parameters.name: sizes
parameters.description: Replacement extents.  Their product must equal ``a.shape[dim]``.

returns.description: Reshaped tensor whose total ``numel`` matches ``a``.

raises.description: If ``a`` is null or $\prod \mathrm{sizes} \ne \mathrm{a.shape}[\mathrm{dim}]$.
If ``dim`` is out of range.

members.name: unfold_dim_op
members.signature: int unfold_dim_op(const int & a, int dim, int size, int step)
members.summary: Sliding-window view of ``a`` along a single dimension.
members.extended:
**Shape**

Adds a trailing ``size``-sized axis; the original ``dim`` is replaced
by the number of windows ``L``.

returns.description: Tensor whose shape is ``(*in_shape[:dim], L, *in_shape[dim+1:], size)`` where $L = \lfloor (\text{in\_shape}[\text{dim}] - \text{size}) / \text{step} \rfloor + 1$.

notes:
Behaves as a view; backward passes through a ``gather`` that
accumulates overlapping windows back into the input.

members.name: unfold_op
members.signature: int unfold_op(const int & x, const int & kernel, const int & stride, const int & pad, const int & dilation)
members.summary: Public entry point for Unfold (im2col).
members.extended:
Functional wrapper around ``UnfoldBackward::forward`` for building
custom convolution-like ops in user code.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Input of shape ``(B, C, S_0, ..., S_{N-1})``.

returns.description: Patch-as-columns tensor of shape ``(B, C * prod(K), prod(O))``.

members.name: uniform_op
members.signature: int uniform_op(const int & shape, double low, double high, Dtype dt, Device device, Generator * gen)
members.summary: Allocates a tensor filled with uniform samples on $[\mathrm{low}, \mathrm{high})$.
members.extended:
**Math**

$$x_i \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathrm{low}, \mathrm{high})$$

**See Also**

:func:`rand_op` — convenience shortcut for $[0, 1)$.

parameters.name: low
parameters.description: Inclusive lower bound of the support.

parameters.name: high
parameters.description: Exclusive upper bound of the support.  Must be strictly greater than ``low``.

parameters.name: gen
parameters.description: Bit-stream state; falls back to :func:`default_generator` on ``nullptr``.

returns.description: Newly allocated leaf tensor (``requires_grad = false``).

raises.description: If ``high <= low`` — surfaced as ``ValueError`` on the Python side.

members.name: unique_op
members.signature: int unique_op(const int & a)
members.summary: Unique sorted elements of a tensor.
members.extended:
Flattens ``a``, removes duplicates, and returns the result sorted in
ascending order as a 1-D tensor.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor.  Supported dtypes: F32, F64, I32, I64.

returns.description: 1-D tensor of the same dtype as ``a`` containing the unique elements in sorted order.  Always resides on ``Device::CPU`` because the output length is data-dependent.

notes: Non-differentiable.

members.name: unsqueeze_op
members.signature: int unsqueeze_op(const int & a, int dim)
members.summary: Insert a new size-1 dimension at a given position.
members.extended:
The new axis is added before position ``dim`` in the output, so the output
rank is ``ndim + 1``.  Negative ``dim`` wraps relative to the output rank
(so ``dim = -1`` inserts before the last existing dimension).

**See Also**

:func:`squeeze_op`, :func:`reshape_op`.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: Insertion position.  Must satisfy ``-(ndim + 1) <= dim <= ndim``.

returns.description: A view with rank ``ndim + 1`` and a size-1 dimension inserted at ``dim``.

raises.description: If ``dim`` is out of range.

examples:
A length-5 1-D tensor unsqueezed at ``dim=0`` becomes a ``(1, 5)`` view;
unsqueezed at ``dim=1`` becomes a ``(5, 1)`` view.

members.name: var_op
members.signature: int var_op(const int & a, const int & axes, bool keepdims)
members.summary: Compute the biased variance of ``a`` along ``axes``.
members.extended:
Uses the population estimator
$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ — i.e. the
denominator is $N$, not $N - 1$.  This corresponds to the reference
framework's ``unbiased=False`` (population) variant; the unbiased /
sample variant is not exposed by this entry point.  The standard
deviation :func:`std_op` is built directly on top by composing
``sqrt`` with this op so the autograd chain is automatic.

During the forward pass the mean is computed once with
``keepdims=true`` and broadcast to the full input shape so that
``VarBackward::apply`` can compute $x - \bar{x}$ without a second
broadcast call.  Both the input ``x`` and the broadcast mean are
saved on the backward node.

**Math**

$$
  \sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i \in \text{axes}}
  (x_i - \bar{x})^2, \qquad
  \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_i} =
  \frac{2}{N}(x_i - \bar{x})\cdot
  \mathrm{broadcast}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}
  {\partial \sigma^2}\right).
$$

**Shape**

If ``keepdims`` is ``false``, the reduced axes are removed; if
``true``, they become size-$1$.

**See Also**

:func:`std_op` — square root of the variance.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Input tensor of float dtype.  Non-null.

parameters.name: axes
parameters.description: Axes to reduce.  May be empty (reduce all axes).  Negative indices wrap around ``ndim``.

parameters.name: keepdims
parameters.description: If ``true``, retains reduced dims as size-1 entries; otherwise they are collapsed.

returns.description: Output tensor with the reduced shape.  Autograd is wired when ``a->requires_grad()`` is true and ``GradMode`` is enabled.

notes:
Internal dispatch: ``IBackend::variance`` — Accelerate on CPU, MLX
on GPU.  The CPU implementation uses Welford-style accumulation for
numerical stability on large $N$.  The empty-axis edge case
(``reduced == 0``) clamps the count to $1$ to avoid a division by
zero in the backward node; in that pathological case the gradient is
not statistically meaningful but is well-defined.

members.name: vstack_op
members.signature: int vstack_op(const int & xs)
members.summary: Vertically stack a sequence of tensors.
members.extended:
Equivalent to NumPy ``vstack``: 1-D inputs of length ``N`` are promoted to
row vectors and stacked into a 2-D tensor of shape ``(len(xs), N)``;
inputs with rank >= 2 are concatenated along axis 0.

**See Also**

:func:`hstack_op`, :func:`stack_op`.

parameters.name: xs
parameters.description: Non-empty list of tensors.  For rank >= 2, every dim except axis 0 must agree; for 1-D inputs, lengths must agree.

returns.description: Vertically stacked tensor with rank ``max(2, input_rank)``.

members.name: where_op
members.signature: int where_op(const int & cond, const int & x, const int & y)
members.summary: Element-wise conditional selection between two tensors.
members.extended:
Returns ``x`` at positions where ``cond`` is true, otherwise ``y``.
This is the tensor analogue of the ternary expression
``cond ? x : y`` applied element-wise.

**Math**

$$\mathrm{out}_i = \begin{cases} x_i & \text{if } \mathrm{cond}_i \\
y_i & \text{otherwise}\end{cases}$$

**Shape**

On the GPU stream all three operands are broadcast by the MLX backend.
On the CPU stream all three must share the same shape.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: Values selected at positions where ``cond`` is true.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: Values selected at positions where ``cond`` is false.

returns.description: Tensor of the broadcast shape and dtype of ``x``/``y``.

notes:
Backward propagates ``grad_out`` masked by ``cond`` to ``x`` and masked
by ``~cond`` to ``y``.  ``cond`` itself receives no gradient.
